topics.title: Introduction: Definitions of Fishing Vessel, Special Features of Fishing Vessels, Regulations for the Safety of Fishing Vessels, Classification of Fishing Vessels, Characteristics of Fish Ground
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction: Definitions of Fishing Vessel, Special Features of Fishing Vessels, Regulations for the Safety of Fishing Vessels, Classification of Fishing Vessels, Characteristics of Fish Ground

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of a Fishing Vessel

A **Fishing Vessel** is any vessel (boat, ship, or other craft) that is used for catching fish, squid, crustaceans, molluscs, or other living resources of the sea, including vessels that process or transship the catch on board, and vessels that provide support or supply services to other fishing vessels. This is the working definition adopted by the **Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO)** of the United Nations and broadly incorporated into the **International Convention for the Safety of Fishing Vessels (Cape Town Agreement, 2012)** and the **Indian Merchant Shipping Act, 1958 (as amended)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Verbatim)**
> A *fishing vessel* is a vessel used commercially for catching, harvesting, or capturing fish, shellfish, or other aquatic organisms from the sea or inland waters, including any vessel engaged in onboard processing, transshipment, or support of such operations, but does not include vessels primarily used for sport, recreation, or research.

**Auxiliary Definitions (Linked Concepts)**

- **Mother Ship / Factory Vessel:** A larger fishing vessel that processes and stores the catch received from smaller catcher boats.
- **Fish Catching Vessel:** A vessel directly engaged in harvesting fish.
- **Fish Carrier:** A refrigerated vessel that transports processed or unprocessed fish from the catching vessel to shore.
- **Fishery Research Vessel:** A non-commercial vessel used to study fish stocks, gear behaviour, and oceanographic conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy**
> Think of a fishing vessel as a *floating factory combined with a tractor*. A tractor on a farm is built for one job (ploughing, harvesting), and a fishing vessel is also built for one specialised job — catching fish in often hostile, remote waters. But unlike a tractor, it must also **store, preserve, and sometimes even process** its "harvest" (the fish), all while withstanding storms, saltwater corrosion, and long voyages away from ports. The deck is the "workshop," the fish hold is the "cold storage," and the wheelhouse is the "control cabin."

### 1.2 Definition of a Fish Ground

A **Fish Ground** (also called *fishing ground* or *fishery ground*) is a geographically defined area of water — coastal, offshore, or deep-sea — where commercial concentrations of fish or other aquatic species are consistently found in quantities that justify commercial exploitation. It is determined by a combination of **bathymetric, oceanographic, and biological factors** such as depth, bottom type, temperature, salinity, currents, and the availability of food organisms.

### 1.3 Definition of Classification (in the Context of Fishing Vessels)

In naval architecture, **classification of fishing vessels** refers to the systematic grouping of vessels based on:
1. **Fishing method** (gear employed — trawl, seine, gillnet, longline, etc.)
2. **Size / length overall (LOA)**
3. **Operational range** (inshore, offshore, distant-water)
4. **Hull construction material**
5. **Method of propulsion**

In a separate but related sense, **Classification Society Classification** (e.g., Indian Register of Shipping, DNV, Lloyd's Register) refers to a system of standards (called *class rules*) governing the design, construction, and maintenance of the vessel for safety and seaworthiness.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Special Features of Fishing Vessels

Fishing vessels differ from conventional cargo ships in several deliberate design features. These features emerge from the dual role of the vessel as a *catcher* and a *processor/storage unit*.

#### 2.1.1 Hull Form & Stability
- **High freeboard forward** with a pronounced **sheer line** to take boarding seas and prevent green-water flooding on the working deck.
- **Tumblehome** or flared bow to provide a large clear working deck area forward.
- **Wide, open, and unobstructed working deck** (clear of bulwarks where possible) for net handling and catch discharge.
- **Rounded, raised forecastle** to give the crew a dry area, and often a **sheltered poop deck** aft.
- **Sliding ways, stern ramp, or side door** for trawlers to haul the gear (cod-end) directly aboard.

#### 2.1.2 Operational & Mechanical Features
- **Powerful deck machinery**: trawl winches, net haulers, line haulers, gurdy, drum winches, purse-seine skiff davits.
- **Auxiliary power take-off (PTO)** from the main engine to drive hydraulic pumps for winches.
- **Refrigerated fish holds (RSW — Refrigerated Sea Water)** with insulated boundaries and cooling coils.
- **Slush-ice tanks** or **CSW (Chilled Sea Water)** systems for rapid pre-cooling of the catch.
- **High manoeuvrability** — short stopping distance, small turning circle — to maintain fishing stations and avoid gear loss.

#### 2.1.3 Structural Features
- **Heavy local reinforcement** at deck knees, bulwarks, mast steps, and winch foundations to absorb large dynamic loads during gear hauling.
- **Corrosion-resistant coatings and sacrificial anodes (cathodic protection)** because of constant exposure to salt water, fish blood, and brine.
- **Non-slip deck plating** in the working deck area.
- **Higher metacentric height (GM)** than cargo vessels, since the fishing operation is highly dynamic (free-surface effect of water in wells, swinging gear, etc.).

#### 2.1.4 Crew Habitability & Safety
- **Compact but adequate accommodation** — cabins, galley, mess room, sanitary blocks.
- **Life-saving appliances** strictly as per international convention (life-rafts, life-jackets, EPIRB, SART).
- **Wheelhouse with 360° visibility** and good lighting for night fishing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility**
> A naval architect who designs a fishing vessel is effectively designing a *floating micro-industry*. Every design decision — bulbous bow, propeller duct, hull form, gear layout, hold volume — directly affects the vessel's **catch efficiency, fuel economy, crew safety, and profitability**.

### 2.2 Regulations for the Safety of Fishing Vessels

#### 2.2.1 International Conventions & Codes

| Regulation / Code | Year | Issuing Body | Salient Provisions |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Torremolinos International Convention for the Safety of Fishing Vessels** | 1977 | IMO | First comprehensive international code; construction, machinery, electrical, fire protection, life-saving. |
| **Cape Town Agreement** | 2012 | IMO | Modern successor to Torremolinos; applies to vessels of **24 m and above**; entered into force in 2020. |
| **FAO/ILO/IMO Code of Safety for Fishermen and Fishing Vessels** | 2005 | FAO/ILO/IMO | Voluntary code covering vessels **< 24 m** (Part A) and **≥ 24 m** (Part B). |
| **ILO Work in Fishing Convention (C188)** | 2007 | ILO | Working conditions, hours of rest, crew accommodation, occupational safety. |
| **STCW-F (International Convention on Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping for Fishing Vessel Personnel)** | 1995 | IMO | Mandatory training & certification for fishing vessel crews. |

#### 2.2.2 Indian Regulations
- **Indian Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** (with subsequent amendments) — provides the parent legislation.
- **Indian Fishing Vessels Rules** — administered by the **Directorate General of Shipping (DGS), Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways**.
- **Registration of Fishing Vessels** under the **State Marine Fishing Regulation Acts (MFRA)** of coastal states (Kerala, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, etc.).
- **Classification Rules** issued by the **Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)**.
- **Coast Guard & Marine Police** enforce safety, registration, and seaworthiness norms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield**
> For the 2024 scheme exam, the student must remember the *two key IMO instruments*: **Torremolinos (1977)** and the **Cape Town Agreement (2012)**. Cape Town is the *currently enforced* global instrument for fishing vessels ≥ 24 m LOA.

### 2.3 Classification of Fishing Vessels

#### 2.3.1 By Fishing Method (Gear)

| Type | Gear Used | Typical LOA | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trawler** (Side / Stern) | Otter trawl, beam trawl | 18 – 45 m | Powerful winches; stern ramp or side door |
| **Seiner** (Purse / Danish) | Purse seine net | 20 – 60 m | Power block, skiff, large net drum |
| **Gillnetter / Drifter** | Static gill nets | 8 – 25 m | Net hauler, net stacker |
| **Long Liner** | Baited hooks on long lines | 15 – 50 m | Line hauler, baiting machine, freezer |
| **Troller** | Trolling lines | 7 – 25 m | Outriggers, rod-holders |
| **Dredger** | Dredges for shellfish | 15 – 30 m | Dredge winches, conveyor systems |
| **Pole & Line Vessel** | Pole and live bait | 15 – 50 m | Spraying system, water tanks for bait |
| **Squid Jigger** | Automatic jigging lines | 30 – 70 m | Jigging machines, strong lights, freezers |

#### 2.3.2 By Operational Range

| Class | Typical Range | LOA | Voyage Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inshore / Coastal** | Within EEZ coastal waters | 5 – 15 m | < 24 hours |
| **Offshore / Near-shore** | Up to 200 NM | 15 – 24 m | 1 – 7 days |
| **Deep-Sea** | Beyond 200 NM, within EEZ | 24 – 35 m | 1 – 4 weeks |
| **Distant-Water** | International waters | 35 – 100+ m | Months |

#### 2.3.3 By Hull Construction Material
- **Wood** — traditional, low cost, used in artisanal craft.
- **Steel** — standard for vessels > 24 m.
- **Aluminium alloy** — fast craft, light displacement, corrosion-resistant.
- **FRP / GRP** (Fibre-Reinforced Plastic) — predominant in modern small and medium craft.
- **Ferro-cement** — niche, low-cost applications.

#### 2.3.4 By Propulsion
- **Diesel-mechanical** (most common)
- **Diesel-electric**
- **Sail-assisted / Hybrid** (eco-friendly modern designs)

### 2.4 Characteristics of a Fish Ground

The performance of a fishing vessel is intimately tied to the **characteristics of the fish ground** it is designed to exploit. A vessel that operates on a smooth, sandy continental shelf behaves very differently from one that works on a rocky, deep seamount.

#### 2.4.1 Bathymetric Characteristics
- **Depth**: 5 – 2000 m depending on target species.
- **Bottom topography**: flat, undulating, seamount, canyon, shelf edge, ridge.
- **Continental shelf width** and **gradient (slope)**.

#### 2.4.2 Bottom-Type Characteristics
| Bottom Type | Common Species | Gear Suitability |
|---|---|---|
| Sandy | Shrimp, flounder, prawn | Trawl, dredge |
| Muddy | Shrimp, eel, catfish | Trawl, long line |
| Rocky / Coral | Lobster, grouper, snapper | Trap, hand line, gillnet |
| Mixed (sand-mud) | Mixed demersal species | Otter trawl |
| Seamount / Reef | Pelagic and demersal | Long line, troll, purse seine |

#### 2.4.3 Oceanographic Characteristics
- **Sea surface temperature (SST)** — typically **4 °C – 30 °C** depending on region.
- **Salinity** — usually **33 – 37 PSU**.
- **Dissolved oxygen (DO)** — minimum 4 mg/L for most commercial species.
- **Currents** — upwelling zones, frontal zones (where warm and cold water meet) are the *most productive* fish grounds.
- **Chlorophyll-a concentration** — proxy for phytoplankton and hence fish food availability.

#### 2.4.4 Biological & Ecological Characteristics
- **Species composition** (single-species vs. multi-species ground).
- **Stock density** (kg per km²) and **biomass**.
- **Seasonal migration patterns** of the target species.
- **Spawning and nursery grounds** — usually protected by regulation.
- **Predator-prey dynamics** and **food chain structure**.

#### 2.4.5 Logistical Characteristics
- **Distance from port** — affects fuel cost, hold capacity, and crew rotation.
- **Weather window** — number of fishing days per year.
- **Proximity to processing infrastructure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in design**
> A vessel designed for a *rocky coral ground* (say, the Lakshadweep Islands for tuna long-lining) must have **corrosion-resistant hull plating**, **reinforced rudder and propeller** (for fouling and strike risk), and **strong, variable-speed winches**. In contrast, a vessel designed for *soft muddy shrimp grounds* (say, the Kerala backwaters) needs **efficient trawl gear**, **plough-type doors**, and a **stern ramp**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although this topic is largely descriptive, a few **dimensional and stability** relations are repeatedly asked in KTU university exams. They are tabulated below.

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LOA** | Length Overall | **m** | Used in Cape Town Agreement applicability (≥ 24 m). |
| **LBP** | Length Between Perpendiculars | **m** | Used in hull form and resistance calculation. |
| **B** | Breadth (moulded) | **m** | Used in block coefficient. |
| **D** | Depth (moulded) | **m** | Used in freeboard and stability. |
| **T** | Draft (design) | **m** | Limits fish ground depth. |
| **C_B** | Block coefficient $C_B = \nabla \div (L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T)$ | 0.50 – 0.65 (trawlers), 0.55 – 0.70 (seiners) | Hull fullness indicator. |
| **C_P** | Prismatic coefficient | 0.60 – 0.72 | Hull form efficiency. |
| **GM** | Metacentric height | **m** (typically **0.6 – 1.2 m** for trawlers) | Intact stability. |
| **GZ** | Righting lever at angle θ | **m** | Damage / large-angle stability. |
| **Area 0–30** | Area under GZ curve from 0° to 30° | **m·rad** (≥ 0.055 m·rad for Cape Town) | IMO intact-stability weather criterion. |
| **Area 30–40** | Area from 30° to 40° | **m·rad** (≥ 0.030 m·rad) | Residual stability. |
| **θ_max** | Angle of vanishing stability | **degrees** (≥ 30° for Cape Town) | Final stability. |
| **SST** | Sea Surface Temperature | **°C** | Indicator of fish ground. |
| **PSU** | Practical Salinity Unit | dimensionless | Oceanographic indicator. |
| **DO** | Dissolved Oxygen | **mg/L** (≥ 4 mg/L) | Habitat indicator. |
| **Hull Material Density (ρ)** | Steel ≈ 7.85, Aluminium ≈ 2.70, FRP ≈ 1.50 – 1.80 | **t/m³** | Lightweight design. |
| **Beam / Draft ratio (B/T)** | Stability indicator | 2.0 – 2.6 typical | Initial stability. |
| **Length / Beam ratio (L/B)** | Speed and manoeuvrability | 4.0 – 6.0 for trawlers | Hull proportion. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Because Module 1 is largely **descriptive and regulatory**, the derivations here are the **mathematical and analytical sub-concepts** that examiners frequently attach to descriptive questions. Each derivation below is **fully worked out** for direct KTU exam use.

### 3.1 Derivation: Block Coefficient of a Fishing Vessel

The **block coefficient $C_B$** quantifies the fullness of the underwater hull. For a fishing vessel, $C_B$ is critical because it directly affects the *fuel consumption* and *stability* of the working platform.

**Step 1 — Volume Displacement**
The displaced volume $\nabla$ of a vessel in cubic metres is obtained by integrating the cross-sectional area $A(x)$ along the length of the waterline:

$$
\nabla = \int_{0}^{L_{BP}} A(x) \, dx
$$

**Step 2 — Reference Volume of the Rectangular Prism**
The reference volume is the volume of a rectangular box that just encloses the underwater hull of length $L_{BP}$, breadth $B$ and draft $T$:

$$
V_{ref} = L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Block Coefficient**
By definition, the block coefficient is the ratio of the displaced volume to the reference volume:

$$
C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange for Displacement**
Multiply both sides by the denominator to obtain displacement $\nabla$ in terms of $C_B$:

$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T
$$

**Step 5 — KTU Exam Use**
The student will frequently be asked: *"Estimate the displacement of a trawler having $L_{BP} = 30\,\text{m}$, $B = 8\,\text{m}$, $T = 3.5\,\text{m}$ and $C_B = 0.55$."*

Substitute:

$$
\nabla = 0.55 \times 30 \times 8 \times 3.5
$$

$$
\nabla = 0.55 \times 840 = 462\,\text{m}^3
$$

Since salt water density $\rho_{sw} \approx 1.025\,\text{t/m}^3$, the displacement in tonnes is:

$$
\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 462 \approx 473.6\,\text{tonnes}
$$

> [!TIP]
> A higher $C_B$ means a *fuller* hull — slower but more stable and fuel-efficient for trawling. A lower $C_B$ means a *finer* hull — faster, suitable for seiners and long-liners chasing migratory species.

### 3.2 Derivation: Condition for Initial Stability (GM > 0)

For a fishing vessel operating in beam seas, the **metacentric height $GM$** must be positive. The classical derivation follows.

**Step 1 — Couple of Buoyancy Shift**
When the vessel heels by a small angle $\theta$, the centre of buoyancy $B$ shifts laterally to a new point $B'$. The vertical line through the new $B'$ intersects the centreline at the metacentre $M$.

**Step 2 — Define $BM$ (Metacentric Radius)**
By metacentric theory:

$$
BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}
$$

where $I_T$ is the **transverse second moment of waterline area** and $\nabla$ is the displaced volume.

For a rectangular waterplane (a good approximation for trawlers):

$$
I_T = \frac{L_{BP} \cdot B^3}{12}
$$

Therefore:

$$
BM = \frac{L_{BP} \cdot B^3}{12 \, \nabla}
$$

**Step 3 — Define $GM$**
The metacentric height is:

$$
GM = KB + BM - KG
$$

where $KB$ is the height of the buoyancy centre above the keel and $KG$ is the height of the centre of gravity above the keel.

**Step 4 — Stability Criterion**
For initial positive stability:

$$
GM > 0
$$

Substituting the expression for $BM$:

$$
\frac{L_{BP} \cdot B^3}{12 \, \nabla} - (KG - KB) > 0
$$

**Step 5 — KTU Exam Form**
A typical question: *"For a fishing vessel, $L_{BP} = 28\,\text{m}$, $B = 7.5\,\text{m}$, $\nabla = 400\,\text{m}^3$, $KB = 1.8\,\text{m}$, $KG = 3.0\,\text{m}$. Find $GM$ and comment on the stability."*

Calculate $BM$:

$$
BM = \frac{28 \times 7.5^3}{12 \times 400} = \frac{28 \times 421.875}{4800}
$$

$$
BM = \frac{11812.5}{4800} \approx 2.46\,\text{m}
$$

Calculate $GM$:

$$
GM = KB + BM - KG = 1.8 + 2.46 - 3.0 = 1.26\,\text{m}
$$

Since $GM > 0$, the vessel has positive initial stability. For a fishing vessel, a $GM$ of **0.6 m to 1.5 m** is considered healthy.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Vessel Type vs. Fish-Ground Characteristic

The matrix below links each fish-ground characteristic to the **vessel feature** it dictates. It is the most common 14-mark KTU Part-B question structure for this module.

| Fish-Ground Characteristic | Vessel Feature Required | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Soft muddy bottom (e.g., prawn grounds) | Otter-board trawl, plough shoes | Reduces snagging; efficient sediment penetration. |
| Rocky / coral bottom | Reinforced rudder, protected propeller, strong winches | Resists strike damage. |
| Shallow shelf (< 50 m) | Low-draft vessel, small propeller diameter | Prevents grounding; lower tip-cavitation risk. |
| Deep shelf (200 – 1000 m) | High-power winch, large net drum, strong warps | Long warp scope = high tension. |
| Variable SST (4 – 28 °C) | Insulated RSW holds with variable T control | Maintains catch quality. |
| Upwelling / frontal zones (high productivity) | High fish-hold capacity, fast catch-handling system | Maximises earnings per trip. |
| Distant-water fish ground | Long endurance fuel tanks, freezer hold | Trips last 30 – 90 days. |
| Coastal / artisanal ground | Open wooden/FRP craft, outboard engine | Low cost, manoeuvrable. |
| Stormy, high-sea-area ground | Higher freeboard, sheer, twin engine | Stability and survivability. |
| Multi-species (mixed) ground | Versatile gear, larger holds with partitions | Flexibility in catch. |

### 3.4 Decision Tree: Applicability of the Cape Town Agreement

A frequently asked KTU question: *"Does the Cape Town Agreement (2012) apply to a given fishing vessel?"*

**Step 1 — Identify the LOA.**
If $\text{LOA} < 24\,\text{m}$, the Cape Town Agreement **does not apply**; the FAO/ILO/IMO Voluntary Code (Part A) is the relevant reference.

If $\text{LOA} \geq 24\,\text{m}$, **proceed to Step 2**.

**Step 2 — Is the vessel engaged in commercial fishing?**
- If **No** (research, recreational, or military vessel) → Cape Town does not apply.
- If **Yes** → **proceed to Step 3**.

**Step 3 — Is the vessel registered in a flag state that has ratified the Agreement?**
- If **Yes** → Cape Town is **legally binding**; the vessel must comply with construction, machinery, electrical, fire protection, life-saving, and radio provisions.
- If **No** → Only the *deemed-to-comply* standards of the flag state apply, but best practice is to follow Cape Town.

This decision tree, written in compact form, is:

$$
\text{CT Applicability} = (\text{LOA} \geq 24\,\text{m}) \,\wedge\, (\text{Commercial Fishing}) \,\wedge\, (\text{Flag Ratification})
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The four diagrams below are drawn in Mermaid syntax. They summarise the *function*, the *regulatory framework*, the *classification logic*, and the *fish-ground influence* on fishing-vessel design. They are board-exam-ready.

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Modern Fishing Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wheelhouse] --> B[Bridge Equipment: Radar, GPS, Echo Sounder, Autopilot]
    A --> C[Communication: VHF, SSB, EPIRB, SART]
    D[Main Engine] --> E[Gearbox] --> F[Propeller]
    D --> G[Power Take Off] --> H[Hydraulic Pumps]
    H --> I[Winches: Trawl, Net Hauler, Line Hauler]
    I --> J[Working Deck: Net Handling, Catch Discharge]
    J --> K[Fish Hold: RSW, CSW, Frozen Storage]
    K --> L[Refrigeration Plant]
    M[Generator] --> N[Electrical Distribution]
    N --> O[Lighting, Navigation, Fish Finding]
    A --> P[Crew Accommodation]
    A --> Q[Life-Saving Appliances]
    A --> R[Safety & Fire-Fighting Systems]
```

### 4.2 Regulatory Framework for Fishing Vessels

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fishing Vessel Safety Regulation] --> B{Vessel LOA}
    B -->|Less than 24 m| C[FAO/ILO/IMO Voluntary Code Part A]
    B -->|24 m and above| D{Cape Town Agreement 2012}
    D -->|Flag State Ratified| E[Legally Binding]
    D -->|Flag State Not Ratified| F[Best Practice Compliance]
    C --> G[ILO C188: Work in Fishing]
    E --> G
    E --> H[STCW-F Training Standards]
    G --> I[Indian MS Act 1958]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Indian Fishing Vessels Rules]
    I --> K[IRS Classification Rules]
    I --> L[State Marine Fishing Regulation Acts]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Q1.a]**
> **Q1. Define a fishing vessel as per the FAO/IMO definition. State two auxiliary types of fishing vessels.**
> **Model Answer:**
> A fishing vessel is any vessel used commercially for catching fish, shellfish, or other aquatic organisms from the sea or inland waters, and includes vessels engaged in on-board processing, transshipment, or supply of other fishing vessels. It excludes vessels used solely for sport, recreation, or scientific research.
> **Two auxiliary types:**
> 1. **Mother-ship / factory vessel** — receives and processes catch from smaller catcher boats.
> 2. **Fish carrier** — refrigerated vessel that transports catch from catcher to shore.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Q1.b]**
> **Q2. What is a "fish ground"? List four oceanographic characteristics that determine a good fish ground.**
> **Model Answer:**
> A fish ground is a geographically defined area of water where commercial concentrations of fish or other aquatic species are consistently available in commercially exploitable quantities.
> **Four oceanographic characteristics:**
> 1. **Sea Surface Temperature (SST)** — optimum range for the target species.
> 2. **Salinity** — typical range 33 – 37 PSU.
> 3. **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** — minimum 4 mg/L.
> 4. **Currents and Upwelling** — bring nutrients to the photic zone and concentrate plankton (fish food).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Model, Module 1, Q9]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a fishing vessel. List and explain **any five special design features** that distinguish a fishing vessel from a conventional cargo ship.
**(b) [7 Marks)** Discuss the salient provisions of the **Cape Town Agreement (2012)**. State how it differs from the **Torremolinos Convention (1977)**.

**Model Solution for (a):**
A fishing vessel is a commercial vessel used for catching, harvesting, or capturing fish, shellfish, or other aquatic organisms, including those engaged in on-board processing, transshipment, or support of fishing operations (FAO/IMO definition).

**Five special design features:**
1. **Wide, open working deck** — unobstructed area forward and aft for handling nets, lines, and catch. Bulwarks are usually low or cut away.
2. **High freeboard and pronounced sheer** — to prevent green-water flooding while trawling or working in beam seas.
3. **Powerful deck machinery** — trawl winches, net haulers, line haulers, gurdies, and purse-seine power blocks, usually hydraulically driven from the main engine.
4. **Refrigerated fish holds (RSW / CSW / brine tanks)** — designed to preserve the catch for the duration of the trip, with high thermal insulation.
5. **Stern ramp / side door (trawlers)** — for direct hauling of the cod-end onto the deck without manual lifting.
6. **High GM and good intact stability** — to safely accommodate dynamic loads from swinging gear and free-surface effects.
7. **Heavy local reinforcement** — at winch foundations, mast steps, and bulwarks to absorb shock loads during gear operation.

**Model Solution for (b):**
**Cape Town Agreement (2012)** is the modern international convention adopted by the IMO for the safety of fishing vessels. Salient provisions:
- Applies to **fishing vessels of 24 m LOA and above**, engaged in commercial fishing.
- Covers construction (hull, watertight integrity, subdivision), machinery and electrical installations, fire protection, life-saving appliances, and radio communication.
- Introduces modern probabilistic and risk-based design principles.
- Linked to Torremolinos Protocol of 1993 but is **independent** and brings in **mandatory carriage of life-saving and radio equipment**.
- Entered into force in 2020 after 12 flag states ratified it.

**Differences from Torremolinos (1977):**

| Aspect | Torremolinos (1977) | Cape Town (2012) |
|---|---|---|
| **Entry into force** | 1977; never entered into force | 2012; entered into force 2020 |
| **Applicability** | 24 m and above | 24 m and above |
| **Approach** | Prescriptive | Goal-based, modern, risk-based |
| **Status** | Practically obsolete | Currently binding |
| **Life-saving / Radio** | Limited | Comprehensive |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify fishing vessels based on **(i) fishing method** and **(ii) operational range**, giving one example of each class and the typical LOA.
**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **bathymetric, bottom-type, and biological characteristics** of a fish ground. How do these characteristics influence the design of a fishing vessel?

**Model Solution for (a):**
**(i) By Fishing Method:**
- **Trawler** (Side / Stern trawler) — uses otter trawl; typical LOA 20 – 45 m.
- **Purse Seiner** — encircles schools with a purse seine; typical LOA 30 – 60 m.
- **Gillnetter** — uses static gill nets; typical LOA 8 – 25 m.
- **Long Liner** — baited hooks on long lines; typical LOA 15 – 50 m.
- **Dredger** — drags a dredge for shellfish; typical LOA 15 – 30 m.
- **Troller** — tows trolling lines; typical LOA 7 – 25 m.
- **Squid Jigger** — automatic jigging machines; typical LOA 30 – 70 m.

**(ii) By Operational Range:**
- **Inshore / Coastal** — up to 12 NM; LOA 5 – 15 m; day-trip boats.
- **Offshore / Near-shore** — up to 200 NM; LOA 15 – 24 m; trips of 1 – 7 days.
- **Deep-Sea** — within EEZ beyond 200 NM; LOA 24 – 35 m; 1 – 4 weeks.
- **Distant-Water** — international waters; LOA 35 – 100+ m; multi-month voyages.

**Model Solution for (b):**
**Bathymetric characteristics** include water depth (5 – 2000 m), bottom topography (flat, undulating, seamount, canyon, shelf edge), and the gradient of the continental shelf.

**Bottom-type characteristics** refer to whether the seabed is sandy, muddy, rocky, coral, or mixed. This determines gear selection (trawl for soft bottoms, long line for rocky bottoms, dredge for shellfish beds).

**Biological characteristics** include the species composition, stock density, spawning seasons, migration patterns, predator-prey relationships, and food availability.

**Influence on vessel design:**
- **Soft muddy / sandy grounds** → otter trawl with plough-type doors; vessels need high bollard pull and clear stern ramp.
- **Rocky / coral grounds** → vessel needs reinforced rudder and propeller (strike protection), heavy-duty winch brake.
- **Deep grounds** → high warp scope; large net drums; high-power winches.
- **Migratory pelagic grounds** → vessels need *faster hull form* (lower $C_B$), good fuel range, and large fish-hold capacity.
- **Mixed-species grounds** → vessel needs versatile gear handling and partitioned fish holds.
- **Distant-water grounds** → vessel needs large fuel capacity, freezer holds, and full crew accommodation for multi-month voyages.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Carefully)**
> 1. **Mixing up LOA 24 m** — students often write 24 m as the *applicability* for Torremolinos; it is also the threshold for Cape Town. State it correctly each time.
> 2. **Torremolinos vs. Cape Town** — many students mistakenly think Torremolinos is *current*. It is *not in force*; the current binding instrument is **Cape Town 2012 (in force from 2020)**.
> 3. **Confusing FAO/ILO/IMO Code with Cape Town** — the FAO/ILO/IMO Code is *voluntary* and applies to vessels **< 24 m** (Part A) and as best-practice for ≥ 24 m (Part B).
> 4. **Definition of fish ground** — students often miss the *oceanographic* part. Always include *SST, salinity, DO, currents, and plankton* when defining.
> 5. **Special features** — examiners expect *both* structural features (sheer, freeboard, working deck) and *operational* features (winches, holds, manoeuvrability).
> 6. **GM derivation** — students frequently forget that $BM$ is divided by $\nabla$ (displaced volume), not by $L \cdot B \cdot T$.
> 7. **Cape Town ratification** — to be in force, **at least 22 flag states** representing **3,600 vessels ≥ 24 m** must ratify; this is often asked.
> 8. **Do not use unverified edition years** — use the convention years stated: Torremolinos 1977, STCW-F 1995, Cape Town 2012, ILO C188 2007.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-page Rapid Revision Checklist**

- A **fishing vessel** is defined under the **FAO/IMO/MS Act 1958** as a vessel used commercially for catching fish and related resources, including support and processing vessels.
- **Fish ground** is defined by *bathymetry, bottom type, oceanography, and biology*.
- **Special features** of a fishing vessel: **wide working deck, high freeboard, pronounced sheer, RSW holds, powerful winches, stern ramp/side door, high GM, heavy local reinforcement, cathodic protection**.
- **Five major regulations**: Torremolinos 1977, Cape Town 2012 (in force 2020), FAO/ILO/IMO Code 2005, ILO C188 2007, STCW-F 1995. India: MS Act 1958 + Indian Fishing Vessels Rules + State MFRA + IRS rules.
- **Classification by gear**: Trawler, Seiner, Gillnetter, Long Liner, Troller, Dredger, Squid Jigger.
- **Classification by range**: Inshore (5 – 15 m), Offshore (15 – 24 m), Deep-Sea (24 – 35 m), Distant-Water (35 – 100+ m).
- **Classification by material**: Wood, Steel, Aluminium, FRP, Ferro-cement.
- **Hull form parameter**: $C_B = \nabla \div (L_{BP} B T)$, typical 0.50 – 0.70.
- **Stability formula**: $GM = KB + BM - KG$, with $BM = I_T / \nabla = (L_{BP} B^3)/(12 \nabla)$.
- **Cape Town threshold**: LOA ≥ 24 m **and** commercial fishing **and** flag-state ratification.
- **Cape Town intact-stability criteria**: $\theta_{max} \geq 30°$, Area 0–30° ≥ 0.055 m·rad, Area 30–40° ≥ 0.030 m·rad.
- **SST for fish grounds**: ~ 4 – 30 °C; **DO** ≥ 4 mg/L; **Salinity** 33 – 37 PSU.
- **Bottom type dictates gear**: sandy → trawl, rocky → long line / trap, coral → hand line, pelagic zone → purse seine.
- **Upwelling and frontal zones** are the most productive fish grounds.
- **Cape Town Agreement** is the *current* binding international instrument; Torremolinos is *not in force*.
- **IRS (Indian Register of Shipping)** is the Indian classification society.
- **KTU mantra**: "Cape Town 24 m, Torremolinos old, FAO/ILO voluntary, ILO C188 labour, STCW-F crew."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fishing Gear and Methods, Drift Net, Long Line, Drag Net, Seine Net, Dredging with Electric Light, Harpoon/Whale Catching, Trawling (Side and Stern Trawlers, Single and Pair Trawling, Pelagic and Bottom Trawling)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fishing Gear and Methods, Drift Net, Long Line, Drag Net, Seine Net, Dredging with Electric Light, Harpoon/Whale Catching, Trawling (Side and Stern Trawlers, Single and Pair Trawling, Pelagic and Bottom Trawling)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a "Fishing Gear"?

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (Marine / Fisheries Engineering), **fishing gear** is the *complete assembly of implements, nets, ropes, floats, weights, warps, and accessories* that is used to capture or harvest aquatic organisms (fish, shellfish, cephalopods, marine mammals). The **method** is the *operational procedure* by which the gear is set, towed, hauled, or retrieved to exploit the natural or aggregated behaviour of the target species.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):**
> *Fishing gear is a tool or device used to capture fish or other aquatic animals. The choice of gear dictates the size, propulsion, deck layout, gear-handling machinery, and stability characteristics of the fishing vessel that carries it.*

### 1.2 Two Broad Families of Gear

| Family | Principle | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Active Gear** | Vessel *actively* moves the gear to herd, filter, or entangle the catch. | Trawl nets, seine nets, drag nets, dredges |
| **Passive Gear** | Gear is *set and left* (drifting, anchored, or soaking) and fish encounter it. | Drift nets (gillnets), longlines, traps, pots |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain-English Analogy):**
> Think of **active gear** like a *moving butterfly net* — you swing it through a swarm. **Passive gear** is like a *spider's web* — you leave it, and the prey comes to it. The vessel (the "angler") must therefore be designed very differently in each case.

### 1.3 The Seven Gears / Methods in This Module

1. **Drift Net** — a wall of netting suspended from a floating headline; fish are *gilled* or *entangled*.
2. **Long Line** — a long main line carrying hundreds or thousands of baited hooks; set at surface or on seabed.
3. **Drag Net** — a net towed along the seabed or through mid-water; *envelopes* the fish.
4. **Seine Net** — a long net set around a school, then closed at the bottom (purse) or hauled from the beach (beach seine).
5. **Dredging with Electric Light** — an iron-framed dredge (for shellfish) towed with an underwater lamp to attract target species.
6. **Harpoon / Whale Catching** — a barbed throwing weapon used for large cetaceans or tuna.
7. **Trawling** — the most important active method: subdivided by *rigging* (side / stern), *deployment* (single / pair), and *depth* (pelagic / bottom).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these matter for VESSEL DESIGN (Module-1 perspective):**
> Each gear imposes a unique set of *design drivers* on the fishing vessel — bollard pull, deck space, freeboard, arrangement of winches and net drums, and seakeeping. For example, a stern trawler needs a *slipway or stern ramp*; a long-liner needs a *line hauler* and *bait tank*.

---

## 1.4 Quick-Reference Definition Cards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drift Net (Gillnet):** A passive, rectangular wall of mono/multifilament netting kept vertical in the water by floats on top (headrope) and weights on bottom (footrope). Fish striking the net get wedged by their gill covers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Long Line:** A passive main line up to **100 km** long carrying branch lines (snoods) with baited hooks at regular intervals. Pelagic longline targets tuna/swordfish; bottom longline targets demersal species (cod, halibut).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drag Net:** A generic term for any net *dragged* through water — usually synonymous with **bottom trawl** for demersal fish. Catches fish that live close to the seabed (shrimp, flatfish, cod).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seine Net:** A long rectangular net with a floatline (top) and leadline (bottom) used to encircle a school of fish. **Purse seine** has a purseline drawn under the net to *close* it like a purse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dredging with Electric Light:** A *rigid-frame metal dredge* with a collecting bag, dragged over the seabed to scoop shellfish (scallops, oysters, clams). An underwater *electric lamp* lures phototactic species (squid, mackerel) toward the gear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Harpoon / Whale Catching:** A barbed spear manually thrown or shot from a gun; used for large cetaceans (traditional whaling) and large pelagic fish (swordfish, tuna). Now restricted by IWC moratorium (1986).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trawling:** Towed conical bag-net (trawl) extended by otter boards (doors) and weighed by ground gear. **Side trawler** tows over the side; **stern trawler** tows over the stern. **Single trawling** uses one vessel; **pair trawling** uses two. **Pelagic trawl** targets mid-water species; **bottom trawl** targets seabed species.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Drift Net — Operating Principle

A drift net (gillnet) exploits the **biomechanical geometry** of a fish: the meshes are sized so that the fish can push its head through but its *gill covers (opercula)* or *maxillary bones* get stuck when it tries to back out.

**Components:**
- **Headline** — upper rope carrying **floats** (buoyancy).
- **Footrope** — lower rope carrying **weights** (sinkers).
- **Webbing** — the net fabric; mesh size determines species selectivity.
- **End bridles / danlenos** — pull the net ends so it hangs vertically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Catenary model:** The hanging shape of the headline between floats is described by the **catenary equation** (see §3.1). For design, the headline is assumed near-horizontal; mesh hang ratio (E) = 0.5 gives a near-circular mesh shape — most efficient gilling.

### KTU Formula — Mesh Hang Ratio

$$
E = \dfrac{2a}{S}
$$

Where:
- $a$ = distance between adjacent knots on the headrope (m)
- $S$ = full mesh perimeter (twice the bar length, $S = 2 \, m_b$)
- **Optimum $E$** $\approx$ **0.5** for gillnets

---

## 2.2 Long Line — Operating Principle

A longline consists of:
- **Main line** (3–10 mm dia. PA or polyester) carrying **branch lines (snoods)** every 20–60 m.
- Each snood ends in a **baited hook**.
- **Dhan buoys** mark the start/end of the line; **flag buoys / radio buoys** locate the gear.

**Two types:**
| Type | Depth | Target |
|---|---|---|
| **Bottom longline (set line)** | On seabed, 1–10 km long | Cod, halibut, rockfish |
| **Pelagic (tuna) longline** | Suspended at 50–400 m depth, up to 100 km long | Tuna, swordfish, billfish |

### KTU Formula — Spacing and Capacity

$$
N_h = \dfrac{L}{s_b}
$$

Where:
- $N_h$ = number of hooks on the line
- $L$ = length of main line (m)
- $s_b$ = spacing between branch lines (m), typically **20–60 m**

$$
T_{soak} = \dfrac{L_{line}}{v_{drift}} \quad \text{(hours)}
$$

Where $v_{drift}$ is the current/setting speed (m/h).

---

## 2.3 Drag Net / Bottom Trawl — Operating Principle

A **drag net** is towed over the seabed; fish are overtaken, herded by the **otter boards (doors)** into the **codend** (the bag at the aft end where catch accumulates).

**Key components:**
- **Otter boards (trawl doors)** — hydrofoils that spread the net horizontally and provide vertical lift (or none, for bottom trawl).
- **Warps (towing bridles)** — steel wire or synthetic rope from vessel to doors.
- **Wing-end, square, belly, tunnel, codend** — sections of the net bag.
- **Ground gear** — chain / rockhopper / rubber discs in contact with seabed.

### KTU Formulas — Trawl Resistance & Towing Power

$$
R_t = R_0 \, \dfrac{A_{net}}{A_0} \, \left(\dfrac{v_t}{v_0}\right)^{1.7 \text{ to } 2.0}
$$

Where:
- $R_t$ = total trawl resistance at speed $v_t$ (N or kN)
- $R_0$ = reference resistance of standard net (≈ **10 kN** at 4 kn for 600 m² spread)
- $A_{net}$ = mouth area of net (m²)
- $v_t$ = towing speed (knots or m/s)
- **Exponent 1.7–2.0** comes from drag coefficient's Reynolds dependence

$$
P_{tow} = R_t \cdot v_t \quad \text{(W)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Bollard Pull Requirement:** The vessel's *bollard pull* (static pull at zero speed) must exceed $R_t$ by a margin of **20–30%** to maintain tow speed in seaway.

---

## 2.4 Seine Net — Operating Principle

A seine net surrounds a school of fish, then *closes the bottom* (purse seine) or is hauled from both ends to a beach (beach seine).

**Purse seine cycle:**
1. **Setting** — vessel encircles the school with the net.
2. **Pursing** — purseline is hauled, closing the bottom of the net.
3. **Brailing** — catch is lifted out using a *power block* or *brailing net*.

### KTU Formula — Net Depth & Stiffness

$$
h_{net} = \dfrac{B_{float}}{W_{lead}} \quad \text{(equilibrium depth ratio)}
$$

For purse seine, buoyancy $B_{float}$ must overcome weight $W_{lead}$ plus hydrodynamic drag $D$:

$$
B_{float} \geq W_{lead} + D_{mesh}
$$

---

## 2.5 Dredging with Electric Light — Operating Principle

A **dredge** is a heavy steel-frame mouth with a *collecting bag* (metal rings or chainmail) towed over the seabed. **Electric light (lamp)** attracts phototactic species — particularly **squid (Loligo), mackerel, and sardine** — toward the gear at night.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phototaxis exploitation:** Most pelagic fish and squid show *positive phototaxis* (movement toward light). A submerged lamp (1–5 kW metal halide / LED) lowers on a light cord *above* the dredge, forming a virtual "feeding zone" of catch.

### KTU Formula — Effective Attraction Radius

$$
r_{eff} = k_{lux} \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{lamp}}{I_{background}}}
$$

Where:
- $r_{eff}$ = effective radius of light attraction (m)
- $P_{lamp}$ = lamp power (W)
- $I_{background}$ = ambient light intensity (lux)
- $k_{lux}$ = empirical constant ≈ **0.5–1.0 m·√(lux/W)**

---

## 2.6 Harpoon / Whale Catching — Operating Principle

A **harpoon** is a long shaft with a detachable, barbed head (toggle) on a line (the "whale line"). When the toggle embeds, it pivots and locks inside the prey. A **harpoon gun** (originally shoulder-fired, now deck-mounted) accelerates the projectile using compressed air or gunpowder.

> [!WARNING]
> **International Whaling Commission (IWC) moratorium:** Commercial whaling was banned in 1986. The method is now only legal for *Aboriginal Subsistence Whaling* (ASW) — limited to a few communities in Greenland, Alaska, and Russia. Mention in exams should note this ethical/regulatory framework.

### KTU Formula — Stopping Power of a Whaling Vessel

$$
E_{k} = \dfrac{1}{2} m_w \, v_w^2 = F_{line} \cdot d_{brake}
$$

Where:
- $E_k$ = kinetic energy of struck whale (J)
- $m_w$ = whale mass (kg), e.g., fin whale ≈ **50,000 kg**
- $v_w$ = whale speed (m/s), can reach **9 m/s (17.5 kn)** in a dive
- $F_{line}$ = line tension from the *whale winch* brake (N)
- $d_{brake}$ = braking distance (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Design consequence:** The whaling vessel's winch brake must absorb up to **2 GJ** of kinetic energy without snapping the line — drove early innovation in hydraulic braking systems.

---

## 2.7 Trawling — Sub-classifications

### 2.7.1 Side Trawler vs Stern Trawler

| Feature | **Side Trawler** | **Stern Trawler** |
|---|---|---|
| Warp deployment | Over the **side** (gallows) | Over the **stern** ramp |
| Deck equipment | Side winches / boom | Net drum / stern ramp |
| Vessel size | Usually < 35 m | Up to 100+ m (factory trawlers) |
| Stability impact | Heel moment during tow | Symmetric — better seakeeping |
| Modern usage | Older fleet, small-scale | Dominant in modern fleets |

### 2.7.2 Single Trawling vs Pair Trawling

| Feature | **Single Trawl** | **Pair Trawl** |
|---|---|---|
| Vessels | One | Two, side-by-side |
| Net size | Larger (up to 2000 m²) | Smaller per vessel |
| Fish finding | Standard sonar | Coordinated acoustic sharing |
| Bollard pull | High (single vessel) | Moderate (shared) |
| Crew exposure | Standard | Higher (towing near other vessel) |

### 2.7.3 Pelagic Trawl vs Bottom Trawl

| Feature | **Pelagic (Mid-water) Trawl** | **Bottom Trawl** |
|---|---|---|
| Depth | Mid-water, 0–1000 m | On seabed |
| Doors | Hydrodynamic (provide lift) | Heavy, ground-contact |
| Ground gear | None | Chains / rockhopper |
| Catches | Herring, mackerel, blue whiting | Cod, haddock, shrimp, flatfish |
| By-catch | Lower | Higher (ecosystem impact) |

---

## 2.8 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Master Table)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Values / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = 2a/S$ | Mesh hang ratio (gillnet) | $E \approx$ **0.5** |
| 2 | $N_h = L/s_b$ | Number of hooks on longline | $s_b \in [20, 60]$ m |
| 3 | $T_{soak} = L/v_{drift}$ | Soaking time (h) | hours |
| 4 | $R_t = R_0 (A_{net}/A_0)(v_t/v_0)^{1.8}$ | Trawl resistance | $R_0 \approx$ **10 kN** |
| 5 | $P_{tow} = R_t \cdot v_t$ | Towing power | kW |
| 6 | $B_{float} \geq W_{lead} + D_{mesh}$ | Seine buoyancy balance | N |
| 7 | $r_{eff} = k_{lux}\sqrt{P/I}$ | Light attraction radius | m |
| 8 | $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m_w v_w^2$ | Whale kinetic energy | J (up to 2 GJ) |
| 9 | $F_{line} = E_k / d_{brake}$ | Line tension on winch | N |
| 10 | $f_b = P_{tow} / P_{BHP}}$ | Bollard pull factor | dimensionless |
| 11 | $A_{sweep} = L_{warp} \cdot v_t \cdot t$ | Swept area (catches) | m²·h |
| 12 | $V_{hold} = \eta \cdot C_{catch}} / \rho_{fish}}$ | Hold capacity | m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **$\rho_{fish}$** (bulk density of catch in hold) ≈ **0.55–0.70 tonne/m³** for whole fish; **0.95 tonne/m³** for block-frozen fish.
> **$\eta$** (hold utilization) ≈ **0.60–0.75**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below follow the **rigorous KTU University-Exam style**: each algebraic step is shown, justified, and cross-referenced to physical meaning.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Catenary Equation for Drift-Net Headline

The headline of a drift net hangs between floats under its own weight, forming a **catenary**.

### Setup

Let the cable have linear weight $w$ (N/m) and horizontal tension $H$ (N). Take a coordinate system with origin at the lowest point; $y$ is vertical, $x$ is horizontal.

At any point, the *vertical equilibrium* of a small element gives:

$$
T \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx} = w \cdot x
$$

The *horizontal equilibrium* gives constant $T_x = H$, so:

$$
T = H \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2}
$$

### Solution

Substituting:

$$
H \sqrt{1 + (y')^2} = w x \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + (y')^2}}{y'}
$$

Simplifying and integrating:

$$
\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{w x}{H}
$$

$$
y = \dfrac{w}{2 H} x^2 + C
$$

### Final Catenary Equation

$$
\boxed{y(x) = \dfrac{w}{2H} x^2 + y_0}
$$

Where $y_0$ = sag at midspan.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** For a gill-net headline, we want $y_0 \to 0$ (taut headline) so the net hangs vertically. This requires **large horizontal tension $H$** — i.e., tightly stretched headline between end bridles.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Hooks on a Longline

Given a mainline of length $L$ and branch-line spacing $s_b$:

$$
\boxed{N_h = \dfrac{L}{s_b}}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A pelagic longline has mainline length $L = 80$ km and branch-line spacing $s_b = 45$ m. Find the number of hooks.

$$
L = 80{,}000 \text{ m}
$$

$$
N_h = \dfrac{80{,}000}{45} = 1777.78 \approx 1778 \text{ hooks}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design implication for vessel storage:** Each hook + bait + swivel takes ≈ **0.5 m of branch line** stowed. Total stowed line = $1778 \times 0.5 \approx$ **889 m** of branch line — sets the size of the *line hauler* drum.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Trawl Resistance — Step by Step

### Start with dimensional baseline

For a reference bottom trawl of area $A_0 = 600$ m² towed at $v_0 = 4$ kn, empirical resistance is $R_0 = 10$ kN.

### Apply scaling laws

Resistance scales with:
- **Area** $\propto R$ (linear)
- **Velocity squared-ish** (drag coefficient in fully turbulent regime): $\propto v^{1.8}$

$$
R_t = R_0 \left(\dfrac{A_{net}}{A_0}\right) \left(\dfrac{v_t}{v_0}\right)^{1.8}
$$

### Step-by-step worked example

**Problem:** A trawl net of mouth area $A_{net} = 1200$ m² is towed at $v_t = 4.5$ kn. Compute the towing resistance.

Step 1 — Convert to consistent units (we will keep $v$ in knots since $R_0$ uses knots):

$$
v_0 = 4 \text{ kn}, \quad v_t = 4.5 \text{ kn}
$$

Step 2 — Substituting:

$$
R_t = 10{,}000 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1200}{600}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{4.5}{4}\right)^{1.8}
$$

Step 3 — Evaluate each factor:

$$
\dfrac{A_{net}}{A_0} = \dfrac{1200}{600} = 2.00
$$

$$
\dfrac{v_t}{v_0} = 1.125
$$

Step 4 — Power term:

$$
(1.125)^{1.8} = e^{1.8 \ln 1.125} = e^{1.8 \times 0.1178} = e^{0.2120} = 1.2362
$$

Step 5 — Multiply:

$$
R_t = 10{,}000 \times 2.00 \times 1.2362 = 24{,}724 \text{ N} \approx 24.7 \text{ kN}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{R_t \approx 24.7 \text{ kN}}
$$

### Power Required

$$
P_{tow} = R_t \cdot v_t = 24{,}724 \times 4.5 \times 0.5144 \text{ m/s} = 57{,}232 \text{ W} \approx 57.2 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Vessel design consequence:** With gearbox + propeller losses ($\eta \approx 0.6$) the engine must deliver $P_{BHP} = 57.2/0.6 \approx$ **95 kW (≈ 128 BHP)** just to tow — does NOT include hotel load or cruising.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Bollard Pull Requirement

The **bollard pull** $F_{BP}$ (static pull of the vessel at zero speed) must exceed $R_t$ by a *sea margin* $M$ (typically 20–30%):

$$
F_{BP} = k_{BP} \cdot R_t, \quad k_{BP} \in [1.20, 1.30]
$$

For our example:

$$
F_{BP} = 1.25 \times 24.7 = 30.9 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why static?** Because during a turn, a wave hit, or a brief stall of the propeller, the *instantaneous* required pull may momentarily exceed the steady-state resistance — the *static* bollard pull is the worst-case benchmark.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Effective Light-Attraction Radius for Dredging

Starting from the phototaxis threshold — fish respond to a *contrast in irradiance*:

$$
C = \dfrac{I_{lamp}}{I_{background}}
$$

Empirically, $C \geq 10$ is the *recognition threshold*. The illuminance at distance $r$ from a point source in water:

$$
I(r) = \dfrac{P_{lamp}}{4 \pi r^2} \cdot \alpha_{attn}
$$

where $\alpha_{attn}$ is the **attenuation factor** (water turbidity, absorption, scattering).

Setting $I(r_{eff}) = I_{background} \cdot 10$:

$$
\dfrac{P_{lamp}}{4 \pi r_{eff}^2} \cdot \alpha_{attn} = 10 I_{background}
$$

Solving for $r_{eff}$:

$$
r_{eff} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha_{attn} \cdot P_{lamp}}{40 \pi \cdot I_{background}}}
$$

$$
\boxed{r_{eff} = k_{lux} \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{lamp}}{I_{background}}}, \quad k_{lux} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha_{attn}}{40 \pi}}}
$$

### Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 3000 W LED lamp is used in clear seawater where $I_{background} = 0.1$ lux (deep night). Take $k_{lux} = 0.7$.

$$
r_{eff} = 0.7 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{3000}{0.1}} = 0.7 \times \sqrt{30{,}000} = 0.7 \times 173.2 = 121.2 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{r_{eff} \approx 121 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design implication:** The lamp's power cable must be at least $r_{eff} \approx$ **120 m** long, and the lamp itself must be deployed *above* the dredge mouth on a separate light cord so it leads the dredge into the school's path.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Whale-Line Energy Absorption

A struck whale (mass $m_w$, initial speed $v_w$) is decelerated by the *whale-line brake* over a distance $d_{brake}$ to a stop.

### Energy balance

Kinetic energy of whale = work done by line tension:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} m_w v_w^2 = F_{line} \cdot d_{brake}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{line} = \dfrac{m_w v_w^2}{2 \, d_{brake}}}
$$

### Worked Example — Fin Whale

Take $m_w = 50{,}000$ kg, $v_w = 9$ m/s, $d_{brake} = 200$ m.

Step 1 — Compute kinetic energy:

$$
E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 50{,}000 \times 9^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 50{,}000 \times 81 = 2{,}025{,}000 \text{ J} = 2.025 \text{ GJ}
$$

Step 2 — Solve for line tension:

$$
F_{line} = \dfrac{2{,}025{,}000}{200} = 10{,}125 \text{ N} \approx 10.1 \text{ kN}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{F_{line} \approx 10.1 \text{ kN}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Vessel design consequence:** 10 kN is well within the capacity of a deck winch. However, *peak* tension during erratic whale thrashing can be **3–5× higher**, requiring the brake to absorb up to **10 GJ** cumulatively over the chase. Whaling winches were the earliest drivers of *hydraulic disc-brake* technology.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Seven Methods

| # | Method | Active/Passive | Vessel Size Implication | Power Implication | Deck Equipment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Drift Net | Passive | Small (8–25 m) | Low (50–200 kW) | Net hauler, flag buoys |
| 2 | Long Line | Passive | Medium (15–40 m) | Low–Moderate | Line hauler, bait cutter, hook stripper |
| 3 | Drag Net | Active | Medium–Large | High (300–1500 kW) | Traction winch, net drum |
| 4 | Seine Net | Active | Medium (20–45 m) | Moderate (200–600 kW) | Power block, purse winch, brailer |
| 5 | Dredge + Light | Active | Small–Medium | Moderate (150–400 kW) | Dredge winch, lamp winch |
| 6 | Harpoon | Active (manual) | Small, fast (10–25 m) | High (sprint), low (cruise) | Harpoon gun, whale winch |
| 7 | Trawl (Bottom) | Active | Large (25–100 m) | Very High (1000–5000 kW) | Net drum, trawl winches (2), stern ramp |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!NOTE]
> Questions below follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Pattern**: Part A (3-mark short answer), Part B (14-mark long answer with internal choice, sub-parts of 7 marks each). All weights and structures match KTU Board regulations.

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Qn]

**"With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a drift net (gill net). Mention the role of float and sinker."** (3 Marks)

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

A **drift net** is a passive wall of netting set in open water, held vertical by floats on the headline (top) and weights on the footrope (bottom). The vessel sets the net at dusk or at night, leaves it drifting with wind/current, and retrieves it the next morning. Fish striking the net are caught by their **gill covers (opercula)** — they can push the head through the mesh but cannot back out, since the mesh snags on the rigid bones of the gill cover. The mesh size is matched to the target species (e.g., 50–60 mm bar for salmon, 120 mm for cod).

**Role of floats:** Provide positive buoyancy so the headline floats at the surface; determine the *upper depth limit* of the net's vertical hang.

**Role of weights (sinkers):** Provide negative buoyancy so the footrope sinks; determine the *lower depth limit* and keep the net vertical.

**Marks split (KTU pattern):**
- 1 mark — definition + principle
- 1 mark — fish-capture mechanism (gilling)
- 1 mark — role of floats and weights

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Qn]

**"Distinguish between a purse seine and a beach seine."** (3 Marks)

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Feature | **Purse Seine** | **Beach Seine** |
|---|---|---|
| Operation | Set in open sea, *bottom closed by purseline* | Set from a beach, *hauled from both ends to shore* |
| Vessel | Single large vessel with pursing winch | Two small boats + shore labour |
| Catch size | Massive (100–1000 tonnes per set) | Small (10–100 kg per haul) |
| Target | Pelagic schooling fish (tuna, sardine) | Coastal species |
| Depth | Can be 100+ m deep | Limited to surf zone |

**Marks split:** 1 mark — purse definition, 1 mark — beach seine definition, 1 mark — table of differences.

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with sub-parts (a) and (b) of **7 marks each**. Internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain with sketches the **constructional features of an otter trawl net** and the **function of the otter boards (trawl doors)**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A bottom trawl net of mouth area **1500 m²** is towed at **5 knots**. If the reference resistance of a 600 m² trawl at 4 knots is **10 kN**, find (i) the towing resistance and (ii) the brake power required at the propeller, assuming overall propulsive efficiency of 60%. (7 Marks)

#### Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction of Otter Trawl Net:**

An otter trawl is a four-panel conical bag net, tapered from a wide mouth (held open by otter boards) to a small closed end (codend). The principal parts are:

1. **Wings (port and starboard)** — outer panels, joined at the top and bottom seams.
2. **Square** — section just aft of the wing tips; transition into the body.
3. **Belly** — lower forward body panel.
4. **Top panel / Back** — upper panel, often reinforced with stronger twine.
5. **Codend** — the closed bag where the catch accumulates; often fitted with a *codend release* for quick dumping.
6. **Headline** (top rope) with floats; **Footrope** (bottom rope) with chain ground gear.
7. **Bridles** — wire ropes from the net wing ends to the otter boards.

**Otter Boards (Trawl Doors):**

Otter boards are hydrofoil-shaped steel plates that perform *two simultaneous functions* while being towed:

- **Spreading** — they present an angled face to the oncoming water; the hydrodynamic force has a *lateral component* that pushes the boards apart, dragging the net's mouth open horizontally.
- **Lifting (pelagic trawl only)** — for mid-water trawls, the boards are angled upward, generating vertical lift that holds the net off the seabed.

**Types:** Rectangular flat boards (simple); Vee doors (most common); Cambered (high lift) doors; Suberkrub doors.

**Marks split:**
- 3 marks — net parts (wings, square, belly, codend, headline, footrope)
- 2 marks — otter board principle (spreading & lifting)
- 2 marks — board types and applications

#### Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $A_{net} = 1500$ m², $v_t = 5$ kn
- $A_0 = 600$ m², $v_0 = 4$ kn, $R_0 = 10$ kN
- $\eta_{prop} = 0.60$

**Step 1 — Resistance formula**

$$
R_t = R_0 \left(\dfrac{A_{net}}{A_0}\right) \left(\dfrac{v_t}{v_0}\right)^{1.8}
$$

**Step 2 — Plug in values**

$$
\dfrac{A_{net}}{A_0} = \dfrac{1500}{600} = 2.50
$$

$$
\dfrac{v_t}{v_0} = \dfrac{5}{4} = 1.25
$$

**Step 3 — Power of velocity ratio**

$$
(1.25)^{1.8} = e^{1.8 \ln 1.25} = e^{1.8 \times 0.2231} = e^{0.4016} = 1.494
$$

**Step 4 — Resistance**

$$
R_t = 10 \times 2.50 \times 1.494 = 37.36 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Towing power**

Convert $v_t$ to m/s:

$$
v_t = 5 \times 0.5144 = 2.572 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
P_{tow} = R_t \cdot v_t = 37{,}360 \times 2.572 = 96{,}090 \text{ W} = 96.09 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Brake power at propeller**

$$
P_{BHP} = \dfrac{P_{tow}}{\eta_{prop}} = \dfrac{96.09}{0.60} = 160.15 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{R_t \approx 37.4 \text{ kN}; \quad P_{BHP} \approx 160 \text{ kW} \ (\approx 215 \text{ BHP})}
$$

**Marks split:**
- 2 marks — formula + substitution
- 2 marks — numerical computation of $R_t$
- 1 mark — conversion of velocity
- 2 marks — computation of $P_{BHP}$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Describe with a sketch the **constructional features of a tuna long line**. Explain why the line must be set and hauled at particular times of day. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A purse seine net of length **800 m** and depth **150 m** is used to encircle a school of sardines. Estimate the (i) **area enclosed**, (ii) **volume of water enclosed**, and (iii) **expected catch weight** assuming a packing density of **3.5 kg/m³** of enclosed water. (7 Marks)

#### Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction of a Tuna Long Line:**

A tuna pelagic longline consists of:

1. **Main line** — typically 3–6 mm diameter polyester or Superlon, length 50–100 km.
2. **Branch lines (snoods)** — 5–25 m long, attached at intervals of 30–50 m; each carries a baited hook (typically J-hook or circle hook) at its end.
3. **Floats (line floats)** — attached at regular intervals to keep the main line suspended at the desired depth (50–400 m) below the surface.
4. **Dhan buoys** — large flag buoys with radar reflectors at the start and end of the line, marking its position.
5. **Radio buoys / GPS buoys** — electronic transmitters to enable recovery, especially useful for lines drifted overnight.
6. **Bait** — frozen squid, mackerel, or sardine threaded onto the hook.

**Why time-of-day matters:**

Tuna longlines are typically **set at dawn or dusk** and **hauled the next morning**. The reason is *thermal stratification and prey behaviour*:

- **Setting at dusk:** Tuna follow prey (squid, small pelagic fish) that migrate vertically toward the surface at night. By setting the line at dusk, the hooks are in the water column *just as tuna begin feeding actively*.
- **Hauling at dawn:** Reduces exposure of bait to scavengers (sharks, dolphins) and limits bird by-catch (which is highest during full daylight).
- **Hooks soak for 6–10 hours** at the temperature-rich thermocline where tuna concentrate.

**Marks split:** 4 marks — components, 3 marks — time-of-day reasoning.

#### Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Net length $L_n = 800$ m, depth $d = 150$ m
- Packing density $\rho_{catch} = 3.5$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Approximate enclosed area (assume rectangular purse-closed shape)**

When the purse line is closed, the net forms an ellipse-like shape. As a simplification, treat the enclosed area as a rectangle (length × depth) since the purseline pulls the bottom up:

$$
A_{enc} = L_n \times d = 800 \times 150 = 120{,}000 \text{ m}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For more accurate estimates, multiply by 0.7–0.8 to account for the curvature of the purse line; we'll use the simple model.

**Step 2 — Enclosed volume (treat as a cylinder for quick estimate)**

$$
V_{enc} = A_{enc} \times \bar{h}
$$

where $\bar{h} \approx d/2 = 75$ m (average depth of cone):

$$
V_{enc} = 120{,}000 \times 75 = 9{,}000{,}000 \text{ m}^3 = 9.0 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Expected catch weight**

$$
M_{catch} = V_{enc} \times \rho_{catch} = 9{,}000{,}000 \times 3.5 = 31{,}500{,}000 \text{ kg} = 31{,}500 \text{ tonnes}
$$

This figure is the *theoretical maximum* if every cubic metre were saturated — in practice, the *school filling ratio* $f_{school} \in [0.05, 0.30]$.

**Step 4 — Realistic catch**

Assuming $f_{school} = 0.20$:

$$
M_{real} = 0.20 \times 31{,}500 = 6300 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{A_{enc} = 1.2 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^2;\ V_{enc} = 9.0 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3;\ M_{real} \approx 6300 \text{ t}}
$$

**Marks split:**
- 2 marks — area calculation
- 2 marks — volume calculation (with assumption)
- 3 marks — catch mass with packing density and school-fill factor

---

## 5.1 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNINGS & PITFALLS

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (and lose marks on):**

1. **Forgetting to convert knots to m/s** in towing-power calculations ($1 \text{ kn} = 0.5144$ m/s). This single error can lose **2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
2. **Confusing "purse seine" with "beach seine"** in conceptual questions. Examiner deducts **1 mark** for each misused term.
3. **Omitting the efficiency term $\eta$** when going from $P_{tow}$ to $P_{BHP}$. Always show the propulsive efficiency in the working.
4. **Forgetting the IWC moratorium (1986)** when answering harpoon/whaling questions. Examiners award marks for *responsible context* — at minimum mention the moratorium.
5. **Mixing up pair trawling and double-rig trawling**. Pair trawling = *two vessels sharing one net*; double-rig = *one vessel towing two separate nets* from outriggers.
6. **Not labelling Mermaid diagrams properly** in script answers — always label *headline, footrope, otter boards, codend, ground gear* when sketching a trawl.
7. **Units of resistance** — use kN for large trawls; do not write $R_t = 37,360$ W (this is a power unit).

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MODULE 1 — KEY TAKEAWAYS (Print & Memorise)**

- ✅ **Drift net** is a passive gillnet — fish caught by opercula; floats on headline, weights on footrope.
- ✅ **Long line** is the *most fuel-efficient* passive gear; pelagic longlines can be **100 km** long with up to **~1800 hooks**.
- ✅ **Drag net** = generic term for towed ground net; usually synonymous with bottom trawl.
- ✅ **Seine net** encircles a school; **purse seine** closes the bottom with a purseline.
- ✅ **Dredging + electric light** exploits phototaxis of squid/mackerel; typical lamp power **1–5 kW**, attraction radius **50–150 m**.
- ✅ **Harpoon** — used for large cetaceans; commercial whaling banned under **IWC moratorium (1986)**.
- ✅ **Side trawler** tows over the side (gallows); **stern trawler** tows over the stern (ramp + net drum).
- ✅ **Single trawling** = one vessel; **pair trawling** = two vessels share one net.
- ✅ **Pelagic trawl** = mid-water; **bottom trawl** = on seabed; both use otter boards.
- ✅ **Trawl resistance** scales with area linearly and velocity to the power **1.7–2.0** ($R \propto A \cdot v^{1.8}$).
- ✅ **Bollard pull** must exceed $R_t$ by **20–30%** for safe towing in seaway.
- ✅ **Mesh hang ratio** for gillnets $E = 2a/S \approx 0.5$.
- ✅ **Whale kinetic energy** can reach **2 GJ per strike**; winch brake must absorb **3–5× peak tension**.
- ✅ **Hold capacity** $V_{hold} = \eta \cdot C_{catch} / \rho_{fish}$, with $\rho_{fish} \approx 0.55\text{–}0.70$ t/m³ for whole fish.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final study tip:** Be ready to *draw and label* at least one diagram from this module (typically a trawl net OR a long line). KTU examiners routinely award **1–2 marks** just for a clean, well-labelled sketch.

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Owner's Specifications** is the legally binding technical and operational document prepared by the *Client (Shipowner)*, either directly or through the *Naval Architect*, which formally expresses every quantitative and qualitative requirement that the proposed fishing vessel must satisfy. It forms the **first and most critical step** of the *Design Spiral* and serves as the **primary design contract** between the Owner and the Designer.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PENST754 – Design of Fishing Vessels)**, the Owner's Specifications constitute the *input vector* of the design process. They define the boundary conditions within which all subsequent decisions on hull form, propulsion, stability, machinery, fishing gear, and outfitting are made. According to established ship design literature (Watson, *Practical Ship Design*, and Vikas, *Design of Fishing Vessels and their Equipment*), the Owner's Specification is essentially a **functional translation of commercial intent into engineering parameters**.

The Owner's Specification typically encompasses:

| S.No | Parameter Category | Example Values (Typical Range) |
|------|--------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Vessel type & fishing method | Trawler, Seiner, Long-liner, Gill-netter |
| 2 | Operating area & climate | Coastal, Mid-water, Deep-sea |
| 3 | Principal dimensions (L, B, D, T) | 18–30 m OAL for typical vessels |
| 4 | Deadweight / Carrying capacity | 30–150 tonnes |
| 5 | Speed (service & max) | 8–14 knots |
| 6 | Endurance (range) | 7–30 days |
| 7 | Crew complement | 8–25 persons |
| 8 | Fish hold capacity | 30–200 m³ |
| 9 | Fishing gear specifications | Winch pull, drum capacity |
| 10 | Regulatory & class requirements | IRS, BV, LR, flag state rules |
| 11 | Cost ceiling / budget constraints | Capital + operational targets |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Owner's Specifications as a "Wedding Shopping List for a Custom Fishing Boat."**

Imagine a fisherman tells a tailor: *"I want a shirt for deep-sea fishing. I need it to be cool in tropical waters, strong enough to withstand ropes pulling against it, have **at least 12 pockets** for tools, dry-fast material, and I should not spend more than ₹10,000."* — That list is essentially the **Owner's Specification**.

Just as the tailor cannot begin stitching without understanding the wearer's *purpose, size, environment, and budget*, the **Naval Architect cannot draw a single hull line** without first understanding:
- **WHAT** the boat will catch (fish/crustacean type → determines hold & gear)
- **WHERE** it will operate (sea state → determines scantlings)
- **HOW FAST** it must reach grounds (service speed → determines engine power)
- **HOW LONG** it can stay at sea (endurance → determines bunker capacity)
- **HOW MUCH** the owner can spend (cost ceiling → constrains design choices)

Without this document, the design becomes **directionless**, leading to vessels that are either over-designed (costly) or under-designed (unsafe/non-functional).

## 1.3 Role of Owner's Specification in the Design Spiral

In the classical **Design Spiral (Evans, 1959)** methodology adopted in KTU naval architecture pedagogy, the Owner's Specification occupies the **topmost position (Phase 0/1)**, governing every subsequent spiral iteration:

1. **Conceptual Phase** → Owner's Specification drives mission profile definition.
2. **Preliminary Phase** → Specifications translate into principal dimensions.
3. **Contract Phase** → Specifications become legally binding via the *Design Contract*.
4. **Detail Phase** → Specifications are verified against the as-built vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Examinations:** The Owner's Specification is **NOT** a vague wish-list. It is a **structured engineering document** with measurable parameters, and any deviation from it by the designer becomes a **contractual liability**.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Owner's Specifications

A comprehensive Owner's Specification document is organized into **distinct logical clusters**. Each cluster addresses a specific domain of vessel design and operation.

### Cluster A: Mission Definition

This cluster establishes **WHAT the vessel is meant to do**. It is the most fundamental specification because every other parameter flows from it.

- **Fishing Method**: Trawling (stern/bottom/mid-water), Purse Seining, Long-lining, Gill-netting, Trolling, Pole-and-line, Squid jigging, Crab potting.
- **Target Species**: Demersal fish, pelagic fish, crustaceans, cephalopods, molluscs.
- **Catch Handling**: Fresh-chilled (RSW/CSW), frozen-at-sea, live, salted/dried.
- **Catch Volume per Voyage**: Determines hold capacity and trip economics.

### Cluster B: Operational Boundaries

Defines **WHERE and WHEN** the vessel operates.

- **Geographical Limits**: Coastal (≤12 nm), EEZ (≤200 nm), High seas.
- **Climatic Limits**: Tropical, temperate, polar — affects HVAC, insulation, ice strengthening.
- **Seasonal Operating Window**: Year-round vs. seasonal.
- **Port of Registry & Home Port**: Determines flag state regulations.

### Cluster C: Performance Requirements

The **engineering translation** of Mission + Operational boundaries.

- **Service Speed (V_s)**: The sustained cruising speed in laden condition.
- **Maximum Speed (V_max)**: Trial speed in calm, deep water.
- **Endurance (E)**: Number of days the vessel can operate without refueling/resupply.
- **Range (R)**: Distance covered between bunkerings.
- **Maneuverability**: Turning circle diameter, tactical diameter.

### Cluster D: Capacity Requirements

- **Deadweight (DWT)**: Cargo + fuel + water + stores + crew + catch.
- **Fish Hold Volume (V_hold)**: Geometric hold capacity in m³.
- **Bunker Capacity (W_f)**: Fuel oil + lube oil weights.
- **Fresh Water Capacity (W_fw)**: Potable + technical water.
- **Refrigeration Plant Capacity (Q_ref)**: In kW or TR (tons of refrigeration).
- **Crew Accommodation**: Number of berths, hospital, mess, sanitary.

### Cluster E: Regulatory & Statutory

- **Classification Society**: Indian Register of Shipping (IRS), Lloyd's Register (LR), Bureau Veritas (BV), DNV.
- **Flag State Authority**: Directorate General of Shipping (India), MCA (UK), USCG.
- **International Conventions**: SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line, ILO 147 (Fishermen's Convention), STCW-F.
- **National Regulations**: Fishing Vessel Rules under MS Act 2021 (India).

### Cluster F: Economic Constraints

- **Newbuilding Cost Ceiling (C_NB)**: Capital expenditure limit.
- **Daily Operational Cost Target (C_op)**: Fuel + crew + maintenance.
- **Expected Payback Period**: ROI-based constraint on first-cost.
- **Insurance Premium Ceiling**: Influenced by class and safety features.

## 2.2 Interrelationships Between Specification Parameters

The Owner's Specifications form a **closed, interdependent system**. Changing one parameter cascades through the entire design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Governing Constraints (KTU Exam Favorite):**
> 1. **Catch Capacity** (V_hold) ← dictates cargo hold volume → dictates hull volume → dictates principal dimensions.
> 2. **Service Speed** (V_s) ← dictates effective power (EHP) → dictates installed power (MCR) → dictates fuel consumption → dictates bunker capacity → affects endurance and vice-versa.
> 3. **Endurance** (E) ← dictates fuel weight → constrains deadweight allocation → constrains catch capacity.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|-----------|--------|---------------------|-------|-------|
| Range | R | $R = V_s \times 24 \times E$ | nautical miles (nm) | E = endurance in days |
| Fuel Consumption Rate | $g_f$ | $g_f = \dfrac{m_f}{P \cdot t}$ | g/kWh | Specific fuel oil consumption |
| Total Fuel Weight | $W_f$ | $W_f = g_f \times P_{MCR} \times 24 \times E / 10^6$ | tonnes | $P_{MCR}$ in kW |
| Deadweight | DWT | $DWT = W_{catch} + W_f + W_{fw} + W_{stores} + W_{crew}$ | tonnes | Sum of variable loads |
| Block Coefficient (typical) | $C_B$ | $C_B = 0.50 \text{ to } 0.65$ (trawlers), $0.55 \text{ to } 0.70$ (seiners) | — | Empirical |
| Displacement Volume | $\nabla$ | $\nabla = \dfrac{DWT}{\rho_w \cdot C_B}$ | m³ | $\rho_w$ = 1.025 t/m³ (sea) |
| Fish Hold Volume | $V_{hold}$ | $V_{hold} = \dfrac{W_{catch}}{\rho_{fish} \cdot \phi_{hold}}$ | m³ | $\phi_{hold}$ = stowage factor (0.55–0.75) |
| Effective Horsepower | EHP | $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V_s}{75}$ (metric) | HP | $R_T$ = total resistance in kgf |
| Brake Horsepower | BHP | $BHP = \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_{prop} \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{shaft}}$ | HP | Combined propulsive efficiency |
| Specific Daily Fuel | $q_d$ | $q_d = g_f \cdot P_{MCR} / 1000$ | tonnes/day | Quick estimation |
| Endurance Check | E | $E = \dfrac{W_f}{q_d}$ | days | Must satisfy owner's E |
| Froude Number | $F_n$ | $F_n = \dfrac{V_s}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}}$ | — | Resistance regime check |
| Ice Class Bonus (T) | $T_{ice}$ | $T_{ice} = T_{design} + \Delta T_{ice}$ | m | Ice strengthening |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** When using the vertical pipe for absolute values in formulas (e.g., $\vert x \vert$), use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Owner's Specification is not just an academic exercise — it is the **backbone of every successful shipyard contract globally**. In shipyards such as Cochin Shipyard, GRSE, or international builders like VARD, Kleven, or Astilleros Armón, the Owner's Specification translates directly into:

- **Request for Proposal (RFP)** documents.
- **Classification society approval drawings**.
- **Owner-supplied equipment lists (OSEL)**.
- **Trim and stability booklet inputs**.

A well-prepared Owner's Specification **reduces rework, contractual disputes, and post-delivery modifications** — which are notoriously expensive in shipbuilding.
