# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Translating Catch Capacity into Hull Principal Dimensions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **classic KTU Module 2 / Module 3 bridge question**. Students are expected to know how Owner's Specifications feed the Preliminary Design phase.

### Given (from Owner's Specification):
- Catch per voyage: $W_{catch} = 80$ tonnes
- Service speed: $V_s = 10$ knots
- Endurance: $E = 14$ days
- Crew: $N = 15$ persons
- Fish stowage density: $\rho_{fish} = 0.85$ t/m³ (typical pelagic)
- Hold stowage factor: $\phi_{hold} = 0.65$
- Block coefficient: $C_B = 0.58$ (typical mid-water trawler)
- Daily fuel consumption rate: $g_f = 220$ g/kWh
- Shaft efficiency (overall propulsive): $\eta = 0.55$

### Step 1: Calculate Fish Hold Volume from Owner's Catch Specification

The Owner specifies a target catch weight. The Naval Architect must back-calculate the geometric volume of the fish hold.

$$V_{hold} = \frac{W_{catch}}{\rho_{fish} \cdot \phi_{hold}}$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{hold} &= \frac{80 \text{ tonnes}}{0.85 \text{ t/m}^3 \times 0.65} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{80}{0.5525} \\[6pt]
&= 144.80 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate Required Fuel Weight from Endurance Specification

Endurance dictates how long the vessel must stay at sea. Assuming preliminary installed power $P_{MCR} = 750$ kW:

$$W_f = \frac{g_f \times P_{MCR} \times 24 \times E}{10^6}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_f &= \frac{220 \times 750 \times 24 \times 14}{10^6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{220 \times 750 \times 336}{10^6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{55{,}440{,}000}{10^6} \\[6pt]
&= 55.44 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compile Deadweight from All Specification-Driven Loads

The Owner's Specification implicitly forces the designer to allocate deadweight among all variable loads:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DWT &= W_{catch} + W_f + W_{fw} + W_{stores} + W_{crew} \\[6pt]
&= 80 + 55.44 + W_{fw} + W_{stores} + W_{crew}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming typical allowances:
- Fresh water: $W_{fw} = 1.5$ t (15 persons × 100 L/day × 14 days × 1.0 kg/L / 1000)
- Stores & provisions: $W_{stores} = 3.0$ t
- Crew + effects + luggage: $W_{crew} = 1.5$ t (15 × 100 kg)

$$
\begin{aligned}
DWT &= 80 + 55.44 + 1.5 + 3.0 + 1.5 \\[6pt]
&= 141.44 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Estimate Displacement Volume

$$\nabla = \frac{DWT}{\rho_{sw} \cdot C_B}$$

Where $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³ (standard seawater density).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= \frac{141.44}{1.025 \times 0.58} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{141.44}{0.5945} \\[6pt]
&= 237.91 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Establish Trial Principal Dimensions

Using empirical length-to-displacement ratios for trawlers ($\nabla / L^3 \approx 0.018$ to $0.025$ for service speeds of 10–11 knots):

$$L = \left(\frac{\nabla}{\nabla/L^3}\right)^{1/3}$$

Taking $\nabla / L^3 = 0.020$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \left(\frac{237.91}{0.020}\right)^{1/3} \\[6pt]
&= (11{,}895.5)^{1/3} \\[6pt]
&\approx 22.81 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rounding up for standard class: $L_{WL} \approx 24.0$ m.

Then breadth (typical B/L for trawlers ≈ 0.30–0.35):
$$B = 0.32 \times 24.0 = 7.68 \text{ m} \approx 7.7 \text{ m}$$

Draft (typical T/L for trawlers ≈ 0.18–0.22):
$$T = 0.20 \times 24.0 = 4.8 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The Owner's Specification (catch = 80 t, speed = 10 kn, endurance = 14 d) has now been mathematically translated into principal dimensions $L \approx 24$ m, $B \approx 7.7$ m, $T \approx 4.8$ m — the starting point of the Preliminary Design phase.

## 3.2 Verification of Endurance After Design Iteration

Once EHP and BHP are refined using resistance estimates, the designer must re-verify whether the Owner's endurance requirement is still satisfied:

$$E_{achieved} = \frac{W_f}{g_f \cdot P_{MCR} \cdot 24 / 10^6}$$

If $E_{achieved} < E_{required}$ → designer must either **increase bunker capacity** or **reduce MCR**. Both have cascading consequences, illustrating the **non-linear coupling** of Owner's Specifications.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Sensitivity

| If this Spec Changes → | Direct Effect → | Cascading Effect → | Design Decision Triggered |
|------------------------|-----------------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| ↑ Catch per voyage ($W_{catch}$) | ↑ Hold volume | ↑ Hull volume, ↑ L/B | Need larger vessel |
| ↑ Service speed ($V_s$) | ↑ EHP (cubic with V) | ↑ BHP, ↑ fuel/day | ↑ Bunker or ↓ Endurance |
| ↑ Endurance (E) | ↑ Fuel weight | ↑ DWT, ↓ payload | Catch capacity reduced |
| ↑ Crew (N) | ↑ Accommodation vol. | ↑ Superstructure size | Affects stability (high CoG) |
| ↑ Ice class | ↑ Scantlings | ↑ Steel weight | ↓ Payload |
| ↓ Cost ceiling | Constraint on MCR, steel wt. | Sub-optimal design | Trade-off negotiation |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]
**Define Owner's Specification in the context of fishing vessel design. List any six key parameters it must contain.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Owner's Specification is the formal, structured document prepared by the shipowner (or his representative) that defines all functional, operational, dimensional, and economic requirements the proposed fishing vessel must satisfy. It serves as the primary input to the design spiral and the basis of the design contract.

**Six Key Parameters:**
1. Type of fishing operation (e.g., trawling, seining).
2. Operating area and climatic zone.
3. Service speed and maximum speed.
4. Catch capacity per voyage (tonnes or m³).
5. Endurance (days at sea) / Range (nm).
6. Crew complement and accommodation standard.
*(Additional acceptable answers: classification society, flag state regulations, cost ceiling, fish hold capacity, fishing gear specifications, bunker capacity.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Explain the role of Owner's Specifications in the design spiral. Why is it called the "input vector" of the design?**

**Model Answer:**

The Design Spiral begins with the Owner's Specification because every other decision — hull form, machinery selection, stability check, equipment outfitting — must be made **within the constraints** defined by the Owner. It is called the *input vector* because, like a vector in mathematics, it has both **magnitude** (numerical values like speed = 10 kn) and **direction** (operational intent, e.g., deep-sea trawling). Without this vector, the design spiral has no starting point and produces no convergent solution. The Owner's Specification thus ensures that the iterative spiral converges toward an **owner-satisfying, buildable, class-approved** design.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 2 questions carry internal choice. Two fully solved alternatives are provided below.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

(a) *Discuss in detail the various components of an Owner's Specification for a 24 m stern trawler intended for operation along the Kerala coast.* **(7 marks)**

(b) *From the given Owner's Specifications, calculate the principal dimensions and verify the endurance:*
   - Catch per voyage = 60 tonnes pelagic fish
   - Service speed = 9.5 knots
   - Endurance = 10 days
   - Crew = 12 persons
   - $g_f = 215$ g/kWh, $P_{MCR}$ (preliminary) = 600 kW
   - $C_B = 0.56$, $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³, $\rho_{fish} = 0.85$ t/m³, $\phi_{hold} = 0.65$
   - Assume: $W_{fw} = 1.0$ t, $W_{stores} = 2.5$ t, $W_{crew} = 1.2$ t. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Components of Owner's Specification for a 24 m Stern Trawler (Kerala Coast)**

1. **Mission Definition:** Stern trawling for demersal species (shrimp, pomfret, mackerel, sole). Catch handling: chilled with ice in CSW holds; trip duration 7–10 days.
2. **Operational Boundaries:** Coastal waters within 50 nm of Kerala coast; monsoon-season operation capability; tropical climate; port of registry Cochin.
3. **Performance:** Service speed 9–10 knots; maximum trial speed 11 knots; endurance 10 days; range 2,500 nm.
4. **Capacity:** Fish hold 100–130 m³; fuel oil 25–30 t; freshwater 8–10 t; RSW/CSW plant 15–20 TR.
5. **Crew & Habitability:** 12–14 crew; 2-berth cabins; hospital; galley; mess; sanitary per ILO 147.
6. **Fishing Gear:** Double-rig trawl winches (2 × 5 t pull), net drum, gallows, stern ramp.
7. **Regulatory:** IRS Class (✉ Fishing Vessel notation); SOLAS Chapter V; ILO C147; MS Act 2021.
8. **Economics:** Newbuilding cost ceiling ₹8–10 Cr; daily operating cost target ₹1.5 lakh.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Fish Hold Volume**

$$V_{hold} = \frac{W_{catch}}{\rho_{fish} \cdot \phi_{hold}} = \frac{60}{0.85 \times 0.65} = \frac{60}{0.5525} = 108.60 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Fuel Weight**

$$W_f = \frac{g_f \cdot P_{MCR} \cdot 24 \cdot E}{10^6} = \frac{215 \times 600 \times 24 \times 10}{10^6} = 30.96 \text{ t}$$

**Step 3: Deadweight**

$$DWT = 60 + 30.96 + 1.0 + 2.5 + 1.2 = 95.66 \text{ t}$$

**Step 4: Displacement Volume**

$$\nabla = \frac{DWT}{\rho_{sw} \cdot C_B} = \frac{95.66}{1.025 \times 0.56} = \frac{95.66}{0.574} = 166.66 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5: Length (using $\nabla/L^3 = 0.020$ for 9.5 knot trawler)**

$$L = \left(\frac{166.66}{0.020}\right)^{1/3} = (8{,}333)^{1/3} \approx 20.27 \text{ m}$$

Adopting $L_{WL} = 21.0$ m; then $B = 0.32 \times 21.0 = 6.72$ m; $T = 0.20 \times 21.0 = 4.20$ m.

**Step 6: Endurance Verification**

$$E_{achieved} = \frac{W_f \cdot 10^6}{g_f \cdot P_{MCR} \cdot 24} = \frac{30.96 \times 10^6}{215 \times 600 \times 24} = 10.00 \text{ days} \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Achieved endurance exactly equals required endurance. Design specification is **satisfied**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Alternate]**

(a) *Explain how Owner's Specifications for fishing vessels differ from those for merchant cargo vessels. Discuss with examples.* **(7 marks)**

(b) *A purse seiner is to be designed with the following Owner's Specifications:*
   - Catch per trip: 120 tonnes (pelagic)
   - Service speed: 12 knots
   - Endurance: 21 days
   - Crew: 22 persons
   - $P_{MCR} = 1{,}200$ kW, $g_f = 210$ g/kWh, $C_B = 0.62$, $\phi_{hold} = 0.70$, $\rho_{fish} = 0.82$ t/m³
   - Assume: $W_{fw} = 3.5$ t, $W_{stores} = 5.0$ t, $W_{crew} = 2.5$ t
   - Compute: Hold volume, fuel weight, total DWT, displacement, and check if the design satisfies endurance criterion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Differences in Owner's Specifications — Fishing vs. Merchant Vessels:**

| Parameter | Fishing Vessel | Merchant Cargo Vessel |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------|
| Primary cargo variability | High (catch is stochastic) | Low (containerized, scheduled) |
| Speed-power priority | High (reach grounds fast) | Moderate (schedule-based) |
| Hold design | Insulated, refrigerated, sloped | Cellular, bulk, or tank |
| Maneuverability | Critical (gear handling) | Standard |
| Crew size | 10–30 | 20–25 (container ship) |
| Class notation | ✉ Fishing vessel symbol | Bulk carrier / Tanker symbol |
| Regulatory driver | ILO C147, FV rules | SOLAS, MARPOL heavy focus |
| Operational risk | Higher (weather exposure) | Lower |
| Bunker share of DWT | 20–35% | 10–20% |

Fishing vessel specifications emphasize **maneuverability, gear-handling capability, fish quality preservation, and crew endurance**, whereas merchant specifications prioritize **cargo density, turnaround time, and fuel efficiency per TEU-tonne-mile**.

**(b) Numerical Solution for Purse Seiner:**

**Step 1: Hold Volume**

$$V_{hold} = \frac{120}{0.82 \times 0.70} = \frac{120}{0.574} = 209.06 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Fuel Weight**

$$W_f = \frac{210 \times 1{,}200 \times 24 \times 21}{10^6} = \frac{210 \times 1{,}200 \times 504}{10^6} = \frac{127{,}008{,}000}{10^6} = 127.01 \text{ t}$$

**Step 3: Deadweight**

$$DWT = 120 + 127.01 + 3.5 + 5.0 + 2.5 = 258.01 \text{ t}$$

**Step 4: Displacement**

$$\nabla = \frac{258.01}{1.025 \times 0.62} = \frac{258.01}{0.6355} = 406.0 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5: Endurance Check**

Daily fuel consumption $q_d = \dfrac{210 \times 1{,}200}{10^6} = 0.252$ t/day per kW... (recalculating properly):

$$q_{day} = \frac{g_f \cdot P_{MCR} \cdot 24}{10^6} = \frac{210 \times 1{,}200 \times 24}{10^6} = 6.048 \text{ t/day}$$

$$E_{achieved} = \frac{W_f}{q_{day}} = \frac{127.01}{6.048} = 21.00 \text{ days} \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Endurance exactly satisfied at 21 days. Design specification **met**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing tonnes/kg/kW incorrectly. Always keep $g_f$ in g/kWh and convert via $10^6$.
> 2. **Forgetting Crew Allowance:** Many students compute DWT without crew weight and stores. This loses 1–2 marks per question.
> 3. **Not Closing the Loop:** Computing dimensions without verifying endurance and catch capacity simultaneously.
> 4. **Confusing $C_B$ (block coefficient) with $C_P$ (prismatic coefficient)** — these are NOT interchangeable.
> 5. **Omitting the regulatory cluster** (Class society, ILO 147) in descriptive answers — KTU specifically tests this.
> 6. **Misinterpreting "Range" vs. "Endurance":** Range is in nautical miles; Endurance is in days. They are related but distinct.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Recap — Read Before Every KTU Exam:**

- **Owner's Specification = Input Vector** of the design spiral.
- It is a **legally binding** document forming the **design contract**.
- Contains **6 logical clusters**: Mission, Operational, Performance, Capacity, Regulatory, Economic.
- **Three governing constraints** dominate fishing vessel specs: **Catch Capacity, Service Speed, Endurance**.
- $V_{hold} = W_{catch} / (\rho_{fish} \cdot \phi_{hold})$ — governs hull volume.
- $W_f = g_f \cdot P_{MCR} \cdot 24 \cdot E / 10^6$ — governs bunker weight.
- $DWT = W_{catch} + W_f + W_{fw} + W_{stores} + W_{crew}$ — sum of variable loads.
- $\nabla = DWT / (\rho_{sw} \cdot C_B)$ — converts DWT to displacement volume.
- Principal dimensions follow from empirical $\nabla/L^3$ ratios (0.018–0.025 for trawlers).
- **Range** $R = V_s \times 24 \times E$ in nautical miles.
- **Fishing vessel specs** differ from merchant specs in: maneuverability priority, gear-handling, fish preservation, ILO C147 compliance.
- **Block coefficient**: 0.50–0.65 trawlers; 0.55–0.70 seiners (typical).
- **Stowage factor** $\phi_{hold}$: 0.55–0.75 (lower for whole fish, higher for processed).
- **Always verify** that endurance, speed, and capacity specifications are simultaneously satisfied — not in isolation.
- **Failure to comply** with any single specification = contractual breach = redesign.
- **Classification notations** (e.g., IRS ✉ FV) are statutory requirements, not optional.
- **Crew accommodation** must comply with **ILO Convention C147** (Fishermen's Articles of Agreement) and **STCW-F**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Economy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Design of Fishing Vessels**, **Economy** refers to the systematic economic analysis and financial evaluation of a fishing vessel throughout its operational lifecycle. It encompasses the study of **capital expenditure (CAPEX)**, **operational expenditure (OPEX)**, **revenue generation from catch**, **profitability**, **return on investment (ROI)**, **payback period**, and the determination of an economically optimal vessel size and configuration.

The economy of a fishing vessel is a critical design parameter because the primary objective of any commercial fishing operation is to maximize profit while minimizing capital and operating costs. The economic dimension essentially determines whether a vessel is financially viable over its projected economic life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Economy in fishing vessel design is not merely about cost estimation — it is a *holistic financial framework* that links hull form parameters, propulsion selection, gear choice, and operational strategy to the long-term profitability of the vessel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Economic Life Constants Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - Economic life of wooden fishing vessel: **15 to 20 years**
> - Economic life of steel fishing vessel: **20 to 25 years**
> - Economic life of FRP fishing vessel: **15 to 20 years**
> - Standard discount rate used: **8% to 12%** (bank lending rate + risk premium)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning to buy an auto-rickshaw to start a small transport business. Before buying it, you would want to know:

1. **How much does it cost to buy?** → This is your **Capital Cost (CAPEX)**
2. **How much fuel does it consume per day? What about driver salary, road tax, insurance, and maintenance?** → These are your **Operating Costs (OPEX)**
3. **How much money will I earn daily from passengers?** → This is your **Revenue**
4. **After how many months will I recover my initial investment?** → This is your **Payback Period**
5. **Should I buy a small auto or a larger one that earns more but costs more?** → This is the **Economic Optimization** question

In exactly the same way, the **Economy** of a fishing vessel involves evaluating all these factors — the price of building the vessel, the daily running expenses (fuel, crew wages, ice, port charges, insurance, maintenance), and the income generated from selling the fish catch. The vessel that gives the **best profit-to-investment ratio** is considered the most **economically optimal** design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** A fishing vessel that is technically superior (faster, more stable, larger) is NOT necessarily the most economical. Often, a smaller, simpler, well-matched vessel earns more profit per rupee invested because its operating costs are lower and its utilization rate is higher.

## 1.3 Why Economy Matters in Fishing Vessel Design

| Aspect | Role in Vessel Design |
|---|---|
| **Cost Estimation** | Determines whether the design is financially feasible for the owner |
| **Vessel Sizing** | Establishes the economically optimal length, tonnage, and power |
| **Material Selection** | Wood vs Steel vs FRP — affects both CAPEX and OPEX |
| **Gear Selection** | Net vs Longline vs Trawl — affects revenue potential and operating cost |
| **Profitability** | Ensures long-term viability and bank loan repayment |
| **Government Subsidies** | Many states offer subsidies; economy analysis determines eligibility |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Vessel Economy — The Four Pillars

The economic analysis of a fishing vessel rests on four fundamental pillars:

### Pillar 1: Capital Cost (CAPEX)

The **one-time initial investment** required to acquire the vessel in operational condition. It includes:

- **Hull construction cost** (material + labour)
- **Machinery cost** (engine, gearbox, propeller, auxiliaries)
- **Equipment cost** (fishing gear, winches, nets, electronics, navigation)
- **Outfit cost** (deck fittings, accommodation, safety equipment)
- **Design and classification cost**
- **Financing cost** (interest during construction)

### Pillar 2: Operating Cost (OPEX)

The **annual recurring cost** to run the vessel. It has two major sub-components:

**(a) Fixed Operating Costs (occur regardless of fishing activity):**
- Insurance premiums
- Depreciation
- Crew wages (minimum guaranteed)
- Port registration and licensing
- Loan repayment (principal + interest)
- Administration overhead

**(b) Variable Operating Costs (proportional to fishing activity):**
- Fuel and lubricating oil
- Ice and salt
- Bait
- Provisions for crew
- Net/gear maintenance and replacement
- Catch handling and processing
- Port charges per trip
- Marketing and selling expenses

### Pillar 3: Revenue (Annual Income)

Revenue is generated from the **sale of fish catch**. It depends on:
- **Catch per unit effort (CPUE)**
- **Number of fishing days per year**
- **Species mix and fish market price**
- **Catch value per kg** (varies with species, season, quality)

### Pillar 4: Profit and Economic Indicators

- **Gross Profit** = Revenue − Total Operating Cost
- **Net Profit** = Gross Profit − Tax − Loan Interest
- **Return on Investment (ROI)** = (Net Profit / Capital Cost) × 100%
- **Payback Period** = Capital Cost / Annual Net Profit

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations — They Appear Repeatedly in KTU Board Exams.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capital Cost | $C_c = C_h + C_m + C_e + C_o$ | ₹ (or USD) | Sum of hull, machinery, equipment, outfit |
| 2 | Annual Operating Cost | $C_{op} = C_f + C_v$ | ₹/year | Fixed + Variable costs |
| 3 | Annual Revenue | $R = Q \times P \times D$ | ₹/year | Catch × Price × Fishing days |
| 4 | Annual Gross Profit | $G = R - C_{op}$ | ₹/year | Revenue minus OPEX |
| 5 | Net Profit | $N = G - T - I$ | ₹/year | After tax and interest |
| 6 | Return on Investment | $ROI = \dfrac{N}{C_c} \times 100$ | % | Higher is better |
| 7 | Payback Period | $T_p = \dfrac{C_c}{N}$ | years | Lower is better |
| 8 | Net Present Value | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{N_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_c$ | ₹ | r = discount rate |
| 9 | Benefit-Cost Ratio | $BCR = \dfrac{\sum \text{PV of benefits}}{\sum \text{PV of costs}}$ | ratio | BCR > 1 is acceptable |
| 10 | Depreciation (Straight Line) | $D = \dfrac{C_c - S}{n}$ | ₹/year | S = scrap value, n = life |
| 11 | Economically Optimal Vessel Size | $\dfrac{dR}{dL} = \dfrac{dC}{dL}$ | m (length) | Revenue gain = Cost increase |
| 12 | Fuel Cost (Annual) | $C_{fuel} = P_{inst} \times SFOC \times H \times C_{fl}$ | ₹/year | SFOC in g/kWh |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions:**
> - For a project to be **economically viable**: $NPV \geq 0$, $BCR \geq 1$, $ROI \geq$ bank lending rate
> - Typical acceptable **payback period** for fishing vessels: **5 to 8 years**
> - **Discount rate (r)** should always exceed the inflation rate

## 2.3 Economic Theory Behind Vessel Sizing

The principle of **economic optimization** in vessel design follows a fundamental economic law: as vessel size (length, tonnage, power) increases:

- **Capital cost increases** roughly as $L^{2.5}$ to $L^{3}$ (cubing law for similar form)
- **Operating cost increases** linearly with size
- **Revenue increases** but at a **decreasing rate** due to diminishing returns in catch capacity

The **economically optimal size** is reached at the point where the **marginal revenue gain** equals the **marginal cost increase**. Beyond this point, building a larger vessel yields diminishing profit despite higher absolute revenue.

**Real-World Utility:** This principle is used by naval architects to advise fishermen cooperatives, shipyards, and government agencies (such as the Department of Fisheries, Government of India) on the most cost-effective vessel size for a given fishing ground and target species.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Annual Revenue from Fishing Operations

**Given:**
- Average catch per fishing day: $Q$ (kg/day)
- Number of fishing days per year: $D$ (days)
- Average selling price of catch: $P$ (₹/kg)

**Step 1 — Express daily revenue**
Daily revenue is the product of the quantity of fish landed and the market price per kilogram.

$$R_{daily} = Q \times P$$

**Step 2 — Express annual revenue**
Annual revenue is the daily revenue multiplied by the total number of active fishing days in the year.

$$R_{annual} = R_{daily} \times D$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression**
Substituting the daily revenue expression into the annual revenue formula:

$$R = Q \times P \times D$$

**Result:** This is the standard annual revenue equation used universally in fishing vessel economic analysis. Every additional kg of catch, every additional rupee in market price, and every additional fishing day directly multiplies the total revenue.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Net Present Value (NPV) of a Fishing Vessel

The NPV method converts all future cash flows to their **present-day equivalent** using a discount rate, allowing the owner to evaluate whether the investment is worthwhile today.

**Given:**
- Capital cost at year 0: $C_c$
- Annual net cash flow (assumed constant): $N$ (₹/year)
- Project life: $n$ (years)
- Discount rate: $r$ (decimal)

**Step 1 — Present value of year 1 cash flow**
Money received in year 1 is worth less today because it could have been earning interest. The present value (PV) factor for year 1 is:

$$PV_1 = \frac{N}{(1+r)^1}$$

**Step 2 — Present value of year 2 cash flow**
The discount is compounded for the second year:

$$PV_2 = \frac{N}{(1+r)^2}$$

**Step 3 — General present value of year t**
By induction, the present value of cash flow received at the end of year $t$ is:

$$PV_t = \frac{N}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Step 4 — Sum of all future present values**
The total present value of all future cash flows over the project life is the summation:

$$PV_{total} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{N}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the geometric series**
Since this is a geometric series with first term $\dfrac{N}{1+r}$ and common ratio $\dfrac{1}{1+r}$:

$$PV_{total} = \frac{N}{1+r} \times \frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+r}\right)^n}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+r}}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the denominator**
The denominator simplifies as:

$$1 - \frac{1}{1+r} = \frac{1+r-1}{1+r} = \frac{r}{1+r}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute and simplify**
Substituting the simplified denominator back:

$$PV_{total} = \frac{N}{1+r} \times \frac{(1+r)}{r} \times \left[1 - \frac{1}{(1+r)^n}\right]$$

**Step 8 — Cancel $(1+r)$ terms**
The $(1+r)$ factors cancel out:

$$PV_{total} = \frac{N}{r} \times \left[1 - \frac{1}{(1+r)^n}\right]$$

**Step 9 — Define the NPV**
NPV is the present value of all future cash flows minus the initial capital outlay:

$$\boxed{NPV = \frac{N}{r} \times \left[1 - \frac{1}{(1+r)^n}\right] - C_c}$$

**Step 10 — Decision rule**
- If $NPV > 0$: The vessel is **economically viable** — it earns more than the discount rate.
- If $NPV = 0$: The vessel **breaks even** at the discount rate.
- If $NPV < 0$: The vessel is **not viable** — the investment loses money in present-value terms.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Economically Optimal Vessel Length

**Given:**
- Revenue function: $R(L)$ increases with length $L$ but with diminishing returns
- Cost function: $C(L)$ increases with length $L$ at an accelerating rate
- Profit function: $\Pi(L) = R(L) - C(L)$

**Step 1 — Condition for maximum profit**
Profit is maximized when its derivative with respect to length equals zero:

$$\frac{d\Pi}{dL} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply the differentiation**
Differentiating the profit function:

$$\frac{dR}{dL} - \frac{dC}{dL} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange the equation**

$$\frac{dR}{dL} = \frac{dC}{dL}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**
This is the famous **Marginal Revenue = Marginal Cost** condition. It states that the vessel is economically optimal when the **extra revenue gained from increasing the length by one metre** exactly equals the **extra cost incurred by that same increase**.

**Step 5 — Practical empirical forms**
In fishing vessel practice, empirical relationships are often used:

$$R(L) = a \cdot L^{1.5} \quad \text{(revenue grows slower than size)}$$

$$C(L) = b \cdot L^{2.5} + c \cdot L \quad \text{(capital cost grows faster)}$$

Setting derivatives equal:

$$\frac{dR}{dL} = 1.5a \cdot L^{0.5}$$

$$\frac{dC}{dL} = 2.5b \cdot L^{1.5} + c$$

**Step 6 — Solve for optimal L**

$$L_{opt} = \left(\frac{1.5a - c}{2.5b}\right)^{\frac{1}{1.0}} = \frac{1.5a - c}{2.5b}$$

**Step 7 — Verify second-order condition**
For a maximum, the second derivative must be negative:

$$\frac{d^2\Pi}{dL^2} = \frac{d^2R}{dL^2} - \frac{d^2C}{dL^2} < 0$$

Since cost accelerates faster than revenue, this condition is generally satisfied for typical fishing vessel parameters.

**Result:** The economically optimal vessel length is the size at which the profit curve peaks — neither too small (underutilization) nor too large (overcapitalization).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 15 m fishing vessel costs ₹50 lakhs to build. Annual fuel cost is ₹6 lakhs, crew wages ₹4 lakhs, maintenance ₹2 lakhs, and other expenses ₹2 lakhs. The vessel fishes 250 days/year, catches an average of 400 kg/day, and the fish sells at ₹150/kg. The expected life is 20 years. The discount rate is 10%. Evaluate the investment using NPV.

**Step 1 — Calculate annual revenue**

$$R = 400 \times 150 \times 250 = 1,50,00,000 \text{ ₹} = \text{₹} 1.50 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate annual operating cost**

$$C_{op} = 6 + 4 + 2 + 2 = 14 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate annual net cash flow**

$$N = 1.5 \text{ Crore} - 0.14 \text{ Crore} = 1.36 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 4 — Apply NPV formula with r = 0.10, n = 20**

$$NPV = \frac{1.36}{0.10} \times \left[1 - \frac{1}{(1.10)^{20}}\right] - 0.50$$

**Step 5 — Compute the discount factor**

$$(1.10)^{20} = 6.7275$$

$$\frac{1}{6.7275} = 0.1486$$

**Step 6 — Compute the bracket term**

$$1 - 0.1486 = 0.8514$$

**Step 7 — Compute present value of future cash flows**

$$PV = 13.6 \times 0.8514 = 11.579 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 8 — Compute NPV**

$$NPV = 11.579 - 0.50 = 11.079 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 9 — Decision**

Since $NPV = 11.079$ Crore $\gg 0$, the investment is **highly economically viable**.

**Step 10 — Calculate payback period**

$$T_p = \frac{0.50}{1.36} = 0.368 \text{ years} \approx 4.4 \text{ months}$$

This is exceptionally fast, indicating excellent profitability.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Vessel Economic System

The following block diagram maps the **interactions between economic components** of a fishing vessel, from initial design decisions through operational phases to final profitability assessment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Inputs Length Beam Depth Power] --> B[Capital Cost Estimation]
    B --> C[Initial Investment CAPEX]
    C --> D{Economic Feasibility Check}
    D -->|Reject| E[Redesign with Smaller Parameters]
    E --> A
    D -->|Accept| F[Vessel Construction]
    F --> G[Operational Phase Year 1 to Year n]
    G --> H[Fixed Operating Costs Insurance Depreciation Wages]
    G --> I[Variable Operating Costs Fuel Ice Bait Maintenance]
    G --> J[Revenue Generation Catch x Price x Days]
    J --> K[Annual Gross Profit Calculation]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    K --> L[Tax and Interest Deductions]
    L --> M[Net Annual Cash Flow]
    M --> N[Profitability Metrics]
    N --> O[ROI Calculation]
    N --> P[Payback Period]
    N --> Q[NPV and BCR]
    O --> R{Final Economic Verdict}
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R -->|NPV Positive and ROI Acceptable| S[Vessel Approved for Operation]
    R -->|NPV Negative or ROI Below Threshold| T[Design Modification Recommended]
```

## 4.2 Cost Component Interaction Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CAPEX_GROUP[Capital Cost Block]
        HULL[Hull Construction] --> TOTC[Total Capital Cost]
        MACH[Machinery and Engine] --> TOTC
        GEAR[Fishing Gear and Equipment] --> TOTC
        OUTFIT[Outfit and Accommodation] --> TOTC
    end
    subgraph OPEX_GROUP[Operating Cost Block]
        FIXED[Fixed Costs Insurance Depreciation Wages] --> TOPC[Total Operating Cost]
        VAR[Variable Costs Fuel Ice Bait Maintenance] --> TOPC
    end
    subgraph REVENUE_GROUP[Revenue Generation Block]
        CATCH[Daily Catch kg] --> TOTR[Total Revenue]
        PRICE[Market Price per kg] --> TOTR
        DAYS[Active Fishing Days] --> TOTR
    end
    TOTC --> PROFIT[Annual Net Profit]
    TOPC --> PROFIT
    TOTR --> PROFIT
    PROFIT --> METRICS[ROI Payback NPV]
```

## 4.3 Vessel Sizing Economics Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Increase Vessel Length L] --> B[Capital Cost Rises as L cubed]
    A --> C[Operating Cost Rises Linearly]
    A --> D[Revenue Rises with Diminishing Returns]
    B --> E[Total Cost Curve]
    C --> E
    D --> F[Revenue Curve]
    E --> G[Profit Curve equals Revenue minus Cost]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Is Marginal Revenue Greater than Marginal Cost}
    H -->|Yes| I[Increase L and Recheck]
    H -->|No| J[Decrease L and Recheck]
    H -->|Equal| K[Optimal Economic Length Lopt]
    I --> H
    J --> H
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain the difference between capital cost and operating cost of a fishing vessel with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Capital Cost (CAPEX)** is the one-time initial expenditure incurred in constructing and outfitting the vessel to make it operational. Examples include hull construction cost (₹25 lakhs for a 15 m steel trawler), main engine cost (₹8 lakhs), fishing gear cost (₹4 lakhs), and electronics and navigation equipment (₹3 lakhs). These are incurred **once during the vessel's life**.

**Operating Cost (OPEX)** is the recurring annual expenditure required to keep the vessel fishing and earning revenue. Examples include fuel cost (₹6 lakhs/year), crew wages (₹4 lakhs/year), ice and provisions (₹2 lakhs/year), insurance premium (₹1.5 lakhs/year), and maintenance (₹2 lakhs/year). These recur **every year of operation**.

The key distinction: CAPEX is a **single investment** recovered over the vessel's life, while OPEX is an **annual recurring expense** that must be recovered from each year's revenue.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by economically optimal vessel size? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **economically optimal vessel size** is the length, tonnage, and engine power at which the vessel earns the **maximum annual net profit** per unit of capital invested. It is determined mathematically by the condition:

$$\frac{dR}{dL} = \frac{dC}{dL}$$

where $R$ is annual revenue and $C$ is the sum of annualized capital and operating cost.

**Importance:**
1. **Prevents overcapitalization** — building a vessel larger than necessary wastes investment capital and increases depreciation.
2. **Prevents undercapitalization** — a too-small vessel cannot generate sufficient revenue to cover costs.
3. **Maximizes profit-to-investment ratio** — gives the owner the best return on every rupee invested.
4. **Guides shipyards and designers** in offering the right vessel size for a given fishing ground and target species.
5. **Helps secure bank loans** — financial institutions prefer lending for vessels that are economically optimized and have strong repayment capacity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various components of **capital cost** and **operating cost** of a fishing vessel. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 17 m fishing trawler is proposed for construction. The estimated capital cost is ₹80 lakhs. The vessel is expected to fish 280 days per year with an average catch of 500 kg per day. The average selling price of fish is ₹180 per kg. The annual operating costs are estimated at ₹18 lakhs. The economic life is 20 years and the discount rate is 12%. Evaluate the project using **NPV method** and determine whether the investment is viable. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Components of Capital and Operating Cost (7 Marks):**

**Capital Cost Components (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Hull Construction Cost** — The largest single component (typically 35–45% of total CAPEX). It includes the cost of raw material (steel plate, section, etc.), labour for cutting, welding, and assembly, and surface preparation and painting. For a steel trawler, hull cost ≈ ₹30–35 lakhs.

2. **Machinery Cost** — Includes main engine, auxiliary engine, gearbox, propeller, shaft, steering gear, and pumps. Typically 20–25% of CAPEX. For a 17 m trawler, ≈ ₹16–20 lakhs.

3. **Fishing Equipment Cost** — Nets, winches, derricks, trawl doors, warp lines, fish hold refrigeration. ≈ 10–15% of CAPEX.

4. **Outfit Cost** — Accommodation, deck fittings, life-saving and fire-fighting equipment, navigation and communication electronics (radar, GPS, echo sounder, VHF), and lighting. ≈ 10–15% of CAPEX.

5. **Design and Classification Cost** — Naval architect fees, classification society fees, and registration cost. ≈ 3–5% of CAPEX.

**Operating Cost Components (3.5 Marks):**

**(i) Fixed Costs** (occur regardless of fishing activity):
- Insurance premium (≈ 2–3% of hull value annually)
- Depreciation (straight-line: $D = \dfrac{C_c - S}{n}$)
- Crew wages (minimum guaranteed portion)
- Port registration and annual licensing fees
- Loan repayment (principal portion)
- Office and administration overhead

**(ii) Variable Costs** (proportional to fishing effort):
- Fuel and lubricating oil cost
- Ice and salt for preservation
- Bait (for longline and hook-and-line vessels)
- Provisions for crew
- Net/gear repair and replacement
- Catch handling, packing, and marketing
- Per-trip port charges (berth, harbor dues)
- Landing and auction fees

The total annual operating cost for a typical 17 m trawler ranges from ₹15 to ₹25 lakhs depending on intensity of operation.

---

**(b) NPV Evaluation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate Annual Revenue**

$$R = 500 \times 180 \times 280 = 2,52,00,000 = ₹ 2.52 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Annual Operating Cost**

$$C_{op} = ₹ 18 \text{ lakhs} = ₹ 0.18 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Annual Net Cash Flow**

$$N = 2.52 - 0.18 = ₹ 2.34 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into NPV Formula**
With $r = 0.12$, $n = 20$, $C_c = ₹ 0.80$ Crore:

$$NPV = \frac{2.34}{0.12} \times \left[1 - \frac{1}{(1.12)^{20}}\right] - 0.80$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Compound Factor**

$$(1.12)^{20} = 9.6463$$

$$\frac{1}{9.6463} = 0.1037$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Bracket Term**

$$1 - 0.1037 = 0.8963$$

**Step 7 — Compute Present Value of Future Cash Flows**

$$PV = 19.50 \times 0.8963 = 17.478 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 8 — Compute Final NPV**

$$NPV = 17.478 - 0.80 = ₹ 16.678 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 9 — Decision**

Since $NPV = +₹ 16.678$ Crore $\gg 0$, the project is **highly economically viable**.

**Step 10 — Payback Period Check (Bonus Point)**

$$T_p = \frac{0.80}{2.34} = 0.342 \text{ years} \approx 4.1 \text{ months}$$

This indicates **exceptional profitability** with an extremely short payback period.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **Return on Investment (ROI)** and **Payback Period**. How are they used in evaluating fishing vessel investments? (7 Marks)

**(b)** A fishing vessel costs ₹60 lakhs. The annual gross revenue is ₹95 lakhs and the annual operating cost is ₹70 lakhs. Calculate the **ROI** and **payback period**. If the owner takes a bank loan of ₹50 lakhs at 10% annual interest, calculate the **annual loan repayment** (assuming equal annual instalments over 10 years) and the revised **net profit**. Comment on the investment viability. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) ROI and Payback Period (7 Marks):**

**Return on Investment (ROI):** ROI is the ratio of **annual net profit** to the **total capital cost**, expressed as a percentage. It measures the efficiency of the investment.

$$ROI = \frac{\text{Annual Net Profit}}{\text{Capital Cost}} \times 100\%$$

**Interpretation:**
- ROI > bank lending rate → **Excellent investment**
- ROI = bank lending rate → **Just viable**
- ROI < bank lending rate → **Not advisable**

For fishing vessels, an ROI of **15% to 25%** is considered attractive.

**Payback Period:** The payback period is the **time required** to recover the initial capital cost from the annual net profit.

$$T_p = \frac{\text{Capital Cost}}{\text{Annual Net Profit}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- Payback < 5 years → **Highly attractive**
- Payback 5–8 years → **Acceptable**
- Payback > 8 years → **Risky** for fishing industry

**Use in Fishing Vessel Evaluation:** Both ROI and payback period are **simple, intuitive metrics** that fishermen and small-scale vessel owners can easily understand. They help in:
1. Comparing two competing vessel designs.
2. Justifying bank loan applications.
3. Communicating financial viability to non-technical stakeholders.
4. Quick preliminary screening before detailed NPV analysis.

**Limitations:** They ignore the **time value of money** and do not account for the project life. Hence, they are used along with NPV for comprehensive evaluation.

---

**(b) ROI, Payback, Loan Repayment, and Viability (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate Annual Gross Profit**

$$G = 95 - 70 = ₹ 25 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate ROI (Before Loan)**

$$ROI = \frac{25}{60} \times 100 = 41.67\%$$

This is **exceptionally high** compared to typical fishing vessel benchmarks.

**Step 3 — Calculate Payback Period (Before Loan)**

$$T_p = \frac{60}{25} = 2.4 \text{ years}$$

This is a **very attractive payback period**, well within the acceptable range.

**Step 4 — Calculate Annual Loan Repayment (Equal Instalments)**
Using the capital recovery formula:

$$A = P \times \frac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$$

where $P = 50$ lakhs, $r = 0.10$, $n = 10$.

**Step 5 — Compute the Factor**

$$(1.10)^{10} = 2.5937$$

$$\frac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1} = \frac{0.10 \times 2.5937}{2.5937 - 1} = \frac{0.25937}{1.5937} = 0.16275$$

**Step 6 — Compute Annual Instalment**

$$A = 50 \times 0.16275 = ₹ 8.1375 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 7 — Calculate Revised Net Profit (After Loan Repayment)**

$$N_{revised} = 25 - 8.1375 = ₹ 16.8625 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 8 — Calculate Revised ROI on Owner's Equity**
Owner's own investment = $60 - 50 = ₹ 10$ lakhs

$$ROI_{revised} = \frac{16.8625}{10} \times 100 = 168.6\%$$

**Step 9 — Revised Payback on Owner's Equity**

$$T_{p,revised} = \frac{10}{16.8625} = 0.593 \text{ years} \approx 7.1 \text{ months}$$

**Step 10 — Viability Comment**

Even after accounting for the loan repayment, the owner's own ₹10 lakh equity is recovered in less than **8 months**, generating a return of over **168%** on equity. The investment is **highly viable and bankable**. The vessel can comfortably service the loan from its operating cash flow.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing gross profit with net profit** — always subtract tax, loan interest, and depreciation for net profit calculations.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — when catch is in kg/day and revenue in lakhs, ensure consistency before substitution.
> 3. **Wrong NPV bracket sign** — the formula has $\left[1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^n}\right]$, NOT $\left[\dfrac{1}{(1+r)^n} - 1\right]$. A sign error gives a negative NPV and an incorrect "not viable" verdict.
> 4. **Ignoring scrap value** — in long-life projects, scrap value at year $n$ should be added as a terminal cash flow.
> 5. **Using nominal discount rate instead of real rate** — if cash flows are in real terms, the real discount rate (nominal rate minus inflation) must be used.
> 6. **Skipping the interpretation step** — KTU examiners award marks for the *decision comment* (viable / not viable / marginal), not just the numerical answer.
> 7. **Mixing up payback period formulas** — the simple payback uses undiscounted cash flow; the discounted payback uses discounted cash flow.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Economy** in fishing vessel design = financial evaluation of CAPEX, OPEX, revenue, and profit over vessel life.
- **CAPEX** is one-time; **OPEX** is recurring (fixed + variable).
- **Revenue** = Catch per day × Price per kg × Fishing days per year.
- **Profit** = Revenue − OPEX − Tax − Interest.
- **ROI** = (Net Profit / Capital Cost) × 100%; target ≥ 15–20%.
- **Payback Period** = Capital Cost / Annual Net Profit; target ≤ 5–8 years.
- **NPV** = $\dfrac{N}{r}\left[1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^n}\right] - C_c$; must be ≥ 0 for viability.
- **BCR** = PV of Benefits / PV of Costs; must be ≥ 1 for viability.
- **Optimal vessel size** occurs where marginal revenue = marginal cost: $\dfrac{dR}{dL} = \dfrac{dC}{dL}$.
- **Depreciation** (straight-line): $D = \dfrac{C_c - S}{n}$.
- **Annual loan instalment** (capital recovery): $A = P \cdot \dfrac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$.
- **Typical vessel economic life:** Steel 20–25 years, Wood 15–20 years, FRP 15–20 years.
- **Typical discount rate:** 8–12% for Indian fishing vessel projects.
- **Decision rules** must always be stated explicitly: viable / marginal / not viable.
- **Interpretation step is mandatory** — every numerical answer in KTU board exams must end with a clear economic verdict.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fuel Efficiency ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Fuel Efficiency** in the context of fishing vessel design is formally defined as the ratio of useful propulsive work output delivered by the vessel's propulsion system to the energy content of the fuel consumed to produce that work. It is a multidimensional performance parameter governed by IMO (International Maritime Organization) regulations, hull-form hydrodynamics, propulsion system thermodynamics, and operational profile. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Design of Fishing Vessels*, fuel efficiency is positioned at the intersection of three engineering domains: **Naval Architecture (hull resistance)**, **Marine Engineering (prime-mover performance)**, and **Energy Economics (operational cost analysis)**. The two principal regulatory indices used globally are: - **EEDI – Energy Efficiency Design Index** (design-stage, regulatory): Regulated under MARPOL Annex VI, Chapter 4. Expressed in **grams of CO₂ per tonne-mile (g CO₂ / t·nm)**. - **EEOI – Energy Efficiency Operational Indicator** (operational-stage, voluntary): An IMO guideline index (MEPC.1/Circ.684) used to monitor in-service performance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Industry Metric — Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC):** For a fishing vessel, SFC is typically quoted as **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** in **g/kWh**, with benchmark values of **165 g/kWh** for modern low-speed two-stroke marine diesel engines and **190–210 g/kWh** for medium-speed four-stroke auxiliaries. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a fisherman driving a small autorickshaw (like our fishing trawler) from the harbor to the fishing ground 50 km offshore. The *fuel efficiency* of his trip depends on: 1. **The shape of his vehicle** (streamlined vs. boxy) → equivalent to **hull form** and **resistance**. 2. **The engine inside** (old diesel vs. modern turbocharged) → equivalent to **propulsion plant SFC**. 3. **His driving style** (slow, steady speed vs. aggressive acceleration) → equivalent to **operational profile**. 4. **The propeller "wheel"** he uses (well-matched vs. mismatched) → equivalent to **propulsive efficiency**. 5. **The load he carries** (full catch vs. empty) → equivalent to **displacement & deadweight**. A fishing vessel is no different. Fuel — typically **Marine Gas Oil (MGO)** or **Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO)** in larger trawlers — represents **30–60 % of the voyage operating cost**. Hence, every design decision, from the block coefficient (C_B) to the propeller pitch ratio (P/D), is a fuel-economy decision. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU exams:** In Module 2 of PENST754, fuel efficiency bridges the gap between the *Resistance & Powering* sub-topic (Module 1) and the *Machinery & Propulsion Selection* sub-topic (Module 3). A 1 % improvement in propulsive efficiency can save a mid-sized trawler roughly **₹4–6 lakhs/year** in fuel bills — examiners love this kind of quantified impact. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Fishing Vessel Value | |---|---|---|---| | Carbon Factor (Diesel) | $C_F$ | — | **3.1144** (t CO₂ / t fuel) | | Reference Speed | $V_{ref}$ | knots | **Service speed** (e.g., 10–12 kn) | | Volumetric Mass of Diesel | $\rho_f$ | kg/m³ | **~860 kg/m³** | | Lower Heating Value (Diesel) | LHV | kJ/kg | **42,700 kJ/kg** | | Sea Water Density | $\rho$ | kg/m³ | **1,025 kg/m³** | | Gravitational Acceleration | $g$ | m/s² | **9.81 m/s²** | | Propulsion Efficiency Target | $\eta_D$ | — | **0.55–0.65** | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five-Pillar Architecture of Fuel Efficiency Fuel efficiency in a fishing vessel is decomposed into **five hierarchical engineering pillars**, each of which is a frequent KTU examination topic: **Pillar 1 — Hull Resistance Minimisation** Total resistance $R_T$ is the sum of frictional ($R_F$), wave-making ($R_W$), appendage ($R_{APP}$), and air ($R_{AA}$) components: $$R_T = R_F + R_W + R_{APP} + R_{AA} + R_{B}$$ where $R_B$ is the correlation allowance (bulbous bow interaction, roughness, etc.). The **Effective Power (EHP)** is then: $$P_E = R_T \cdot V_S$$ with $V_S$ the ship speed in m/s. **Pillar 2 — Propulsive Efficiency Chain** The **Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient (QPC)** $\eta_D$ is the multiplicative product of four sub-efficiencies: $$\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_T$$ - **Hull efficiency $\eta_H$**: $\eta_H = \dfrac{1-t}{1-w}$, where $t$ is the thrust deduction fraction and $w$ is the wake fraction. - **Propeller open-water efficiency $\eta_O$**: Determined from B-Series or Wageningen charts; typically 0.55–0.70. - **Rotative efficiency $\eta_R$**: Accounts for rotational losses; ~0.95–0.98 for direct drive. - **Transmission efficiency $\eta_T$**: ~0.97–0.99 for gear-driven shafts. **Pillar 3 — Prime-Mover Thermal Efficiency** The Brake Thermal Efficiency $\eta_{th}$ relates fuel calorific value to brake power: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{P_B \cdot 3600}{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV}$$ For modern marine diesels, $\eta_{th}$ ranges from **0.38–0.46**; for gas turbines, only **0.28–0.32**. **Pillar 4 — Operational Profile** Power scales with the **cube of speed** ($P \propto V^3$). A 10 % reduction in service speed yields approximately a **27 % reduction in fuel consumption** (a classic KTU problem statement). **Pillar 5 — Energy Recovery & Hybridisation** Waste-heat recovery systems, shaft generators, hybrid battery drives, and solar PV auxiliary systems contribute to overall vessel energy economy. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Boundary Condition | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_B}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh | | 2 | $P_E = R_T \cdot V_S$ | Effective Power | W (or kW) | | 3 | $\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_T$ | Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient | dimensionless | | 4 | $P_S = \dfrac{P_E}{\eta_D}$ | Shaft Power (delivered to propeller) | W (or kW) | | 5 | $P_B = \dfrac{P_S}{\eta_{GB}}$ | Brake Power (with gearbox) | W (or kW) | | 6 | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{BSFC \cdot P_B}{10^6}$ | Hourly fuel mass flow | t/h | | 7 | $EEDI = \dfrac{CO_2 \text{ emissions}}{Capacity \cdot V_{ref}}$ | Energy Efficiency Design Index | g CO₂ / (t·nm) | | 8 | $CO_2 = C_F \cdot \dot{m}_f$ | CO₂ mass per tonne of fuel | t CO₂ / t fuel | | 9 | $EEOI = \dfrac{\sum_j C_{Fj} \cdot m_{fj}}{\sum_k m_{cargo,k} \cdot D_k}$ | Operational Indicator (per voyage) | g CO₂ / (t·nm) | | 10 | $R_F = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_F$ | Frictional resistance (ITTC-78) | N | | 11 | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$ | ITTC-78 friction line | dimensionless | | 12 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{P_B}{Q_{fuel} \cdot \dot{m}_f}$ | Thermal efficiency | dimensionless | | 13 | $V_{opt} \approx V_{design} \cdot \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{C_{B,design}}{C_{B,opt}}}$ | Hull-form optimal speed rule | m/s | | 14 | $A_{adm} = \dfrac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V_S^3}{P_S}$ | Admiralty coefficient | — | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The Admiralty Coefficient $A_{adm}$ is a powerful single-number metric that captures hull form, displacement, and propulsive efficiency. Vessels with $A_{adm} \approx 500$–$700$ are well-designed for trawling duty; values below $400$ indicate design inefficiency and are a red flag in naval-architecture peer review. ### 2.3 The EEDI Reference Line For a fishing vessel of deadweight $DWT$, the **required EEDI** (the regulatory cap, Phase 2 for 2025–2030) is: $$EEDI_{req} = a \cdot DWT^{-c}$$ where $a$ and $c$ are IMO-defined coefficients depending on vessel type and phase. For fishing vessels (per IMO Res. MEPC.364(79)), Phase 2 requires approximately a **10–15 % reduction** from the baseline. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility In the Indian fishing industry — particularly the deep-sea trawler fleet operating from Veraval, Kochi, and Visakhapatnam — fuel efficiency drives **fleet economics**. Indian fishing vessels typically operate at 9–12 knots and burn **1.5–3.5 tonnes of fuel per day**. A 5 % improvement across a 50-vessel fleet translates to **₹4–6 crore/year** in savings and **~3,000 tonnes of CO₂ avoided**, aligning directly with India's **Panchamrit** climate commitments. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From BSFC to Total Fuel Consumption We derive the daily fuel consumption of a fishing trawler given shaft power $P_S$, gearbox efficiency $\eta_{GB}$, and brake specific fuel consumption $BSFC$. **Step 1 — Convert shaft power to brake power.** The engine must deliver more power than the shaft receives because of gearbox mechanical losses. The brake power at the engine output is: $$P_B = \frac{P_S}{\eta_{GB}}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the BSFC definition.** By definition, $BSFC$ is the fuel mass flow per unit brake power per unit time: $$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_B} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{m}_f = BSFC \cdot P_B$$ **Step 3 — Express in consistent units.** $BSFC$ is given in g/kWh; if $P_B$ is in kW and we want $\dot{m}_f$ in kg/h: $$\dot{m}_f \,[\text{kg/h}] = \frac{BSFC \,[\text{g/kWh}] \cdot P_B \,[\text{kW}]}{1000}$$ **Step 4 — Daily consumption.** If the vessel operates $T$ hours per day at average power $P_{B,avg}$: $$\boxed{m_{f,day} = \frac{BSFC \cdot P_{B,avg} \cdot T}{1000} \quad \text{[kg/day]}}$$ **Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example.** Given: $P_S = 750$ kW, $\eta_{GB} = 0.97$, $BSFC = 195$ g/kWh, $T = 16$ h/day. $$P_B = \frac{750}{0.97} = 773.2 \text{ kW}$$ $$\dot{m}_f = \frac{195 \times 773.2}{1000} = 150.77 \text{ kg/h}$$ $$m_{f,day} = 150.77 \times 16 = 2412 \text{ kg/day} \approx 2.41 \text{ tonnes/day}$$ This is fully consistent with mid-sized Indian trawler consumption patterns. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — EEDI Calculation for a Fishing Vessel **Step 1 — Identify all consumers of mechanical/electrical power on board.** For a fishing vessel, the EEDI denominator-multiplier includes: - Main engines (ME): propulsion + auxiliary drive via shaft generator - Auxiliary engines (AE): pumps, refrigeration, winches, processing plant **Step 2 — Write the CO₂ mass flow equation.** $$m_{CO_2} = C_F \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( SFC_{ME,i} \cdot P_{ME,i} + SFC_{AE,i} \cdot P_{AE,i} \right)$$ **Step 3 — Divide by the transport work.** The "useful work" of a fishing vessel is conventionally expressed as deadweight $DWT$ multiplied by the reference speed $V_{ref}$ (in nautical miles per voyage): $$\boxed{EEDI = \frac{C_F \cdot \sum_i (SFC_i \cdot P_i)}{DWT \cdot V_{ref}} \quad \text{[g CO}_2\text{/t·nm]}}$$ **Step 4 — Sample Calculation.** Given a fishing trawler: - Main engine: $SFC_{ME} = 180$ g/kWh, $P_{ME} = 800$ kW - Aux engine: $SFC_{AE} = 210$ g/kWh, $P_{AE} = 60$ kW - $DWT = 250$ tonnes, $V_{ref} = 11$ knots $$m_{CO_2/h} = 3.1144 \cdot (180 \cdot 800 + 210 \cdot 60) \cdot 10^{-3} \text{ t/h}$$ $$m_{CO_2/h} = 3.1144 \cdot (144{,}000 + 12{,}600) \cdot 10^{-3}$$ $$m_{CO_2/h} = 3.1144 \cdot 156.6 = 487.7 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{h}$$ $$\text{Transport work} = 250 \text{ t} \cdot 11 \text{ nm/h} = 2750 \text{ t·nm/h}$$ $$EEDI = \frac{487{,}700 \text{ g}}{2750 \text{ t·nm}} = 177.3 \text{ g CO}_2/\text{t·nm}$$ This value falls well within Phase 1 IMO limits for small fishing vessels. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Speed Reduction and Fuel Savings **Step 1 — Resistance-Speed Relation.** At a given displacement, $R_T \propto V^n$ where $n \approx 3.0$ for modern fishing trawlers (between 3.0 and 3.3 in the high-speed regime). **Step 2 — Power-Speed Relation.** $$P_E = R_T \cdot V \propto V^3$$ **Step 3 — Fuel-Speed Relation.** Since $\dot{m}_f \propto P_B \propto V^3$ (at constant SFC), a reduction in speed $\Delta V$ yields: $$\frac{\dot{m}_{f,new}}{\dot{m}_{f,old}} = \left(\frac{V_{new}}{V_{old}}\right)^3$$ **Step 4 — Worked Calculation.** A vessel currently operating at 12 knots considers slow steaming at 10 knots: $$\frac{\dot{m}_{f,new}}{\dot{m}_{f,old}} = \left(\frac{10}{12}\right)^3 = (0.833)^3 = 0.579$$ **Fuel saving ≈ 42.1 %**, but **voyage time increases by 20 %**. The vessel master must therefore weigh *time-cost* (perishable catch) against *fuel-cost* — a classic KTU essay-style question. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Propulsive Efficiency Decomposition Starting from the fundamental energy flow: $$\eta_D = \frac{P_E}{P_S} = \frac{P_E}{P_D} \cdot \frac{P_D}{P_T} \cdot \frac{P_T}{P_S}$$ where: - $P_D$ = power delivered to the propeller disc = thrust $T$ × advance velocity $V_A$ - $P_T$ = thrust power at the propeller shaft - $P_S$ = delivered shaft power $$\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_T = \frac{1-t}{1-w} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q} \cdot \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_T$$ This compact relationship is a KTU high-yield result linking hull, propeller, and machinery efficiencies. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fuel-Efficiency System of a Fishing Vessel ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Fuel Tank
MGO or HFO] --> B[Main Engine
2-stroke Diesel] A --> C[Auxiliary Engine
4-stroke Diesel] B --> D[Gearbox] D --> E[Propeller Shaft] E --> F[Propeller
Open Water Efficiency] F --> G[Hull
Wakes Thrust Deduction] G --> H[Effective Resistance
Friction Wave Appendage] C --> I[Electrical Generators
Refrigeration Winches] I --> J[Auxiliary Load
Lighting Processing Plant] B --> K[Brake Power Output
SFC Benchmark 165 to 195 g/kWh] K --> L[Quasi Propulsive Coefficient
eta_D 0.55 to 0.65] L --> M[Effective Power
P_E kW] M --> N[EEDI EEOI
Compliance Indices] H --> O[Total Resistance R_T] O --> M N --> P[Operational Decisions
Speed Trim Route] P --> L ``` **Caption:** Block diagram illustrating the cascading energy losses from the fuel tank (left) to the regulatory EEDI/EEOI output (right). Each block represents a stage of efficiency loss or conversion. --- ### 4.2 Energy Flow Sankey-Style Concept Map ```mermaid flowchart LR F1[Fuel Chemical Energy
100 percent] --> F2[Engine Losses
55 to 60 percent] F2 --> F3[Brake Power P_B
40 to 45 percent] F3 --> F4[Gearbox Losses
3 percent] F4 --> F5[Shaft Power P_S
38 to 43 percent] F5 --> F6[Propeller Losses
30 to 35 percent] F6 --> F7[Thrust Power P_T
25 to 30 percent] F7 --> F8[Hull Losses
Wake Thrust Deduction
8 to 12 percent] F8 --> F9[Effective Power P_E
15 to 22 percent] F9 --> F10[Useful Work at Sea
Vessel Moving Forward] ``` **Caption:** Energy cascade from fuel calorific value to effective towing work. Each block shows the typical loss percentage in modern trawler-class vessels. --- ### 4.3 EEDI Compliance Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1 Identify Vessel Type] --> S2[Step 2 Determine DWT and V_ref] S2 --> S3[Step 3 Compute Baseline EEDI from IMO Formula] S3 --> S4{Step 4 Calculated EEDI less than Required EEDI} S4 -- Yes --> S5[Compliant No Action] S4 -- No --> S6[Step 5 Apply Reduction Measures] S6 --> S7[Option A Hull Form Optimisation] S6 --> S8[Option B Engine Downgrade and Hybrid] S6 --> S9[Option C Slow Steaming Operational Profile] S6 --> S10[Option D Waste Heat Recovery] S7 --> S11[Recompute EEDI] S8 --> S11 S9 --> S11 S10 --> S11 S11 --> S4 ``` **Caption:** Iterative design loop used by naval architects to achieve EEDI compliance through a portfolio of engineering interventions. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** *Define Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC). State its typical range for a modern medium-speed marine diesel engine.* **Model Answer:** BSFC is defined as the mass of fuel consumed per unit of brake power delivered per unit of time: $$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_B} \quad [\text{g/kWh}]$$ For modern medium-speed four-stroke marine diesel engines used as auxiliary units in fishing vessels, BSFC typically lies in the range of **190–210 g/kWh**, while low-speed two-stroke main propulsion engines achieve **160–175 g/kWh**. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for typical values)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** *State the formula for the Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI). Why is this index important for fishing vessels?* **Model Answer:** EEDI is the ratio of the predicted CO₂ mass emissions of a vessel per tonne of deadweight per nautical mile of reference speed: $$EEDI = \frac{C_F \cdot \sum_i (SFC_i \cdot P_i)}{DWT \cdot V_{ref}} \quad [\text{g CO}_2/\text{t·nm}]$$ **Importance for fishing vessels:** 1. Regulatory compliance under MARPOL Annex VI Chapter 4. 2. Direct cost control — fuel represents 30–60 % of operating expenses. 3. Environmental stewardship — India's commitments under the **IMO MARPOL** convention and the **Panchamrit** climate framework require measurable CO₂ reductions even in small-craft categories. *(3 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for importance)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]* **(a)** Explain in detail the **five-pillar architecture of fuel efficiency** in a fishing vessel. Discuss the role of hull form, propulsive efficiency, prime-mover performance, operational profile, and energy-recovery systems. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A fishing trawler has the following particulars: - Shaft power $P_S$ = 900 kW - Gearbox efficiency $\eta_{GB}$ = 0.96 - Brake Specific Fuel Consumption $BSFC$ = 185 g/kWh - Daily operation $T$ = 18 hours Compute the **(i)** brake power, **(ii)** hourly fuel consumption, and **(iii)** monthly (30-day) fuel cost, assuming diesel price ₹85/litre and density 860 kg/m³. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Five-Pillar Architecture:** | Pillar | Key Parameter | Typical Range | KTU Engineering Lever | |---|---|---|---| | Hull Resistance | $R_T$ | Minimize via $C_B$ optimisation | Bulbous bow, stern flaps, hull coating | | Propulsive Efficiency | $\eta_D$ | 0.55–0.65 | Propeller design, wake tuning | | Prime-Mover Efficiency | $\eta_{th}$ | 0.38–0.46 | Engine selection, turbo-compounding | | Operational Profile | $V$ | Service speed ±10 % | Slow steaming, weather routing | | Energy Recovery | $\eta_{recovery}$ | 5–10 % | Shaft generator, ORC waste-heat | *(7 marks: 1 per pillar × 5 + 2 for synthesis and examples)* **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1 — Brake Power:** $$P_B = \frac{P_S}{\eta_{GB}} = \frac{900}{0.96} = 937.5 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Hourly Fuel Consumption:** $$\dot{m}_f = \frac{BSFC \cdot P_B}{1000} = \frac{185 \times 937.5}{1000} = 173.44 \text{ kg/h}$$ Converting to litres (using $\rho_f = 860$ kg/m³): $$\dot{V}_f = \frac{173.44}{860} \times 1000 = 201.7 \text{ L/h}$$ **Step 3 — Daily and Monthly Consumption:** $$m_{f,day} = 173.44 \times 18 = 3122 \text{ kg/day} \approx 3630 \text{ L/day}$$ $$m_{f,month} = 3630 \times 30 = 108{,}900 \text{ L/month}$$ **Step 4 — Monthly Cost:** $$\text{Cost} = 108{,}900 \times 85 = ₹92{,}56{,}500 \approx ₹92.6 \text{ lakhs/month}$$ *(7 marks: 1 each for steps 1–4 + 1 each for unit conversion and cost calculation + 1 for final answer with units)* --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]* **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **components of the propulsive efficiency chain** of a fishing vessel. Derive the relationship between effective power, shaft power, and brake power. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A fishing trawler currently operates at 12 knots and consumes 3.0 tonnes of fuel per day. The owner proposes slow steaming at 10 knots to reduce costs. Calculate the **percentage fuel saving per day** and discuss the **trade-offs**. Assume resistance scales with $V^{3.2}$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Propulsive Efficiency Chain — Explanation & Derivation:** The energy flow from fuel to effective propulsion undergoes four sequential losses: $$\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_T$$ - **Hull Efficiency $\eta_H$**: Power delivered to the water vs. useful thrust power behind the hull. $\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w}$. - **Propeller Open Water Efficiency $\eta_O$**: Thrust power delivered by the propeller vs. power absorbed at the propeller shaft in open water. - **Rotative Efficiency $\eta_R$**: Accounts for bearing friction and rotational losses. - **Transmission Efficiency $\eta_T$**: Gearbox and shaft-bearing losses. **Derivation of Power Cascade:** $$P_B \cdot \eta_{GB} \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_T = P_S \quad \text{(Shaft Power)}$$ $$P_S \cdot \eta_O = P_T \quad \text{(Thrust Power)}$$ $$P_T \cdot \eta_H = P_E \quad \text{(Effective Power)}$$ Multiplying: $$P_E = P_B \cdot \eta_{GB} \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_T \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_H$$ $$\boxed{P_E = P_B \cdot \eta_{GB} \cdot \eta_D}$$ *(7 marks: 4 for component explanation + 3 for derivation with units)* **(b) Slow-Steaming Numerical Solution:** **Step 1 — Establish the fuel-speed relationship.** With $R_T \propto V^{3.2}$, power scales as $P \propto V^{4.2}$, and fuel consumption scales the same way: $$\frac{\dot{m}_{f,new}}{\dot{m}_{f,old}} = \left(\frac{V_{new}}{V_{old}}\right)^{3.2}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the ratio:** $$\frac{10}{12} = 0.8333$$ $$(0.8333)^{3.2} = e^{3.2 \cdot \ln(0.8333)} = e^{3.2 \cdot (-0.1823)} = e^{-0.5834} = 0.558$$ **Step 3 — Compute new daily consumption:** $$m_{f,new} = 3.0 \times 0.558 = 1.674 \text{ tonnes/day}$$ **Step 4 — Fuel saving percentage:** $$\text{Saving} = (1 - 0.558) \times 100 = \boxed{44.2 \%}$$ **Trade-offs:** | Aspect | Impact | |---|---| | Fuel cost | ↓ 44 % — significant savings | | Voyage time | ↑ 20 % — fewer trips possible | | Catch freshness | ↓ Risk of spoilage on long hauls | | Engine load | ↓ Low-load operation causes fouling | | Crew efficiency | ↓ Long voyages increase fatigue | **Conclusion:** Slow steaming is viable on outbound laden voyages when return is with light load and perishable-catch time budget permits. *(7 marks: 2 for power-cube formula derivation, 2 for numerical substitution, 1 for trade-off table, 2 for engineering reasoning)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mix-up in BSFC:** Students frequently forget to divide by 1000 when converting g/kWh to kg/kWh. Always write the conversion explicitly. > 2. **Confusing SFC and BSFC:** BSFC uses **brake** power, not indicated or shaft power. Examiners deduct marks for the wrong denominator. > 3. **EEDI arithmetic:** $C_F = 3.1144$ applies to *diesel/gasoil*. Using 2.75 (LNG) by mistake costs 2 marks. > 4. **Hull efficiency formula:** $\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w}$, **not** $\frac{1-w}{1-t}$. This inversion error is a classic mistake. > 5. **Slow steaming cube law:** Examiners expect the **3.0 exponent for low-speed hulls** but **3.0–3.3 for high-speed fishing trawlers**. Always quote the regime and exponent explicitly. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Fuel Efficiency in Fishing Vessels** - **BSFC** is the master fuel-economy metric for the engine: typically **160–175 g/kWh** (2-stroke main) or **190–210 g/kWh** (4-stroke aux). - **Effective Power** $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ — purely a hull hydrodynamics problem. - **Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient** $\eta_D = \eta_H \eta_O \eta_R \eta_T$ — multiplicative, never additive. - **Hull Efficiency** $\eta_H = \dfrac{1-t}{1-w}$ — wake fraction $w$ and thrust deduction $t$ are always positive fractions. - **Power scales as $V^3$** (low-speed regime) or **$V^{3.2}$** (high-speed trawler regime) — speed reduction is the single most powerful operational fuel-saving lever. - **EEDI** uses $C_F = 3.1144$ for diesel — divide by $DWT \times V_{ref}$ to obtain g CO₂ / t·nm. - **EEOI** is a voyage-based operational indicator — applicable to fishing fleet managers monitoring in-service efficiency. - **Admiralty Coefficient** $A_{adm} = \dfrac{\Delta^{2/3} V^3}{P_S}$ — a single-number yardstick; values **500–700** indicate good design. - **Frictional resistance** follows the **ITTC-78 line**: $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$. - **Five fuel-efficiency pillars:** Hull form, Propulsive chain, Prime-mover, Operational profile, Energy recovery. - **Energy cascade:** Roughly **15–22 %** of fuel calorific value becomes useful effective power — the rest is lost to heat, friction, and wake. - **Regulatory anchor:** IMO MARPOL Annex VI Chapter 4 (EEDI) — applies to commercial fishing vessels ≥ 100 GT engaged in international voyages; Indian flag vessels follow DG Shipping guidelines harmonised with IMO. - **Practical Indian context:** Kerala's deep-sea squid jigging fleet and Gujarat's trawler fleet are prime candidates for hybrid propulsion and waste-heat recovery retrofits under the **PMMSY** (Pradhan Mantri Matsya Sampada Yojana) scheme. - **Exam tip:** Always carry forward significant figures to **three decimal places** in BSFC and EEDI calculations — KTU examiners are strict about this.

topics.title: Hull Form
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hull Form

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Hull Form

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hull Form** of a fishing vessel refers to the three-dimensional geometric shape of the underwater and above-water portions of the ship's body, defined mathematically by a set of coordinated offsets (points of intersection between station, waterline, and buttock planes) and characterized quantitatively by a family of dimensionless **form coefficients** (block, prismatic, midship, and waterplane coefficients).

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, hull form encompasses the lines plan, hull geometry parameters (principal dimensions and their ratios), the form coefficients that describe hull fullness, and the structural cross-sectional shapes (round bilge, hard chine, multi-chine) most suitable for fishing operations.

The hull form of any ship is fundamentally defined by three orthogonally intersecting sets of reference planes:

- **Station planes** — vertical transverse planes perpendicular to the ship's centerline, used to define the cross-sectional shape of the hull at any longitudinal position.
- **Waterline planes** — horizontal planes at various heights, used to define the shape of the hull at any draft level.
- **Buttock planes** — vertical longitudinal planes parallel to the ship's centerline, used to define the longitudinal curvature of the hull.

The intersection of these three sets of planes produces a network of **offsets** (or "lines") that completely describe the hull form.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Watermelon Seed" Concept

Imagine you drop a smooth, oval watermelon seed into still water. It glides forward smoothly because its shape is streamlined — narrow at the ends, fuller in the middle, with gentle curves. Now imagine trying to push a flat wooden block through water; it will tumble and resist because it has abrupt shape changes (hard chines, flat surfaces).

A fishing vessel's hull form is essentially the designer's answer to the question: *"What shape will move efficiently through water at our service speed, carry our fishing gear and catch, and stay stable in rough seas?"*

For a trawler working in the North Atlantic, the answer leans toward a **rounded, full-bodied form** (like the watermelon seed) that gives volume for fuel and fish rooms. For a fast longline vessel, the designer picks a **slimmer, finer form** that prioritizes speed and fuel economy.

> [!NOTE]
> The hull form is the single most important geometric decision in fishing vessel design — it dictates resistance, propulsion efficiency, stability, seakeeping, cargo capacity, and even the type of fishing gear that can be deployed.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard dimensional ratios and metrics are universally used to characterize hull form:

- **L** — Length Overall (LOA) or Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP) in **meters (m)**
- **B** — Moulded Breadth in **meters (m)**
- **T** — Moulded Draft in **meters (m)**
- **D** — Moulded Depth in **meters (m)**
- **Δ** — Displacement (volume) in **cubic meters (m³)**
- **W** — Displacement (weight) in **tonnes (t)**, where $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot \Delta$
- **ρ (rho)** — Density of seawater = **1025 kg/m³** (standard seawater at 4 °C)
- **g** — Gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²**

Standard length-to-beam ratios (L/B) for fishing vessels typically range from **3.5 to 5.5**, and beam-to-draft ratios (B/T) from **2.0 to 3.5**, depending on vessel type.

## 1.4 Lines Plan — The "Blueprint" of Hull Form

The **Lines Plan** (or Lines Drawing) is the traditional 2D engineering drawing that fully defines the 3D hull form using three principal views:

| View Name | What It Shows | Plane of Projection |
|---|---|---|
| **Sheer Plan** (Profile View) | Side view showing waterlines, buttock lines, and station numbers | Vertical longitudinal plane |
| **Body Plan** | Cross-section shapes (forward of midship on right, aft on left) | Transverse station planes |
| **Half-Breadth Plan** | Top view showing waterline shapes and buttock intersections | Horizontal waterline plane |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mastery of the Lines Plan is essential for KTU university exam questions on hull form — examiners often test the student's ability to interpret these views and identify form coefficients from given dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principal Dimensions of the Hull Form

The hull form is fully characterized by a set of **principal dimensions** that together define the overall size and proportions. Each dimension serves a specific naval architectural purpose.

### 2.1.1 Length Definitions

- **Length Overall (LOA):** Total tip-to-tip length of the vessel, measured along the design waterline including any bow or stern protrusions.
- **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP or Lpp):** Distance between the forward perpendicular (FP, usually at the design waterline intersection with the stem) and the aft perpendicular (AP, usually through the rudder post or centerline of the rudder).
- **Length on the Waterline (Lwl):** Length of the design waterline intersection with the hull.

### 2.1.2 Breadth, Draft, and Depth

- **Moulded Breadth (B):** Maximum horizontal width measured at the midship section, measured from the inside of the shell plating (or moulded surface).
- **Moulded Draft (T):** Vertical distance from the design waterline to the lowest point of the keel (or baseline) at the midship section.
- **Moulded Depth (D):** Vertical distance from the baseline (keel) to the top of the freeboard deck (sheer line) at the midship section.

### 2.1.3 Dimensional Ratios — The Designer's "Fingerprint"

The ratios of principal dimensions are the most powerful single indicators of a vessel's intended behavior. Each ratio has a clear physical interpretation:

- **Length-to-Beam Ratio (L/B):** A high L/B (e.g., 6.0) indicates a slim, fast hull with low resistance; a low L/B (e.g., 3.5) indicates a full-bodied hull with high cargo capacity but more resistance.
- **Beam-to-Draft Ratio (B/T):** A high B/T (e.g., 3.5) gives good initial stability and a large deck area (good for trawlers); a low B/T (e.g., 2.0) gives better directional stability in following seas.
- **Length-to-Depth Ratio (L/D):** Affects longitudinal bending strength and hull stiffness; typical values for fishing vessels are 10–14.
- **Depth-to-Draft Ratio (D/T):** A high D/T (e.g., 1.6) provides reserve buoyancy and a larger freeboard (good for North Sea trawlers); a low D/T (e.g., 1.2) is common in tropical fishing vessels.

## 2.2 Form Coefficients — The Quantitative Descriptors

Form coefficients are **dimensionless numbers** that describe the "fullness" or "fineness" of the hull form. They are the most frequently tested concept in KTU exams on hull form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every form coefficient is a ratio of an "actual" hull area or volume to a "bounding box" area or volume. The coefficient always lies between 0 (a theoretical point) and 1 (a perfectly rectangular box).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Hull Form Coefficients

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **definitive formula table** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination) questions on Hull Form. Memorize the symbol definitions, the geometric meanings, and the typical value ranges for each coefficient.

| Coefficient | Symbol | Formula | Geometric Meaning | Typical Range (Fishing Vessels) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Block Coefficient | $C_b$ | $\dfrac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Hull fullness vs. rectangular box of $L \times B \times T$ | 0.45 – 0.65 |
| Midship Coefficient | $C_m$ | $\dfrac{A_m}{B \cdot T}$ | Fullness of the cross-section at midship | 0.85 – 0.95 |
| Prismatic Coefficient | $C_p$ | $\dfrac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{pp}} \;=\; \dfrac{C_b}{C_m}$ | Longitudinal distribution of volume | 0.55 – 0.75 |
| Waterplane Coefficient | $C_{wp}$ | $\dfrac{A_{wp}}{L_{wl} \cdot B}$ | Fullness of the waterplane area | 0.65 – 0.85 |
| Longitudinal Prismatic Coefficient | $C_{pl}$ | $\dfrac{\nabla \cdot 2}{A_{wp} \cdot L_{wl}}$ | Slenderness of underwater volume | 0.55 – 0.75 |

Where the symbols represent:
- $\nabla$ — Displacement volume of the hull (m³) — note: $\nabla$ is the naval architectural symbol for volume of displacement
- $A_m$ — Transverse sectional area at midship (m²)
- $A_{wp}$ — Waterplane area at the design waterline (m²)
- $L_{pp}$ — Length between perpendiculars (m)
- $L_{wl}$ — Length on the waterline (m)
- $B$ — Moulded breadth (m)
- $T$ — Moulded draft (m)

## 2.4 Hull Cross-Sectional Shapes (Chine Configurations)

The shape of the hull's cross-section (transverse section) has a major effect on resistance, stability, and ease of construction. For fishing vessels, three types dominate:

### 2.4.1 Round Bilge Hull

- A continuously curved transition between the bottom and the side of the hull.
