- **Advantages:** Lowest frictional resistance, smooth flow, better seakeeping, easier to scale (less marine growth drag).
- **Disadvantages:** More complex and expensive to construct (compound curves in plating).
- **Best for:** Trawlers, purse seiners, and other vessels prioritizing low resistance and good seakeeping.

### 2.4.2 Hard Chine Hull

- A sharp, angular intersection between the bottom and the side, with flat or nearly flat side and bottom panels.
- **Advantages:** Simpler and cheaper to construct (flat panels), larger deck area for the same length, good transverse stability due to hard shoulders.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher resistance due to flow separation at the chine, wetter deck in head seas, more slamming in waves.
- **Best for:** Small gill-netters, longliners, and work boats under 20 m where construction cost dominates.

### 2.4.3 Multi-Chine Hull

- A compromise with two or more chines (knuckle lines) on each side, creating intermediate flat panels.
- **Advantages:** A compromise between construction simplicity and hydrodynamic performance.
- **Disadvantages:** No single outstanding benefit; performance is a compromise.
- **Best for:** Mid-sized fishing vessels in tropical waters.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The hull form decision drives nearly every downstream design choice:

- **Resistance and powering:** $C_b$ and $C_p$ are the primary inputs to empirical resistance prediction methods (Holtrop-Mennen, Series 60).
- **Stability:** $C_m$ and $B/T$ determine the initial transverse metacentric height (GM).
- **Seakeeping:** $C_b$, $C_p$, and sheer line shape govern motion in waves.
- **Construction cost:** Chine configuration determines plating layout, number of parts, and labor hours.
- **Fishing gear compatibility:** Stern trawlers need a clear, high-freeboard working deck; longliners need a low, quiet hull to avoid spooking fish.

> [!TIP]
> For a KTU 14-mark question on hull form, the examiner expects: (1) clear definitions of all four coefficients, (2) the relationship $C_b = C_p \cdot C_m$, (3) typical value ranges for fishing vessels, and (4) a discussion of how the form affects resistance, stability, and seakeeping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Relationship $C_b = C_p \cdot C_m$

This is the single most important relationship among the form coefficients and is tested almost every year in KTU university exams. We derive it carefully from first principles.

### Step 1: Write the Definitions of Each Coefficient

From the formula sheet, we begin with the formal definitions of the block, prismatic, and midship coefficients.

$$
C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

$$
C_p = \frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{pp}}
$$

$$
C_m = \frac{A_m}{B \cdot T}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** We want to express $C_b$ in terms of $C_p$ and $C_m$ only, eliminating the volume $\nabla$ and the midship area $A_m$. We do this by multiplying the right-hand sides of $C_p$ and $C_m$.

### Step 2: Multiply $C_p$ and $C_m$

We perform the product $C_p \cdot C_m$:

$$
C_p \cdot C_m = \left(\frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{pp}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{A_m}{B \cdot T}\right)
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The terms $A_m$ and $L_{pp}$ appear in both numerator and denominator; we cancel them algebraically.

### Step 3: Algebraic Cancellation

$$
C_p \cdot C_m = \frac{\nabla \cdot A_m}{A_m \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

Cancel $A_m$ from the numerator and denominator:

$$
C_p \cdot C_m = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

### Step 4: Identify the Result

The right-hand side of the simplified product is exactly the definition of the block coefficient $C_b$ from Step 1. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,C_b = C_p \cdot C_m\,}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This elegant relationship tells us that the overall fullness of the hull ($C_b$) is the product of how full the midship section is ($C_m$) and how the volume is distributed longitudinally ($C_p$). A high block coefficient can be achieved either by a fat midship, a long full-bodied middle, or both.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Displacement Weight Formula

In fishing vessel design, the volume of displacement $\nabla$ is converted to weight displacement $W$ using the density of water and gravitational acceleration.

### Step 1: Start from the Definition of Density

Density is mass per unit volume:

$$
\rho = \frac{m}{\nabla}
$$

Solving for mass:

$$
m = \rho \cdot \nabla
$$

### Step 2: Convert Mass to Weight

Weight is mass times gravitational acceleration:

$$
W = m \cdot g = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Standard Values

For seawater at standard conditions, $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$
W = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \nabla
$$

$$
W = 10058.25 \cdot \nabla \;\; \text{ (in Newtons)}
$$

### Step 4: Express in Tonnes (the Naval Architectural Convention)

One metric tonne = 1000 kg = 1000 × 9.81 = 9810 N. Therefore:

$$
W_{\text{tonnes}} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla}{9810} = \frac{1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \nabla}{9810}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,W \approx 1.025 \cdot \nabla \;\; \text{ tonnes (in seawater)}\,}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the famous "**1.025 factor**" that naval architects use: a cubic meter of seawater weighs approximately 1.025 tonnes. So a vessel with a displacement volume of 500 m³ has a weight displacement of about 512.5 tonnes in seawater.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Finding Missing Form Coefficients

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark problem** on hull form. The examiner gives some dimensions and coefficients and asks the student to compute the missing ones. Practice this entire derivation.

**Given Data (typical KTU problem):**
- Length between perpendiculars, $L_{pp} = 50$ m
- Moulded breadth, $B = 9$ m
- Moulded draft, $T = 4$ m
- Midship section area, $A_m = 28.8$ m²
- Volume of displacement, $\nabla = 945$ m³
- Waterplane area, $A_{wp} = 320$ m²
- Length on waterline, $L_{wl} = 52$ m

**Required:** Compute $C_b$, $C_m$, $C_p$, and $C_{wp}$.

### Solution Step 1: Block Coefficient

Using the formula $C_b = \nabla \,/\, (L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T)$:

$$
C_b = \frac{945}{50 \cdot 9 \cdot 4}
$$

$$
C_b = \frac{945}{1800} = 0.525
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Numerator 945, denominator 50 × 9 × 4 = 1800. Dividing: 945/1800 = 0.525.

### Solution Step 2: Midship Coefficient

Using $C_m = A_m \,/\, (B \cdot T)$:

$$
C_m = \frac{28.8}{9 \cdot 4} = \frac{28.8}{36} = 0.800
$$

**Conversion Logic:** A midship coefficient of 0.80 is on the finer side — typical of a mid-sized trawler.

### Solution Step 3: Prismatic Coefficient

Using $C_p = \nabla \,/\, (A_m \cdot L_{pp})$:

$$
C_p = \frac{945}{28.8 \cdot 50} = \frac{945}{1440} = 0.656
$$

**Verification using $C_b = C_p \cdot C_m$:**

$$
C_p \cdot C_m = 0.656 \cdot 0.800 = 0.5248 \approx 0.525 \;\; \checkmark
$$

The tiny discrepancy (0.5248 vs 0.525) is due to rounding of $C_p$ to three decimal places — both methods are consistent.

**Conversion Logic:** The product confirms the fundamental relationship; this self-check should always be done in exams.

### Solution Step 4: Waterplane Coefficient

Using $C_{wp} = A_{wp} \,/\, (L_{wl} \cdot B)$:

$$
C_{wp} = \frac{320}{52 \cdot 9} = \frac{320}{468} = 0.684
$$

**Conversion Logic:** A waterplane coefficient of 0.684 indicates a fairly fine waterline — consistent with a vessel designed for moderate speed and good seakeeping.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Form Coefficients and Their Engineering Implications

| Coefficient | What a HIGH Value Means | What a LOW Value Means | Effect on Resistance | Effect on Stability | Effect on Capacity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_b$ | Full, bluff hull | Fine, slender hull | Higher resistance, lower speed | Higher initial stability | Higher displacement, more cargo |
| $C_m$ | Full midship section | Fine, V-shaped midship | Higher wave resistance, lower frictional drag | Wider waterline, higher GM | More volume for given $B$, $T$ |
| $C_p$ | Volume concentrated amidships | Volume distributed to ends (fine ends) | Higher wave resistance in bluff form, lower in fine form | Higher GM if full amidships | More length available for cargo |
| $C_{wp}$ | Full, blunt waterplane | Fine waterplane (sharp ends) | Higher wave resistance | Higher initial GM | Less length-effective cargo area |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Designer's Dilemma:** Increasing fullness (high $C_b$) gives more capacity and stability but more resistance. The optimal fishing vessel hull form balances these competing demands based on operational profile — a North Sea stern trawler (high $C_b$, ~0.60) is built very differently from a tropical longline vessel (lower $C_b$, ~0.45).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hull Form Parameter Architecture

The following block diagram maps the functional architecture of how principal dimensions, form coefficients, and operational performance are interconnected in fishing vessel hull form design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Principal Dimensions] --> B[Length Lpp]
    A --> C[Breadth B]
    A --> D[Draft T]
    A --> E[Depth D]

    B --> F[Form Coefficient Calculation]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    G[Volume of Displacement] --> F
    H[Midship Area] --> F
    I[Waterplane Area] --> F

    F --> J[Block Coefficient Cb]
    F --> K[Midship Coefficient Cm]
    F --> L[Prismatic Coefficient Cp]
    F --> M[Waterplane Coefficient Cwp]

    J --> N[Performance Prediction]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N

    N --> O[Hull Resistance]
    N --> P[Initial Stability]
    N --> Q[Seakeeping Behavior]
    N --> R[Cargo Capacity]

    P --> S[Fishing Vessel Type Selection]
    Q --> S
    O --> S
    R --> S

    S --> T[Stern Trawler]
    S --> U[Side Trawler]
    S --> V[Purse Seiner]
    S --> W[Longline Vessel]
    S --> X[Gill Netter]
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The diagram flows from top to bottom in three layers: **(1) Input Layer** at the top provides the principal dimensions and measured geometric quantities; **(2) Computation Layer** in the middle transforms these into dimensionless form coefficients; **(3) Output Layer** at the bottom maps the form coefficients to engineering performance predictions and ultimately to fishing vessel type selection.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Relationship Between Form Coefficients

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Volume of Displacement Nabla] --> B[Block Coefficient Cb]
    C[Midship Area Am] --> B
    D[Length Lpp] --> B
    E[Breadth B] --> B
    F[Draft T] --> B

    A --> G[Prismatic Coefficient Cp]
    C --> G
    D --> G

    C --> H[Midship Coefficient Cm]
    E --> H
    F --> H

    B --> I[Fundamental Relationship]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Cb equals Cp times Cm]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Hull Cross-Section Configuration Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Fishing Vessel Hull Selection] --> Q1{Vessel Length?}
    Q1 -->|Under 15 m| Q2{Priority?}
    Q1 -->|15 to 25 m| Q3{Priority?}
    Q1 -->|Over 25 m| Q4{Priority?}

    Q2 -->|Low Cost| HardChine[Hard Chine Hull]
    Q2 -->|Low Resistance| RoundBilgeA[Round Bilge Hull]

    Q3 -->|Balanced| MultiChine[Multi Chine Hull]
    Q3 -->|Seakeeping| RoundBilgeB[Round Bilge Hull]

    Q4 -->|Heavy Weather Ops| RoundBilgeC[Round Bilge Hull]
    Q4 -->|Tropical Ops| MultiChine2[Multi Chine Hull]
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This decision-tree architecture illustrates the trade-offs in selecting a hull cross-section configuration based on vessel size and operational priority. For larger vessels in heavy-weather fisheries (e.g., North Atlantic trawlers), the round bilge form is almost always selected despite its higher construction cost, because the operational benefits (low resistance, dry deck, good seakeeping) dominate over the entire vessel life.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU answer sheets, a well-labeled block diagram demonstrating the relationship between principal dimensions, form coefficients, and vessel performance is a high-scoring visual element. Examiners typically award **2 to 3 additional marks** for a clear, correctly-labeled diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Define the following with respect to fishing vessel hull form: (i) Block coefficient $C_b$, (ii) Prismatic coefficient $C_p$, and (iii) Waterplane coefficient $C_{wp}$.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Block Coefficient ($C_b$):** The block coefficient is the ratio of the volume of displacement ($\nabla$) of the hull to the volume of a rectangular box having the same length ($L_{pp}$), breadth ($B$), and draft ($T$):

$$
C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

It is a primary indicator of hull fullness. Typical values for fishing vessels lie between **0.45 and 0.65**.

**(ii) Prismatic Coefficient ($C_p$):** The prismatic coefficient is the ratio of the volume of displacement to the volume of a prism whose cross-section is the midship area ($A_m$) and whose length is $L_{pp}$:

$$
C_p = \frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{pp}} = \frac{C_b}{C_m}
$$

It characterizes the longitudinal distribution of volume. Typical fishing-vessel values: **0.55 to 0.75**.

**(iii) Waterplane Coefficient ($C_{wp}$):** The waterplane coefficient is the ratio of the waterplane area ($A_{wp}$) at the design waterline to the area of a rectangle of length $L_{wl}$ and breadth $B$:

$$
C_{wp} = \frac{A_{wp}}{L_{wl} \cdot B}
$$

Typical values for fishing vessels: **0.65 to 0.85**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**State and explain the fundamental relationship between block, prismatic, and midship coefficients. Mention typical ranges of these coefficients for a small fishing trawler.**

**Model Answer:**

The fundamental relationship is:

$$
C_b = C_p \cdot C_m
$$

This means the **block coefficient (overall fullness)** equals the product of the **prismatic coefficient (longitudinal distribution)** and the **midship coefficient (transverse fullness)**.

**Derivation Logic:** Multiplying the definitions of $C_p = \nabla / (A_m \cdot L_{pp})$ and $C_m = A_m / (B \cdot T)$ cancels $A_m$ and reproduces the definition of $C_b$.

**Typical Ranges for a Small Fishing Trawler (15–25 m):**
- $C_b$ = **0.50 – 0.60**
- $C_m$ = **0.85 – 0.92**
- $C_p$ = **0.58 – 0.68**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse $C_{wp}$ (waterplane coefficient) with $C_p$ (prismatic coefficient). They are completely different — remember: $C_{wp}$ involves the waterplane *area*; $C_p$ involves the displacement *volume* and the midship area.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**A fishing trawler has the following principal dimensions and form data: Length between perpendiculars $L_{pp}$ = 48 m, moulded breadth $B$ = 8.5 m, moulded draft $T$ = 3.8 m, volume of displacement $\nabla$ = 853 m³, midship section area $A_m$ = 26.5 m², waterplane area $A_{wp}$ = 295 m², length on waterline $L_{wl}$ = 50 m.**

**Calculate:**
- **(a) [7 Marks]** The block coefficient, midship coefficient, prismatic coefficient, and waterplane coefficient. Verify the fundamental relationship $C_b = C_p \cdot C_m$.
- **(b) [7 Marks)** The displacement weight in tonnes (use $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²). Comment on the fullness of the hull form and recommend whether this vessel is more suitable for trawling or for longline fishing operations.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Calculation of Form Coefficients [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Block Coefficient**

$$
C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{853}{48 \cdot 8.5 \cdot 3.8}
$$

$$
C_b = \frac{853}{1550.4} = 0.550 \;\; \text{(3 marks for correct value)}
$$

**Step 2: Midship Coefficient**

$$
C_m = \frac{A_m}{B \cdot T} = \frac{26.5}{8.5 \cdot 3.8} = \frac{26.5}{32.3} = 0.820
$$

**Step 3: Prismatic Coefficient**

$$
C_p = \frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{pp}} = \frac{853}{26.5 \cdot 48} = \frac{853}{1272} = 0.671
$$

**Step 4: Waterplane Coefficient**

$$
C_{wp} = \frac{A_{wp}}{L_{wl} \cdot B} = \frac{295}{50 \cdot 8.5} = \frac{295}{425} = 0.694
$$

**Step 5: Verification of the Fundamental Relationship**

$$
C_p \cdot C_m = 0.671 \cdot 0.820 = 0.550 \; \approx \; C_b \;\; \checkmark
$$

The relationship is verified to within rounding accuracy (4 marks distributed across Steps 2 to 5).

**Part (b) — Displacement Weight and Operational Recommendation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Convert Volume to Mass**

$$
m = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1025 \cdot 853 = 874{,}325 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2: Convert Mass to Weight (Newtons)**

$$
W = m \cdot g = 874{,}325 \cdot 9.81 = 8{,}577{,}128 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to Tonnes**

$$
W_{\text{tonnes}} = \frac{8{,}577{,}128}{9810} = 874.3 \text{ tonnes}
$$

Using the convenient shortcut: $W = 1.025 \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \cdot 853 = 874.3$ tonnes.

**Step 4: Operational Assessment**

- The block coefficient of 0.55 indicates a **moderately fine hull** — not extremely bluff, not extremely slender.
- The prismatic coefficient of 0.671 (above the typical trawler range of 0.55–0.65) suggests the volume is distributed somewhat evenly along the length, with a slightly fuller midship region.
- The waterplane coefficient of 0.694 is on the finer side, indicating a **clean, fine entry** at the bow.

**Recommendation:** This vessel with $C_b = 0.55$ is **more suitable for trawling operations** (typical trawler $C_b$ = 0.55–0.65) than for longline fishing (where $C_b$ = 0.42–0.50 is preferred for higher speed and lower fuel consumption). The full midship and good waterplane fineness give it the volume needed for fish-hold capacity and the form needed for towing resistance.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of neat sketches, describe the **three principal views of a ship's lines plan** (sheer plan, body plan, and half-breadth plan). Explain how the **form coefficients** are extracted from the lines plan.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **round bilge, hard chine, and multi-chine** hull cross-sections in the context of fishing vessel design. Recommend the most appropriate type for: (i) a 12 m fiberglass gill-netter operating in sheltered backwaters, and (ii) a 28 m steel stern trawler operating in the North Atlantic.

#### Model Solution Outline

**Part (a) — Lines Plan and Form Coefficient Extraction [7 Marks]**

**The Three Views:**

1. **Sheer Plan (Profile View):** A side elevation of the hull showing the design waterline, the sheer line (top of deck at side), all horizontal waterlines, and the vertical buttock lines. The forward perpendicular (FP) and aft perpendicular (AP) are also shown.

2. **Body Plan:** A view consisting of the cross-sectional shapes of the hull, with the forward half drawn on the right of the centerline and the aft half on the left (or vice-versa). The midship section appears as a full symmetrical section at the center.

3. **Half-Breadth Plan:** A top-down view showing the shape of the hull at every waterline. The half-breadth of the hull at each station is plotted along its length.

**Extraction of Form Coefficients from the Lines Plan:**

- The volume of displacement $\nabla$ is calculated by **Simpson's Rule** applied to the cross-sectional areas at each station (which are integrated from the body plan).
- The midship area $A_m$ is measured directly from the body plan (planimeter or by offsets).
- The waterplane area $A_{wp}$ is calculated from the half-breadth plan using Simpson's Rule.
- The coefficients $C_b$, $C_m$, $C_p$, and $C_{wp}$ are then computed using the formulas in the KTU Formula Sheet (Section 2.3).

**Part (b) — Comparison of Chine Types and Recommendations [7 Marks]**

| Aspect | Round Bilge | Hard Chine | Multi-Chine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Construction Cost | High (compound curves) | Low (flat panels) | Medium |
| Resistance | Lowest | Highest (flow separation) | Intermediate |
| Seakeeping | Best | Wettest deck | Acceptable |
| Stability | Smooth GM curve | Sharp shoulders, high GM | Stepped GM |
| Build Complexity | Complex jigging | Simple framing | Moderate |

**Recommendation (i) — 12 m fiberglass gill-netter, backwaters:** **Hard Chine** is the clear choice. Low cost, simple fiberglass layup, adequate for sheltered waters where seakeeping demands are minimal. The flat panels can be cut from plywood or molded in glass-reinforced plastic (GRP) with very little labor.

**Recommendation (ii) — 28 m steel stern trawler, North Atlantic:** **Round Bilge** is mandatory. The vessel must tow heavy trawl gear through rough seas for weeks at a time; only the round bilge form provides the low resistance, dry deck, and good seakeeping needed. Construction cost is justified by operational performance over a 25–30 year service life.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Hull Form Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in displacement weight calculation** — always state the answer in tonnes with the standard conversion factor 1.025.
> 2. **Mixing up $L_{pp}$ and $L_{wl}$** — $L_{pp}$ is used in $C_b$ and $C_p$ formulas; $L_{wl}$ is used in $C_{wp}$. Using one in place of the other will cost **2 marks** silently.
> 3. **Not verifying the fundamental relationship** — always multiply $C_p \times C_m$ at the end of any hull form problem to confirm $C_b$. This self-check catches arithmetic errors and earns a "methodology" mark.
> 4. **Drawing chine sketches without labeling** — every chine diagram in an exam should label: the chine line (knuckle), the bottom panel, the side panel, and the centerline. Unlabeled sketches earn partial credit only.
> 5. **Omitting the difference between $C_p$ (prismatic) and $C_{wp}$ (waterplane)** — these are the two most confused symbols in naval architecture. State explicitly which is which in the answer.
> 6. **Using fresh water density (1000 kg/m³) instead of seawater (1025 kg/m³)** — for a fishing vessel that will operate in coastal/sea waters, always use 1025 unless the question explicitly says fresh water.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Hull form** is the 3D geometric shape of a vessel's body, defined by the lines plan.
- [x] **Three principal views** of a lines plan: **Sheer Plan**, **Body Plan**, and **Half-Breadth Plan**.
- [x] **Four principal dimensions:** Length ($L_{pp}$ or $L_{wl}$), Breadth ($B$), Draft ($T$), Depth ($D$).
- [x] **Four key form coefficients:** Block ($C_b$), Midship ($C_m$), Prismatic ($C_p$), Waterplane ($C_{wp}$).
- [x] **Fundamental relationship:** $C_b = C_p \cdot C_m$ — must always hold.
- [x] **Block Coefficient formula:** $C_b = \nabla \,/\, (L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T)$ — typical fishing-vessel range 0.45–0.65.
- [x] **Midship Coefficient formula:** $C_m = A_m \,/\, (B \cdot T)$ — typical range 0.85–0.95.
- [x] **Prismatic Coefficient formula:** $C_p = \nabla \,/\, (A_m \cdot L_{pp}) = C_b / C_m$ — typical range 0.55–0.75.
- [x] **Waterplane Coefficient formula:** $C_{wp} = A_{wp} \,/\, (L_{wl} \cdot B)$ — typical range 0.65–0.85.
- [x] **Displacement weight formula:** $W$ (tonnes) = $1.025 \times \nabla$ (m³) in seawater.
- [x] **Standard seawater density:** $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³; gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- [x] **Three chine configurations:** Round Bilge (best seakeeping, highest cost), Hard Chine (lowest cost, highest resistance), Multi-Chine (compromise).
- [x] **Length-to-beam ratio $L/B$** for fishing vessels: typically **3.5 to 5.5**; lower = fuller hull.
- [x] **Beam-to-draft ratio $B/T$** for fishing vessels: typically **2.0 to 3.5**; higher = better initial stability.
- [x] **Depth-to-draft ratio $D/T$** for fishing vessels: typically **1.2 to 1.6**; higher = larger freeboard and reserve buoyancy.
- [x] **High $C_b$** means full hull (more capacity, more resistance, better stability); **low $C_b$** means fine hull (less resistance, higher speed, less capacity).
- [x] **Hull form selection** must balance resistance, stability, seakeeping, capacity, and construction cost — there is no single "best" form, only the form best suited to the operational profile.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip (5 minutes before entering the hall):** Write the four formulas ($C_b$, $C_m$, $C_p$, $C_{wp}$) and the relationship $C_b = C_p \cdot C_m$ on the back of your hall ticket. This single sheet of memory covers about 70% of typical KTU hull form questions. The displacement weight formula $W = 1.025 \cdot \nabla$ is the other essential tool — between these five lines, you can solve almost any 14-mark problem on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Investment Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Investment Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Investment Cost (Capital Cost)** of a fishing vessel is defined as the total capitalized expenditure required to design, construct, equip, and commission a fishing vessel to a state of operational readiness, including all direct construction costs, indirect (overhead) costs, and the initial working capital needed before the vessel generates its first revenue.

In standard KTU naval architecture economics terminology, the **Total Investment Cost (TIC)** is split into two primary heads:

1. **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** – The one-time sunk capital tied up in the physical asset.
2. **Working Capital (WC)** – The liquid funds required to operate the vessel until it earns its first catch-revenue.

$$\text{Total Investment Cost (TIC)} = \text{FCI} + \text{WC}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of Investment Cost as the "total bill you pay when you set up a food truck business." It is *not* just the price of the truck itself. It includes the cost of the truck body, the kitchen equipment inside it, the LPG cylinders, the permit/license fees, the chef's first month's salary, and a small cash float for buying vegetables before the first customer pays you.

For a fishing vessel, the "truck" is the **hull + propulsion**, the "kitchen equipment" is the **fishing gear + winches + electronics**, the "permits" are the **classification fees and insurance**, and the "cash float" is the **working capital** (bunkers, lubricants, provisions, crew wages for the first fishing voyage).

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

The KTU syllabus highlights three standard yardsticks used by naval architects to size up investment cost:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| Cost per unit of Length | $C_L$ | USD/m |
| Cost per unit of Displacement | $C_\Delta$ | USD/tonne |
| Cost per unit of Installed Power | $C_P$ | USD/kW |
| Cost per Gross Registered Tonnage | $C_{GRT}$ | USD/GRT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The internationally accepted baseline benchmark for a modern steel-hulled fishing trawler is approximately **USD 3,500 – 6,000 per GRT** (2024 market values). For FRP (Fibreglass) small craft below 24 m, the benchmark is **USD 1,800 – 3,500 per GRT**.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

Investment Cost is a **high-weightage, high-yield topic** in Module 2 because:
- It is the **input variable** for every downstream economic calculation (ROI, NPV, payback period, depreciation).
- A KTU examiner typically tests whether a student can correctly classify a given cost (direct vs indirect, fixed vs working).
- It is the only Module 2 topic that bridges **engineering design** with **commercial feasibility**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition Logic — The "Anatomy" of Investment Cost

A KTU-valid Investment Cost breakdown follows the hierarchical logic used by classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, IRS) and shipyards worldwide:

**Level 1 — Total Investment Cost (TIC)**
- **Level 2A — Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)**
  - **Level 3 — Direct Costs (DC)**
    - Hull Steel (or FRP) and Shell Construction
    - Main Propulsion Machinery
    - Auxiliary Machinery (generators, compressors, pumps, purifiers)
    - Deck Machinery (winches, cranes, gantries, net haulers)
    - Fishing Equipment (nets, longlines, pots, trawl gear)
    - Electronic & Navigation Equipment (radar, GPS, sonar, VHF, AIS, autopilot)
    - Accommodation & Outfitting (cabins, galley, cold rooms, sanitary)
    - Refrigeration & Fish Hold Plant
  - **Level 3 — Indirect Costs (IC)**
    - Design and Engineering Fees
    - Project Supervision & Yard Overheads
    - Classification and Statutory Survey Fees
    - Builder's Profit Margin
    - Insurance During Construction (IDC)
    - Interest During Construction (IDC)
    - Contingency Reserve (typically 5–10% of DC)
- **Level 2B — Working Capital (WC)**
  - Initial Bunker Fuel Load (HFO + MGO + LO)
  - Provisions & Fresh Water
  - Crew Wages for First Voyage Cycle
  - Spare Parts Inventory
  - Pre-launch Port Charges & Agent Fees

### 2.2 Cost Component Ratios (Empirical Benchmarks)

> [!NOTE]
> These empirical percentages are extracted from published FAO Fishing Vessel Design manuals and BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) data — they are the **exact ratios** examiners expect students to reproduce.

| Cost Head | % of FCI (Steel Trawler) | % of FCI (FRP Small Craft) |
|---|---|---|
| Hull (Steel/FRP) | 25 – 30 % | 35 – 40 % |
| Main & Auxiliary Machinery | 15 – 22 % | 18 – 25 % |
| Fishing Gear & Deck Machinery | 12 – 18 % | 8 – 12 % |
| Electronics & Navigation | 5 – 8 % | 6 – 10 % |
| Outfitting & Accommodation | 8 – 12 % | 10 – 15 % |
| Refrigeration & Fish Hold | 5 – 8 % | 4 – 7 % |
| Indirect Costs (Design + Profit + IDC) | 15 – 20 % | 12 – 18 % |
| Contingency | 5 – 10 % | 5 – 10 % |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master this table — every KTU numerical question on Module 2 draws from these equations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{TIC} = \text{FCI} + \text{WC}$ | Total Investment Cost | USD |
| 2 | $\text{FCI} = \text{DC} + \text{IC}$ | Fixed Capital Investment | USD |
| 3 | $\text{IC} = 0.15\,\text{DC} + \text{Contingency}$ | Indirect Costs (~15% rule) | USD |
| 4 | $C_\Delta = \dfrac{\text{Hull Cost}}{\Delta}$ | Cost per unit displacement | USD/tonne |
| 5 | $C_P = \dfrac{\text{Machinery Cost}}{P_{\text{installed}}}$ | Cost per unit installed power | USD/kW |
| 6 | $C_L = \dfrac{\text{FCI}}{L_{OA}}$ | Cost per unit overall length | USD/m |
| 7 | $C_{GRT} = \dfrac{\text{FCI}}{\text{GRT}}$ | Cost per gross registered tonnage | USD/GRT |
| 8 | $\text{WC} = B_f + P_w + C_w + S_p + A_f$ | Working Capital components | USD |
| 9 | $\text{Contingency} = 0.05\,\text{FCI}$ to $0.10\,\text{FCI}$ | Risk reserve | USD |
| 10 | $P_{\text{installed}} = P_{\text{M/E}} + P_{\text{A/E}}$ | Total installed power | kW |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

Investment Cost estimation is the **gateway** to:
- **Shipbuilding feasibility studies** (yard tender preparation)
- **Bank loan appraisal** (financial institutions require cost breakups)
- **Government subsidy calculations** (PMMSY schemes in India)
- **Insurance valuation** (Hull & Machinery insurance premiums)
- **Comparative design studies** (steel vs FRP, single vs twin rig)

A 5% error in Investment Cost estimation can swing the **Net Present Value (NPV)** by 20–30% over a 20-year vessel life — making accuracy in this step the most economically critical part of vessel design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Full Investment Cost Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Numerical Style):** A 24 m steel-hulled stern trawler has the following design particulars:
> - Overall Length, $L_{OA}$ = 24.0 m
> - Displacement, $\Delta$ = 180 tonnes
> - Main Engine Power, $P_{M/E}$ = 600 kW
> - Auxiliary Engine Power, $P_{A/E}$ = 100 kW
> - Gross Registered Tonnage, GRT = 95
>
> Given component costs:
> - Hull Steel: USD 380,000
> - Main + Auxiliary Machinery: USD 240,000
> - Fishing Gear & Deck Machinery: USD 150,000
> - Electronics & Navigation: USD 60,000
> - Outfitting & Accommodation: USD 90,000
> - Refrigeration & Fish Hold Plant: USD 55,000
>
> **Compute:**
> 1. Total Direct Cost (DC)
> 2. Indirect Cost @ 15% of DC + 8% contingency of DC
> 3. Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)
> 4. Working Capital: Bunkers USD 18,000 + Provisions USD 6,000 + Crew Wages (1 voyage) USD 12,000 + Spares USD 5,000
> 5. Total Investment Cost (TIC)
> 6. Cost ratios: $C_L$, $C_\Delta$, $C_P$, $C_{GRT}$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

#### **Step 1 — Compute Total Direct Cost (DC)**

Sum up all physically visible construction components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DC} &= \text{Hull} + \text{Machinery} + \text{Fishing Gear} + \text{Electronics} \\
&\quad + \text{Outfitting} + \text{Refrigeration} \\[4pt]
&= 380{,}000 + 240{,}000 + 150{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 90{,}000 + 55{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 975{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 2 — Compute Indirect Cost (IC)**

Indirect Cost = 15% of DC (for design, supervision, profit, IDC) + 8% contingency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IC}_{15\%} &= 0.15 \times 975{,}000 = 146{,}250 \text{ USD} \\[4pt]
\text{Contingency}_{8\%} &= 0.08 \times 975{,}000 = 78{,}000 \text{ USD} \\[4pt]
\text{Total IC} &= 146{,}250 + 78{,}000 = 224{,}250 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 3 — Compute Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FCI} &= \text{DC} + \text{IC} \\[4pt]
&= 975{,}000 + 224{,}250 \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}199{,}250 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 4 — Compute Working Capital (WC)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{WC} &= B_f + P_w + C_w + S_p + A_f \\[4pt]
&= 18{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 5{,}000 + 0 \\[4pt]
&= 41{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Agent fees assumed included in indirect cost)*

#### **Step 5 — Compute Total Investment Cost (TIC)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TIC} &= \text{FCI} + \text{WC} \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}199{,}250 + 41{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}240{,}250 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 6 — Compute Cost Ratios**

**Cost per unit Length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_L &= \frac{\text{FCI}}{L_{OA}} = \frac{1{,}199{,}250}{24.0} \\[4pt]
&= 49{,}968.75 \text{ USD/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cost per unit Displacement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_\Delta &= \frac{\text{Hull Cost}}{\Delta} = \frac{380{,}000}{180} \\[4pt]
&= 2{,}111.11 \text{ USD/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cost per unit Installed Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{installed}} &= P_{M/E} + P_{A/E} = 600 + 100 = 700 \text{ kW} \\[4pt]
C_P &= \frac{\text{Machinery Cost}}{P_{\text{installed}}} = \frac{240{,}000}{700} \\[4pt]
&= 342.86 \text{ USD/kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cost per GRT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{GRT} &= \frac{\text{FCI}}{\text{GRT}} = \frac{1{,}199{,}250}{95} \\[4pt]
&= 12{,}623.68 \text{ USD/GRT}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Verification Matrix

| Metric | Computed Value | Empirical Benchmark | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_L$ | 49,969 USD/m | 40,000 – 60,000 USD/m | ✅ Within range |
| $C_\Delta$ | 2,111 USD/tonne | 1,800 – 2,500 USD/tonne | ✅ Within range |
| $C_P$ | 343 USD/kW | 300 – 450 USD/kW | ✅ Within range |
| $C_{GRT}$ | 12,624 USD/GRT | 3,500 – 6,000 USD/GRT | ⚠️ Above range |
| Hull % of FCI | 31.7 % | 25 – 30 % | ⚠️ Slightly high |

> [!WARNING]
> The $C_{GRT}$ value exceeds the empirical benchmark — this indicates either an **over-spec'd outfitting** or **under-declared GRT**. In a KTU valuation context, you must **flag such deviations** with a brief justification comment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Cost Breakdown Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TIC Total Investment Cost"] --> B["FCI Fixed Capital Investment"]
    A --> C["WC Working Capital"]

    B --> D["DC Direct Costs"]
    B --> E["IC Indirect Costs"]

    D --> D1["Hull Steel or FRP"]
    D --> D2["Main and Aux Machinery"]
    D --> D3["Fishing Gear and Deck Machinery"]
    D --> D4["Electronics and Navigation"]
    D --> D5["Outfitting and Accommodation"]
    D --> D6["Refrigeration and Fish Hold"]

    E --> E1["Design and Engineering Fees"]
    E --> E2["Yard Overheads and Profit"]
    E --> E3["Insurance and IDC Interest During Construction"]
    E --> E4["Contingency Reserve 5 to 10 percent"]

    C --> C1["Initial Bunker Fuel"]
    C --> C2["Provisions and Fresh Water"]
    C --> C3["Crew Wages First Voyage"]
    C --> C4["Spare Parts Inventory"]
    C --> C5["Port Charges and Agent Fees"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cost Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V["Vessel Principal Particulars L B T Delta GRT P_me"] --> S1["Step 1 Estimate DC from Cost Ratios"]
    S1 --> S2["Step 2 Apply Indirect Cost Multipliers"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 Compute FCI"]
    V --> W1["Step 4 Estimate WC from Voyage Cycle"]
    W1 --> S3
    S3 --> F1["Step 5 Final TIC and Benchmark Ratios"]
    F1 --> V1["Validation Check C_GRT and C_L within Empirical Range"]
    V1 --> R1["Final Investment Cost Report for Bank Loan or Subsidy"]
```

### 4.3 Cost Component Pie-Chart Representation (Steel Trawler — Empirical)

```mermaid
pie title Empirical FCI Distribution for Steel Stern Trawler
    "Hull Steel" : 28
    "Machinery" : 19
    "Fishing Gear and Deck" : 15
    "Electronics" : 7
    "Outfitting" : 10
    "Refrigeration" : 6
    "Indirect Costs" : 15
```

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid pie-chart values are illustrative empirical percentages — for any design problem, the student must recompute from actual component costs rather than copying these defaults.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Investment Cost" of a fishing vessel. Distinguish between Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) and Working Capital (WC) with one suitable example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Investment Cost is the total capitalized expenditure required to design, build, equip, and commission a fishing vessel to operational status, comprising Fixed Capital Investment and Working Capital.

**FCI (1 Mark):** FCI refers to the one-time sunk cost on the physical vessel — hull, machinery, fishing gear, electronics, outfitting, plus indirect costs (design, profit, insurance). Example: USD 240,000 spent on the main and auxiliary propulsion machinery.

**WC (1 Mark):** WC refers to the liquid funds required to operate the vessel before earning revenue. Example: USD 18,000 spent on initial bunker fuel load for the first fishing voyage.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*List any four (4) standard cost ratios used by naval architects for quick estimation of fishing vessel investment cost. State their SI/engineering units.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — any 4 valid ratios):**

| # | Cost Ratio | Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cost per unit Length, $C_L$ | USD/m |
| 2 | Cost per unit Displacement, $C_\Delta$ | USD/tonne |
| 3 | Cost per unit Installed Power, $C_P$ | USD/kW |
| 4 | Cost per Gross Registered Tonnage, $C_{GRT}$ | USD/GRT |

*(½ mark per correct entry + ½ mark for units)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates that every Module 2 question in Part B carry an **internal choice** — i.e., the student must answer either Option A or Option B.

---

#### **OPTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

A 28 m steel-hulled tuna long-liner is to be designed with the following particulars:

- $L_{OA}$ = 28.0 m, $\Delta$ = 240 tonnes, GRT = 140
- Main Engine: 750 kW; Auxiliary Engine: 120 kW
- Hull Steel Cost: USD 520,000
- Main + Auxiliary Machinery Cost: USD 310,000
- Fishing Gear (longlines + winches): USD 180,000
- Electronics & Navigation: USD 75,000
- Outfitting & Accommodation: USD 110,000
- Refrigeration Plant: USD 65,000
- Indirect Costs @ 18% of DC; Contingency @ 7% of DC
- Working Capital: Bunkers USD 22,000 + Provisions USD 8,000 + Crew Wages USD 14,000 + Spares USD 6,000

**(a)** Compute the Total Direct Cost, Total Indirect Cost, and Fixed Capital Investment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the Total Investment Cost and evaluate the four cost ratios $C_L$, $C_\Delta$, $C_P$, and $C_{GRT}$. Comment on whether $C_{GRT}$ falls within the empirical benchmark range. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Option A**

**(a) Computation of DC, IC, and FCI (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Total Direct Cost (DC) (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DC} &= 520{,}000 + 310{,}000 + 180{,}000 + 75{,}000 + 110{,}000 + 65{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}260{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Indirect Cost (IC) (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IC} &= 0.18 \times 1{,}260{,}000 + 0.07 \times 1{,}260{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 226{,}800 + 88{,}200 \\[4pt]
&= 315{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FCI} &= 1{,}260{,}000 + 315{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}575{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Computation of TIC and Cost Ratios (7 Marks)**

**Step 4 — Working Capital (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{WC} = 22{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 14{,}000 + 6{,}000 = 50{,}000 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Investment Cost (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{TIC} = 1{,}575{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 1{,}625{,}000 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 6 — Cost Ratios (4 Marks — 1 each):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_L &= \frac{1{,}575{,}000}{28.0} = 56{,}250 \text{ USD/m} \\[4pt]
C_\Delta &= \frac{520{,}000}{240} = 2{,}166.67 \text{ USD/tonne} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{inst}} &= 750 + 120 = 870 \text{ kW} \\[4pt]
C_P &= \frac{310{,}000}{870} = 356.32 \text{ USD/kW} \\[4pt]
C_{GRT} &= \frac{1{,}575{,}000}{140} = 11{,}250 \text{ USD/GRT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Validation Comment (1 Mark):** The computed $C_{GRT}$ = 11,250 USD/GRT exceeds the empirical benchmark of 3,500–6,000 USD/GRT for a steel tuna long-liner. This indicates either an over-spec'd outfitting/refrigeration package (typical for tuna vessels with brine tanks and blast freezers) or a high-cost shipyard region. The deviation is **justifiable** for a tuna long-liner carrying premium refrigeration, but the student must state this caveat to earn full marks.

---

#### **OPTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the hierarchical classification of Total Investment Cost (TIC) of a fishing vessel into Direct Costs, Indirect Costs, and Working Capital. List at least **three (3)** items under each sub-head. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 19 m FRP (Fibreglass) shrimp trawler has the following data:

- $L_{OA}$ = 19.0 m, GRT = 48, Displacement $\Delta$ = 65 tonnes
- Hull (FRP) Cost: USD 130,000
- Machinery: USD 95,000
- Fishing Gear & Deck Machinery: USD 42,000
- Electronics: USD 28,000
- Outfitting: USD 38,000
- Indirect Costs @ 12% of DC; Contingency @ 5% of DC
- Working Capital: USD 15,000

Calculate the Total Investment Cost and the cost ratios $C_L$ and $C_{GRT}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Option B**

**(a) Hierarchical Classification (7 Marks — see Section 4.1 flowchart for full block diagram)**

TIC splits into FCI + WC (1 Mark).
FCI splits into DC + IC (1 Mark).
Three items each (4 Marks — ½ Mark each, minimum 3 items per head as required):

- **Direct Costs:** Hull, Machinery, Fishing Gear, Electronics, Outfitting, Refrigeration *(list any 3)*
- **Indirect Costs:** Design fees, Yard profit, IDC, Insurance during construction, Contingency *(list any 3)*
- **Working Capital:** Bunkers, Provisions, Crew wages, Spares *(list any 3)*

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — DC (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{DC} = 130{,}000 + 95{,}000 + 42{,}000 + 28{,}000 + 38{,}000 = 333{,}000 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 2 — IC (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{IC} = (0.12 + 0.05) \times 333{,}000 = 56{,}610 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 3 — FCI and TIC (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FCI} &= 333{,}000 + 56{,}610 = 389{,}610 \text{ USD} \\[4pt]
\text{TIC} &= 389{,}610 + 15{,}000 = 404{,}610 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cost Ratios (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_L &= \frac{389{,}610}{19.0} = 20{,}506.84 \text{ USD/m} \\[4pt]
C_{GRT} &= \frac{389{,}610}{48} = 8{,}116.88 \text{ USD/GRT}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Conflating FCI with TIC** — Many students add only DC and forget WC. WC (typically 3–5% of FCI) is a **mandatory** component.
> 2. **Wrong percentage base for contingency** — Some students apply contingency on FCI instead of DC. KTU convention: apply contingency on **Direct Cost**.
> 3. **Missing units in cost ratios** — A cost of "49,968" without "USD/m" loses ½ mark per ratio.
> 4. **Forgetting the $P_{A/E}$ in installed power** — Total installed power = M/E + A/E. Missing the A/E loses 1 full mark on $C_P$.
> 5. **No validation comment** — KTU 2024 Scheme rewards students who **cross-check** their computed ratios against empirical ranges.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Investment Cost (Module 2)**

- ✅ **Two-head split:** TIC = **FCI + WC** (memorize verbatim).
- ✅ **Three-tier FCI:** FCI = **DC + IC**, where DC has 6 sub-heads and IC has 4–5 sub-heads.
- ✅ **Hull cost is 25–30% of FCI** for steel trawlers and **35–40%** for FRP small craft.
- ✅ **Indirect cost is typically 15–20% of DC**, plus a separate **5–10% contingency**.
- ✅ **Working capital** = Bunkers + Provisions + Crew wages + Spares + Agent fees.
- ✅ **Installed Power** for cost ratio = $P_{M/E} + P_{A/E}$ (don't forget auxiliaries).
- ✅ **Empirical benchmark:** $C_{GRT}$ ≈ **3,500 – 6,000 USD/GRT** for steel trawlers; **1,800 – 3,500 USD/GRT** for FRP.
- ✅ **Cost per unit displacement** uses **HULL COST only** (not FCI) in the numerator.
- ✅ **Cost per unit length** uses **FCI** in the numerator.
- ✅ **Validation step is compulsory** in KTU numericals — always cross-check at least one ratio against empirical range.
- ✅ **Currency & year note** — Always write **"USD (2024 market values)"** in your answer for full professional credit.
- ✅ **Order of magnitude check:** A 24 m steel trawler should cost **~USD 1.0–1.5 million**, not USD 100,000 or USD 10 million. If your answer is off by an order of magnitude, recheck.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Revenues and Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operating Revenues and Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 2 · Operating Revenues and Costs

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Operating Revenues* of a fishing vessel are the gross monetary inflows earned during normal fishing operations over a defined accounting period (usually **one financial year**), generated primarily from the sale of catch (main species, by-catch and by-products), and any auxiliary charter or license income. *Operating Costs* are the recurring annual expenditures required to keep the vessel operational, classified as **fixed costs** (independent of fishing activity) and **variable costs** (proportional to fishing effort).

### 1.1 Intuitive Overview — The "Tea Stall" Analogy

Imagine a small tea stall on a Kerala highway. The **daily revenue** is the cash collected from selling tea, snacks and biscuits. The **operating costs** are the milk, sugar, gas, paper cups, rent of the stall, the stall-keeper's salary and the electricity bill. Some costs (rent, salary) occur *whether you sell 10 cups or 100 cups* — these are the **fixed costs**. Others (milk, sugar, cups) scale up as you sell more cups — these are the **variable costs**.

A fishing vessel behaves identically, only the scale and the elements change. The *catch* is the product, the *fuel, ice, crew wages, gear and provisions* are the running inputs, and the *insurance, depreciation and harbour dues* are the unavoidable overheads. The difference between annual revenue and annual operating cost is the **Net Operating Income (NOI)**, which decides whether the owner makes a profit or a loss.

### 1.2 Why this Topic Matters in Vessel Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The economic dimension of vessel design answers the most fundamental question asked by any ship-owner or bank financing the vessel: **"How much money will the boat actually make?"** A fishing vessel that is technically excellent but uneconomical will never be built. Operating Revenues and Costs form the input to **payback period, NPV, IRR and break-even analysis**, which are mandatory KTU Board exam topics under *Module 5 (Economics of Fishing Vessels)*.

### 1.3 Standard Economic Metrics used by KTU

The following **bold** indicators are universally adopted in fishing-vessel economics and appear frequently in KTU exam questions:

- **Catch rate (kg / fishing day)** — productivity of the vessel.
- **Fishing days per year (N_f)** — usually 150 to 220 for a small trawler.
- **Average ex-vessel price (₹ / kg)** — realised at the landing centre.
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) ≈ 200 – 220 g/kWh** — typical for marine diesel engines.
- **Crew size (n)** — function of vessel length and gear type.
- **Vessel economic life (L_e) ≈ 15 – 20 years** for steel-hull fishing boats.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of Operating Revenues

A fishing vessel typically generates revenue from **three primary streams**:

1. **Sale of Main Catch** — the dominant species targeted by the gear (e.g., prawns, mackerel, ribbon fish).
2. **Sale of By-catch** — incidental species caught along with the main catch.
3. **By-products** — fish meal, oil, shells, processed or value-added products.
4. **Charter / Lease Income** *(rare for small vessels)* — hiring the boat for research or tourism.

Because different species fetch very different prices, the revenue must be computed **species-wise** and then summed.

## 2.2 Classification of Operating Costs

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Standard Two-Bucket Classification**

| **Category** | **Sub-Category** | **Examples** | **Behaviour** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Costs (C_F)** | Vessel overhead | Insurance, Depreciation, Administrative overhead, Base/contract wages of permanent crew | Independent of fishing days |
| **Variable Costs (C_V)** | Voyage & catch-related | Fuel, Lubricating oil, Ice, Bait, Salt, Provisions, Port/harbour dues, Maintenance linked to hours run, Gear replacement | ∝ Fishing days |

## 2.3 The Core Economic Engine

The annual economics of a fishing vessel can be expressed through **three hierarchical equations**:

$$
\text{Annual Revenue } (R) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot P_i \cdot N_f
$$

$$
\text{Annual Operating Cost } (C_{op}) \;=\; C_F + C_V
$$

$$
\text{Net Operating Income } (\text{NOI}) \;=\; R - C_{op}
$$

Where $C_i$ is the catch of species *i* per fishing day (kg), $P_i$ is the price of species *i* (₹/kg), $N_f$ is the number of fishing days per year, $C_F$ is the annual fixed cost and $C_V$ is the annual variable cost.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All symbols below are evaluated in **Indian Rupees (₹)** unless stated otherwise. Keep this table handy for both Part A and Part B questions.

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Daily Revenue per species | $R_{d,i} = C_i \cdot P_i$ | ₹/day | Subscript *i* denotes species |
| 2 | Annual Revenue | $R = \left(\sum C_i P_i\right) N_f$ | ₹/year | Total of all species |
| 3 | Daily Variable Cost | $C_{V,d} = C_{V,total} / N_f$ | ₹/day | Treated as average |
| 4 | Fuel Cost (per year) | $C_Fuel = \dot{V}_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot t_{op} \cdot c_f$ | ₹/year | $\dot{V}_f$ in L/hr, $\rho_f$ ≈ **0.85 kg/L** |
| 5 | Lub Oil Cost | $C_{LO} = k_{LO} \cdot C_{Fuel}$ | ₹/year | $k_{LO}$ = 5 – 10 % of fuel cost |
| 6 | Depreciation (Straight Line) | $D = \dfrac{C_{vessel} - S_v}{L_e}$ | ₹/year | $S_v$ = scrap value, $L_e$ = life in years |
| 7 | Annual Fixed Cost | $C_F = I_{ann} + D + A_{adm} + W_{base}$ | ₹/year | Insurance + Depreciation + Admin + Base wages |
| 8 | Annual Variable Cost | $C_V = C_{Fuel} + C_{LO} + C_{ice} + C_{prov} + C_{port} + C_{maint} + C_{gear}$ | ₹/year | Per-day cost × $N_f$ |
| 9 | Annual Operating Cost | $C_{op} = C_F + C_V$ | ₹/year | Total recurring cost |
| 10 | Net Operating Income | $\text{NOI} = R - C_{op}$ | ₹/year | Profitability indicator |
| 11 | Break-even Fishing Days | $N_{BE} = \dfrac{C_F}{R_{d} - C_{V,d}}$ | days | Vessel must fish at least $N_{BE}$ days |
| 12 | Fuel Consumption (from engine) | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{\text{BHP} \cdot \text{SFC}}{1000}$ | kg/hr | SFC in g/kWh |

> [!NOTE]
> **Absolute-value / modulus symbols**: When you need a modulus expression such as $\vert R - C_{op} \vert$ inside a table, write it with the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` so that markdown tables do not break.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Bank Loan Sanction**: Indian banks use the projected NOI and break-even days to determine loan eligibility for fishing trawlers under the *Blue Revolution* scheme.
- **Vessel Sizing**: Designers use revenue-vs-cost curves to choose the *optimum vessel length* that maximises NOI.
- **Policy Decisions**: The Ministry of Fisheries uses aggregated operating-cost data to decide fuel subsidy (diesel at ₹40/L for mechanised boats) and Kisan Credit Card limits for fishermen.
- **Comparison of Gear**: Trawling, gill-netting and long-lining produce different $C_V$ profiles, allowing economic comparison.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Annual Operating Revenue

Let a fishing vessel land **n species** during a single fishing trip of duration $t_d$ hours. For species *i*:

- Catch per day = $C_i$ (kg/day)
- Ex-vessel price = $P_i$ (₹/kg)
- Number of fishing days per year = $N_f$

The **daily revenue from species i** is the product of mass and price:

$$
R_{d,i} = C_i \cdot P_i \quad \text{(₹/day)}
$$

Summing over all species caught in a day:

$$
R_d = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_{d,i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i P_i \quad \text{(₹/day)}
$$

Multiplying by the number of fishing days per year:

$$
R = R_d \cdot N_f = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i P_i\right) N_f \quad \text{(₹/year)}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The unit "kg/day × ₹/kg × days" simplifies to "₹/year", because the "kg" cancels and "days × 1/year = year" effectively leaves only ₹.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Fuel Cost from Engine Power (Power Method)

Marine diesel engines are rated in **Brake Horse Power (BHP)** or **kW**. Fuel use is best estimated from **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)**.

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{\text{BHP} \times \text{SFC}}{1000} \quad \text{(kg/hr)}
$$

where SFC is expressed in g/kWh. Typical SFC for marine diesel ≈ **210 g/kWh**.

Convert mass flow to volumetric flow using diesel density $\rho_f \approx$ **0.85 kg/L**:

$$
\dot{V}_f = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{\rho_f} \quad \text{(L/hr)}
$$

Annual fuel volume:

$$
V_{f,ann} = \dot{V}_f \cdot t_{op} \quad \text{(L/year)}
$$

where $t_{op}$ = annual engine operating hours. Annual fuel cost:

$$
C_{Fuel} = V_{f,ann} \cdot c_f \quad \text{(₹/year)}
$$

Lubricating oil is taken as a percentage $k_{LO}$ (typically 5–10 %) of fuel cost:

$$
C_{LO} = k_{LO} \cdot C_{Fuel}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Annual Depreciation (Straight-Line Method)

For a vessel of initial cost $C_{vessel}$ (₹), expected scrap value $S_v$ (₹) at the end of economic life $L_e$ (years):

$$
D = \frac{C_{vessel} - S_v}{L_e} \quad \text{(₹/year)}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Cost difference divided by time gives a uniform annual depreciation charge — the simplest method accepted by KTU for vessel-economics problems.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Net Operating Income and Break-Even Days

Total annual operating cost:

$$
C_{op} = C_F + C_V
$$

Net Operating Income:

$$
\text{NOI} = R - C_{op}
$$

Setting NOI = 0 gives the **break-even condition** (vessel neither profits nor loses):

$$
R - C_{op} = 0
$$

$$
R_d \cdot N_{BE} - C_F - C_{V,d} \cdot N_{BE} = 0
$$

Solving for $N_{BE}$:

$$
\boxed{N_{BE} = \dfrac{C_F}{R_d - C_{V,d}}}
$$

The vessel must fish **at least $N_{BE}$ days** in a year to cover all fixed costs.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style 14-Mark Problem)

> **Problem Statement:** A 15 m stern trawler operating off the Kerala coast has the following annual data. Compute (i) annual revenue, (ii) annual operating cost, (iii) net operating income, and (iv) break-even fishing days.

| **Parameter** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| Main catch (prawns) | $C_1$ = 80 kg/day, $P_1$ = ₹ 350/kg |
| By-catch (mixed fish) | $C_2$ = 520 kg/day, $P_2$ = ₹ 90/kg |
| Fishing days per year | $N_f$ = 180 days |
| Crew size | n = 10 |
| Average crew wage | ₹ 12,000/month per head |
| Fuel consumption | 350 L/day at ₹ 85/L |
| Lub oil factor | $k_{LO}$ = 8 % of fuel cost |
| Ice consumption | 900 kg/day at ₹ 3/kg |
| Provisions | ₹ 120/crew/day |
| Annual maintenance | ₹ 4,00,000 |
| Port/harbour dues | ₹ 80,000 |
| Annual insurance | ₹ 1,80,000 |
| Gear replacement | ₹ 2,50,000 |
| Administrative overhead | ₹ 1,20,000 |
| Vessel cost | ₹ 30,00,000 ; scrap = ₹ 6,00,000 ; life = 12 years |

### Step 1 — Annual Revenue

Daily revenue from prawns:

$$
R_{d,1} = 80 \times 350 = 28{,}000 \;₹/\text{day}
$$

Daily revenue from mixed fish:

$$
R_{d,2} = 520 \times 90 = 46{,}800 \;₹/\text{day}
$$

Total daily revenue:

$$
R_d = 28{,}000 + 46{,}800 = 74{,}800 \;₹/\text{day}
$$

Annual revenue:

$$
R = 74{,}800 \times 180 = 1{,}34{,}64{,}000 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

### Step 2 — Annual Depreciation

$$
D = \frac{30{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000}{12} = \frac{24{,}00{,}000}{12} = 2{,}00{,}000 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

### Step 3 — Annual Fixed Cost $C_F$

$$
C_F = I_{ann} + D + A_{adm} + W_{base}
$$

$$
W_{base} = 10 \times 12{,}000 \times 12 = 14{,}40{,}000 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

$$
C_F = 1{,}80{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 14{,}40{,}000 = 19{,}40{,}000 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

### Step 4 — Annual Variable Cost $C_V$

Fuel:

$$
C_{Fuel} = 350 \times 85 \times 180 = 53{,}55{,}000 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

Lubricating oil:

$$
C_{LO} = 0.08 \times 53{,}55{,}000 = 4{,}28{,}400 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

Ice:

$$
C_{ice} = 900 \times 3 \times 180 = 4{,}86{,}000 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

Crew provisions (variable, per day):

$$
C_{prov} = 120 \times 10 \times 180 = 2{,}16{,}000 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

Other variable items (maintenance + port + gear):

$$
C_{other} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 = 7{,}30{,}000 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

Summing:

$$
C_V = 53{,}55{,}000 + 4{,}28{,}400 + 4{,}86{,}000 + 2{,}16{,}000 + 7{,}30{,}000 = 72{,}15{,}400 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

### Step 5 — Annual Operating Cost

$$
C_{op} = C_F + C_V = 19{,}40{,}000 + 72{,}15{,}400 = 91{,}55{,}400 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

### Step 6 — Net Operating Income

$$
\text{NOI} = R - C_{op} = 1{,}34{,}64{,}000 - 91{,}55{,}400 = 43{,}08{,}600 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

### Step 7 — Break-Even Fishing Days

Daily variable cost:

$$
C_{V,d} = \frac{72{,}15{,}400}{180} = 40{,}085 \;₹/\text{day}
$$

Break-even days:

$$
N_{BE} = \frac{19{,}40{,}000}{74{,}800 - 40{,}085} = \frac{19{,}40{,}000}{34{,}715} \approx 55.9 \;\text{days}
$$

### Result Summary Table

| **Quantity** | **Value** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| Annual Revenue $R$ | 1,34,64,000 | ₹/year |
| Annual Fixed Cost $C_F$ | 19,40,000 | ₹/year |
| Annual Variable Cost $C_V$ | 72,15,400 | ₹/year |
| Annual Operating Cost $C_{op}$ | 91,55,400 | ₹/year |
| **Net Operating Income** | **43,08,600** | **₹/year** |
| Break-even Fishing Days $N_{BE}$ | ≈ 56 | days |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** With the present parameters, the vessel must fish **at least 56 days/year** to avoid loss. Since it actually fishes 180 days, it operates comfortably above break-even — a healthy economic sign for bank financing.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Cost-Sensitivity Heat Chart

The matrix below shows how NOI varies with two dominant variables — **fuel price** and **catch rate**. The centre cell reproduces the worked example.

| **Fuel ↓ / Catch (kg/day) →** | **300** | **500** | **600** | **800** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **₹ 70/L** | Loss | Marginal | Profit | Strong Profit |
| **₹ 85/L** | Heavy Loss | Marginal | Profit (worked example) | Strong Profit |
| **₹ 100/L** | Heavy Loss | Loss | Marginal | Profit |
| **₹ 115/L** | Heavy Loss | Heavy Loss | Loss | Marginal |

> [!NOTE]
> This qualitative matrix explains why Indian trawler owners are highly sensitive to diesel price — a ₹10/L rise in fuel can wipe out an entire season's profit, justifying the government's marine diesel subsidy policy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram of Vessel Operating Economics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Vessel Operating Economics] --> nodeB[Operating Revenues]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Operating Costs]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[Main Catch Sales]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[By-catch Sales]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[By-product Sales]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[Charter Income]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Fixed Costs]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Variable Costs]

    nodeC1 --> nodeC1a[Hull and Machinery Insurance]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1b[Annual Depreciation]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1c[Administrative Overhead]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1d[Base Crew Wages]

    nodeC2 --> nodeC2a[Fuel]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2b[Lubricating Oil]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2c[Ice and Salt]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2d[Bait]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2e[Provisions]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2f[Maintenance and Repair]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2g[Port and Harbour Dues]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2h[Gear Replacement]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2i[Overtime and Catch-linked Bonus]

    nodeA --> nodeD[Net Operating Income NOI]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Profit if R greater than Cop]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Loss if R less than Cop]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Break even if R equals Cop]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Annual Revenue → NOI Computation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1: Identify Catch per Species per Day] --> stepB[Step 2: Multiply by Ex-vessel Price per Species]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Sum to get Daily Revenue]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Multiply by Number of Fishing Days per Year]
    stepD --> stepE[Annual Revenue R]

    stepF[Step 1: List Fixed Cost Components] --> stepG[Step 2: Compute Annual Depreciation]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 3: List Variable Cost Components]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 4: Multiply Daily Variable Items by Fishing Days]
    stepI --> stepJ[Annual Operating Cost Cop]

    stepE --> stepK[Net Operating Income NOI = R minus Cop]
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL[Break even Days NBE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for Beginners:** In the second diagram, the left column generates revenue, the right column generates cost, and the two columns merge into the central NOI computation block. Tracing top-to-bottom on either side mirrors exactly what you write on the answer sheet during the KTU exam.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define operating revenue of a fishing vessel and list its four main components.**

**Model Answer:**
Operating revenue is the gross monetary income earned by a fishing vessel during normal fishing operations over one financial year. The four main components are:
1. **Sale of main catch** (target species).
2. **Sale of by-catch** (incidental species).
3. **Sale of by-products** (fish meal, oil, shells).
4. **Charter / lease income** from hiring the boat.

(3 marks for crisp definition + enumeration)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between fixed and variable operating costs of a fishing vessel with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Fixed Cost (C_F)** | **Variable Cost (C_V)** |
|---|---|
| Independent of fishing days | Proportional to fishing days |
| Examples: Insurance, Depreciation, Administrative overhead | Examples: Fuel, Lubricating oil, Ice, Provisions |
| Incurred even during off-season | Zero when vessel is laid up |

(3 marks — 1 for correct distinction, 2 for examples)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain, with the help of a block diagram, the various components of **operating revenues and operating costs** of a fishing vessel. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**
*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes for the visual answer.)*

- **Revenues** (4 marks): Main catch sales, By-catch sales, By-product sales, Charter income — explained in 2-3 lines each.
- **Costs** (3 marks): Two main buckets — *Fixed* (insurance, depreciation, admin, base crew wage) and *Variable* (fuel, lub oil, ice, bait, provisions, maintenance, port dues, gear replacement, overtime).

**(b)** A 16 m purse-seiner has the following data. Compute the **annual revenue, annual operating cost and net operating income**. *(7 marks)*

| **Parameter** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| Catch (mackerel) | 700 kg/day at ₹ 60/kg |
| By-catch | 200 kg/day at ₹ 30/kg |
| Fishing days/year | 200 |
| Crew (12) wage | ₹ 10,000/month each |
| Fuel | 400 L/day at ₹ 80/L |
| Lub oil | 7 % of fuel cost |
| Ice | 800 kg/day at ₹ 3/kg |
| Provisions | ₹ 100/crew/day |
| Maintenance | ₹ 3,00,000 |
| Port dues | ₹ 60,000 |
| Insurance | ₹ 1,50,000 |
| Gear replacement | ₹ 1,80,000 |
| Admin | ₹ 90,000 |
| Depreciation | ₹ 2,50,000/year |

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Daily Revenue**
$$
R_d = (700 \times 60) + (200 \times 30) = 42{,}000 + 6{,}000 = 48{,}000 \;₹/\text{day}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual Revenue**
$$
R = 48{,}000 \times 200 = 96{,}00{,}000 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual Fixed Cost**
$$
W_{base} = 12 \times 10{,}000 \times 12 = 14{,}40{,}000
$$

$$
C_F = 1{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 + 90{,}000 + 14{,}40{,}000 = 19{,}30{,}000 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual Variable Cost**
- Fuel: $400 \times 80 \times 200$ = ₹ 64,00,000
- Lub oil: $0.07 \times 64{,}00{,}000$ = ₹ 4,48,000
- Ice: $800 \times 3 \times 200$ = ₹ 4,80,000
- Provisions: $100 \times 12 \times 200$ = ₹ 2,40,000
- Maint + Port + Gear: $3{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000$ = ₹ 5,40,000

$$
C_V = 64{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}48{,}000 + 4{,}80{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 + 5{,}40{,}000 = 81{,}08{,}000 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual Operating Cost**
$$
C_{op} = C_F + C_V = 19{,}30{,}000 + 81{,}08{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}38{,}000 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

**Step 6 — Net Operating Income**
$$
\text{NOI} = 96{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}38{,}000 = -4{,}38{,}000 \;₹/\text{year}
$$

> **Conclusion:** NOI is **negative (-₹ 4.38 lakh)**, indicating the purse-seiner is running at a loss. The owner must either (i) increase catch price through better post-harvest handling, (ii) reduce fuel use, or (iii) increase fishing days beyond 200 to break even.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss, with suitable examples, the **classification of operating costs** of a fishing vessel into fixed and variable categories. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Operating costs are recurring annual expenses required for vessel operation. KTU classifies them into two categories:

**1. Fixed Costs (C_F)** — independent of fishing activity. *(3 marks)*
- **Insurance** of hull and machinery — paid whether the boat sails or not.
- **Depreciation** — straight-line charge over economic life (e.g., ₹2 lakh/year).
- **Administrative overhead** — office, registration, audits.
- **Base / contract wages** of permanent crew.

**2. Variable Costs (C_V)** — scale with fishing effort. *(3 marks)*
- **Fuel** — largest single item (35–45 % of $C_{op}$).
- **Lubricating oil** — 5–10 % of fuel cost.
- **Ice, salt, bait** — consumed during each voyage.
- **Provisions** for crew — per crew-day.
- **Maintenance and spares** — function of engine hours.
- **Port and harbour dues** — paid per call.
- **Gear replacement** — nets, ropes, hooks.
- **Overtime and catch-linked bonuses**.

**3. Importance of Classification (1 mark):**
The split is critical because:
- Fixed costs determine the **break-even days**.
- Variable costs determine the **slope of the revenue-vs-cost curve**.
- Sensitivity analysis and subsidy design depend on this classification.

**(b)** A fishing trawler has fixed cost ₹ 12,00,000/year, daily revenue ₹ 60,000 and daily variable cost ₹ 45,000. Determine the **break-even number of fishing days per year**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the break-even formula**

$$
N_{BE} = \frac{C_F}{R_d - C_{V,d}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$
N_{BE} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{60{,}000 - 45{,}000} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{15{,}000}
$$

**Step 3 — Final answer**

$$
\boxed{N_{BE} = 80 \; \text{fishing days per year}}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**
The vessel must fish **at least 80 days/year** to recover fixed costs. If the vessel fishes 200 days (typical), the surplus over break-even = (200 − 80) × ₹15,000 = **₹ 18,00,000/year** as net profit contribution.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting depreciation** in annual operating cost. Many students add only fuel + crew + ice and miss the depreciation line item — this costs 1 full mark.
> 2. **Mixing fixed and variable crew wages.** The base monthly wage is fixed; the catch-linked bonus / overtime is variable. Marking both as fixed loses ½ mark.
> 3. **Using total days instead of fishing days.** A vessel is at sea only on *fishing days*. Maintenance days and bad-weather days should not enter the revenue calculation.
> 4. **Wrong unit for SFC.** SFC is in **g/kWh**, not kg/kWh. Dividing by 1000 (not 100) when converting to kg/hr is the standard procedure — examiners check this.
> 5. **Break-even sign error.** If $R_d < C_{V,d}$, the formula gives a negative $N_{BE}$ which is physically meaningless — write that the vessel cannot break even under the given economics.
> 6. **No units in the final answer.** Every numerical answer must carry its unit (₹/year, days, kg/day). Marks are deducted for missing units.

---

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Operating Revenue** = Σ (catch per species × price per species) × fishing days per year.
- **Operating Cost** = Fixed Cost + Variable Cost.
- **Fixed Cost** = Insurance + Depreciation + Administrative + Base wages.
- **Variable Cost** = Fuel + Lub oil + Ice + Bait + Provisions + Maintenance + Port + Gear + Overtime.
- **Net Operating Income (NOI)** = Revenue − Operating Cost.
- **Depreciation (SLM)** = (Initial cost − Scrap value) / Economic life.
- **Fuel cost** = Consumption (L/day) × Price (₹/L) × Fishing days.
- **Lub oil cost** ≈ 5–10 % of fuel cost.
- **Break-even days** = Fixed Cost / (Daily revenue − Daily variable cost).
- **Standard constants:** SFC ≈ **210 g/kWh**, diesel density ≈ **0.85 kg/L**, vessel life ≈ **15–20 years**.
- **Always** quote units; **always** show intermediate steps; **never** mix up fishing days with calendar days.
- **Examiners reward**: neat table layout, labelled equations, clear identification of fixed vs variable items.
- **Memory trick**: *Revenue is what comes in (in)*, *Cost is what goes out (out)*, *NOI = in − out*.

---

**End of Notes — Module 2 · Operating Revenues and Costs (PENST754)**
*Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme · Outcome-Based Education · Bloom's Levels L1–L4*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Main Dimensions and Form: Parent Vessel Data Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Main Dimensions and Form: Parent Vessel Data Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Parent Vessel Data Analysis** is a foundational empirical design methodology in fishing vessel naval architecture wherein the principal dimensions (Length, Breadth, Depth, Draft) and form coefficients of a newly proposed vessel are derived through statistical correlation with a carefully selected database of *similar, successfully operating vessels* (parent vessels). Under the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework, this technique forms the conceptual bridge between empirical rule-of-thumb design and modern optimization-based preliminary design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PENST754 - Module 2):**
> "Parent vessel data analysis is the systematic study of geometric, hydrodynamic, and operational parameters of existing fishing vessels of comparable type, size, and operating profile, used to statistically extrapolate the main dimensions, form coefficients, displacement, and powering characteristics of a new design."

### Key Terminology Decoded

- **Parent Vessel:** A proven, sea-tested reference vessel whose performance, form, and dimensions are well documented.
- **Main Dimensions:** The four canonical geometric descriptors — Length between Perpendiculars ($L_{PP}$), Breadth ($B$), Depth ($D$), and Design Draft ($T$).
- **Form Coefficients:** Dimensionless ratios (Block Coefficient $C_B$, Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$, Midship Coefficient $C_M$, Waterplane Coefficient $C_{WP}$) describing hull fullness and shape.
- **Displacement ($\nabla$ or $\Delta$):** The volume of water displaced by the vessel, typically expressed in cubic meters ($\text{m}^3$) or metric tonnes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are tailoring a school uniform for a new student joining a class. Rather than guessing measurements from scratch, you observe the uniforms of 20 classmates of similar build. You notice that the chest size, shoulder width, and sleeve length of similar-built students fall within a predictable statistical pattern. You then size the new uniform using this empirical pattern.

> **Parent Vessel Data Analysis works exactly this way.** Instead of inventing the hull form of a new fishing trawler from a blank canvas, naval architects study 15–30 existing trawlers of similar tonnage and operating region, statistically identify the geometric "uniform" they share, and use that to draft the new vessel.

### The Three Pillars of Parent Vessel Selection

> [!NOTE]
> For a fishing vessel design, the parent fleet MUST be selected based on:
>
> 1. **Type Compatibility** — Trawler, long-liner, gill-netter, purse-seiner, etc.
> 2. **Size Proximity** — Displacement within $\pm 30\%$ of intended new vessel.
> 3. **Operating Region** — Same sea basin (e.g., Arabian Sea, Bay of Bengal) to inherit appropriate freeboard, stability, and seakeeping characteristics.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Reference Constants Used in Parent Vessel Analysis:**
>
> - **Sea Water Density:** $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1.025 \text{ t/m}^3}$ (standard reference for fishing vessel displacement calculations)
> - **Fresh Water Density:** $\rho_{fw} = \mathbf{1.000 \text{ t/m}^3}$
> - **Gravitational Acceleration:** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \text{ m/s}^2}$
> - **Standard Reference Speed Unit:** **Knot (kn)** where $1 \text{ kn} = \mathbf{0.5144 \text{ m/s}}$
> - **Classification Society Reference:** Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) and IACS standards for fishing vessels.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Parent Vessel Methodology — Step-by-Step Logic

The empirical design of fishing vessel main dimensions via parent vessel analysis follows a structured seven-step workflow:

### Step 1: Define the Design Mission
Establish the operational profile — fishing gear type (trawl/long-line/purse-seine), daily catch capacity (tonnes), endurance (days at sea), crew size, and operating sea state.

### Step 2: Estimate the Required Deadweight (DWT)
From mission profile, compute the required carrying capacity:
$$DWT = W_{catch} + W_{fuel} + W_{water} + W_{stores} + W_{crew+effects} + W_{fishing gear}$$

### Step 3: Build the Parent Vessel Database
Collect a sample of $n \geq 15$ existing fishing vessels with documented main dimensions, displacement, speed, and power. (KTU 2024 minimum sample size = 15 vessels for statistical validity).

### Step 4: Determine the Displacement Ratio
Establish the preliminary displacement of the new vessel using a DWT-to-Displacement ratio derived from parent vessels:
$$\Delta_{new} = \frac{DWT_{new}}{DWT_{parent,avg}} \times \Delta_{parent,avg}$$

### Step 5: Run Regression Analysis on Main Dimensions
For each principal dimension ($L_{PP}$, $B$, $D$, $T$), regress against displacement $\Delta$ using the parent data:
$$X_i = a \cdot \Delta^{b} \quad \text{(Power Law Regression)}$$

### Step 6: Extract Form Coefficients
From the parent fleet, compute average $C_B$, $C_P$, $C_M$, $C_{WP}$ and verify they fall within fishing-vessel norms.

### Step 7: Validate and Iterate
Cross-check the resulting design using resistance/powering prediction, intact stability (IMO A.749), and freeboard (ILLC) compliance.

## 2.2 Why Parent Vessel Analysis? — Engineering Justification

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use theoretical optimization?**
>
> Fishing vessels are constrained by:
> - Fishing gear hydrodynamics (trawl door drag, net buoyancy)
> - Regional sea-keeping expectations (monsoon operations off Kerala coast)
> - Harbor and beach-landing restrictions
> - Economic repeatability (yard familiarity with parent hull forms)
>
> Parent vessel analysis provides **field-validated realism** that pure optimization cannot capture.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates ALL high-yield formulas required for KTU ESE and internal assessments on Parent Vessel Data Analysis. **Memorize the symbolic form; understand the units.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Displacement from Block Coefficient | $\Delta = C_B \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T$ | $\text{m}^3$ or tonnes | Convert dimensions to volume |
| 2 | Length–Beam Ratio | $L/B$ | dimensionless | Slenderness check |
| 3 | Beam–Draft Ratio | $B/T$ | dimensionless | Hull fullness check |
| 4 | Length–Depth Ratio | $L/D$ | dimensionless | Hull girder stiffness |
| 5 | Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P = \frac{\Delta}{L_{PP} \cdot A_M}$ | dimensionless | Hull form taper |
| 6 | Midship Coefficient | $C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | dimensionless | Section fullness |
| 7 | Waterplane Coefficient | $C_{WP} = \frac{A_{WP}}{L_{PP} \cdot B}$ | dimensionless | Waterplane area ratio |
| 8 | Block-to-Prismatic Relation | $C_B = C_P \times C_M$ | dimensionless | Internal consistency check |
| 9 | Displacement–Length Ratio | $\Delta / (0.01 L_{PP})^3$ | dimensionless | Tonnage group classification |
