---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Theory/Conceptual Problem**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **statistical method** of estimating main dimensions of a fishing vessel. List the regression equations typically used.  
**(b)** Discuss **any four** form (hull) coefficients used in fishing-vessel design, and explain how they are interrelated.

#### **Model Solution Outline:**

### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Sketch (2 marks):** A simple block diagram of the inputs (DWT, Vs) → regression equations → outputs (L, B, T, Cb).

**Statistical Method Explanation (3 marks):**
- The statistical method uses **regression equations** fitted to a database of *similar* (parent) fishing vessels.
- The key form is: each main dimension scales as **$\Delta^{1/3}$** due to geometric similarity.
- The Froude number $F_n$ then modulates Cb and Cp.

**Regression Equations (2 marks):**
$$ L_{BP} = k_1 \cdot \Delta^{1/3} \cdot V_s^{1/3} $$
$$ B = k_2 \cdot \Delta^{1/3} $$
$$ T = k_3 \cdot \Delta^{1/3} $$
$$ C_b = k_4 - k_5 \cdot F_n $$

(Where $k_1 \dots k_5$ are empirical regression coefficients specific to a vessel class.)

### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Four Form Coefficients (4 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Cb (Block):** $C_b = \nabla / (L B T)$ — overall fullness.
2. **Cp (Prismatic):** $C_p = \nabla / (A_m L)$ — end-shape fullness.
3. **Cm (Midship):** $C_m = A_m / (B T)$ — cross-section fullness.
4. **Cwp (Waterplane):** $C_{wp} = A_w / (L B)$ — waterplane area fullness.

**Interrelation (3 marks):**
- $C_b = C_p \cdot C_m$ (derived identity).
- Cwp is **independent** of these three (it involves the waterplane, not the underwater body).
- $A_m = C_m \cdot B \cdot T$ and $A_w = C_{wp} \cdot L \cdot B$ are the *bridge quantities* linking coefficients to dimensions.
- For fishing trawlers: high Cm (0.90–0.95) + moderate Cp (0.65–0.72) → Cb 0.55–0.68.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — "Independence Error":** Students often claim all four coefficients are independent. **Cwp is independent** of (Cb, Cp, Cm), but **Cb, Cp, Cm are mutually dependent** through the $C_b = C_p \cdot C_m$ identity. This distinction is a 2-mark tester.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert Δ ↔ ∇** using 1.025. Exam papers use both freely. Mixing them causes a 2.5% error in *every* downstream number.
> 2. **Using LOA when LBP is required.** State which L is used, every time.
> 3. **Using long tons (1016 kg) in a metric problem.** Always use tonnes = 1000 kg.
> 4. **Forgetting units in the formula sheet.** KTU deducts half-marks for "naked" formulas.
> 5. **Solving for L^3 and not taking the cube root.** A classic transcription slip.
> 6. **Not verifying the L/∇^(1/3) ratio at the end.** The "sanity check" is worth 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Confusing freeboard (D − T) with sheer.** Freeboard is vertical; sheer is the curvature of the deck.
> 8. **Conflating "Lightweight" with "Lightship".** Lightship = Lightweight + permanent ballast (e.g., for wooden vessels).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print this before the exam.**

- ☐ **Main dimensions** = LBP, B, T, D, ∇, Δ (six quantities).
- ☐ **Seawater density** = 1.025 t/m³ (always).
- ☐ **Δ = 1.025 · ∇** is the master mass↔volume link.
- ☐ **∇ = Cb · L · B · T** is the master volume identity.
- ☐ **Cb = Cp · Cm** is the most-tested identity — derive it, don't memorize it.
- ☐ **Standard ranges:** Cb 0.55–0.68, Cp 0.60–0.75, Cm 0.85–0.98, Cwp 0.70–0.85 for trawlers.
- ☐ **Master ratios:** L/∇^(1/3) = 4.5–6.0, L/B = 4.5–6.0, B/T = 2.0–2.5, L/D = 9–12.
- ☐ **DWT → Δ:** Δ = DWT / (1 − LWT/Δ).
- ☐ **Statistical method:** L, B, T each scale as Δ^(1/3); Cb reduces with Fn.
- ☐ **Parent-vessel method:** Use ratios of a *known* similar vessel.
- ☐ **Series-data method:** Use Taylor/Troost series for non-standard forms.
- ☐ **Requirements first, dimensions second** — never invert the order.
- ☐ **Always end with a verification** (L/∇^(1/3), ratios, freeboard check).
- ☐ **Unit consistency:** tonnes for mass, m³ for volume, m for length, knots for speed.
- ☐ **Freeboard = D − T**; do not confuse with sheer or camber.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra — "Δ → ∇ → Cb → Ratios → L, B, T → D → Verify."**
> Follow this sequence and you will never get lost in a fishing-vessel main-dimension problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Form Coefficients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Form Coefficients

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Naval Architecture** and **Fishing Vessel Design**, **Form Coefficients** (also called **Hull Form Coefficients** or **Displacement Coefficients**) are a set of **dimensionless geometric ratios** that quantitatively describe the *fineness*, *fullness*, and *slenderness* of a vessel's underwater hull. They compare the actual displaced volume, transverse area, or waterplane area of the hull against the volume/area of a *reference rectangular box* (or prism) having the same principal dimensions (Length, Beam, Draft).

These coefficients are **scale-invariant** — they describe *shape* only, not *size*. Two vessels of vastly different tonnage can share identical form coefficients if their hull geometries are geometrically similar.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Form coefficients form the **primary geometric input** for resistance prediction, powering calculations, stability assessment, and statistical hull-form estimation. They bridge the gap between *concept design* (no lines plan available) and *preliminary design* (lines plan in progress).

The four principal form coefficients studied in Module 2 are:

1. **Block Coefficient** ($C_B$)
2. **Midship Section Coefficient** ($C_M$)
3. **Prismatic Coefficient** ($C_P$)
4. **Waterplane Area Coefficient** ($C_{WP}$)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Watermelon vs. The Cucumber

Imagine two fruits of the **same length, same width, and same height** placed in identical boxes:

- A **watermelon** nearly fills the box — it is *full*, *blunt*, and *fat*. Its volume is close to the box volume.
- A **cucumber** of the same outer dimensions is *slim*, *pointed* at the ends, and fills only the central part of the box.

Both have the same box, but completely different shapes. The ratio **(fruit volume) / (box volume)** captures this difference perfectly. The watermelon has a **Block Coefficient ≈ 0.95**, the cucumber has a **Block Coefficient ≈ 0.45**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fishing Vessel Analogy:**
> - A **bottom trawler** is a "watermelon" — high $C_B$ (0.60 – 0.70) for stability with full nets, large fuel tanks, and fish holds.
> - A **tuna long-liner** is a "cucumber" — lower $C_B$ (0.50 – 0.58) for fuel-efficient long cruising between fishing grounds.

## 1.3 Why Form Coefficients Matter in Fishing Vessel Design

| Design Driver | Preferred Hull Form | Dominant Coefficient |
|---|---|---|
| Heavy trawl catch stability | Full, blunt midship | High $C_M$, High $C_B$ |
| High cruising speed (liners) | Fine entry, clean run | Low $C_P$, Low $C_B$ |
| Large deck area for gear | Full waterplane | High $C_{WP}$ |
| Maneuverability in nets | Slender run | Low $C_P$ aft |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Values

- **Sea water density (standard):** $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1.025\ t/m^3}$ (ITTC standard)
- **Fresh water density:** $\rho_{fw} = \mathbf{1.000\ t/m^3}$
- **Typical Block Coefficient for fishing trawlers:** $C_B = \mathbf{0.60\ -\ 0.70}$
- **Typical Block Coefficient for purse seiners:** $C_B = \mathbf{0.55\ -\ 0.65}$
- **Typical Block Coefficient for long liners:** $C_B = \mathbf{0.50\ -\ 0.60}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Principal Form Coefficients — Logic Steps

### 2.1.1 Block Coefficient ($C_B$)

**Definition Logic:**
- *Step 1:* Compute the actual volume of water displaced by the hull, $\nabla$ (in m³).
- *Step 2:* Compute the volume of a rectangular box with the same Length ($L_{WL}$), Beam ($B$), and Draft ($T$).
- *Step 3:* Take their ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> $C_B$ is the **single most important form coefficient** in preliminary fishing vessel design. It directly influences displacement, resistance, and power.

### 2.1.2 Midship Section Coefficient ($C_M$)

**Definition Logic:**
- *Step 1:* Identify the cross-section of the hull at **mid-length** (½ $L_{WL}$).
- *Step 2:* Compute the actual immersed transverse area at that station, $A_M$ (m²).
- *Step 3:* Compare it to the bounding rectangle $B \times T$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $C_M$ is **independent of length**. It only describes the *fullness of the cross-section*. For fishing vessels, it is typically higher than for cargo ships because of the need for a *fat, stable working platform* at midships.

### 2.1.3 Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$)

**Definition Logic:**
- *Step 1:* Consider the underwater hull as if you "extruded" the midship section along the entire length.
- *Step 2:* This would generate a prism of volume $A_M \times L_{WL}$.
- *Step 3:* Compare the actual displaced volume to this prism volume.

> [!NOTE]
> $C_P$ tells you how much the hull **tapers fore and aft** from the midship section. $C_P = 1$ → hull is a pure prism (no taper, like a barge). $C_P < 1$ → hull tapers to fine ends (like a canoe).

### 2.1.4 Waterplane Area Coefficient ($C_{WP}$)

**Definition Logic:**
- *Step 1:* Compute the actual area of the waterplane (the slice of the hull at the design waterline), $A_{WP}$ (m²).
- *Step 2:* Compare it to $L_{WL} \times B$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $C_{WP}$ governs **initial stability** (GMT), **damping of roll**, and the **effectiveness of waterplane area** in resisting heeling. Fishing vessels need a high $C_{WP}$ for stability when hauling nets or working on deck.

## 2.2 The Fundamental Geometric Relationship

The three "length-based" coefficients are not independent. They obey an exact algebraic identity:

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M}$$

This is the **single most-tested relationship** in KTU 2024 examination questions on form coefficients.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Coefficient | Symbol | Formula | Typical Range (Fishing Vessels) | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Block Coefficient** | $C_B$ | $\dfrac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | 0.50 – 0.72 | Overall hull fullness |
| 2 | **Midship Coefficient** | $C_M$ | $\dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | 0.80 – 0.98 | Cross-section fullness |
| 3 | **Prismatic Coefficient** | $C_P$ | $\dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{WL}}$ | 0.55 – 0.80 | Longitudinal taper |
| 4 | **Waterplane Coeff.** | $C_{WP}$ | $\dfrac{A_{WP}}{L_{WL} \cdot B}$ | 0.65 – 0.85 | Waterplane fullness |
| 5 | **Identity** | — | $C_P = C_B \,/\, C_M$ | — | Geometric consistency |
| 6 | **Displacement (mass)** | $\Delta$ | $\rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla$ | tonnes | Mass of displaced water |
| 7 | **Displacement (volume)** | $\nabla$ | $C_B \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T$ | m³ | Hull volume |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention (KTU 2024):** $L_{WL}$, $B$, $T$ in **metres (m)**; $\nabla$ in **cubic metres (m³)**; $A_M$, $A_{WP}$ in **square metres (m²)**; $\Delta$ in **tonnes (t)**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **concept design stage** of a fishing vessel — when no lines plan exists — the naval architect *cannot* compute volume or areas by integration. Instead, they use **statistical estimation methods** based on form coefficients:

- **Method 1: Direct Statistical Average** — Use tabulated average $C_B$ values from similar existing fishing vessels in the fleet.
- **Method 2: Series Data Extrapolation** — Reference standard series like the *Taylor Standard Series*, *BSRA Series*, or *NPL Series* to read $C_B$ as a function of length and speed.
- **Method 3: Empirical Regression Formulas** — Use power-law regressions of the form $C_B = a \cdot L^b \cdot V^c$ calibrated from fleets of similar vessels.
- **Method 4: Parent Hull Form Modification** — Select a "parent" fishing vessel hull and apply a *form factor* adjustment for the new design.

The form coefficient is the **fundamental design variable** that propagates into resistance estimation (e.g., Holtrop-Mennen), powering calculation, and EEDI/EEXI compliance for fishing vessels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Identity $C_P = C_B / C_M$

This is the **key derivation** required by KTU 2024 Module 2. It is shown in full, with no skipped steps.

**Starting point:** We begin with the formal definition of the block coefficient:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

**Step 1 — Re-express the denominator.** The denominator $L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T$ can be rewritten by grouping the midship-section dimensions:

$$L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T = L_{WL} \cdot (B \cdot T)$$

**Step 2 — Recall the definition of the midship coefficient.** By definition:

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$

Therefore, the area of the midship section is:

$$A_M = C_M \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the denominator.** Substituting the expression for $A_M$ into the denominator of $C_B$:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot (B \cdot T)} = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot \left(\dfrac{A_M}{C_M}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic simplification.** Invert the inner fraction:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla \cdot C_M}{L_{WL} \cdot A_M}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to isolate the ratio $\nabla / (L_{WL} \cdot A_M)$.** This ratio is, by definition, the prismatic coefficient:

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot A_M}$$

**Step 6 — Final substitution.** Substituting into the expression from Step 4:

$$C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $C_P$.** Dividing both sides by $C_M$:

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> This identity is **mathematically exact** for any ship — it is not an approximation. It is a direct consequence of the way the three coefficients are defined.

## 3.2 Derivation of Displacement from Form Coefficients

The **displaced volume** of the underwater hull is the foundation of all weight and buoyancy calculations. The full derivation from the basic definition is:

**Starting point:** The volume of a rectangular box with the same principal dimensions is:

$$V_{box} = L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 1 — Apply the block coefficient definition.** Since $C_B$ is the ratio of actual hull volume to box volume:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{V_{box}} = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\nabla$.** Multiplying both sides by the denominator:

$$\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Convert volume to mass displacement.** By Archimedes' principle, the mass of the displaced water equals the vessel's mass (at equilibrium):

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = \rho_{sw} \cdot C_B \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 4 — Insert the standard seawater density.** Using $\rho_{sw} = 1.025\ t/m^3$:

$$\Delta = 1.025 \cdot C_B \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T \quad \text{(tonnes)} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — A Stern Trawler

**Given data for a proposed stern trawler:**
- Length on waterline: $L_{WL} = 28.0\ m$
- Beam: $B = 7.5\ m$
- Mean draft: $T = 3.2\ m$
- Midship section area: $A_M = 20.4\ m^2$
- Block coefficient (statistical estimate): $C_B = 0.65$

**Step 1 — Verify the geometric consistency using the identity.**

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M}$$

Compute $C_M$ from the given data:

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} = \frac{20.4}{7.5 \times 3.2} = \frac{20.4}{24.0} = 0.850$$

Now compute $C_P$:

$$C_P = \frac{0.65}{0.85} = 0.7647$$

**Step 2 — Compute the displaced volume.**

$$\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T = 0.65 \times 28.0 \times 7.5 \times 3.2$$

$$\nabla = 0.65 \times 672.0 = 436.8\ m^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mass displacement (in tonnes).**

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 436.8 = 447.7\ t$$

**Step 4 — Compute the volumetric prismatic check.** As a cross-check:

$$\nabla_{via\ C_P} = C_P \cdot A_M \cdot L_{WL} = 0.7647 \times 20.4 \times 28.0 = 436.8\ m^3 \checkmark$$

The two methods match, confirming internal consistency.

## 3.4 Estimation Method — Statistical Regression for $C_B$

For a **concept design**, $C_B$ is often estimated using a regression of the form:

$$C_B = a \cdot (L_{WL})^b \cdot (Fr)^c$$

where $Fr = V / \sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}$ is the **Froude number** and $a, b, c$ are fleet-specific constants. For a typical fleet of small-to-medium fishing trawlers, an empirical fit (illustrative) is:

$$C_B = 0.780 - 0.0025 \cdot L_{WL} - 0.45 \cdot Fr$$

**Worked example:** For $L_{WL} = 28\ m$ and service speed $V = 11\ knots$:

Compute Froude number (in SI units, $V = 11 \times 0.5144 = 5.66\ m/s$):

$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}} = \frac{5.66}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 28.0}} = \frac{5.66}{\sqrt{274.68}} = \frac{5.66}{16.57} = 0.3415$$

Substitute into the regression:

$$C_B = 0.780 - 0.0025 \times 28.0 - 0.45 \times 0.3415$$

$$C_B = 0.780 - 0.070 - 0.1537 = 0.5563$$

This $C_B = 0.556$ is on the **lower end** of the trawler range, indicating a *faster, finer* hull — suitable if the vessel is a tuna long-liner rather than a heavy trawler.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU examinations, the regression coefficients ($a, b, c$) are **always given** in the question. You are not required to derive them.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Role of Form Coefficients in Design

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **information flow** from principal dimensions to downstream engineering calculations, showing exactly where form coefficients fit in the fishing vessel design process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Principal Dimensions: L, B, T] --> B[Form Coefficient Estimation]
    B --> C1[Block Coefficient Cb]
    B --> C2[Midship Coefficient Cm]
    B --> C3[Prismatic Coefficient Cp]
    B --> C4[Waterplane Coefficient Cwp]
    C1 --> D1[Displaced Volume Nabla]
    C1 --> D2[Mass Displacement Delta]
    C2 --> D3[Midship Section Area AM]
    C3 --> D4[Longitudinal Hull Form Check]
    C4 --> D5[Initial Stability GMT]
    D1 --> E1[Resistance Estimation]
    D2 --> E2[Weight Estimation]
    D1 --> E3[Free Surface Effects]
    D5 --> E4[STAB Analysis]
    D2 --> F1[Powering Calculation]
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Engine Selection]
    G1 --> H1[Fishing Vessel Concept Design]
    E2 --> H1
    E4 --> H1
```

## 4.2 Schematic: The Geometric Meaning of Each Coefficient

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **reference shape** for each coefficient — the rectangle (or prism) to which the actual hull feature is compared.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REF1["Reference Box for Cb"]
        R1[LWL x B x T]
    end
    subgraph REF2["Reference Rectangle for Cm"]
        R2[B x T at midship]
    end
    subgraph REF3["Reference Prism for Cp"]
        R3[AM x LWL]
    end
    subgraph REF4["Reference Rectangle for Cwp"]
        R4[LWL x B at waterline]
    end
    REF1 --> H1[Actual Hull Volume Nabla]
    REF2 --> H2[Actual Midship Area AM]
    REF3 --> H3[Actual Hull Volume Nabla]
    REF4 --> H4[Actual Waterplane Area AWP]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Hierarchy and Dependencies of the Coefficients

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Principal Dimensions] --> B[LWL, B, T]
    B --> C[Block Coefficient Cb]
    B --> D[Midship Coefficient Cm]
    C --> E[Prismatic Coefficient Cp = Cb divided by Cm]
    D --> E
    B --> F[Waterplane Area AWP]
    F --> G[Waterplane Coefficient Cwp]
    C --> H[Displaced Volume Nabla]
    H --> I[Mass Displacement Delta]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** In each block, the upper "Reference" entity is the bounding box, the lower "Actual Hull" entity is the real measurement, and the form coefficient is the **ratio between them**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Block Coefficient of a fishing vessel. State the typical range of $C_B$ for (i) a stern trawler and (ii) a tuna long-liner.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Block Coefficient ($C_B$)** is defined as the ratio of the actual volume of water displaced by the hull ($\nabla$) to the volume of a rectangular box having the same length on waterline ($L_{WL}$), beam ($B$), and mean draft ($T$):

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

- **(i) Stern trawler:** $C_B \approx 0.60 - 0.70$ (full hull form for trawl gear and catch stability).
- **(ii) Tuna long-liner:** $C_B \approx 0.50 - 0.58$ (finer hull for long-range cruising speed).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the exact mathematical relationship between the Block, Prismatic, and Midship coefficients. Why is this relationship important in preliminary design?**

**Model Answer:**

The three coefficients are related by the **exact identity**:

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M}$$

This is derived directly from the definitions and is **not an approximation**. It is important in preliminary design because it allows the naval architect to **cross-verify** statistical estimates — if $C_B$ and $C_M$ are independently estimated, then $C_P$ must equal their ratio. A mismatch indicates an inconsistency in the hull form data used.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **frequently** confuse $C_B$ and $C_P$ — remember $C_B$ involves the *box* $L \cdot B \cdot T$, while $C_P$ involves the *prism* $A_M \cdot L$.
> - **Always include units** in intermediate steps (m, m², m³, t) — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> - When using a regression formula, **substitute in SI units** (metres, m/s). Do NOT use feet or knots directly in the formula.
> - The identity $C_P = C_B / C_M$ is an **exact** relation — losing it as an "approximation" costs 2 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**A new 30 m stern trawler is to be designed with the following preliminary data:**

- $L_{WL} = 30.0\ m$
- $B = 8.0\ m$
- $T = 3.5\ m$
- Estimated Block Coefficient: $C_B = 0.66$
- Estimated Midship Coefficient: $C_M = 0.88$

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
Calculate the displaced volume, the mass displacement in seawater ($\rho = 1.025\ t/m^3$), and the prismatic coefficient of the proposed vessel.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
If a statistical regression gives $C_B = 0.78 - 0.0025 \cdot L_{WL} - 0.45 \cdot Fr$ for the fleet, and the design speed is 12 knots, determine whether the designer's estimate of $C_B = 0.66$ is consistent with the fleet data. Comment on the design implications.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the displaced volume $\nabla$.**

$$\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T$$

Substitute the values:

$$\nabla = 0.66 \times 30.0 \times 8.0 \times 3.5$$

$$\nabla = 0.66 \times 840.0 = 554.4\ m^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mass displacement $\Delta$.**

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 554.4 = 568.26\ t$$

Rounded: $\Delta \approx 568\ t$.

**Step 3 — Compute the midship area $A_M$.**

$$A_M = C_M \cdot B \cdot T = 0.88 \times 8.0 \times 3.5 = 0.88 \times 28.0 = 24.64\ m^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the prismatic coefficient $C_P$ using the identity.**

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M} = \frac{0.66}{0.88} = 0.75$$

(Equivalently, one can write $C_P = \nabla / (A_M \cdot L_{WL}) = 554.4 / (24.64 \times 30.0) = 554.4 / 739.2 = 0.75$ — both methods must agree.)

**Summary of Part (a):**
- $\nabla = 554.4\ m^3$
- $\Delta = 568.3\ t$
- $C_P = 0.75$
- $A_M = 24.64\ m^2$

##### Part (b) — Solution

**Step 1 — Convert 12 knots to SI (m/s).**

$$V = 12 \times 0.5144 = 6.173\ m/s$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Froude number.**

$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}} = \frac{6.173}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 30.0}}$$

$$\sqrt{9.81 \times 30.0} = \sqrt{294.3} = 17.16\ m/s$$

$$Fr = \frac{6.173}{17.16} = 0.3598$$

**Step 3 — Apply the regression formula.**

$$C_B^{regression} = 0.78 - 0.0025 \cdot L_{WL} - 0.45 \cdot Fr$$

$$C_B^{regression} = 0.78 - 0.0025 \times 30.0 - 0.45 \times 0.3598$$

$$C_B^{regression} = 0.78 - 0.075 - 0.1619 = 0.5431$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the designer's estimate.**

| Source | $C_B$ |
|---|---|
| Designer estimate | 0.660 |
| Fleet regression | 0.543 |
| Difference | 0.117 |

**Step 5 — Comment on design implications.**

The designer's $C_B = 0.66$ is **substantially higher** (by 0.117) than the fleet regression value of 0.54. This implies:

- The proposed hull is **fuller/blunter** than what the fleet data suggests for a 12-knot vessel.
- The full form will give **better trawl stability and catch volume**, but will incur **higher frictional + wave-making resistance** at 12 knots.
- A high $C_P$ (0.75) confirms that the hull has **moderate fore-aft taper** — typical of a working trawler, not a high-speed vessel.
- **Recommendation:** If the 12-knot speed is firm, consider reducing $C_B$ to ~0.55 by sharpening the entry and run, or accept a higher installed power. If heavy trawl operation is the priority, retain the high $C_B$ and reduce the design speed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**For a proposed purse seiner:**

- $L_{WL} = 35.0\ m$
- $B = 8.5\ m$
- $T = 3.8\ m$
- $\nabla = 620\ m^3$
- $A_{WP} = 245\ m^2$

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
Calculate the Block Coefficient, the Waterplane Area Coefficient, and the corresponding mass displacement.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
If the design is later modified by adding a bulbous bow that increases $\nabla$ by 4% and $A_M$ by 2% (with $B$ and $T$ unchanged), find the new $C_B$, new $C_M$, and new $C_P$. Comment on the change in hull character.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1 — Block Coefficient $C_B$.**

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{620}{35.0 \times 8.5 \times 3.8}$$

$$C_B = \frac{620}{1130.5} = 0.5484 \approx 0.55$$

**Step 2 — Waterplane Area Coefficient $C_{WP}$.**

$$C_{WP} = \frac{A_{WP}}{L_{WL} \cdot B} = \frac{245}{35.0 \times 8.5} = \frac{245}{297.5} = 0.8235 \approx 0.82$$

**Step 3 — Mass displacement.**

$$\Delta = 1.025 \times 620 = 635.5\ t$$

**Summary of Part (a):**
- $C_B = 0.55$
- $C_{WP} = 0.82$
- $\Delta = 635.5\ t$

##### Part (b) — Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the new displaced volume.**

$$\nabla_{new} = 620 \times 1.04 = 644.8\ m^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute the new Block Coefficient.**

The bounding box is **unchanged** ($B$ and $T$ unchanged, and $L_{WL}$ at the waterline effectively unchanged for a bulbous bow addition at the design waterline).

$$C_B^{new} = \frac{\nabla_{new}}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{644.8}{1130.5} = 0.5703 \approx 0.57$$

**Step 3 — Compute the original $A_M$ (needed for the comparison).**

The original $C_M$ is not directly given, but the original $A_M$ can be found from the original $C_B$ if we assume an original $C_P$, OR more directly — since the question states $A_M$ increases by 2%, we need a reference value. We can derive it from a reasonable original $C_M$ of about 0.85 (typical purse seiner). Then:

$$A_M^{old} = 0.85 \times 8.5 \times 3.8 = 27.455\ m^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the new midship area.**

$$A_M^{new} = 27.455 \times 1.02 = 28.004\ m^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute the new Midship Coefficient.**

$$C_M^{new} = \frac{A_M^{new}}{B \cdot T} = \frac{28.004}{32.30} = 0.8670 \approx 0.87$$

**Step 6 — Compute the new Prismatic Coefficient.**

$$C_P^{new} = \frac{C_B^{new}}{C_M^{new}} = \frac{0.5703}{0.8670} = 0.6578 \approx 0.66$$

**Step 7 — Comment on the change in hull character.**

| Coefficient | Original | Modified | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_B$ | 0.55 | 0.57 | +0.02 (fuller overall) |
| $C_M$ | 0.85 | 0.87 | +0.02 (fuller midship) |
| $C_P$ | 0.647 | 0.66 | +0.013 (less taper) |

The bulbous bow has made the hull **marginally fuller** at the bow region, which:
- **Increases** displacement and buoyancy at the design draft.
- **Reduces** wave-making resistance at the design speed (bulb bows are designed to interfere destructively with the bow wave system).
- **Slightly raises** $C_P$ because the bow volume increase exceeds the midship area increase proportionally — the hull is now "prism-like" near the bow.
- The overall effect is **favorable for powering** at moderate Froude numbers (typical of purse seine operations).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Estimation of Form Coefficients**

- [x] **Block Coefficient** $C_B = \nabla / (L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T)$ — most important, range 0.50–0.72 for fishing vessels.
- [x] **Midship Coefficient** $C_M = A_M / (B \cdot T)$ — cross-section fullness, range 0.80–0.98.
- [x] **Prismatic Coefficient** $C_P = \nabla / (A_M \cdot L_{WL})$ — longitudinal taper, range 0.55–0.80.
- [x] **Waterplane Coefficient** $C_{WP} = A_{WP} / (L_{WL} \cdot B)$ — initial stability driver, range 0.65–0.85.
- [x] **The Identity** $C_P = C_B / C_M$ — **exact**, not approximate; use it for cross-checking.
- [x] **Displacement formula** $\Delta = 1.025 \cdot C_B \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T$ tonnes (seawater).
- [x] **Trawlers have HIGHER $C_B$** (0.60–0.70); **liners and seiners have LOWER $C_B$** (0.50–0.60).
- [x] **Higher $C_B$** → more displacement → better stability & cargo → higher resistance.
- [x] **Higher $C_{WP}$** → larger GMT → stiffer vessel → less roll.
- [x] **Statistical methods** for $C_B$: direct averaging, standard series, regression, parent hull modification.
- [x] **SI units only** in all KTU calculations: m, m², m³, t, m/s.
- [x] **Froude number** $Fr = V / \sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}$ — required for regression-based estimation.
- [x] **Bulbous bow** increases $C_B$ and $A_M$ but **reduces wave-making resistance** at design speed.
- [x] **Always show units** — KTU examiners deduct marks for unitless answers.
- [x] **Verify consistency**: recompute $\nabla$ via both $C_B$ and $C_P$ — they must agree.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Light Ship Weight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Light Ship Weight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Light Ship Weight (LSW)** is defined as the **complete weight of the ship in operating condition, but excluding the following items**: cargo, fuel, lubricating oil, ballast water, fresh water, boiler feed water, consumable stores, crew, their personal effects, and any other loadable items for which specific allowances are made in the **Deadweight (DW)** calculation.

Mathematically, the relationship between **Displacement (Δ)**, **Light Ship Weight (LSW)**, and **Deadweight (DW)** is expressed as:

$$\Delta = LSW + DW$$

Where:
- **Δ (Displacement)** = Total weight of water displaced by the ship = LSW + DW (in tonnes)
- **LSW** = Weight of the empty, ready-to-sail ship (tonnes)
- **DW (Deadweight)** = Variable load = Cargo + Fuel + Water + Stores + Crew

For a **fishing vessel**, the deadweight primarily consists of the **fish catch, fuel for the fishing voyage, ice, water, provisions, and fishing gear**, while the light ship weight comprises the **hull structure, propulsion machinery, fishing equipment permanently installed, accommodation, and outfit**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Estimation of LSW is a **Module 2 core competency** for fishing vessel designers. It directly feeds into the **Powering Calculation (Module 3)**, **Stability Assessment (Module 4)**, and **Cost Estimation (Module 5)**. A 10% error in LSW cascades into significant errors in the resistance, freeboard, and trim predictions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Empty Tin Can" Model

Imagine you have a **sealed tin can filled with sand, a small motor, a fishing rod, and tackle** — that's your *Light Ship Weight*. Now, suppose you pour in **fuel, water, bait, and a day's catch** — that additional weight is your *Deadweight*. The **total weight of the can plus all contents underwater equals the displacement** (because of Archimedes' principle — the buoyant force equals the displaced water weight).

In ship design, the **fishing vessel is the tin can**, the **engine, hull plates, deckhouse, winches** are part of the LSW, and the **fuel tanks, ice holds (when filled), catch, water, crew effects** are the Deadweight.

**Analogy Summary Table:**

| Component | Tin Can Analogy | Ship Equivalent | KTU Term |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|----------|
| Empty sealed vessel | The can itself with motor & rod | Hull + Machinery + Outfit | **LSW** |
| Variable contents | Fuel, bait, fish catch | Cargo, fuel, stores, water | **DW** |
| Total submerged weight | Can + all contents | Total weight the sea supports | **Δ (Displacement)** |

---

## 1.3 The Light Ship Weight Coefficient (C_LSW)

The **Light Ship Weight Coefficient** is a dimensionless coefficient used to non-dimensionalize the LSW for comparison between vessels:

$$C_{LSW} = \frac{LSW}{L \times B \times D}$$

Where:
- **L** = Length Between Perpendiculars (m)
- **B** = Breadth Moulded (m)
- **D** = Depth Moulded (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **fishing vessels**, the value of **C_LSW** typically ranges between **0.15 to 0.30**, depending on:
> - Vessel size (smaller vessels have higher C_LSW due to relatively heavier outfit)
> - Construction material (steel vs. FRP vs. wood)
> - Fishing gear type (trawlers > seiners > gill-netters)
> - Refrigeration plant presence (RSW vessels > ice-cooled vessels)
> - Crew accommodation size

**Typical C_LSW Values for Fishing Vessels:**

| Vessel Type | L (m) | C_LSW Range |
|-------------|-------|-------------|
| Small artisanal boats (wood/FRP) | 6–10 | 0.25 – 0.40 |
| Small mechanized trawlers | 10–18 | 0.20 – 0.30 |
| Medium stern trawlers | 18–28 | 0.18 – 0.25 |
| Large factory trawlers | 28–45+ | 0.15 – 0.22 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is LSW Estimation So Critical in Fishing Vessel Design?

The Light Ship Weight is the **anchor parameter** in early-stage design. Unlike cargo ships where LSW is well-predicted by classification society rules, **fishing vessels are highly customized** (one-off design with varying gear, processing equipment, refrigeration), making LSW estimation a **non-trivial art**.

A robust LSW estimate must:
1. **Predict resistance and powering** → affects engine selection and fuel tank sizing
2. **Establish initial intact stability** → GM depends on displacement, which depends on LSW
3. **Determine freeboard and load line** → IMO LLLC compliance
4. **Drive cost estimates** → steel, outfit, machinery cost
5. **Feed structural design** → still water bending moment (SWBM) depends on LSW distribution

---

## 2.2 Methodologies for LSW Estimation

There are **four principal approaches** taught in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

### **Method 1: Empirical (Cubic) Number Method**
This is the **most common preliminary design method** for fishing vessels.

$$LSW = C_{LSW} \times L \times B \times D \quad \text{(tonnes)}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
- **Why this works:** Weight scales roughly with the *volume* of the vessel envelope (L × B × D).
- **How to apply:** Look up C_LSW for the vessel type, multiply by principal dimensions.
- **Typical C_LSW for fishing vessels:** 0.18 to 0.25 (steel trawlers, 15–25 m)

### **Method 2: Deadweight Coefficient Method**
Useful when deadweight is known from cargo hold/fish hold volume.

$$LSW = \frac{DW}{C_{DW}}$$

Where **C_DW = DW / Δ** (Deadweight Coefficient) typically lies between 0.30 and 0.55 for fishing vessels.

Rearranging: 
$$\Delta = \frac{DW}{C_{DW}}$$

And since Δ = LSW + DW:
$$LSW = \Delta - DW = \frac{DW}{C_{DW}} - DW = DW \left(\frac{1 - C_{DW}}{C_{DW}}\right)$$

### **Method 3: Component-Wise (Building Block) Method**
The most **accurate** method; used in contract design.

$$LSW = W_{Hull} + W_{Machinery} + W_{Outfit} + W_{Fishing\ Equipment}$$

Each sub-component has its own empirical formula (see Section 2.3).

### **Method 4: Admiralty / Regression-Based Method**
Based on the **Admiralty Coefficient** and historical databases:

$$LSW = K \times \Delta^{2/3}$$

For fishing vessels, **K** typically ranges from 100 to 250 (depending on construction type).

---

## 2.3 Component-Wise LSW Breakdown for Fishing Vessels

### **A. Hull Steel Weight (W_H)**

$$W_H = C_H \times L \times B \times D$$

Where **C_H = Hull Steel Coefficient**:
- Small fishing vessels (10–15 m): C_H ≈ 0.08 – 0.12
- Medium trawlers (15–25 m): C_H ≈ 0.07 – 0.10
- Large trawlers (>25 m): C_H ≈ 0.06 – 0.08

> [!NOTE]
> The hull steel weight includes: shell plating, deck plating, bulkheads, stiffeners, frames, superstructure (if welded to deck), and foundations.

### **B. Machinery Weight (W_M)**

$$W_M = C_M \times L \times B \times D$$

**C_M** values:
- Outboard engines: separate installation, not in cubic formula
- Inboard diesel: 0.04 – 0.07
- With reduction gear & shaft: 0.05 – 0.08
- With RSW (Refrigerated Sea Water) plant: 0.06 – 0.10

### **C. Outfit Weight (W_O)**

$$W_O = C_O \times L \times B \times D$$

**C_O** values:
- Basic outfit (rudder, steering, piping, electrical): 0.03 – 0.05
- With accommodation, galley, sanitary: 0.04 – 0.07
- With processing plant (factory trawler): 0.06 – 0.10

### **D. Fishing Equipment Weight (W_FE)**

**Fishing equipment is fishing-vessel-specific** and is often given as a fixed allowance:

| Equipment | Approx. Weight (tonnes) |
|-----------|------------------------|
| Trawl winches (2 nos.) | 1.5 – 4.0 |
| Net drum & gantry | 1.0 – 3.0 |
| Booms, derricks, mast | 0.5 – 2.0 |
| Hydraulics power pack | 0.5 – 1.5 |
| Fish pump | 0.3 – 1.0 |
| Refrigeration plant (RSW) | 2.0 – 8.0 |
| Fish processing line | 1.0 – 5.0 |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Typical Range / Unit | Application |
|---|---------|-------------------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $LSW = C_{LSW} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D$ | C_LSW = LSW coefficient | 0.15 – 0.30 (steel fishing vessel) | **Preliminary** LSW |
| 2 | $C_{LSW} = \dfrac{LSW}{L \cdot B \cdot D}$ | Dimensionless coefficient | 0.15 – 0.30 | Comparison metric |
| 3 | $LSW = W_H + W_M + W_O + W_{FE}$ | Component sum | tonnes | **Component** LSW |
| 4 | $W_H = C_H \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D$ | Hull steel coefficient | 0.06 – 0.12 | Hull weight |
| 5 | $W_M = C_M \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D$ | Machinery coefficient | 0.04 – 0.10 | Machinery weight |
| 6 | $W_O = C_O \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D$ | Outfit coefficient | 0.03 – 0.10 | Outfit weight |
| 7 | $\Delta = LSW + DW$ | Displacement = LSW + DW | tonnes | Fundamental relation |
| 8 | $LSW = DW \cdot \dfrac{1 - C_{DW}}{C_{DW}}$ | Deadweight method | C_DW = 0.30 – 0.55 | From DW |
| 9 | $LSW = K \cdot \Delta^{2/3}$ | Admiralty regression | K = 100 – 250 | Statistical |
| 10 | $\Delta_{LSW} = LSW / \rho_{sw}$ | Volume displacement | m³ (ρ_sw = **1.025 t/m³**) | Displacement volume |
| 11 | $C_B = \dfrac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient | 0.45 – 0.65 (fishing) | Hull form check |
| 12 | $C_{DW} = \dfrac{DW}{\Delta}$ | Deadweight coefficient | 0.30 – 0.55 | Capacity metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember the seawater density: ρ_sw = 1.025 t/m³** (used in 99% of KTU numerical problems)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

1. **Concept Design (Preliminary):** Naval architects use Method 1 (Cubic) for **first-pass estimates** when only principal dimensions are known.
2. **Contract Design (Tender Stage):** Shipyards use Method 3 (Component) for **bid estimation** and steel weight commitments.
3. **Detailed Design:** Classification societies verify LSW by **inclining experiment** post-launch. Any deviation from design LSW must be reconciled.
4. **Refit / Modification:** When adding a new RSW plant or a fish processing line, the LSW increase is calculated using **Method 3** to assess impact on stability.
5. **Fishing Vessel Regulatory Compliance:** IMO Torremolinos Protocol, FAO/ILO Code of Safety for Fishermen, Indian MS Act — all require LSW + DW to verify freeboard, stability, and crew safety.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: LSW from Displacement and Deadweight Coefficient

**Starting Point (KTU Theorem):** The total displacement Δ of a fishing vessel is the sum of Light Ship Weight (LSW) and Deadweight (DW):

$$\Delta = LSW + DW$$

**Step 1:** The Deadweight Coefficient is defined as the ratio of deadweight to total displacement:

$$C_{DW} = \frac{DW}{\Delta}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange for DW:

$$DW = C_{DW} \cdot \Delta$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the displacement equation:

$$\Delta = LSW + C_{DW} \cdot \Delta$$

**Step 4:** Isolate LSW:

$$LSW = \Delta - C_{DW} \cdot \Delta$$

**Step 5:** Factor out Δ:

$$LSW = \Delta \cdot (1 - C_{DW})$$

**Step 6:** Substitute Δ = DW / C_DW (from Step 1, inverted):

$$LSW = \frac{DW}{C_{DW}} \cdot (1 - C_{DW})$$

**Step 7:** Final form — expressed purely in terms of DW and C_DW:

$$\boxed{LSW = DW \cdot \frac{(1 - C_{DW})}{C_{DW}}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** If you know how much fish, fuel, ice, and stores the vessel must carry (DW), and you have a reasonable C_DW (from sister vessels or design spiral), this single equation gives you the LSW directly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Component-Wise LSW Formula from Cubic Number

**Starting Assumption:** Each major weight group is approximately proportional to the *cubic capacity* (L × B × D) of the vessel envelope.

**Step 1:** Define the cubic number (also called the **moulded volume envelope**):

$$V_{env} = L \times B \times D \quad \text{(m}^3\text{)}$$

**Step 2:** Each weight group is written as a coefficient times the cubic number:

$$W_i = C_i \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D$$

**Step 3:** Sum the individual weight groups:

$$LSW = W_H + W_M + W_O + W_{FE}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the cubic number forms:

$$LSW = (C_H + C_M + C_O + C_{FE}) \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the total LSW coefficient:

$$C_{LSW} = C_H + C_M + C_O + C_{FE}$$

**Step 6:** Final form:

$$\boxed{LSW = C_{LSW} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The total LSW coefficient C_LSW is the sum of partial coefficients for hull, machinery, outfit, and fishing equipment. This is why **sophisticated designers** tune each C_i separately rather than relying on a single C_LSW.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship between LSW Coefficient and Block Coefficient

The **Block Coefficient (C_B)** at lightship condition relates to the LSW coefficient as follows:

**Step 1:** Define block coefficient:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla_{LSW}}{L \cdot B \cdot T_{LSW}}$$

Where ∇_LSW is the volume of displacement at lightship, and T_LSW is the mean draft at lightship.

**Step 2:** Recognize that volume of displacement = mass / density:

$$\nabla_{LSW} = \frac{LSW}{\rho_{sw}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$C_B = \frac{LSW}{\rho_{sw} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T_{LSW}}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize LSW = C_LSW × L × B × D:

$$C_B = \frac{C_{LSW} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D}{\rho_{sw} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T_{LSW}}$$

**Step 5:** Cancel L and B:

$$C_B = \frac{C_{LSW} \cdot D}{\rho_{sw} \cdot T_{LSW}}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for C_LSW:

$$\boxed{C_{LSW} = \frac{C_B \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot T_{LSW}}{D}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The lightship draft T_LSW depends on hull form (C_B) and weight distribution. This formula lets you cross-check your assumed C_LSW against the geometric C_B obtained from hull form design.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **[KTU University Exam Style Question]**
> *A 20 m fishing trawler has principal dimensions L_pp = 20.0 m, B = 6.5 m, D = 3.0 m. The deadweight comprises: 8 tonnes of fish, 3.5 tonnes of fuel, 2.0 tonnes of fresh water, 1.5 tonnes of ice, 0.8 tonnes of stores, and 0.7 tonnes of crew/effects. The deadweight coefficient C_DW = 0.45. Estimate the LSW and the displacement.*

**Solution (Step-by-step):**

**Step 1: Calculate Deadweight (DW):**

$$DW = W_{fish} + W_{fuel} + W_{water} + W_{ice} + W_{stores} + W_{crew}$$

$$DW = 8.0 + 3.5 + 2.0 + 1.5 + 0.8 + 0.7 = 16.5 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 2: Apply C_DW to find displacement Δ:**

$$\Delta = \frac{DW}{C_{DW}} = \frac{16.5}{0.45} = 36.67 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3: Find LSW using Δ = LSW + DW:**

$$LSW = \Delta - DW = 36.67 - 16.5 = 20.17 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 4: Verify using the derived formula:**

$$LSW = DW \cdot \frac{(1 - C_{DW})}{C_{DW}} = 16.5 \cdot \frac{(1 - 0.45)}{0.45} = 16.5 \cdot 1.2222 = 20.17 \text{ tonnes} \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Cross-check with cubic formula (C_LSW = 0.21 for medium trawler):**

$$LSW_{cubic} = 0.21 \times 20.0 \times 6.5 \times 3.0 = 0.21 \times 390 = 81.9 \text{ tonnes}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Mismatch Alert!** The cubic formula gives **81.9 t** while the C_DW method gives **20.17 t**. This huge discrepancy suggests an error in either the DW inventory or the assumed C_DW. In a real KTU exam, the student would recheck: either the deadweight inventory is too small, or the C_DW assumption is wrong. **Cubic formula is usually more reliable for trawlers of this size** — so the actual LSW should be closer to ~80 t, and the C_DW method needs revision.

**Step 6: Reconciliation.** Realistic LSW for a 20 m steel trawler ≈ 70–90 tonnes. The corrected deadweight should be:

$$DW_{actual} = LSW \cdot \frac{C_{DW}}{1 - C_{DW}} = 80 \cdot \frac{0.45}{0.55} = 65.5 \text{ tonnes}$$

This shows that the **stated 16.5 t DW was clearly an undersized inventory** (perhaps for one trip only, not a full design DW).

---

## 3.5 LSW Estimation — Comparison Matrix (When to Use What)

| Method | Input Required | Accuracy | Best Stage | Cost | KTU Marks Weight |
|--------|---------------|----------|------------|------|------------------|
| **Cubic Number** | L, B, D + C_LSW | ±15–20% | **Concept** | Low | 5 marks (short answer) |
| **Deadweight Coeff.** | DW + C_DW | ±10–15% | Preliminary | Low | 5 marks (numerical) |
| **Component-wise** | L, B, D + component C's | ±5–10% | **Contract** | Medium | 12–14 marks (full Q) |
| **Admiralty Regression** | Historical database | ±10% | Statistical | High | Rare in KTU |
| **Inclining Experiment** | Actual vessel (post-launch) | ±1–2% | **Verification** | Very High | Not in exam |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: LSW Estimation Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start LSW Estimation] --> B{Available Inputs?}
    B -- Principal Dimensions Only --> C[Cubic Number Method]
    B -- Deadweight Known --> D[Deadweight Coefficient Method]
    B -- Detailed Equipment List --> E[Component-Wise Method]
    
    C --> F[Apply C_LSW × L × B × D]
    D --> G[Apply DW × 1 - C_DW / C_DW]
    E --> H[Sum W_Hull + W_Mach + W_Outfit + W_FE]
    
    F --> I[Cross-Check with Sister Vessels]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    
    I --> J{Discrepancy Greater Than 15 percent?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Re-examine Inputs and Coefficients]
    J -- No --> L[Accept LSW Estimate]
    
    L --> M[Compute Displacement Delta = LSW + DW]
    M --> N[Compute Light Ship Draft T_LSW]
    N --> O[Feed into Powering and Stability Modules]
    
    K --> B
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Component-Wise LSW Decomposition (Fishing Vessel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LSW[Light Ship Weight LSW] --> WH[Hull Steel Weight W_H]
    LSW --> WM[Machinery Weight W_M]
    LSW --> WO[Outfit Weight W_O]
    LSW --> WFE[Fishing Equipment Weight W_FE]
    
    WH --> WH1[Shell Plating]
    WH --> WH2[Deck Plating]
    WH --> WH3[Bulkheads]
    WH --> WH4[Frames and Stiffeners]
    WH --> WH5[Superstructure]
    
    WM --> WM1[Main Engine]
    WM --> WM2[Auxiliary Engines]
    WM --> WM3[Reduction Gear]
    WM --> WM4[Shaft and Propeller]
    WM --> WM5[Refrigeration Plant]
    
    WO --> WO1[Rudder and Steering]
    WO --> WO2[Piping System]
    WO --> WO3[Electrical System]
    WO --> WO4[Accommodation]
    WO --> WO5[Galley and Sanitary]
    
    WFE --> WFE1[Trawl Winches]
    WFE --> WFE2[Net Drum]
    WFE --> WFE3[Booms and Gantry]
    WFE --> WFE4[Hydraulic Power Pack]
    WFE --> WFE5[Fish Pump]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: LSW Impact on Downstream Design

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant D as Designer
    participant LSW as LSW Estimate
    participant P as Powering Module
    participant S as Stability Module
    participant FL as Freeboard Module
    participant C as Cost Module
    
    D->>LSW: Compute LSW via Cubic or Component
    LSW-->>D: LSW plus DW equals Displacement Delta
    D->>P: Feed Delta to Resistance Calculation
    D->>S: Feed Delta and KG to GM Computation
    D->>FL: Feed Delta to Draft T Computation
    D->>C: Feed Component Weights to Cost Roll-up
    P-->>D: Effective Power PE
    S-->>D: Metacentric Height GM
    FL-->>D: Freeboard F compliance check
    C-->>D: Newbuilding Cost CNB
    D->>D: Iterate if any check fails
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### **Q1. Define Light Ship Weight (LSW) of a fishing vessel. List four items included in LSW and four items excluded from LSW.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Light Ship Weight is the complete weight of the ship in operating condition, but excluding all variable loads such as cargo (fish), fuel, lubricating oil, fresh water, ballast, stores, and crew effects.

**Items INCLUDED in LSW (1 mark):**
1. Hull structure (shell, deck, bulkheads, frames)
2. Main engine, auxiliary engines, reduction gear, shaft
3. Installed fishing equipment (winches, net drum, booms)
4. Accommodation, galley, sanitary fittings, outfit (piping, electrical, steering)

**Items EXCLUDED from LSW (1 mark):**
1. Fish catch (cargo)
2. Fuel oil and lubricating oil
3. Fresh water and boiler feed water
4. Consumable stores, crew, and personal effects

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### **Q2. Write the cubic number formula for estimating Light Ship Weight. State the typical range of the LSW coefficient (C_LSW) for steel fishing trawlers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula (2 marks):**
$$LSW = C_{LSW} \times L \times B \times D \quad \text{(tonnes)}$$

Where L, B, D are in metres.

**Typical C_LSW range (1 mark):** For steel fishing trawlers, **C_LSW typically lies between 0.15 and 0.30**, with values around **0.20–0.25** being common for medium-sized trawlers (15–25 m).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - ESE Model Paper]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Component-Wise Method**

> A fishing trawler has principal dimensions: L_pp = 24.0 m, B = 7.2 m, D = 3.6 m. Using the component-wise method, estimate the **Light Ship Weight (LSW)** with the following sub-systems:

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**
Compute the **hull steel weight** and the **machinery weight** using the appropriate coefficients.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- L_pp = 24.0 m, B = 7.2 m, D = 3.6 m
- Cubic Number = L × B × D = 24.0 × 7.2 × 3.6 = **622.08 m³**

**Hull Steel Weight (4 marks):**

For a medium trawler of 24 m, take C_H = 0.085 (mid-range 0.07–0.10):

$$W_H = C_H \times L \times B \times D = 0.085 \times 622.08$$

$$\boxed{W_H = 52.88 \text{ tonnes}}$$

**Machinery Weight (3 marks):**

For a diesel-powered trawler with RSW plant, take C_M = 0.075:

$$W_M = C_M \times L \times B \times D = 0.075 \times 622.08$$

$$\boxed{W_M = 46.66 \text{ tonnes}}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**
Compute the **outfit weight** and **fishing equipment weight** (assumed as 6.0 tonnes for trawl winches, net drum, and gantry). Hence, find the **total LSW** and the **displacement at full deadweight of 28 tonnes**.

**Model Solution:**

**Outfit Weight (3 marks):**

For a medium trawler with full accommodation, take C_O = 0.055:

$$W_O = C_O \times L \times B \times D = 0.055 \times 622.08$$

$$\boxed{W_O = 34.21 \text{ tonnes}}$$

**Fishing Equipment Weight (1 mark):**

$$W_{FE} = 6.0 \text{ tonnes (given)}$$

**Total LSW (2 marks):**

$$LSW = W_H + W_M + W_O + W_{FE}$$

$$LSW = 52.88 + 46.66 + 34.21 + 6.0$$

$$\boxed{LSW = 139.75 \text{ tonnes}}$$

**Displacement at full deadweight (1 mark):**

$$\Delta = LSW + DW = 139.75 + 28.0$$

$$\boxed{\Delta = 167.75 \text{ tonnes}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Deadweight Coefficient Method (Alternative Choice)**

> A 19 m fishing vessel has the following **deadweight inventory** for a typical fishing voyage: fish = 12 t, fuel = 4.5 t, lubricating oil = 0.5 t, fresh water = 1.8 t, ice = 3.2 t, stores = 0.8 t, crew effects = 0.4 t. The deadweight coefficient is **C_DW = 0.42**. Principal dimensions: L = 19.0 m, B = 6.0 m, D = 3.2 m.

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**
Calculate the **total deadweight (DW)** and the **displacement (Δ)** at full load. Hence, find the **Light Ship Weight (LSW)**.

**Model Solution:**

**Total Deadweight (3 marks):**

$$DW = 12.0 + 4.5 + 0.5 + 1.8 + 3.2 + 0.8 + 0.4$$

$$DW = 23.2 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Displacement (2 marks):**

$$\Delta = \frac{DW}{C_{DW}} = \frac{23.2}{0.42}$$

$$\Delta = 55.24 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Light Ship Weight (2 marks):**

$$LSW = \Delta - DW = 55.24 - 23.2$$

$$\boxed{LSW = 32.04 \text{ tonnes}}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**
Verify the LSW using the **cubic number method** with C_LSW = 0.20. Comment on the deviation, and compute the **lightship draft T_LSW** assuming a block coefficient C_B = 0.55 and a waterplane coefficient C_WP = 0.78.

**Model Solution:**

**Verification using Cubic Number (3 marks):**

$$LSW_{cubic} = C_{LSW} \times L \times B \times D = 0.20 \times 19.0 \times 6.0 \times 3.2$$

$$LSW_{cubic} = 0.20 \times 364.8 = 72.96 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Deviation Analysis (2 marks):**

$$\text{Deviation} = \frac{|72.96 - 32.04|}{72.96} \times 100\% = 56.1\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** The 56% deviation is **unacceptable** for a preliminary design. This indicates that the assumed **C_DW = 0.42 is too low** for this vessel size. A 19 m fishing trawler with these characteristics should have C_DW ≈ 0.25–0.30. The deadweight inventory is also **underestimated** for a full design voyage.

**Lightship Draft Calculation (2 marks):**

Volume of displacement at lightship:

$$\nabla_{LSW} = \frac{LSW}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{32.04}{1.025} = 31.26 \text{ m}^3$$

Lightship draft from block coefficient:

$$T_{LSW} = \frac{\nabla_{LSW}}{L \times B \times C_B} = \frac{31.26}{19.0 \times 6.0 \times 0.55}$$

$$\boxed{T_{LSW} = 0.499 \text{ m} \approx 0.50 \text{ m}}$$

This is unrealistically low — confirming the LSW is significantly underestimated. A realistic T_LSW for a 19 m trawler is **1.0 – 1.4 m**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting ρ_sw = 1.025 t/m³** when converting LSW to displacement volume. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 2. **Using C_LSW values inappropriate for the vessel size** (e.g., using trawler values for a gill-netter). (-2 marks)
> 3. **Not cross-checking** between the cubic number method and the deadweight coefficient method. (-2 marks)
> 4. **Mixing units** (using cm instead of m, or long tons instead of metric tonnes). (-2 marks)
> 5. **Forgetting to add fishing equipment weight** as a separate term in the component method. (-3 marks)
> 6. **Not stating the range of coefficients** used (examiners want justification). (-1 mark)
> 7. **Confusing LSW with displacement or deadweight** — a classic conceptual error. (-3 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Estimation of Light Ship Weight**

- ✅ **LSW = Ship's empty weight** in ready-to-sail condition; **excludes** fuel, water, fish, stores, crew.
- ✅ **Δ = LSW + DW** — the master equation linking all three weights.
- ✅ **Cubic number method** uses **LSW = C_LSW × L × B × D** — fastest for concept design.
- ✅ **C_LSW = 0.15–0.30** for steel fishing vessels; **0.25–0.40** for small wood/FRP boats.
- ✅ **Component method** is **4-part**: W_Hull + W_Machinery + W_Outfit + W_Fishing Equipment.
- ✅ **C_H = 0.06–0.12**, **C_M = 0.04–0.10**, **C_O = 0.03–0.10** (coefficient ranges must be justified).
- ✅ **C_DW = DW / Δ**; typical range **0.30–0.55** for fishing vessels.
- ✅ **Cross-check** between methods; deviations > 15% demand input review.
- ✅ **ρ_sw = 1.025 t/m³** is the **standard seawater density** for displacement volume calculations.
- ✅ **Fishing equipment** (winches, gantry, RSW plant, processing line) is **fishing-vessel-specific** and must be added separately.
- ✅ **Inclining experiment** is the **gold standard** for LSW verification (post-launch, ±1–2% accuracy).
- ✅ **LSW errors cascade** into resistance, stability, freeboard, and cost errors — be conservative.
- ✅ **Smaller vessels have higher C_LSW** because of proportionally heavier outfit and equipment.
- ✅ **Refrigerated and factory trawlers** have C_LSW shifted to the upper end of the range.
- ✅ **In KTU numericals**, always state the **assumed coefficients and their justification**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Dead Weight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Dead Weight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deadweight (DW or DWT)** of a fishing vessel is defined as the **total variable weight** that the vessel must carry at any given draft to perform its intended operational function. It is the difference between the **displacement at the load waterline (Δ_L)** and the **lightship weight (W_LS)**.

Mathematically, the canonical relationship is expressed as:

$$
\Delta_{L} \;=\; W_{LS} \;+\; DW
$$

For a **fishing vessel**, the deadweight is **NOT a fixed value** — it fluctuates continuously during the voyage as the catch is loaded, fuel is consumed, water is used, and ice melts. Designers must therefore estimate deadweight for **two extreme conditions**:

| Condition | Description | Use in Design |
|---|---|---|
| **Loaded Departure Condition** | Maximum fuel + water + provisions + full catch hold | Worst-case displacement, freeboard check |
| **Arrival / Light Condition** | Minimal consumables, empty or partial catch hold | Lightest displacement, stability check |

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (The Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are a **fishmonger with a delivery auto-rickshaw**. The empty auto (its chassis, engine, body) is the *lightship*. What you pile onto it — **fish boxes, ice blocks, fuel, drinking water, your lunch, the weighing scale, and the day's catch** — that is the *deadweight*. 

If you pile on too much, the auto sinks into potholes (draft increases, freeboard reduces, stability suffers). If you under-load it, the auto bounces on bad roads (slams, pitch motion, prop racing). The **deadweight estimation is exactly this "what does my boat have to carry" calculation** — done rigorously using physics and empirical data from fleet operations.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Examination

- **Displacement (Δ)**: Total weight of water displaced by the hull, expressed in **tonnes (t)**. Note: 1 tonne = 1000 kg.
- **Lightship Weight (W_LS)**: Weight of the empty, fully-equipped vessel (hull, machinery, outfit, permanent ballast) in **tonnes (t)**.
- **Deadweight (DW)**: Sum of all variable loads, in **tonnes (t)**.
- **Deadweight Ratio (DWR)**: $DWR = \dfrac{DW}{\Delta}$ — typically **0.45 to 0.65** for fishing vessels.
- **Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC)**: **190–220 g/kWh** for modern marine diesel engines.
- **Fish (in ice) density (ρ_fish)**: approximately **0.90 to 1.00 t/m³**.
- **Sea water density (ρ_sw)**: **1.025 t/m³**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Memory Anchor**: Always remember the three-tier hierarchy — **Lightship → + Deadweight → = Displacement**. Examiners frequently test this simple relationship as a 3-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component-Wise Breakdown of Deadweight

A fishing vessel's deadweight is composed of the following **eleven (11) major categories**. Each is estimated using distinct empirical, statistical, or analytical methods.

### 2.1.1 Category A — Cargo / Catch (W_CARGO)

This is the **principal and most variable** component. It depends on:
- Catch hold geometric volume $V_h$ (in m³)
- Volumetric filling factor $\phi$ (typically **0.70 to 0.85**)
- Fish storage density $\rho_{fish}$ (tonnes per m³)

$$
W_{CARGO} \;=\; V_h \cdot \phi \cdot \rho_{fish}
$$

### 2.1.2 Category B — Fuel Oil (W_FO)

Fuel must be estimated for **two consumers**: the **Main Engine (ME)** and the **Auxiliary Engine(s) (AE)**.

For a voyage of duration $t_v$ hours:

$$
W_{FO,ME} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{ME} \cdot SFOC_{ME} \cdot t_v}{10^6}
$$

$$
W_{FO,AE} \;=\; \sum_{i} \dfrac{P_{AE,i} \cdot L_F \cdot SFOC_{AE,i} \cdot t_v}{10^6}
$$

where $L_F$ is the **load factor** of the auxiliary engine (typically 0.40 to 0.65 for fishing vessels, since winches, refrigeration, and processing machinery drive intermittent loads).

$$
W_{FO,total} \;=\; W_{FO,ME} + W_{FO,AE}
$$

### 2.1.3 Category C — Lubricating Oil (W_LO)

Empirical rule for diesel-powered fishing vessels:

$$
W_{LO} \;\approx\; (0.04 \text{ to } 0.06) \cdot W_{FO,total}
$$

### 2.1.4 Category D — Fresh Water (W_FW)

$$
W_{FW} \;=\; n_{crew} \cdot q_{FW} \cdot t_v
$$

where $q_{FW} \approx \mathbf{0.15 \text{ to } 0.20 \text{ t/person/day}}$ for fresh drinking + cooking water, and $t_v$ is in days.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fresh water for **fish hold washing, boiler make-up, and engine cooling (if freshwater-cooled)** is **NOT included here** — those are tank capacities fixed at the design stage, and often counted under *stores* or considered as part of the *lightship outfit*.

### 2.1.5 Category E — Provisions (W_PROV)

$$
W_{PROV} \;=\; n_{crew} \cdot q_{PROV} \cdot t_v
$$

where $q_{PROV} \approx \mathbf{0.005 \text{ to } 0.010 \text{ t/person/day}}$ (food, dry stores).

### 2.1.6 Category F — Crew & Personal Effects (W_CREW)

$$
W_{CREW} \;=\; n_{crew} \cdot m_{per\,person}
$$

where $m_{per\,person} \approx \mathbf{0.075 \text{ t}}$ (including luggage and personal gear).

### 2.1.7 Category G — Stores, Spares, and Miscellaneous (W_ST)

$$
W_{ST} \;\approx\; (0.01 \text{ to } 0.02) \cdot W_{FO,total}
$$

This includes engine spares, paints, ropes, safety gear, navigation consumables.

### 2.1.8 Category H — Fishing Gear (W_FG)

For trawlers, purse seiners, or long-liners, the gear weight depends on gear type:

| Vessel Type | Typical Gear Weight |
|---|---|
| Stern Trawler (small, 18–24 m) | **2.0 – 4.0 t** |
| Side Trawler | **3.0 – 5.0 t** |
| Purse Seiner (24–35 m) | **8.0 – 15.0 t** (incl. net, lines, rings) |
| Long-line / Tuna vessel | **3.0 – 7.0 t** |

### 2.1.9 Category I — Ice / Refrigeration Mass (W_ICE)

Used when catch is preserved with crushed ice or RSW (Refrigerated Sea Water):

$$
W_{ICE} \;=\; m_{catch} \cdot R_{ice}
$$

where $R_{ice} \approx \mathbf{0.25 \text{ to } 0.50}$ (kg of ice per kg of fish). For **RSW systems**, the mass of refrigerant in coils is included in the lightship.

### 2.1.10 Category J — Reserve / Sea Margin (W_MARGIN)

A design safety margin (typically **3% to 5%** of the total deadweight) to absorb unforeseen loads, water in tanks, sludge in fuel tanks, etc.

$$
W_{MARGIN} \;=\; k_{m} \cdot \sum_{i=A}^{I} W_i \quad\quad k_m \approx 0.03 \text{ to } 0.05
$$

### 2.1.11 Category K — Fishing Equipment & Catch Handling (W_EQ)

Includes brailers, conveyor belts, sorting equipment, processing tables. For small vessels, often lumped into $W_{FG}$ or $W_{ST}$.

## 2.2 Consolidated KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\Delta_L$ | Load displacement | $\Delta_L = W_{LS} + DW$ | t |
| $W_{CARGO}$ | Catch weight | $V_h \cdot \phi \cdot \rho_{fish}$ | t |
| $W_{FO,ME}$ | Main engine fuel | $P_{ME} \cdot SFOC_{ME} \cdot t_v / 10^6$ | t |
| $W_{FO,AE}$ | Auxiliary engine fuel | $P_{AE} \cdot L_F \cdot SFOC_{AE} \cdot t_v / 10^6$ | t |
| $W_{LO}$ | Lubricating oil | $(0.04 - 0.06) \cdot W_{FO,total}$ | t |
| $W_{FW}$ | Fresh water | $n_{crew} \cdot q_{FW} \cdot t_v$ | t |
| $W_{PROV}$ | Provisions | $n_{crew} \cdot q_{PROV} \cdot t_v$ | t |
| $W_{CREW}$ | Crew + effects | $n_{crew} \cdot 0.075$ | t |
| $W_{ST}$ | Stores | $(0.01 - 0.02) \cdot W_{FO,total}$ | t |
| $W_{FG}$ | Fishing gear | Vessel-type empirical table | t |
| $W_{ICE}$ | Ice for catch | $m_{catch} \cdot R_{ice}$ | t |
| $W_{MARGIN}$ | Sea margin | $0.03 - 0.05 \cdot \sum W_i$ | t |
| $\rho_{fish}$ | Fish density (iced) | $0.90 - 1.00$ | t/m³ |
| $\rho_{sw}$ | Sea water density | $1.025$ | t/m³ |
| $DWR$ | Deadweight ratio | $DW / \Delta_L$ | — |
| $SFOC$ | Specific fuel oil cons. | $190 - 220$ | g/kWh |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility**: Deadweight estimation feeds directly into **powering**, **propulsion**, **stability (GM, GZ curves)**, **trim**, and **freeboard calculation**. A 5% error in deadweight propagates into a 3–4% error in required displacement, which can render the vessel non-compliant with **IMO Load Line** regulations.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Voyage-Based Fuel Oil Equation

Let the vessel operate for a continuous voyage of duration $t_v$ hours.

**Step 1 — Mechanical work done by the main engine:**

The mechanical energy delivered by the main engine over the voyage equals shaft power times time:

$$
E_{ME} \;=\; P_{ME} \cdot t_v \quad \quad [\text{kWh}]
$$

**Step 2 — Fuel mass required:**

The specific fuel oil consumption $SFOC$ is defined as the **mass of fuel (in grams) consumed per unit of energy (in kWh)** delivered at the engine output shaft.

$$
SFOC_{ME} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{FO,ME}}{E_{ME}} \quad \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{kWh}}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Rearranging for fuel mass:**

$$
m_{FO,ME} \;=\; SFOC_{ME} \cdot P_{ME} \cdot t_v \quad \quad [\text{g}]
$$

**Step 4 — Converting grams to tonnes:**

Since 1 tonne = $10^6$ grams:

$$
W_{FO,ME} \;=\; \dfrac{SFOC_{ME} \cdot P_{ME} \cdot t_v}{10^6} \quad \quad [\text{t}]
$$

**Step 5 — Auxiliary engine contribution:**

The auxiliary engine does not run at full load continuously. The **load factor** $L_F$ scales down the rated power:

$$
W_{FO,AE} \;=\; \dfrac{SFOC_{AE} \cdot P_{AE} \cdot L_F \cdot t_v}{10^6} \quad \quad [\text{t}]
$$

**Step 6 — Total fuel oil:**

$$
W_{FO,total} \;=\; W_{FO,ME} + W_{FO,AE}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Catch Hold Capacity

**Step 1 — Geometric volume:**

For a rectangular or cylindrical catch hold of length $l_h$, breadth $b_h$, and height $h_h$:

$$
V_h \;=\; l_h \cdot b_h \cdot h_h \quad \quad [\text{m}^3]
$$

**Step 2 — Effective fish-storing volume:**

Real catches do not perfectly fill the geometric volume due to void spaces between fish, melting ice water, and structural obstructions (beams, gutters, ice chutes). The **volumetric filling factor** $\phi$ accounts for this:

$$
V_{eff} \;=\; \phi \cdot V_h \quad \quad [\text{m}^3]
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of catch:**

The fish (preserved in ice or in RSW) has an effective density $\rho_{fish}$:

$$
W_{CARGO} \;=\; V_{eff} \cdot \rho_{fish} \;=\; \phi \cdot V_h \cdot \rho_{fish} \quad \quad [\text{t}]
$$

## 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example (Board-Exam Style, 14-Mark Question)

> **Problem Statement (KTU Model Question):**
> 
> A 24 m stern trawler is to be designed for a **12-day fishing voyage** in the Arabian Sea. The vessel has the following particulars:
> 
> - Main Engine: $P_{ME} = 735$ kW, $SFOC_{ME} = 198$ g/kWh
> - Auxiliary Engine: $P_{AE} = 120$ kW, $SFOC_{AE} = 215$ g/kWh, load factor $L_F = 0.55$
> - Catch hold volume: $V_h = 75$ m³, filling factor $\phi = 0.82$, fish density $\rho_{fish} = 0.95$ t/m³
> - Crew: $n_{crew} = 14$ persons
> - Fresh water: $q_{FW} = 0.18$ t/person/day
> - Provisions: $q_{PROV} = 0.008$ t/person/day
> - Crew & effects: $m_{per\,person} = 0.075$ t
> - Lubricating oil coefficient: $k_{LO} = 0.05$
> - Stores coefficient: $k_{ST} = 0.015$
> - Fishing gear (stern trawler): $W_{FG} = 3.2$ t (assumed)
> - Ice ratio: $R_{ice} = 0.30$
> - Sea margin: $k_m = 0.04$
> 
> **Estimate the total deadweight at the loaded departure condition.**

### Solution — Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Voyage duration in hours:**

$$
t_v \;=\; 12 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ h/day} \;=\; 288 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 2 — Main engine fuel oil:**

$$
W_{FO,ME} \;=\; \dfrac{198 \times 735 \times 288}{10^6}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{FO,ME} &= \dfrac{198 \times 735}{10^6} \times 288 \\
&= \dfrac{145530}{10^6} \times 288 \\
&= 0.14553 \times 288 \\
&= 41.913 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Auxiliary engine fuel oil:**

$$
W_{FO,AE} \;=\; \dfrac{215 \times 120 \times 0.55 \times 288}{10^6}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{FO,AE} &= \dfrac{215 \times 120 \times 0.55}{10^6} \times 288 \\
&= \dfrac{14190}{10^6} \times 288 \\
&= 0.01419 \times 288 \\
&= 4.087 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total fuel oil:**

$$
W_{FO,total} \;=\; 41.913 + 4.087 \;=\; 46.000 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 5 — Lubricating oil:**

$$
W_{LO} \;=\; 0.05 \times 46.000 \;=\; 2.300 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 6 — Fresh water:**

$$
W_{FW} \;=\; 14 \times 0.18 \times 12 \;=\; 30.240 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 7 — Provisions:**

$$
W_{PROV} \;=\; 14 \times 0.008 \times 12 \;=\; 1.344 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 8 — Crew and effects:**

$$
W_{CREW} \;=\; 14 \times 0.075 \;=\; 1.050 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 9 — Stores and spares:**

$$
W_{ST} \;=\; 0.015 \times 46.000 \;=\; 0.690 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 10 — Fishing gear:**

$$
W_{FG} \;=\; 3.200 \text{ t} \quad \text{(assumed for stern trawler)}
$$

**Step 11 — Maximum cargo (catch) weight:**

$$
W_{CARGO} \;=\; \phi \cdot V_h \cdot \rho_{fish} \;=\; 0.82 \times 75 \times 0.95
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{CARGO} &= 0.82 \times 75 \times 0.95 \\
&= 61.50 \times 0.95 \\
&= 58.425 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 12 — Ice for catch preservation:**

$$
W_{ICE} \;=\; 0.30 \times 58.425 \;=\; 17.528 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 13 — Subtotal before sea margin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DW_{sub} &= 46.000 + 2.300 + 30.240 + 1.344 + 1.050 \\
&\quad + 0.690 + 3.200 + 58.425 + 17.528 \\
&= 160.777 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 14 — Sea margin:**

$$
W_{MARGIN} \;=\; 0.04 \times 160.777 \;=\; 6.431 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 15 — Total Deadweight:**

$$
\boxed{DW_{total} \;=\; 160.777 + 6.431 \;\approx\; 167.21 \text{ t}}
$$

### 3.4 Component Distribution Matrix (for the Above Example)

| Component | Symbol | Value (t) | % of Total DW |
|---|---|---|---|
| Main Engine Fuel | $W_{FO,ME}$ | 41.91 | 25.06 |
| Aux Engine Fuel | $W_{FO,AE}$ | 4.09 | 2.44 |
| Lubricating Oil | $W_{LO}$ | 2.30 | 1.38 |
| Fresh Water | $W_{FW}$ | 30.24 | 18.09 |
| Provisions | $W_{PROV}$ | 1.34 | 0.80 |
| Crew & Effects | $W_{CREW}$ | 1.05 | 0.63 |
| Stores | $W_{ST}$ | 0.69 | 0.41 |
| Fishing Gear | $W_{FG}$ | 3.20 | 1.91 |
| Catch (Cargo) | $W_{CARGO}$ | 58.43 | 34.95 |
| Ice | $W_{ICE}$ | 17.53 | 10.49 |
| Sea Margin | $W_{MARGIN}$ | 6.43 | 3.85 |
| **TOTAL** | $DW_{total}$ | **167.21** | **100.00** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation**: For a typical fishing vessel, the **catch (cargo) and ice together account for ~45%** of the deadweight, while **fuel and water account for ~45%**. The remaining 10% is split among crew, stores, gear, and margin. This distribution is critical when designing tank capacities and hull form.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Deadweight Component Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the hierarchical decomposition of fishing vessel deadweight, the data inputs to each sub-block, and the operational flow from ship particulars to final $DW$ value.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Ship Particulars] --> A1[Vessel Type, LOA, B, D, Crew, Voyage t_v]
    A1 --> B0[Deadweight Estimation]
    B0 --> C0{Category 1: Fuel Oil}
    C0 --> C1[Main Engine: P_ME, SFOC_ME, t_v]
    C0 --> C2[Aux Engine: P_AE, L_F, SFOC_AE, t_v]
    C1 --> D1[W_FO_ME in tonnes]
    C2 --> D2[W_FO_AE in tonnes]
    D1 --> D3[W_FO_total = W_FO_ME + W_FO_AE]
    D2 --> D3
    B0 --> E0{Category 2: Lub Oil}
    D3 --> E1[k_LO equals 0.04 to 0.06]
    E1 --> E2[W_LO = k_LO x W_FO_total]
    B0 --> F0{Category 3: Fresh Water}
    F0 --> F1[n_crew, q_FW, t_v]
    F1 --> F2[W_FW = n_crew x q_FW x t_v]
    B0 --> G0{Category 4: Provisions}
    G0 --> G1[n_crew, q_PROV, t_v]
    G1 --> G2[W_PROV = n_crew x q_PROV x t_v]
    B0 --> H0{Category 5: Crew Effects}
    H0 --> H1[n_crew, m_per_person]
    H1 --> H2[W_CREW = n_crew x 0.075 t]
    B0 --> I0{Category 6: Stores}
    D3 --> I1[k_ST equals 0.01 to 0.02]
    I1 --> I2[W_ST = k_ST x W_FO_total]
    B0 --> J0{Category 7: Fishing Gear}
    J0 --> J1[Vessel Type Table Lookup]
    J1 --> J2[W_FG from empirical table]
    B0 --> K0{Category 8: Catch Cargo}
    K0 --> K1[V_h, phi, rho_fish]
    K1 --> K2[W_CARGO = V_h x phi x rho_fish]
    B0 --> L0{Category 9: Ice Mass}
    K2 --> L1[R_ice ratio, m_catch]
    L1 --> L2[W_ICE = m_catch x R_ice]
    B0 --> M0{Category 10: Sea Margin}
    M0 --> M1[k_m equals 0.03 to 0.05]
    M1 --> M2[W_MARGIN = k_m x sum of all W_i]
    D3 --> N0[Aggregator Node]
    E2 --> N0
    F2 --> N0
    G2 --> N0
    H2 --> N0
    I2 --> N0
    J2 --> N0
    K2 --> N0
    L2 --> N0
    M2 --> N0
    N0 --> O0[TOTAL DEADWEIGHT DW]
    O0 --> P0[Input to Displacement: Delta = W_LS + DW]
    O0 --> Q0[Input to Stability: GM, GZ]
    O0 --> R0[Input to Trim and Freeboard]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Departure to Arrival Deadweight Variation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Departure: t = 0] --> S1[Fuel full, Water full, Hold empty]
    S1 --> S2[Steam to Fishing Grounds, t = T_1]
    S2 --> S3[Fishing Operations, t = T_2]
    S3 --> S4[Hold fills with catch and ice]
    S4 --> S5[Return Voyage, t = T_3]
    S5 --> S6[Arrival: Fuel low, Water low, Hold full]
    S6 --> S7[Unloading Catch]
    S7 --> S8[Hold empty, Bunkers low]
    S8 --> S0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation**: The "Departure" node represents the **maximum fuel/water + empty hold** state, while the "Arrival with Full Catch" node represents the **minimum fuel/water + maximum cargo** state. Designers must verify **both extreme conditions** against IMO Load Line and stability criteria. The **displacement itself remains nearly constant** in a free-floating vessel (Archimedes' principle), but the **distribution of weight** shifts, causing **trim and draft changes**.
