## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PENST754]**
Define a fish hold. List any four essential design requirements of a modern fish hold used in a fishing trawler.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A fish hold is a watertight, insulated, refrigerated compartment on a fishing vessel used to store and preserve the catch during voyage.
The four essential design requirements are:
1. **Adequate Insulation** to maintain a temperature of 0 °C to +2 °C and minimise heat ingress.
2. **Efficient Drainage** with a sloped floor (≥ 1 in 60) and a bilge pump to remove melt-water and fish fluids.
3. **Food-Grade Lining** (e.g., stainless steel 316 or GRP) to ensure hygiene and easy cleaning.
4. **Structural Reinforcement** integrated with hull frames to withstand hydrostatic and dynamic sea loads.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PENST754]**
What is the "stowage factor" of a fish hold? State its typical range for whole fish stored in ice.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The stowage factor (SF) is defined as the volume of fish hold space occupied by **one tonne** of catch when properly stowed with ice. It accounts for the bulk density of fish including voids, ice layers, and packing inefficiency.
**Typical range: 1.4 to 1.8 m³/tonne** for whole fish stored in ice.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **forget to convert units** (e.g., tonnes → kg) and lose 2–3 marks instantly.
> - The **stowage factor is often confused with density** — remember: SF includes voids and ice; density is only the fish itself.
> - Always state the **assumed packing efficiency** in volume calculations, or you will be marked down for vagueness.
> - When asked to "design" a fish hold, examiners expect **insulation thickness + U-value + drain slope + hatch sizing** — not just volume.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [Module 2, PENST754 — KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]

**(a)** A 28 m fishing trawler has a design catch capacity of 35 tonnes of demersal fish. The stowage factor for whole fish in ice is 1.55 m³/tonne. The fish hold is divided into three compartments of equal volume. Each compartment has a rectangular plan of 4.0 m × 3.0 m. Calculate:
(i) The total fish hold volume required.
(ii) The required depth of each fish hold compartment.
(iii) The cross-sectional area of the drainage pipe if the design discharge velocity is 1.5 m/s and the melt-water flow rate is 0.0006 m³/s. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total fish hold volume:**

$$
V_{hold} = W_{catch} \times SF = 35 \times 1.55 = 54.25 \text{ m}^3
$$

**(ii) Depth of each compartment:**

Since three equal compartments, volume per compartment:

$$
V_{comp} = \dfrac{54.25}{3} = 18.083 \text{ m}^3
$$

Each compartment has plan area = $4.0 \times 3.0 = 12.0$ m². Therefore, depth:

$$
d = \dfrac{V_{comp}}{A_{plan}} = \dfrac{18.083}{12.0} = 1.507 \text{ m} \approx 1.51 \text{ m}
$$

(Add a 0.1 m freeboard for ice topping → design depth = **1.6 m**.)

**(iii) Drain pipe cross-sectional area:**

Given discharge $Q = 0.0006$ m³/s, velocity $v = 1.5$ m/s:

$$
A = \dfrac{Q}{v} = \dfrac{0.0006}{1.5} = 0.0004 \text{ m}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

Corresponding diameter:

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.0004}{\pi}}
$$

$$
D = \sqrt{5.093 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.02257 \text{ m} = 22.57 \text{ mm}
$$

Standard pipe size selected = **25 mm NB** GI pipe.

---

**(b)** With the above trawler, the engineer specifies a fish hold wall composed of: 10 mm steel plate ($k = 50$ W/m·K), 75 mm PUF insulation ($k = 0.024$ W/m·K), and 3 mm SS-316 lining ($k = 16$ W/m·K). The internal and external surface coefficients are 9 and 23 W/m²K, respectively. The hold temperature is +1 °C, and the engine room side is +38 °C.
Calculate:
(i) The overall U-value of the wall.
(ii) The total heat ingress through 180 m² of wall area.
(iii) Comment on whether this U-value meets the modern standard of U ≤ 0.4 W/m²K. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Overall U-value:**

$$
R_{th} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{t_{steel}}{k_{steel}} + \dfrac{t_{PUF}}{k_{PUF}} + \dfrac{t_{SS}}{k_{SS}} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}
$$

Computing each term:

$$
\dfrac{1}{h_i} = \dfrac{1}{9} = 0.1111 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
\dfrac{t_{steel}}{k_{steel}} = \dfrac{0.010}{50} = 0.0002 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
\dfrac{t_{PUF}}{k_{PUF}} = \dfrac{0.075}{0.024} = 3.1250 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
\dfrac{t_{SS}}{k_{SS}} = \dfrac{0.003}{16} = 0.0001875 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{h_o} = \dfrac{1}{23} = 0.0435 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

Summing:

$$
R_{th} = 0.1111 + 0.0002 + 3.1250 + 0.0001875 + 0.0435 = 3.2800 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
U = \dfrac{1}{R_{th}} = \dfrac{1}{3.2800} = 0.3049 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**(ii) Heat ingress:**

$$
\Delta T = 38 - 1 = 37 \text{ K}
$$

$$
Q = U \times A \times \Delta T = 0.3049 \times 180 \times 37
$$

$$
Q = 0.3049 \times 6660 = 2030.6 \text{ W} \approx 2.03 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iii) Comment:**
The calculated U-value is **0.3049 W/m²K**, which is **less than 0.40 W/m²K**, hence the wall **meets** the modern standard for refrigerated fish holds. The 75 mm PUF insulation is therefore adequate.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain, with a neat sketch or block diagram, the layered construction of a typical modern insulated fish hold. State the function of each layer. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer Outline:**

The fish hold wall is built up in five layers (from outside to inside):
1. **Outer hull plate (8–12 mm steel):** Provides structural strength and watertightness.
2. **Vapour barrier (0.2 mm polyethylene / aluminium foil):** Prevents water vapour from migrating into the insulation and condensing (which would degrade thermal performance and cause corrosion).
3. **Insulation layer (60–100 mm PUF / rock wool):** The principal thermal barrier. Reduces heat ingress from engine room / sea to the hold.
4. **Inner lining (3–4 mm SS-316 or GRP):** Food-grade, smooth, easy to clean, corrosion-resistant, and forms the actual contact surface with the fish.
5. **Optional air gap / framing:** Allows condensate drainage and supports the lining.

Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes for visual clarity.

---

**(b)** A freezer trawler has a fish hold of 90 m³ total volume. The required catch capacity is 60 tonnes. The insulation is 80 mm thick PUF ($k = 0.022$ W/m·K), the inner lining is 4 mm SS-316 ($k = 16$ W/m·K), and the outer hull is 12 mm steel ($k = 50$ W/m·K). Internal and external surface coefficients are 8 and 25 W/m²K respectively. The hold is maintained at −25 °C and the engine room at +35 °C. The total envelope area is 220 m². Calculate:
(i) The overall U-value.
(ii) The steady-state heat load (excluding respiration and pull-down).
(iii) The required compressor capacity in kW if the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of the refrigeration plant is 2.2. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) U-value calculation:**

$$
R_{th} = \dfrac{1}{8} + \dfrac{0.080}{0.022} + \dfrac{0.004}{16} + \dfrac{0.012}{50} + \dfrac{1}{25}
$$

$$
R_{th} = 0.1250 + 3.6364 + 0.000250 + 0.000240 + 0.0400
$$

$$
R_{th} = 3.8019 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
U = \dfrac{1}{3.8019} = 0.2630 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**(ii) Heat load:**

$$
\Delta T = 35 - (-25) = 60 \text{ K}
$$

$$
Q = U \times A \times \Delta T = 0.2630 \times 220 \times 60 = 3471.6 \text{ W} \approx 3.47 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iii) Compressor capacity:**

$$
\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{compressor}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_{compressor} = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{COP}
$$

$$
W_{compressor} = \dfrac{3.47}{2.2} = 1.578 \text{ kW} \approx 1.58 \text{ kW}
$$

In practice, with safety factor of 1.25 (for pull-down, door openings, fan heat, and respiration):
$$
W_{design} = 1.58 \times 1.25 = 1.98 \text{ kW} \approx 2.0 \text{ kW}
$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fish hold** = watertight + insulated + refrigerated + drained + lined volume — all five attributes are mandatory.
- **Stowage factor (SF) ≈ 1.4–1.8 m³/t** for whole fish in ice; use this to convert catch weight → hold volume.
- **Hold temperature for icing ≈ 0 to +2 °C**; for brine freezing ≈ −18 to −25 °C; for freezer holds ≈ −25 to −30 °C.
- **Insulation:** PUF foam is industry-preferred ($k ≈ 0.020–0.024$ W/m·K). Thickness is chosen such that overall $U \leq 0.40$ W/m²K.
- **Vapour barrier** must always be on the **warm side** of the insulation (engine room side), not the cold side.
- **Floor slope ≥ 1 in 60** to drain well; sump with non-return valve and bilge pump mandatory.
- **Drainage** prevents slime, blood, and melt-water accumulation → reduces bacterial load and corrosion.
- **Lining** is typically SS-316 for premium vessels, GRP for mid-range, GI for small inshore boats.
- **Lining must be food-grade, smooth, non-absorbent, and easy to wash down.**
- **Heat load equation** = $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ — add respiration, door-opening, and pull-down loads in real design.
- **Compressor sizing** requires a **safety factor of 1.20–1.30** over steady-state conduction load.
- **Forward location of large fish holds** can compromise stability (loss of GM when flooded) — designers must check the **flooded-compartment condition** in the stability booklet.
- **Classification society rules** (IRS, DNV, Lloyd's, BV) prescribe minimum insulation thickness, drain sizes, and access hatch dimensions — these are exam-favourite questions.
- **Modern trends:** RSW (Refrigerated Sea Water) systems are increasingly replacing traditional icing for tuna and pelagic species due to superior quality retention.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Erections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Erections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Erection (Shipbuilding Definition):** Erection is the stage of ship/vessel construction in which the **two-dimensional (2D) sub-assemblies, panels, and three-dimensional (3D) blocks** fabricated in the panel fabrication shop and sub-assembly shop are **transported, lifted, positioned, aligned, and joined together** on the building berth, slipway, or in the building dock to form the complete hull structure of the vessel.

In the context of **fishing vessel design and construction**, erection is the critical structural assembly phase that transforms pre-cut and pre-fabricated steel (or FRP/wood for smaller boats) components into a watertight, load-bearing hull capable of withstanding the marine environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 scheme, erection is studied as a part of **production engineering applied to fishing vessels**, with emphasis on sequence of assembly, lifting capacity calculations, alignment tolerances, and modern modular construction techniques (e.g., block construction and unit erection).

## 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Erection Mark (E. Mark)** | A unique alphanumeric code assigned to every sub-assembly or block to identify its position in the vessel |
| **Block** | A 3D pre-assembled unit of the hull (e.g., engine room block, bow block) |
| **Unit** | A larger 3D assembly comprising multiple blocks (e.g., bow unit, stern unit) |
| **Module** | The highest level of pre-assembly containing outfit components (piping, machinery, etc.) |
| **Piece (Part)** | The smallest identifiable item from a drawing (plate, stiffener, frame) |
| **Sub-Assembly** | A 2D or 3D group of pieces joined together before main erection |
| **Erection Berth** | The location (slipway, dock, or hard stand) where final hull assembly takes place |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a **giant 3D jigsaw puzzle** of a boat. Instead of cutting and painting each piece on the floor where the final boat will sit, workers **pre-cut and pre-assemble** small chunks (blocks) of the puzzle in a workshop, then **crane them onto a platform** and weld them together. This process of lifting the pre-made chunks and joining them into the final boat shape is called **Erection**.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Erection is essentially the **"construction LEGO assembly"** stage of shipbuilding — small parts → panels → blocks → units → complete ship. Each step happens at a different location to maximize efficiency, and erection is where everything comes together.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics in Erection:**
> - **Hull construction tolerance:** $\pm$**5 mm** for shell plating alignment
> - **Block lifting tolerance:** $\pm$**10 mm** from theoretical center of gravity
> - **Welding tolerance during erection:** **2 mm to 4 mm** root gap for butt welds
> - **Crane lifting factor of safety:** **1.5 to 2.0 times** the calculated block weight
> - **Maximum permissible hull deflection:** $L/1000$ where $L$ is the block length
> - **Density of shipbuilding steel:** **7850 kg/m³**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phases of Ship Construction Leading to Erection

The complete construction cycle of a fishing vessel follows a logical progression:

1. **Design Phase** — Lines plan, structural design, stability calculations
2. **Lofting** — Full-scale transfer of design lines to the loft floor (1:1 scale)
3. **Nestling / Cutting Plan** — Optimizing plate nesting for minimum waste
4. **Material Preparation** — Shot blasting, priming, cutting
5. **Fabrication** — Bending, rolling, machining of plates and sections
6. **Sub-Assembly** — Small parts joined into T-sections, L-sections, panels
7. **Panel Assembly** — Plates stiffened with frames/beams to form flat/curved panels
8. **Block Construction** — Panels joined to form 3D blocks
9. **Erection** — Blocks/units lifted and joined on the berth *(FOCUS OF THIS MODULE)*
10. **Outfitting** — Installation of machinery, piping, electrical, accommodation
11. **Launching** — Transfer of vessel to water
12. **Commissioning & Trials** — Sea trials and handover

## 2.2 Types of Erection Methods

### 2.2.1 Conventional (Stick) Erection
- Components are erected **piece-by-piece** starting from the keel
- Suitable for **small fishing vessels** (< 20 m LOA)
- **Advantages:** Low capital investment, suitable for small yards
- **Disadvantages:** Slow, high labor content, weather-dependent

### 2.2.2 Block Erection
- Hull is divided into **pre-assembled 3D blocks** (typically 15–30 tonnes each)
- Blocks are fabricated in workshops, then lifted onto the berth
- **Advantages:** Better quality control, parallel work, faster turnaround
- **Disadvantages:** Requires heavy-lift cranes

### 2.2.3 Unit Erection
- Larger 3D assemblies (e.g., **bow unit, midship unit, stern unit**) comprising multiple blocks
- Common for **medium fishing vessels (20–35 m)**

### 2.2.4 Modular (Mega-Block) Erection
- Entire hull sections (up to 1000+ tonnes) are outfitted and painted before joining
- Pre-outfitted blocks reduce **post-erection work**
- Used in **modern fishing trawlers and tuna purse seiners**

### 2.2.5 Section Erection
- The vessel is built in **complete transverse or longitudinal sections** on ground level
- Sections are then rotated/lifted into position — common for **aluminum fishing boats and FRP vessels**

## 2.3 Sequence of Erection (Hull Build-Up Order)

The erection sequence follows a **bottom-up approach** for structural stability:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Erection Sequence for a Steel Fishing Vessel:**
>
> **Step 1:** Keel plate (center girder) → laid first on the building blocks
>
> **Step 2:** Bottom shell plates (port + starboard) → joined to center girder
>
> **Step 3:** Bottom floors / transverses → welded to bottom plating
>
> **Step 4:** Bilge plates and lower side shell plating
>
> **Step 5:** Side shell frames and stringers
>
> **Step 6:** Upper side shell plating and sheer strake
>
> **Step 7:** Deck beams and deck girders
>
> **Step 8:** Deck plating (working from centerline outward)
>
> **Step 9:** Bulkheads (transverse and longitudinal) → inserted as units
>
> **Step 10:** Superstructure blocks (wheelhouse, accommodation)
>
> **Step 11:** Stem and stern frame assemblies
>
> **Step 12:** Hatch coamings, bulwarks, and external fittings

## 2.4 Erection Equipment and Heavy Lift Systems

| Equipment Type | Typical Capacity | Application |
|----------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Portal / Goliath Crane** | 100 – 800 tonnes | Large shipyards, block erection |
| **Tower Crane** | 10 – 60 tonnes | Block fabrication shops |
| **Mobile Hydraulic Crane** | 20 – 500 tonnes | Small fishing vessel yards |
| **Self-Propelled Modular Transporter (SPMT)** | 40 – 1000+ tonnes per axle line | Mega-block transport |
| **Straddle Carrier** | 50 – 600 tonnes | Yard logistics |
| **Floating Crane / Derrick Barge** | 100 – 10,000 tonnes | Offshore/launching operations |
| **Hydraulic Jacking System** | Up to 50,000 tonnes | Dry-dock and horizontal launch |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formulas for Erection Calculations (KTU Board Exam Focus)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $W_{block} = \rho_{steel} \cdot V_{block}$ | Block weight = density × volume | tonnes |
| 2 | $CG_{x} = \frac{\sum (W_i \cdot x_i)}{\sum W_i}$ | Center of gravity location along x-axis | m |
| 3 | $M_{lifting} = W_{block} \cdot \ell_{pendant}$ | Lifting moment (overturning) | tonne·m |
| 4 | $S_{cable} = \frac{W_{block}}{2 \cos\theta}$ | Tension in each sling for symmetric 2-leg lift | tonnes |
| 5 | $FOS = \frac{W_{crane}}{W_{block}}$ | Factor of Safety of crane (≥ **1.5 to 2.0**) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\delta_{hull} = \frac{5 W L^{4}}{384 E I}$ | Mid-span deflection of block during lift | mm |
| 7 | $\sigma_{bending} = \frac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Maximum bending stress in plate/section | N/mm² |
| 8 | $t_{cycle} = t_{prep} + t_{lift} + t_{align} + t_{weld}$ | Total erection cycle time per block | hours |
| 9 | $P_{weld} = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{1000}$ | Welding heat input | kJ/mm |
| 10 | $\eta_{erection} = \frac{W_{hull}}{T_{erection} \cdot N_{workers}}$ | Erection productivity | tonnes/man-hour |

## 2.6 Alignment and Surveying During Erection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alignment tolerance during erection must not exceed:**
>
> $$\Delta_{max} = \pm 5 \text{ mm over 10 m length}$$
>
> $$\Delta_{squareness} = \pm 1.5 \text{ mm/m of breadth}$$

Modern yards use **laser theodolites, total stations, and 3D laser scanners** to maintain alignment. Each erected block's position is verified against the **Moulded Baseline** reference.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Application:** Erection is the most **time-critical and capital-intensive** phase of fishing vessel construction. Approximately **30–40%** of the total hull construction labor is consumed during erection and welding. For a typical 24 m fishing trawler built using block construction, the total erection cycle is reduced by **40–50%** compared to stick erection, translating to significant economic benefit for the shipyard.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Block Weight and Center of Gravity Calculation

### Problem Setup

A fishing vessel block is composed of the following sub-assemblies:

| Component | Mass (tonnes) | x-position (m from AP) | y-position (m from CL) | z-position (m from BL) |
|-----------|--------------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Bottom panel | 18.5 | 12.0 | 0.0 | 1.2 |
| Side panel (P) | 12.0 | 12.0 | +3.0 | 2.8 |
| Side panel (S) | 12.0 | 12.0 | −3.0 | 2.8 |
| Deck panel | 9.5 | 12.0 | 0.0 | 4.5 |
| Internal frames | 4.0 | 12.0 | 0.0 | 2.5 |

### Step 1: Compute Total Block Weight

$$\sum W_i = 18.5 + 12.0 + 12.0 + 9.5 + 4.0 = 56.0 \text{ tonnes}$$

### Step 2: Compute Longitudinal Center of Gravity (LCG)

Since all components share the same x-coordinate (12.0 m):

$$x_{CG} = \frac{\sum (W_i \cdot x_i)}{\sum W_i} = \frac{56.0 \times 12.0}{56.0} = 12.0 \text{ m from AP}$$

### Step 3: Compute Transverse Center of Gravity (TCG)

By symmetry (port = starboard), $y_{CG} = 0$ m from centerline.

### Step 4: Compute Vertical Center of Gravity (VCG)

$$z_{CG} = \frac{\sum (W_i \cdot z_i)}{\sum W_i}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
z_{CG} &= \frac{(18.5)(1.2) + (12.0)(2.8) + (12.0)(2.8) + (9.5)(4.5) + (4.0)(2.5)}{56.0} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{22.20 + 33.60 + 33.60 + 42.75 + 10.00}{56.0} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{142.15}{56.0} \\[4pt]
z_{CG} &= 2.539 \text{ m from baseline}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $W_{block} = 56.0$ tonnes, CG located at $(12.0, 0, 2.54)$ m

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sling Tension for a 4-Leg Lifting Sling

### Problem Setup

A **56-tonne block** is to be lifted using a 4-leg sling system. Each sling makes an angle of $\theta = 45°$ with the vertical. The lifting head weighs $W_{head} = 1.5$ tonnes.

### Step 1: Total Lifting Load

$$W_{total} = W_{block} + W_{head} = 56.0 + 1.5 = 57.5 \text{ tonnes}$$

### Step 2: Vertical Equilibrium

For a 4-leg symmetric sling with angle $\theta$ to the vertical:

$$T \cdot 4 \cos\theta = W_{total}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{W_{total}}{4 \cos\theta} \\[4pt]
T &= \frac{57.5}{4 \times \cos 45°} \\[4pt]
T &= \frac{57.5}{4 \times 0.7071} \\[4pt]
T &= \frac{57.5}{2.8284} \\[4pt]
T &= 20.33 \text{ tonnes per sling leg}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Apply Factor of Safety (FOS = 2.0)

$$T_{design} = T \times FOS = 20.33 \times 2.0 = 40.66 \text{ tonnes}$$

### Step 4: Recommended Sling Capacity

Standard wire rope slings of **42 mm dia** have a Safe Working Load (SWL) of **~42 tonnes** at vertical lift — suitable for this application.

> [!TIP]
> **Practical note:** When the sling angle is shallower (e.g., 60° from vertical), the tension increases sharply. Always maintain $\theta \leq 45°$ for safe lifting.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Erection Cycle Time Estimation

### Given Parameters
- Hull mass = $W_{hull} = 280$ tonnes (steel hull of 24 m fishing trawler)
- Number of erection blocks = $n = 14$
- Workers per shift = $N = 18$
- Shift duration = $T_{shift} = 8$ hours
- Erection rate = $r = 1.5$ blocks/shift (typical)

### Step 1: Total Shifts Required

$$n_{shifts} = \frac{n}{r} = \frac{14}{1.5} = 9.33 \text{ shifts}$$

### Step 2: Total Erection Time (Calendar Days)

$$T_{calendar} = \frac{n_{shifts}}{1 \text{ shift/day (single shift)}} = 9.33 \text{ days} \approx 10 \text{ days}$$

### Step 3: Total Man-Hours Consumed

$$M_{hours} = n_{shifts} \times T_{shift} \times N = 9.33 \times 8 \times 18 = 1343.5 \text{ man-hours}$$

### Step 4: Erection Productivity

$$\eta_{erection} = \frac{W_{hull}}{M_{hours}} = \frac{280}{1343.5} = 0.2084 \text{ tonnes/man-hour}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Erection Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparative Decision Matrix for Fishing Vessel Yards**

| Parameter | Stick Erection | Block Erection | Unit Erection | Modular Erection |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Suitable LOA** | < 20 m | 20 – 35 m | 25 – 45 m | > 40 m |
| **Initial Investment** | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Construction Time** | High | Medium | Low | Very Low |
| **Quality Control** | Difficult | Good | Very Good | Excellent |
| **Welding Productivity** | Low | High | High | Very High |
| **Labor Intensity** | Very High | Medium | Low | Very Low |
| **Weather Dependency** | Very High | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Outfitting Pre-install** | 0% | 10–20% | 30–40% | 60–80% |
| **Yard Space Required** | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Crane Capacity Needed** | < 20 t | 50 – 200 t | 100 – 400 t | > 500 t |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Welding Heat Input for Erection Joints

### Given
- Welding current $I = 220$ A
- Arc voltage $V = 28$ V
- Travel speed $S = 4$ mm/s

### Step 1: Heat Input Formula

$$P = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \text{ kJ/mm}$$

### Step 2: Substitution

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{28 \times 220 \times 60}{4 \times 1000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{369600}{4000} \\[4pt]
P &= 92.4 \text{ kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner Pitfall:** Heat input above **100 kJ/mm** for high-strength shipbuilding steel (AH36, DH36) can cause **lamellar tearing** and **HAZ softening**. Students often forget to multiply by 60 to convert seconds to minutes — this is a guaranteed mark-loser.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define the term "Erection" in the context of fishing vessel construction. List the four main types of erection methods commonly employed in shipyards.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Erection** is the stage of ship construction in which pre-fabricated sub-assemblies, panels, and 3D blocks are lifted, positioned, aligned, and welded together on the building berth to form the complete hull structure.

**Four main types of erection methods:**
1. **Stick (Piece-by-Piece) Erection** — Small vessels (< 20 m), low investment
2. **Block Erection** — 3D blocks of 30–100 tonnes, medium-sized fishing vessels
3. **Unit Erection** — Larger assemblies combining multiple blocks
4. **Modular (Mega-Block) Erection** — Pre-outfitted sections for modern trawlers

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for listing with brief relevance)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**State the standard sequence of hull erection for a steel fishing vessel. Why is the keel plate placed first?**

**Model Answer:**

**Sequence:**
1. Keel plate (center girder)
2. Bottom shell plates
3. Floors and bottom transverses
4. Bilge and lower side shell plating
5. Side shell frames
6. Upper side shell plating
7. Deck beams and girders
8. Deck plating
9. Bulkheads
10. Superstructure

**Why keel first?** The keel (center girder) acts as the **primary structural backbone** and the **reference datum line** (baseline) for all alignment and dimensional checks during the entire erection process.

*(3 marks: 1 for sequence + 1 for keel-first reason + 1 for presentation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**With the help of a neat sketch, explain the block erection method for a 24 m fishing trawler. State its advantages over conventional stick erection.**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Erection Method for a 24 m Fishing Trawler:**

The hull of a 24 m fishing trawler is divided into approximately **12 to 16 pre-fabricated blocks**, each weighing between 25 and 80 tonnes. Typical block division:

- **1 Engine Room Block** (heavy with machinery base)
- **4 Bottom Blocks** (port and starboard, forward and aft)
- **4 Side Shell Blocks** (matching the bottom blocks)
- **2 Deck Blocks** (forward and aft deck)
- **2 Transverse Bulkhead Assemblies**
- **2 Bow/Stern Units**

**Procedure:**
1. The center keel block is laid first on the building berth using **gallows supports and building blocks**
2. Bottom shell blocks are lifted using a **gantry crane** and welded to the keel
3. Side shell blocks are hoisted and joined to the bottom using tack welds
4. The structure is **survey-checked** for alignment using **laser total station**
5. Deck blocks and bulkheads complete the hull ring
6. Welding is completed using **SAW (Submerged Arc Welding)** for butts and **FCAW** for fillets

**Advantages over Stick Erection:**

| Aspect | Stick | Block |
|--------|-------|-------|
| Welding productivity | Low | 2–3× higher |
| Total erection time | 100% | 50–60% |
| Labor intensity | Very High | Medium |
| Quality | Variable | Controlled |
| Weather impact | Severe | Reduced |

*(7 marks: 3 for procedure + 2 for sketch description + 2 for advantages)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A fishing vessel hull block weighs 48 tonnes. It is to be lifted using a 4-leg sling system where each sling makes an angle of 60° with the horizontal. Calculate: (i) tension in each sling leg, and (ii) the recommended Safe Working Load (SWL) of the sling if Factor of Safety = 2.0.**

**Given:** $W_{block} = 48$ tonnes, $n = 4$ legs, $\alpha = 60°$ from horizontal, FOS = 2.0

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert sling angle to vertical reference. The angle from the **vertical** is:

$$\theta = 90° - 60° = 30°$$

**Step 2:** Apply vertical equilibrium for 4-leg symmetric sling:

$$T \cdot 4 \cos\theta = W_{block}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{W_{block}}{4 \cos\theta} \\[4pt]
T &= \frac{48}{4 \times \cos 30°} \\[4pt]
T &= \frac{48}{4 \times 0.866} \\[4pt]
T &= \frac{48}{3.464} \\[4pt]
T &= 13.86 \text{ tonnes per leg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Apply Factor of Safety:

$$SWL = T \times FOS = 13.86 \times 2.0 = 27.72 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Answer:** Tension per sling = **13.86 tonnes**, Recommended SWL = **27.72 tonnes** (use standard 30-tonne sling).

*(7 marks: 2 for setup + 3 for calculation + 1 for FOS + 1 for final answer with practical selection)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the various erection equipment used in modern shipyards for fishing vessel construction. Discuss the role of Self-Propelled Modular Transporters (SPMTs) in mega-block erection.**

**Model Answer:**

**Erection Equipment in Modern Fishing Vessel Yards:**

1. **Gantry / Goliath Cranes** — Rail-mounted, span up to 150 m, capacity 100–800 t. Used for lifting blocks onto the berth.
2. **Portal Jib Cranes** — Stationary, used for repetitive block lifting in series production.
3. **Mobile Hydraulic Cranes** — Truck-mounted, flexible positioning for small fishing vessels.
4. **Tower Cranes** — For block fabrication shops and superstructure erection.
5. **Derrick Cranes** — Vessel-mounted, used in outfitting and self-launching.
6. **Self-Propelled Modular Transporters (SPMTs)** — Hydraulic multi-axle vehicles capable of carrying 40–1000+ tonnes per axle line. Used for **horizontal movement of mega-blocks** from assembly area to erection berth without lifting.

**Role of SPMTs in Mega-Block Erection:**

- SPMTs enable **out-of-position assembly** of blocks at ground level (safer, faster)
- Allow **horizontal launch techniques** (blocks joined on SPMTs, then rolled onto the vessel)
- Reduce **crane dependency** — SPMTs can carry blocks where cranes cannot reach
- Enable **just-in-time delivery** of blocks to the erection berth
- Used extensively in **modern tuna longliner and purse seiner construction**

*(7 marks: 4 for equipment types + 3 for SPMT role)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Calculate the vertical center of gravity (VCG) of a hull block composed of: bottom panel (W = 20 t at z = 1.0 m), port side panel (W = 10 t at z = 2.5 m), starboard side panel (W = 10 t at z = 2.5 m), and deck panel (W = 12 t at z = 4.0 m). If the lifting sling is attached at z = 6.0 m, determine the maximum sling tension per leg for a 2-leg symmetric lift at 30° from vertical, including FOS = 1.8.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Total Block Weight

$$\sum W_i = 20 + 10 + 10 + 12 = 52 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 2:** VCG Calculation

$$\begin{aligned}
z_{CG} &= \frac{(20)(1.0) + (10)(2.5) + (10)(2.5) + (12)(4.0)}{52} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{20.0 + 25.0 + 25.0 + 48.0}{52} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{118.0}{52} \\[4pt]
z_{CG} &= 2.269 \text{ m from baseline}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Sling Tension for 2-leg symmetric lift at $\theta = 30°$ from vertical

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{W_{block}}{2 \cos\theta} \\[4pt]
T &= \frac{52}{2 \times \cos 30°} \\[4pt]
T &= \frac{52}{2 \times 0.866} \\[4pt]
T &= \frac{52}{1.732} \\[4pt]
T &= 30.02 \text{ tonnes per leg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Apply Factor of Safety = 1.8

$$SWL_{design} = 30.02 \times 1.8 = 54.04 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Answer:** VCG = **2.27 m**, Sling tension = **30.02 t/leg**, Design SWL = **54.04 t** (use 55-tonne rated sling).

*(7 marks: 2 for VCG + 3 for tension + 1 for FOS + 1 for final answer)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Erection Questions (KTU 2024 Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "angle from horizontal" vs "angle from vertical"** in sling tension problems — this alone costs **2–3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to add lifting head weight** to the block weight in lifting calculations — examiners specifically test this oversight.
>
> 3. **Skipping the Factor of Safety (FOS)** in design calculations — always state the FOS used (typically 1.5–2.0 for marine lifts).
>
> 4. **Wrong units** — mixing kg and tonnes, or forgetting to convert mm to m in deflection formulas.
>
> 5. **No sketch** in block erection questions — KTU board examiners award **1–2 marks** specifically for a neat labeled sketch; absence is heavily penalized.
>
> 6. **Out-of-sequence erection description** — students often start with deck panels or superstructure, which is structurally incorrect. Always start with the **keel**.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning alignment tolerance** in procedure questions — examiners expect reference to **$\pm 5$ mm tolerance** and survey methods.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Erections (Module 2)**

- **Erection** = lifting + aligning + joining pre-fabricated blocks into the final hull
- **Four types:** Stick / Block / Unit / Modular — chosen by vessel LOA and yard capacity
- **Sequence:** Keel → Bottom → Bilge → Side Shell → Deck → Bulkheads → Superstructure
- **Block weight formula:** $W = \rho_{steel} \times V$ where $\rho_{steel} = 7850$ kg/m³
- **Center of gravity:** $x_{CG} = \frac{\sum W_i x_i}{\sum W_i}$ (apply for x, y, z separately)
- **Sling tension (n legs):** $T = \frac{W}{n \cos\theta}$ where $\theta$ is angle from vertical
- **Factor of Safety:** Always **1.5 to 2.0** for marine lifting operations
- **Alignment tolerance:** $\pm 5$ mm over 10 m length
- **Heat input limit:** ≤ **100 kJ/mm** for AH36/DH36 shipbuilding steel
- **Goliath/Portal cranes:** Standard equipment for block erection
- **SPMTs:** Used for mega-block movement in modern modular construction
- **Productivity metric:** $\eta_{erection} \approx 0.2$ tonnes/man-hour (typical)
- **Stick erection:** Suitable only for vessels < 20 m LOA
- **Block erection:** Reduces hull construction time by **40–50%** vs stick method
- **Welding during erection:** SAW for butts, FCAW/GMAW for fillets
- **Always start erection with the keel plate** — it is the structural backbone and the dimensional reference
- **Survey tools:** Total station, laser scanner, theodolite — used to verify block alignment

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deck Machinery Arrangement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Deck Machinery Arrangement # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Deck Machinery** refers to the complete assembly of mechanical equipment installed on the exposed weather deck of a vessel, used for handling anchors, mooring lines, fishing gear (nets, lines, pots), catch, stores, and auxiliary loads. On a fishing vessel, deck machinery constitutes the **operational heart of the working deck** and is designed to withstand harsh marine environments, dynamic shock loading from waves and gear, and prolonged cyclic duty. The **arrangement** of deck machinery is the discipline of positioning, aligning, and integrating these units so that: 1. The fishing operation can be carried out safely and efficiently. 2. The transverse and longitudinal stability of the vessel is preserved. 3. The longitudinal centre of gravity (LCG) and vertical centre of gravity (VCG) remain within permissible limits. 4. Power transmission paths (mechanical, hydraulic, or electric) are minimized. 5. Crew safety (ILO Work in Fishing Convention, 2007 — C188) is ensured. The principal classification groups recognised by classification societies (IRS, DNV, Lloyd's Register) and used in the KTU 2024 syllabus are: | S.No. | Group | Representative Units | |:----:|:------|:--------------------| | 1 | Mooring & Anchoring | Anchor windlass, mooring winches, chain stoppers, hawse pipes | | 2 | Fishing Winches | Trawl winches, seine winches, line haulers, gilson winches | | 3 | Net Handling | Net drums, power blocks, gallows, stern ramps, net bins | | 4 | Lifting Gear | Auxiliary deck cranes, derricks, gantries, mast cranes | | 5 | Auxiliary | Store cranes, hatch cover winches, life-boat davits | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the **deck of a fishing vessel as the workshop floor of a factory on a floating, tilting platform**. Every machine on this floor must: - Be **bolted down** against motion (the ship rolls and pitches up to ±30°). - Be **reachable** by the workers without crossing dangerous lines under tension. - Be **powered** from a central source (engine room) through shafts, hydraulic lines, or cables. - Not **unbalance** the platform when its load shifts. Just as a car-assembly plant arranges its robots in a logical sequence (welding → painting → assembly), a fishing vessel arranges its winches, drums, and cranes in the **chronological order of fishing operations**: shoot the net ➜ tow ➜ haul the net ➜ empty the catch ➜ stow the gear. > [!NOTE] > **The three governing rules** (worth remembering for the KTU exam): > 1. **Rule of Reach** — the operator must reach every control without leaving a safe zone. > 2. **Rule of Flow** — gear moves in one logical direction (typically bow → stern). > 3. **Rule of Balance** — heavy machinery must be placed symmetrically about the centreline to avoid **list** (transverse tilt). ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Symbols | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | |:------:|:---------|:------------:| | $D$ | Drum/barrel diameter | **mm** | | $d$ | Wire rope diameter | **mm** | | $L$ | Rope length on drum | **m** | | $F$ | Line pull / tension | **kN** | | $v$ | Line speed | **m/min** | | $P$ | Power | **kW** | | $T$ | Torque | **kN·m** | | $\eta$ | Mechanical efficiency | **0.70 – 0.85** | | $N$ | Number of rope layers | dimensionless | > [!TIP] > Two numbers you must **memorise** for any KTU numerical: > - Minimum drum-to-rope ratio for fishing winches: **$D/d \geq 20$** > - Typical overall efficiency of a hydraulic trawl winch: **$\eta \approx 0.75$** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Logic of Deck Machinery Arrangement The arrangement is decided through an **iterative design process** that satisfies operational, structural, stability, and regulatory constraints. The logical sequence is: 1. **Identify Fishing Method** → stern trawling, side trawling, purse seining, longlining, gillnetting, squid jigging. 2. **Determine Required Winch Pull & Speed** → based on gear drag, water depth, vessel size. 3. **Select Winch Type & Size** → drum capacity, line pull, braking torque. 4. **Plot Working Deck Layout** → define shooting/ hauling arcs. 5. **Check Stability** → transverse metacentric height (GM), free surface effect, list angle during haul. 6. **Power Transmission Route** → shaft tunnel, hydraulic piping, cable trays. 7. **Crew Safety Zones** → per ILO C188 and MGN 612 (UK MCA guidance). 8. **Classification Society Approval** → IRS / DNV drawing approval cycle. ## 2.2 Why Arrangement Matters — The Engineering Rationale | Concern | Engineering "Why" | |:--------|:------------------| | **Longitudinal balance** | Heavy winches (e.g. trawl winches weighing 8–15 tonnes) placed too far forward raise the trim angle and slow the vessel. | | **Transverse balance** | Asymmetric placement induces a permanent list, lowering the freeboard on one side — dangerous when shooting nets. | | **Heeling during haul** | Net full of catch hauled over the side creates a large heeling moment; machinery layout must provide counter-weight or symmetrical haul. | | **Cable & pipe runs** | Long hydraulic runs lose pressure and generate heat; long shafting wastes power. Shorter runs = higher $\eta$. | | **Ergonomics** | A winch control placed 6 m from the operator during a 5-tonne haul is a recipe for disaster. | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **exam-defining formula set**. Every numerical problem in Module 2 will use one or more of these. | # | Formula | Variables | Engineering Use | |:-:|:--------|:----------|:----------------| | 1 | $D \geq 20\,d$ | drum & rope diameter | Minimum drum sizing (IMO/IRS rule) | | 2 | $L = \pi\,N\,(D + N\,d)$ | total rope length on N layers | Drum capacity calculation | | 3 | $n_t = (W - d)/d$ | turns per layer, $W$ = flange-to-flange width | Layer-by-layer analysis | | 4 | $P = \dfrac{F\,v}{60\,\eta}$ | kW when $F$ in kN, $v$ in m/min | Winch power sizing | | 5 | $P = \dfrac{F\,v}{75\,\eta}$ | HP when $F$ in kgf, $v$ in m/s | Legacy imperial unit | | 6 | $T_{drum} = F\,R_{drum}$ | holding/driving torque | Brake & gear sizing | | 7 | $M_{heel} = F_{haul}\cdot y$ | heeling moment due to haul force $F_{haul}$ at lever arm $y$ | Stability check | | 8 | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{M_{heel}}{W\,\overline{GM}}$ | list angle $\phi$ | Transverse stability | | 9 | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu\theta}$ | capstan / Euler belt equation | Band brake design | | 10 | $F_{brake}\,a = (T_1 - T_2)\,R_{drum}$ | brake lever equilibrium, $a$ = lever arm | Holding brake sizing | > [!NOTE] > In every formula above, the vertical-bar notation (such as absolute value) has been written using `$\vert$` and `$\mid$` so it never breaks the markdown table renderer. Example: $\vert F \vert$ rather than $|F|$. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility The same principles taught here are applied by: - **Naval architects** at shipyards (Cochin Shipyard, ABG Shipyard) when designing new fishing trawlers. - **Classification society surveyors** (IRS, DNV) who approve GA drawings. - **Fishing fleet operators** who retrofit older vessels with modern hydraulic systems. - **Marine equipment manufacturers** (Mustad, Rapp Hydema, Brattvaag, Ibercisa) who publish standard winch catalogues that the designer selects from. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Length of Wire Rope on a Multi-Layer Drum This is the **single most important derivation** for the KTU Module 2 exam. We derive the closed-form expression for the total rope length $L$ wound on a drum in $N$ full layers. ### Setup - Let $D$ = bare barrel diameter of the drum (the steel core before any rope is wound). - Let $d$ = nominal diameter of the wire rope. - Let $N$ = total number of complete layers. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Effective winding diameter of the $n^{th}$ layer** When the first layer is wound, the centreline of the rope lies on a circle of diameter: $$D_1 = D + d$$ When the second layer is wound on top, the rope centre traces: $$D_2 = D + 3d$$ Generalising, for the $n^{th}$ layer: $$D_n = D + (2n - 1)d$$ **Step 2 — Length of one full turn on the $n^{th}$ layer** One complete wrap is the circumference of the corresponding circle: $$\ell_n = \pi D_n = \pi \left[D + (2n - 1)d\right]$$ **Step 3 — Sum the lengths of all $N$ layers** The total rope length is the sum of the circumferences of all $N$ layers: $$L = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \pi \left[D + (2n - 1)d\right]$$ **Step 4 — Split the sum and evaluate** Separate the constant and variable terms: $$L = \pi \sum_{n=1}^{N} D \;+\; \pi\,d \sum_{n=1}^{N}(2n - 1)$$ The first sum is trivial: $$\pi \sum_{n=1}^{N} D = \pi\,N\,D$$ The second sum is the well-known sum of the first $N$ odd numbers: $$\sum_{n=1}^{N}(2n - 1) = 1 + 3 + 5 + \dots + (2N - 1) = N^{2}$$ (Proof by induction: for $N=1$, $1^{2}=1$ ✓; assuming true for $N$, the next odd term is $2N+1$, giving $N^{2}+2N+1=(N+1)^{2}$ ✓.) **Step 5 — Combine** $$L = \pi N D + \pi d N^{2}$$ $$\boxed{L = \pi N \left(D + N d\right)}$$ ### Worked Numerical (KTU-style) > A trawl winch drum has barrel diameter $D = 420$ mm and is to wind wire rope of diameter $d = 24$ mm for $N = 5$ layers. Find the total rope capacity. $$L = \pi \times 5 \times (420 + 5 \times 24)\;\text{mm}$$ $$L = \pi \times 5 \times (420 + 120) = \pi \times 5 \times 540\;\text{mm}$$ $$L = 5 \times 540 \times \pi = 8482.3\;\text{mm} \approx \mathbf{8.48\;m}$$ > [!TIP] > This is the length for **one turn on each layer**. To find total capacity in **metres of rope**, multiply by the **number of turns per layer**, which depends on flange width $W$. A typical turn count is $n_t = (W - d)/d$. The full formula is therefore $L_{total} = \pi N (D + Nd) \times n_t$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Winch Power Requirement We derive the shaft power required to deliver a line pull $F$ at a line speed $v$, accounting for mechanical losses. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Output (useful) power** The useful work done per second in lifting the load through a vertical equivalent of the rope speed is: $$P_{out} = F \times v$$ **Step 2 — Include mechanical efficiency** Power lost in gears, bearings, hydraulic motor, and braking is captured by the overall efficiency $\eta$. Therefore shaft power is: $$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \dfrac{F\,v}{\eta}$$ **Step 3 — Convert units** If $F$ is in **kN** and $v$ is in **m/min**, then $F\,v$ has units of kN·m/min = kJ/(60 s). To express in kW (= kJ/s), divide by 60: $$P_{shaft}\;\;[\text{kW}] = \dfrac{F\,[\text{kN}]\,\times\,v\,[\text{m/min}]}{60 \times \eta}$$ If $F$ is in **kgf** and $v$ is in **m/s**, then 1 HP = 75 kgf·m/s: $$P_{shaft}\;\;[\text{HP}] = \dfrac{F\,[\text{kgf}]\,\times\,v\,[\text{m/s}]}{75 \times \eta}$$ **Step 4 — Final boxed expression** $$\boxed{P\,[\text{kW}] = \dfrac{F\,[\text{kN}]\,\times\,v\,[\text{m/min}]}{60\,\eta}}$$ ### Worked Numerical > A trawl winch must deliver $F = 60$ kN at $v = 45$ m/min with $\eta = 0.78$. Calculate the required prime-mover power. $$P = \dfrac{60 \times 45}{60 \times 0.78} = \dfrac{2700}{46.8}$$ $$\boxed{P \approx 57.69\;\text{kW} \approx 77.4\;\text{HP}}$$ A safety margin of 10–15 % is then applied, so a **65 kW electric motor** or equivalent hydraulic power pack is selected. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Brake Holding Torque & List Angle **Step 1 — Holding torque on the drum** If the maximum static line pull is $F_{max}$ and the effective drum radius is $R_{drum}$ (taken at the middle of the rope layers, or conservatively at the outer layer): $$T_{hold} = F_{max}\,R_{drum}$$ **Step 2 — Band brake sizing (capstan equation)** For a differential band brake wrapped through angle $\theta$ (radians) with friction coefficient $\mu$: $$\dfrac{T_{tight}}{T_{slack}} = e^{\mu\theta}$$ For a simple brake with one end fixed: $$F_{brake}\,\cdot\,a = (T_{tight} - T_{slack})\,\cdot\,R_{drum}$$ **Step 3 — List angle due to haul moment** If a net is hauled over one side at a transverse lever arm $y$ from the centreline with force $F_{haul}$: $$M_{heel} = F_{haul}\cdot y$$ For small angles: $$\tan\phi \approx \phi \approx \dfrac{M_{heel}}{W \cdot \overline{GM}}$$ where $W$ is the vessel displacement and $\overline{GM}$ is the metacentric height. The IMO Load Line Convention typically requires $\phi \leq \tfrac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}(0.5) \approx 12°$ for the working condition. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Winch Selection Workflow | Step | Question to Answer | Tool / Formula | Output | |:----:|:-------------------|:---------------|:-------| | 1 | What is the maximum gear drag? | Empirical curves (FAO Fishing Tech papers) | $F_{max}$ | | 2 | What rope is used? | Classification rules | $d$ | | 3 | What drum diameter? | $D \geq 20 d$ | $D$ | | 4 | How much rope must be stored? | Vessel length × warping depth × safety factor | $L_{req}$ | | 5 | How many layers? | $N$ from $L = \pi N (D + Nd)$ | $N$ | | 6 | What drum flange width? | $W \geq N \cdot d$ | $W$ | | 7 | What line speed? | Operational requirement | $v$ | | 8 | What power? | $P = Fv/(60\eta)$ | $P_{shaft}$ | | 9 | What holding torque? | $T_{hold} = F_{max} R_{drum}$ | $T$ | | 10 | What brake? | Band / disc / hydraulic | Selection | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 General Arrangement — Top View of a Stern Trawler The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the canonical **longitudinal arrangement** of deck machinery on a 25–30 m class stern trawler, showing both the physical order of components and the power / control flow between them. ```mermaid graph TB subgraph FWD["FORWARD SECTION - MOORING DECK"] nA["Anchor Windlass
combined with Mooring Winches"] nB["Hawse Pipes
port and starboard"] nC["Chain Stopper
and Bitter End Release"] end subgraph MID["MID SECTION - BRIDGE & SUPPORT"] nD["Wheelhouse
with Winch Controls"] nE["Auxiliary Deck Crane
2 to 5 tonne SWL"] nF["Mast and Signal Boom"] end subgraph AFT["AFT WORKING DECK - FISHING ZONE"] nG["Trawl Winches
port and starboard"] nH["Net Drum
split drum design"] nI["Power Block
on upper gallows"] nJ["Gallows Frames
port and starboard"] nK["Stern Ramp and Bulwark Gate"] nL["Fish Hatch Cover
main hold access"] end subgraph BELOW["BELOW DECK - POWER SOURCE"] nM["Main Engine
and Hydraulic PTO"] nN["Hydraulic Power Pack
and HPU Reservoir"] end nB --> nA nA --> nC nA --> nD nD --> nE nE --> nF nE --> nG nM --> nN nN --> nG nG --> nH nH --> nI nI --> nJ nJ --> nK nK --> nL nG --> nJ ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Power & Control Architecture This second diagram captures the **functional interactions** of the deck machinery system, mapping the flow of mechanical power, hydraulic fluid, and operator commands. ```mermaid graph LR nP1["Main Engine"] --> nP2["Hydraulic PTO
Power Take-Off"] nP2 --> nP3["HPU
Hydraulic Power Unit"] nP3 --> nP4["Control Valves
and Manifold"] nP4 --> nP5["Trawl Winch Motor"] nP4 --> nP6["Net Drum Motor"] nP4 --> nP7["Power Block Motor"] nP4 --> nP8["Aux Crane Motor"] nP4 --> nP9["Windlass Motor"] nP5 --> nP10["Drum and Band Brake"] nP6 --> nP11["Net Drum Clutch"] nP10 --> nP12["Wire Rope
to Trawl Net"] nP11 --> nP13["Stowed Net"] nP12 --> nP14["Sea"] nP13 --> nP14 nP15["Wheelhouse
Push Button Console"] --> nP4 nP16["Local Lever
on Winch"] --> nP4 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows indicate the **direction of energy/control flow**. The wheelhouse operator can either control the winches remotely (via push-button console) or locally (using a manual lever at each winch). For IMO-class vessels, **both control stations are mandatory**. ## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow (Block-Level View) For drawing-board reference, the canonical block view of a deck machinery arrangement design is: | Block Layer | Component Block | Engineering Output | |:-----------:|:----------------|:-------------------| | **L1 — Fishing Method** | Stern Trawl / Side Trawl / Purse Seine / Longline | Operational requirement | | **L2 — Load Estimation** | Drag curves, vessel resistance, sea state | Maximum $F$ and $v$ | | **L3 — Winch Selection** | Standard catalogue winch | Model number, mass, $R_{drum}$ | | **L4 — Arrangement Plotting** | GA drawing, centreline, transverse positions | Symmetry check, GM check | | **L5 — Stability Verification** | Heel / trim / GM / free-surface | Pass/Fail | | **L6 — Power Transmission** | Shaft, hydraulic, electric | Routing, efficiency | | **L7 — Safety & Class Approval** | ILO C188, IMO, IRS rules | Compliance certificate | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]` **Define deck machinery. List any four major deck machinery units found on a typical fishing vessel.** **Model Answer:** > Deck machinery refers to the set of mechanical equipment mounted on the weather deck of a vessel, used for handling fishing gear, anchors, mooring lines, cargo, and auxiliary loads. The four major units are: > 1. **Anchor windlass** — for dropping and weighing the anchor. > 2. **Trawl winch** — for hauling the trawl net and warps. > 3. **Net drum** — for storing the trawl net during transit. > 4. **Auxiliary deck crane** — for catch and stores handling. *(Word-limit per the KTU 2024 answer-book convention: 70–90 words, 1 neat sketch earns 1 bonus mark.)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]` **State the rule for minimum drum diameter for wire-rope winches on fishing vessels. Why is this rule important?** **Model Answer:** > The **classification society rule** states that the drum barrel diameter $D$ must satisfy $D \geq 20\,d$, where $d$ is the wire-rope diameter. For smaller fishing winches, a relaxed ratio of $D \geq 15\,d$ is sometimes accepted. > > **Importance:** The rule limits the bending stress in the rope wires during spooling. A drum that is too small causes high cyclic bending fatigue and drastically shortens rope life. It also prevents the rope from "kinking" when paying out under load, which is critical during trawling operations where dynamic loads are high. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For Part-B questions, the model answer MUST contain: > - A labelled **diagram** (carries **2 marks** out of 14). > - All **assumptions listed explicitly** (carries 1 mark). > - **Units** in every numerical step (losing units = −½ mark per occurrence). > - Final answer **boxed**. > > Skipping the diagram or omitting units is the **#1 reason** students lose 3–5 marks on a 14-mark question. **Never write a Part-B answer without a sketch!** --- ### Question A (14 Marks) #### (a) With the help of a neat sketch, describe the deck machinery arrangement on a typical stern trawler. Discuss the design considerations that govern this arrangement. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer Outline:** 1. **Sketch (2 marks)** — top-view GA showing: - Forward: anchor windlass + mooring winches at bow. - Midship: wheelhouse, auxiliary crane, mast. - Aft working deck: twin trawl winches, net drum, gallows, stern ramp. 2. **Design considerations (5 marks)** — discuss any FIVE of the following: - Longitudinal placement to maintain trim. - Symmetry about centreline to avoid permanent list. - Adequate clear working area (≥ 1.5 m walking space around machinery). - Short hydraulic / shaft runs for high efficiency. - Crew safety per ILO C188 (no one in the snap-back zone). - Free-surface effect from fish-hold flooding. - Power transmission layout (avoid crossing of high-pressure lines). --- #### (b) A trawl winch drum has a barrel diameter $D = 500$ mm. It must wind wire rope of diameter $d = 22$ mm over $N = 6$ layers. The flange width is $W = 380$ mm. Calculate: **(i)** the total rope length wound across all six layers assuming one complete turn on each layer, and **(ii)** the total rope capacity in metres if each layer accommodates the maximum possible turns. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **(i) Length for one turn per layer** $$L_1 = \pi N(D + Nd) = \pi \times 6 \times (500 + 6 \times 22)\;\text{mm}$$ $$L_1 = \pi \times 6 \times (500 + 132) = \pi \times 6 \times 632 = 11\,909.8\;\text{mm}$$ $$\boxed{L_1 \approx 11.91\;\text{m}}$$ **(ii) Total turns per layer** Maximum number of turns in the outermost (6th) layer: $$n_{t,6} = \frac{W - d}{d} = \frac{380 - 22}{22} \approx 16.3 \Rightarrow \mathbf{16\;turns}$$ For inner layers, the available width increases as the rope builds outward, so the inner-layer turn count is slightly higher. For a conservative estimate (commonly accepted in KTU solutions), use the outermost-layer turn count: $$L_{total} = L_1 \times n_{t,6} = 11.91 \times 16 = 190.6\;\text{m}$$ $$\boxed{L_{total} \approx 190.6\;\text{m}}$$ > [!NOTE] > A more rigorous derivation would compute turn count layer by layer using $n_{t,n} = (W - 2(n-1)d)/d$, then sum each layer's length. The examiner will accept either method as long as the logic is shown. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) #### (a) Explain in detail the different types of winches used on fishing vessels. Compare their applications, drive systems, and braking mechanisms. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer Outline:** 1. **Trawl Winches** — high-pull, two-drum or split-drum; hydraulic drive; band brakes. Used on stern trawlers and side trawlers. 2. **Line Haulers** — for longline and tuna pole fishing; smaller pull, higher speed; often motor-driven with disc brakes. 3. **Seine Winches** — very high pull, large drum; used on purse seiners; often twin-drum with synchronous control. 4. **Mooring / Warping Winches** — moderate pull, intermittent duty; combined with anchor windlass. 5. **Auxiliary Winches** — small pull, lifting jobs; electric or hand-operated. 6. **Comparison Table (5 marks):** present a tabular comparison on drive type, line pull, line speed, brake type, and application. | Winch Type | Typical Pull (kN) | Drive | Brake | Application | |:----------:|:-----------------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----------:| | Trawl | 40 – 120 | Hydraulic | Band brake | Stern/side trawling | | Seine | 80 – 250 | Hydraulic | Band + disc | Purse seining | | Line hauler | 5 – 20 | Electric / hyd | Disc | Longline, pole | | Mooring | 30 – 80 | Electric / hyd | Band | Berthing | | Auxiliary | 5 – 30 | Manual / electric | Spring-loaded | Stores, catch | --- #### (b) A trawl winch on a fishing vessel develops a maximum line pull of $F = 75$ kN at a line speed of $v = 50$ m/min. The mechanical efficiency of the gear train and hydraulic motor is $\eta = 0.80$. Calculate: **(i)** the shaft power required, **(ii)** the holding torque at the drum if the effective drum radius (mid-rope) is $R = 280$ mm, and **(iii)** the list angle developed during a single-side haul when the haul force is $F_{haul} = 60$ kN acting at a transverse lever arm $y = 4.5$ m. Take displacement $W = 1800$ kN and $\overline{GM} = 0.65$ m. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **(i) Shaft power** $$P = \frac{F \cdot v}{60\,\eta} = \frac{75 \times 50}{60 \times 0.80} = \frac{3750}{48} = 78.125\;\text{kW}$$ $$\boxed{P \approx 78.1\;\text{kW} \approx 105\;\text{HP}}$$ Applying a 15 % service factor: $P_{design} = 78.1 \times 1.15 \approx 89.9$ kW ⇒ select a **90 kW** hydraulic motor. **(ii) Holding torque** $$T_{hold} = F \cdot R = 75 \times 0.280 = 21.0\;\text{kN·m}$$ $$\boxed{T_{hold} = 21.0\;\text{kN·m}}$$ **(iii) List angle** Heeling moment: $$M_{heel} = F_{haul} \cdot y = 60 \times 4.5 = 270\;\text{kN·m}$$ For small angles: $$\tan\phi = \frac{M_{heel}}{W \cdot \overline{GM}} = \frac{270}{1800 \times 0.65} = \frac{270}{1170} = 0.2308$$ $$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.2308) \approx 13.0°$$ $$\boxed{\phi \approx 13°}$$ > [!WARNING] > This list angle **exceeds** the IMO working-condition limit of ~12°. The examiner would deduct 1 mark here and expect the student to **comment on remediation** (e.g., reduce haul speed, increase ballast, use symmetrical twin-rig haul, or improve GM by relocating heavy weights lower). Always conclude a stability numerical with a **pass/fail remark**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Last-minute revision checklist — print this and read 30 minutes before the exam:** - ☐ **Definition:** Deck machinery = all mechanical equipment on the open deck used for fishing, mooring, and auxiliary operations. - ☐ **Five groups:** Mooring/Anchoring, Fishing Winches, Net Handling, Lifting Gear, Auxiliary. - ☐ **Key rule:** $D \geq 20 d$ (drum-to-rope ratio for wire-rope winches). - ☐ **Drum capacity formula:** $L = \pi N (D + N d)$ — derived from sum of odd numbers $1+3+5+\dots+(2N-1)=N^2$. - ☐ **Power formula (SI):** $P\,[\text{kW}] = Fv/(60\eta)$ when $F$ in kN, $v$ in m/min. - ☐ **Power formula (Legacy):** $P\,[\text{HP}] = Fv/(75\eta)$ when $F$ in kgf, $v$ in m/s. - ☐ **Holding torque:** $T = F \cdot R$ at effective drum radius. - ☐ **Band brake (capstan):** $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$ and $F_{brake} \cdot a = (T_1 - T_2) R_{drum}$. - ☐ **List angle:** $\tan\phi = M_{heel}/(W \overline{GM})$ — IMO limit ~12° in working condition. - ☐ **Arrangement principles:** Longitudinal balance, transverse symmetry, short power runs, ergonomics, ILO C188 safety zones. - ☐ **Always include:** labelled sketch, units in every line, assumptions listed, final answer boxed. - ☐ **Pitfall to avoid:** forgetting the **service factor** (typically 1.10 – 1.20) when selecting the prime mover. - ☐ **Pitfall to avoid:** computing rope length per layer and **forgetting to multiply by number of turns per layer**. - ☐ **Pitfall to avoid:** stating the drum capacity without converting **mm → m** in the final answer. - ☐ **Regulatory hooks to memorise:** IMO Load Line Convention, ILO C188, IRS Rules for Fishing Vessels (Part 2, Chapter 4 — Deck Machinery). --- **End of Module 2 Notes — Deck Machinery Arrangement** *(PENST754, KTU 2024 Scheme)*

topics.title: Crew Accommodation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Crew Accommodation # Crew Accommodation in Fishing Vessels ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Crew Accommodation** refers to the comprehensive arrangement, sizing, and outfitting of all onboard spaces dedicated to the **habitation, well-being, hygiene, rest, and operational support of the fishing vessel's crew**. It is a specialized branch of naval architecture governed by international maritime conventions, national maritime safety codes, and classification society rules. The discipline ensures that crew spaces satisfy minimum statutory requirements for **floor area, headroom, ventilation, lighting, heating, sanitary provisions, noise control, fire safety, and ergonomics** during prolonged operations at sea. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Design of Fishing Vessels*, crew accommodation is studied as a critical sub-system of the vessel's general arrangement, directly influencing: - **Hull form and freeboard determination** - **Superstructure sizing and center of gravity (KG) calculations** - **Stability criteria** (added weight above waterline) - **Economic feasibility** of the vessel > [!IMPORTANT] > **Statutory Note for KTU Examinations:** > Crew accommodation in Indian fishing vessels is primarily governed by: > 1. **ILO Work in Fishing Convention, 2007 (No. 188)** – The international benchmark. > 2. **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958 (India)** – Sections 356–359 covering accommodation. > 3. **Indian Fishing Vessels Rules (Draft/Regional Directives)** – Administered by the Ministry of Fisheries / Mercantile Marine Departments. > 4. **Classification Society Rules** – IRS (Indian Register of Shipping) / DNV / Lloyd's Register supplemental rules. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a fishing vessel as a **floating township** that must remain self-sufficient for weeks or months at sea. The crew cannot commute home each evening — they live, sleep, eat, bathe, and recover from grueling labor *inside* the vessel. Therefore, crew accommodation is analogous to designing a **miniature serviced apartment complex** mounted on a constantly rocking, salt-sprayed, vibration-prone platform. To make the analogy concrete: - The **wheelhouse** is the office. - The **engine room** is the factory. - The **fish hold** is the warehouse. - The **accommodation block** is the *combined hotel + dormitory + hospital + restaurant* — all within a few hundred square meters. **The "Why"**: Bad accommodation causes fatigue, illness, accidents, and crew turnover. Maritime authorities discovered historically (post-WWII) that vessels with cramped, damp, poorly lit quarters suffered **higher accident rates and lower catches**. Hence, accommodation is not a luxury — it is a **safety-critical, productivity-critical** design parameter. ### 1.3 Key Quantitative Benchmarks (KTU High-Yield Facts) | Parameter | Standard Value | Source | |---|---|---| | Minimum headroom | **2.00 m** (2.03 m in ILO 188) | ILO 188 / IRS Rules | | Floor area per crew (overtime/seasonal) | **1.4 m² (≈ 15 ft²)** | ILO 188 Annex III | | Floor area per crew (regular berths) | **2.0 – 2.5 m²** | IRS / Lloyd's | | Free ventilation rate (no AC) | **≥ 6 air changes/hour** | ILO 188 | | Mechanical ventilation supply | **≥ 30 m³/hour per person** | IMO / IRS | | Sleeping room illumination | **≥ 150 lux** | ILO 188 | | Mess room illumination | **≥ 150 lux** | ILO 188 | | Maximum ambient noise in berths | **≤ 60 dB(A)** | MLC 2007 | | Maximum noise in mess/recreation | **≤ 65 dB(A)** | MLC 2007 | | Sanitary fixture ratio (toilet:crew) | **1:6** | ILO 188 | | Bath/shower ratio | **1:8** | ILO 188 | > [!NOTE] > **Why these numbers matter for KTU exams:** Numerical questions on crew accommodation almost always involve verifying whether a proposed GA (General Arrangement) satisfies these statutory minima. Memorize the floor area and headroom figures above as "anchor constants." --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Hierarchical Logic of Crew Accommodation Design The design process follows a **top-down hierarchy**. Each decision cascades into spatial, structural, and economic consequences: 1. **Determine the Manning Scale (Crew Complement)** - Function of vessel size, fishing method, endurance. - For trawlers: roughly 1 crew per 15–25 GT plus specialized roles. - For purse seiners / longliners: typically 6–25 persons. 2. **Apply Statutory Floor-Area Standards** - For vessels < 24 m LOA, exemptions apply (see Section 2.4). - For vessels ≥ 24 m LOA, full ILO 188 compliance is mandatory if operating internationally. 3. **Zone the Accommodation Block** - **Sleeping quarters (berths)** – quietest part of vessel. - **Mess room / Galley (pantry)** – central, ventilated. - **Sanitary block (WC, shower)** – adjacent to sleeping. - **Recreation / Day room** – optional on smaller vessels. - **Hospital / First-aid cabin** – only if > 15 crew or > 3-day voyages. 4. **Resolve Spatial Conflicts** - Heat from galley vs. proximity to sleeping. - Noise from engine room vs. sleeping – requires acoustic isolation. - Drainage fall for sanitary fixtures – favors placement near hull bottom. 5. **Verify Stability and Freeboard Impact** - Accommodation block typically sits in the superstructure or raised deckhouse. - **High placement** = increases KG (vertical center of gravity) → reduces GM (metacentric height). - Designers must balance comfort with intact and damage stability criteria. 6. **Outfit with M&E Services** - Ventilation ducts, plumbing, electrical cabling, fire detection. - Each adds weight and consumes volume — must be deducted from usable floor area. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step | Step | Underlying Reason | |---|---| | Manning scale first | Accommodation size depends linearly on crew count; overestimating wastes space, underestimating violates law. | | Apply ILO 188 | These are ratified international minima; failure leads to detention in foreign ports. | | Zoning | Prevents cross-contamination (noise, odors, vibration), supports crew mental health. | | Spatial conflict resolution | A fishing vessel is integrated; bad zoning reduces operational efficiency and safety. | | Stability check | A heavy superstructure can capsize a small vessel in turning maneuvers — a leading cause of fishing fatalities. | | M&E integration | Ventilation loss through ducting can be 15–25% of nominal airflow; must be oversized at design stage. | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside any markdown table cell containing absolute-value or set notation. | # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Total minimum floor area | $A_{tot} = N_{crew} \cdot A_{per}$ | m² | Sum of all sleeping rooms | | 2 | Floor area per crew (regular) | $A_{per} = 2.0 \text{ to } 2.5$ | m²/person | ILO 188 / IRS | | 3 | Floor area per crew (seasonal) | $A_{per,s} = 1.4$ | m²/person | Vessels operating ≤ 24 h trips | | 4 | Headroom minimum | $h_{min} = 2.00$ | m | Statutory | | 5 | Required air volume | $V_{air} = N_{crew} \cdot Q_{vent} \cdot t$ | m³ | Ventilation sizing | | 6 | Ventilation rate (ILO 188) | $Q_{vent} \geq 30$ | m³/h per person | Mechanical | | 7 | Air changes per hour | $ACH = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{V_{room}}$ | h⁻¹ | Free ventilation ≥ 6 | | 8 | Lighting requirement | $E \geq 150$ | lux | Sleeping and mess rooms | | 9 | Noise limit (berths) | $L_p \leq 60$ | dB(A) | MLC 2007 | | 10 | Noise limit (mess) | $L_p \leq 65$ | dB(A) | MLC 2007 | | 11 | Total berth count | $N_{berth} = N_{crew} + N_{reserve}$ | — | Include 1–2 spare berths | | 12 | Galley sizing | $A_{galley} = 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \, m^2 \text{ per crew}$ | m² | Empirical rule | | 13 | Sanitary fixture count | $N_{WC} = \lceil N_{crew} / 6 \rceil$ | units | Ceiling function | | 14 | Shower count | $N_{shower} = \lceil N_{crew} / 8 \rceil$ | units | Ceiling function | | 15 | Hospital cabin requirement | Required if $N_{crew} \geq 15$ | — | Threshold rule | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas appear directly in KTU Part-B problems. A typical question asks: *"For a 28 m trawler with 12 crew, determine the minimum accommodation floor area, number of WCs, and shower stalls."* You must apply equations 1, 13, and 14 simultaneously. ### 2.4 Special Provision for Small Fishing Vessels Vessels under **24 m LOA** are often exempt from full ILO 188 compliance, but **Indian Rules** still mandate: - Separate sleeping quarters for men and women (when applicable). - Weather-tight sleeping spaces protected from engine/cargo odors. - At least **one toilet and one washbasin** for crews of 6+. - Ventilation openings of **≥ 5% of floor area** if no mechanical ventilation. ### 2.5 Real-World Utility The crew accommodation design directly affects: - **Crew retention** in commercial fisheries — a major economic issue in India. - **Insurance premiums** — non-compliant vessels are denied P&I (Protection & Indemnity) cover. - **Port State Control inspections** — detention risk in EU/USA ports. - **Export eligibility** of fish catches from non-compliant vessels (EU IUU regulation). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Minimum Accommodation Floor Area **Problem (KTU-Style):** A 26 m stern trawler operates with a crew complement of 14, including 2 officers. The vessel conducts multi-day voyages (5–7 days). Determine: (a) The minimum total sleeping-room floor area as per ILO 188. (b) The number of toilets and shower stalls required. #### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify Applicable Standard** The vessel is 26 m LOA (> 24 m threshold) and operates multi-day trips. Therefore, full **ILO Convention No. 188, Annex III** applies. The floor-area standard is the *regular berth* category, **not** the seasonal 1.4 m² allowance. Thus: $$A_{per} = 2.0 \text{ m}^2 \text{ per person}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate Total Minimum Floor Area** Using Equation (1) from the cheat sheet: $$A_{tot} = N_{crew} \cdot A_{per}$$ Substituting $N_{crew} = 14$ and $A_{per} = 2.0 \text{ m}^2$: $$ \begin{aligned} A_{tot} &= 14 \times 2.0 \text{ m}^2 \\ A_{tot} &= 28.0 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Distribute by Rank (Officers vs. Crew)** Although ILO 188 sets the minimum, naval architectural practice gives officers larger cabins. A typical split: $$ \begin{aligned} A_{officers} &= 2 \text{ officers} \times 3.5 \text{ m}^2 \text{ per officer} = 7.0 \text{ m}^2 \\ A_{crew} &= 12 \text{ ratings} \times 2.0 \text{ m}^2 \text{ per rating} = 24.0 \text{ m}^2 \\ A_{total, design} &= 7.0 + 24.0 = 31.0 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ > The design value (31.0 m²) exceeds the statutory minimum (28.0 m²) by ~10.7% — a reasonable safety/comfort margin. **Step 4 — Calculate Sanitary Fixture Count** Using Equations (13) and (14): $$ \begin{aligned} N_{WC} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{N_{crew}}{6} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{14}{6} \right\rceil = \lceil 2.33 \rceil = 3 \text{ toilets} \\ N_{shower} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{N_{crew}}{8} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{14}{8} \right\rceil = \lceil 1.75 \rceil = 2 \text{ showers} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Final Answer Summary** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Minimum statutory sleeping floor area} &= 28.0 \text{ m}^2 \\ \text{Practical design floor area} &= 31.0 \text{ m}^2 \\ \text{Required WCs} &= 3 \\ \text{Required showers} &= 2 \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Ventilation Sizing for Accommodation Block **Problem:** A purse seiner has 18 crew. The accommodation block consists of sleeping rooms totalling 60 m² floor area with 2.1 m headroom. Verify the natural ventilation requirement against ILO 188 standards. #### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Compute the Air Volume of the Block** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{room} &= A_{floor} \times h_{clear} \\ V_{room} &= 60 \text{ m}^2 \times 2.1 \text{ m} \\ V_{room} &= 126.0 \text{ m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Required Mechanical Ventilation Flow** Using ILO 188 minimum of 30 m³/h per person: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{req} &= N_{crew} \times 30 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \\ Q_{req} &= 18 \times 30 \\ Q_{req} &= 540 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Air Changes per Hour (ACH)** $$ \begin{aligned} ACH &= \dfrac{Q_{req}}{V_{room}} \\ ACH &= \dfrac{540 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}{126.0 \text{ m}^3} \\ ACH &= 4.29 \text{ h}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compare with ILO 188 Standard** ILO 188 mandates **≥ 6 air changes per hour** for free (natural) ventilation. Since the calculated ACH of 4.29 < 6, **natural ventilation alone is insufficient**. **Step 5 — Decision** The designer must specify **mechanical supply-and-exhaust ventilation** delivering at least 540 m³/h, or upgrade to deliver: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{min, ILO} &= 6 \times 126.0 = 756 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Mechanical fans of combined capacity ≥ 756 m³/h must be installed; duct losses of ~20% require nominal fan rating: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{fan} &= 756 \times 1.25 = 945 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Trade-offs in Accommodation Placement | Placement Option | Vertical CG Impact | Noise Exposure | Ventilation Ease | Stability Risk | KTU Trade-off Verdict | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Aft of midship, top deck | +++ (high) | Low (far from engine) | Excellent | High (raises KG) | Best for crew, worst for stability | | Midship, below main deck | + (low) | Medium | Poor (engine heat) | Low | Best for small vessels | | Forward superstructure | ++ | High (bow slamming) | Good | Medium | Avoid for > 24 m vessels | | Integrated deckhouse | + | Low–Medium | Good | Medium | Standard modern solution | > [!NOTE] > **Stability Equation Reminder:** Increasing KG reduces GM according to $GM = KM - KG$. KTU examiners often link accommodation placement to free-surface / vertical-CG effects. --- ### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Galley and Mess Room Sizing **Problem:** For the same 14-crew stern trawler, determine the minimum mess room seating area and galley size. #### 3.4.1 Solution **Step 1 — Mess Room Area** Standard practice (and IRS guidelines) requires mess seating of **0.9 m² per person**, assuming **two seatings** (since not all 14 crew eat simultaneously at sea). $$ \begin{aligned} A_{mess} &= \dfrac{N_{crew}}{2} \times 0.9 \\ A_{mess} &= 7 \times 0.9 \\ A_{mess} &= 6.3 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Galley Area** Using the empirical rule from Equation (12): $$ \begin{aligned} A_{galley} &= 14 \times 1.75 \text{ (mean of 1.5–2.0)} \\ A_{galley} &= 24.5 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > Some KTU questions test whether students distinguish between **stove + pantry** combined versus a dedicated galley. Combined pantry-mess setups on small vessels (< 18 m) can reduce galley area by ~30%. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Crew Accommodation Systems ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Vessel Hull Form] --> B[Define Manning Scale] B --> C{LOA >= 24 m?} C -- Yes --> D[Apply ILO 188 Annex III Full] C -- No --> E[Apply Indian Small Vessel Rules] D --> F[Statutory Floor Area Calc] E --> F F --> G[Zone Accommodation Block] G --> G1[Sleeping Quarters] G --> G2[Mess Room] G --> G3[Galley Pantry] G --> G4[Sanitary Block] G --> G5[Recreation Day Room] G --> G6[Hospital Cabin] G1 --> H[Outfit M and E Services] G2 --> H G3 --> H G4 --> H G5 --> H G6 --> H H --> H1[Ventilation Ducts] H --> H2[Plumbing System] H --> H3[Electrical Cabling] H --> H4[Fire Detection] H --> I[Verify Stability KG Impact] I --> J[Intact Stability OK?] J -- Yes --> K[Final General Arrangement] J -- No --> L[Relocate or Downsize Block] L --> G K --> M[Port State Compliance Check] M --> N[Approved Design] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Spatial Layout of Accommodation Block ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Accommodation_Block_Deck_Level direction TB P1[Officer Cabin 1
3.5 m2] P2[Officer Cabin 2
3.5 m2] P3[Crew Berth Room A
12 m2 six berths] P4[Crew Berth Room B
12 m2 six berths] P5[Mess Room
6.3 m2] P6[Galley
24.5 m2] P7[Sanitary Block WC 1 to 3 plus Shower 1 to 2] P8[Hospital Cabin
if crew >= 15] end P1 --- P5 P2 --- P5 P3 --- P6 P4 --- P6 P5 --- P7 P6 --- P7 P3 --- P4 P1 --- P2 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Statutory Compliance Path ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Vessel Parameters] --> S1{Operating Area} S1 -- Coastal less than 24h --> R1[Indian Fishing Vessel Rules] S1 -- Coastal greater than 24h --> R2[Indian Rules plus ILO 188 Light] S1 -- International Waters --> R3[Full ILO 188 plus MLC 2007] S1 -- EU Port Calls --> R4[EU Directive plus ILO 188] R1 --> Q1[Min headroom 2.0 m] R2 --> Q2[Min headroom 2.03 m plus ACH 4] R3 --> Q3[Min headroom 2.03 m plus ACH 6 plus noise limits] R4 --> Q4[All R3 plus hospital cabin plus laundry] Q1 --> Z[GA Finalized] Q2 --> Z Q3 --> Z Q4 --> Z ``` ### 4.4 Layout Description for Students For KTU descriptive answers, sketch the following conceptual arrangement (top view) when describing crew accommodation: - **Port-side forward:** Officer cabins (1 + 1) — premium location, minimal motion. - **Starboard forward:** Crew berth room (6-person, 2-tier bunks). - **Aft of officer cabins:** Mess room — central social hub. - **Aft of mess:** Galley — bulkheaded from mess by a partial wall, with vent hood. - **Aft of galley:** Sanitary block — deck drains for wash-down. - **Top-deck or main passage:** Hospital cabin (if ≥ 15 crew). --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: Define "Crew Accommodation" as applied to fishing vessels. List any four statutory standards governing it. **Model Answer:** > Crew accommodation refers to all onboard spaces provided for the crew's residence, rest, hygiene, and welfare on a fishing vessel.
