> **Final KTU Board Mantra:** *"A fishing vessel is only as safe as her ballast — empty at sea, the only thing holding her up is good naval architecture and well-managed ballast."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulkhead Positions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bulkhead Positions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **bulkhead** in a fishing vessel is a transverse or longitudinal vertical partition wall, generally of steel or aluminum plating, that subdivides the hull into separate watertight or weathertight compartments. The strategic placement of these bulkheads — termed **Bulkhead Positions** — is governed by stability, subdivision, damage control, cargo (fish) handling, and structural continuity requirements as prescribed by classification societies and statutory regulations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In Module 2, *Bulkhead Positions* refers to the longitudinal framing, location rules (collision bulkhead, engine room bulkhead, fish hold bulkheads, peak bulkheads), the number of bulkheads, and the concept of **stepped bulkheads** characteristic of fishing trawlers with fine aft lines.

## 1.2 Classification of Bulkheads by Position

| Bulkhead Type | KTU 2024 Designation | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| Collision Bulkhead (CB) | Watertight transverse at stem | Prevents flooding after forward collision |
| Aft Peak Bulkhead (APB) | Watertight at rudder stock | Houses stern tube; protects propulsive machinery |
| Engine Room Bulkhead (ERB) | Forward and aft ER bulkheads | Isolates main machinery from cargo spaces |
| Fish Hold Bulkheads (FHB) | Multiple transverse | Divides catch into grades, prevents shifting |
| Bulkhead Deck/Tween Deck | Partial height | Supports refrigerated air circulation |
| Stepped Bulkhead | Offset athwartships | Permits hull narrowing aft without rectangular compartments |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Watermelon Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "A Ship is Like a Watermelon with Stops":**
> Imagine slicing a watermelon into wedges. Each cut keeps one bad slice from spoiling the whole fruit. Similarly, every bulkhead is a **slice** that limits flooding to one compartment. In a fishing vessel, however, the aft slices are also **stepped inward** (like cutting wedges of decreasing width) so the boat has a fine, hydrodynamic stern — this is the essence of *stepped bulkhead design* unique to trawlers.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Code References

- **Regulatory Body:** International Maritime Organization (IMO), under **SOLAS Chapter II-1** for subdivision and damage stability.
- **Classification Society Standards:** Indian Register of Shipping (IRS), Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, DNV — applied to fishing vessels via the *Code of Safety for Fishermen and Fishing Vessels*.
- **Standard position of CB:** Not less than **5%** and not more than **10%** of the length **L** from the forward perpendicular (FP), depending on the rule set.
- **Freeboard deck (FL):** Reference line for all bulkhead deck measurements.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Bulkhead Positions

The placement of bulkheads is **not arbitrary**. It is the result of four overlapping engineering constraints:

1. **Subdivision (Floodable Length Criterion):** After damage, the vessel must not sink. The required number of bulkheads is dictated by the permissible *floodable length* `l_f`.
2. **Structural Continuity:** Bulkheads act as ring frames, increasing hull girder strength and reducing panel buckling.
3. **Operational Layout (Fishing Logic):** Fish hold, engine room, brine tanks, fuel tanks, and crew spaces have *fixed* volumetric requirements that determine bulkhead spacing.
4. **Hull Form Geometry (Stepped Bulkhead Logic):** Fishing vessels taper sharply aft, so rectangular compartments would either waste space or weaken the hull — solved by stepping the bulkhead inboard.

## 2.2 Position of the Collision Bulkhead (CB)

The collision bulkhead is the single most important bulkhead on any ship.

### Key Rules (KTU / SOLAS aligned):
- The CB must be placed **forward** of the foremost extent of the machinery space.
- The distance `X_c` from FP to CB is given by:

$$
X_c = (0.05\,L) \;\;\text{to}\;\; (0.10\,L)
$$

where:
- $X_c$ = longitudinal position of collision bulkhead from FP (m).
- $L$ = rule length of the vessel (m).

- A **step** in the CB is permitted (e.g., to clear a bow thruster or bulbous bow recess), but it must remain within the **5%–10% L envelope**.
- The CB must extend **weathertight** to the freeboard deck.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stepped Collision Bulkhead — A KTU Favourite Question:**
> When a bulbous bow or forward thruster is fitted, the CB may have a recess. The upper edge must still be at the freeboard deck, and the forward extension must be watertight. The total "effective" CB plane must be at least **5% L abaft FP**.

## 2.3 Position of the Aft Peak Bulkhead (APB)

The APB is located at the **after end** of the vessel, just forward of the rudder trunk. It separates the **steering gear / stern tube space** from the machinery or fish hold.

$$
X_{APB} = L - L_{AP}
$$

where:
- $X_{APB}$ = position of aft peak bulkhead from FP (m).
- $L_{AP}$ = length of aft peak = **0.03 L to 0.05 L** (typical for fishing trawlers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The APB must be watertight up to the bulkhead deck and must not be pierced by any pipe carrying sea-water or fuel below the flood level.

## 2.4 Engine Room Bulkhead Positions

The machinery space is enclosed by:
- **Forward Engine Room Bulkhead (FERB):** Usually situated just aft of the collision bulkhead by ~0.10L to 0.25L of L.
- **Aft Engine Room Bulkhead (AERB):** Often doubles as the forward boundary of the fish hold or refrigerated space.

For a typical fishing trawler of length L = 25 m, the engine room is centred amidships at about **0.45L to 0.60L**.

## 2.5 Fish Hold Bulkhead Spacing

Fish holds require:
- **Temperature zoning** (RSW tanks, ice holds, dry hold).
- **Catch separation by species / grade.**
- **Even distribution of weight** to maintain trim.

Typical spacing:
- 3 to 5 transverse bulkheads in the cargo region.
- Compartment length: **2.5 m to 4.0 m** (allowing ~5–10 tonnes of catch per compartment).

$$
n_{FHB} = \frac{L_{FH}}{L_{comp}}
$$

where:
- $n_{FHB}$ = number of fish hold bulkheads.
- $L_{FH}$ = fish hold length (m).
- $L_{comp}$ = compartment length (m), typically **2.5–4.0 m**.

## 2.6 The Stepped Bulkhead (Fishing Vessel Specialty)

A fishing trawler with a fine **aft body** (e.g., L/B > 5.5) has narrowing frames aft. A conventional rectangular compartment would:
- Result in unusable triangular voids, or
- Force the bulkhead to be sloped (which violates SOLAS rules for vertical subdivision).

**Solution:** Step the bulkhead inboard at a frame, creating a *double-plane* compartment. The step is usually taken at a deck or platform level, never arbitrarily.

$$
\theta_{bh} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}\right)
$$

where:
- $\theta_{bh}$ = effective angle of stepped bulkhead (deg).
- $\Delta x$ = longitudinal step length (m).
- $\Delta y$ = transverse step offset (m).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Rule:** The maximum permissible *offset* in a stepped bulkhead is generally **1.5 m** transverse per **3.0 m** longitudinal, ensuring the slope does not exceed ~30° from the vertical plane.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X_c = 0.05\,L$ to $0.10\,L$ | $X_c, L$ | m | Collision bulkhead position |
| 2 | $X_{APB} = L - L_{AP}$ | $X_{APB}, L_{AP}$ | m | Aft peak bulkhead position |
| 3 | $L_{AP} = 0.03\,L$ to $0.05\,L$ | $L_{AP}, L$ | m | Aft peak length |
| 4 | $n_{FHB} = L_{FH} / L_{comp}$ | $n_{FHB}, L_{FH}, L_{comp}$ | m, dimensionless | Fish hold bulkhead count |
| 5 | $X_{FERB} \approx 0.40\,L$ | $X_{FERB}, L$ | m | Forward ER bulkhead |
| 6 | $X_{AERB} \approx 0.60\,L$ | $X_{AERB}, L$ | m | Aft ER bulkhead |
| 7 | $\theta_{bh} = \tan^{-1}(\Delta y / \Delta x)$ | $\theta_{bh}, \Delta y, \Delta x$ | deg, m | Stepped bulkhead angle |
| 8 | $L_{comp,opt} = 2.5 \text{ to } 4.0\;\text{m}$ | $L_{comp,opt}$ | m | Optimum hold length |
| 9 | $l_f = f(L, B, d, n)$ | $l_f$ | m | Floodable length (subdivision) |
| 10 | $P_{bh} = \sigma \cdot t \cdot h$ | $P_{bh}, \sigma, t, h$ | Pa, m | Bulkhead plate strength check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Bulkhead positioning is critical during **vessel damage scenarios** — insurers and classification societies verify that flooding of **one** compartment leaves a residual freeboard of at least **1/3 of the intact freeboard** in tropical fisheries. The stepwise logic also directly impacts **fish quality preservation** by isolating brine tanks from the engine heat.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Position of Collision Bulkhead (CB)

**Given:** Length of fishing trawler $L = 30\;\text{m}$, gross tonnage considerations as per SOLAS.

**Goal:** Find the permissible range of $X_c$ from the forward perpendicular (FP).

**Step 1 — Apply the SOLAS lower bound:**

$$
X_{c,\min} = 0.05 \times L = 0.05 \times 30
$$

$$
X_{c,\min} = 1.5\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the SOLAS upper bound:**

$$
X_{c,\max} = 0.10 \times L = 0.10 \times 30
$$

$$
X_{c,\max} = 3.0\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The CB of a 30 m fishing vessel must lie in the longitudinal band **1.5 m ≤ $X_c$ ≤ 3.0 m** from the FP.

> [!NOTE]
> A **stepped** CB (e.g., to clear a forward mooring winch) is acceptable provided the *effective* plane of the CB — measured along the *mean* transverse plane — falls within this range.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Number and Position of Fish Hold Bulkheads

**Given:**
- Rule length $L = 30\;\text{m}$.
- Engine room extends from $0.40L$ to $0.60L$.
- Fish hold is between the engine room and the forward peak/collision bulkhead.

**Step 1 — Compute fish hold longitudinal extent:**

$$
X_{FERB} = 0.40 \times 30 = 12.0\;\text{m}
$$

$$
X_{CB} = 0.07 \times 30 = 2.1\;\text{m}\;\;\text{(assume midpoint of CB range)}
$$

$$
L_{FH} = X_{FERB} - X_{CB} = 12.0 - 2.1 = 9.9\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Choose compartment length:**

For a 30 m trawler, $L_{comp} = 3.3\;\text{m}$ is a sensible choice (allows ~3 t of iced fish per compartment).

**Step 3 — Compute number of bulkheads:**

$$
n_{FHB} = \frac{L_{FH}}{L_{comp}} = \frac{9.9}{3.3} = 3
$$

**Step 4 — Recompute the exact positions (including the engine room forward bulkhead and the CB):**

The fish hold is therefore subdivided by **2 internal bulkheads** (since 3 compartments need 2 internal partitions), at:

$$
X_1 = X_{CB} + L_{comp} = 2.1 + 3.3 = 5.4\;\text{m}
$$

$$
X_2 = X_1 + L_{comp} = 5.4 + 3.3 = 8.7\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Summary of longitudinal bulkhead positions (from FP):**

| Bulkhead ID | Position $X$ (m) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| CB | 2.1 | Collision |
| FHB-1 | 5.4 | Fish hold subdivision |
| FHB-2 | 8.7 | Fish hold subdivision |
| FERB | 12.0 | Engine room forward |
| AERB | 18.0 | Engine room aft |
| APB | 28.5 | Aft peak (assuming $L_{AP}=0.05L = 1.5\;\text{m}$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The vessel now has **6 watertight transverse bulkheads** — a typical configuration for a 30 m stern trawler.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Stepped Bulkhead Geometry

**Problem:** A 25 m trawler has a half-beam at frame 0 (mid-hold) of **4.5 m**, while at frame 10 (aft hold) the half-beam is only **3.2 m**. A vertical bulkhead at frame 10 in the same plane as frame 0 would not reach the hull plating.

**Step 1 — Compute the transverse gap:**

$$
\Delta y = 4.5 - 3.2 = 1.3\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Longitudinal spacing between frames (assume 0.6 m frame spacing):**

$$
\Delta x = 10 \times 0.6 = 6.0\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Stepped bulkhead angle:**

$$
\theta_{bh} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.3}{6.0}\right)
$$

$$
\theta_{bh} = \tan^{-1}(0.2167) \approx 12.24^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — Compliance check:**

Since $12.24^{\circ} < 30^{\circ}$ (per KTU / SOLAS tolerance), the **step is acceptable** as a stepped bulkhead.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Position Rules Summary

| Bulkhead | Position from FP (fraction of L) | Special Conditions | KTU / SOLAS Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Collision Bulkhead | $0.05\,L$ – $0.10\,L$ | May be stepped; weathertight to freeboard deck | SOLAS II-1/11 |
| Chain Locker Bulkhead | Just aft of CB | Sufficient to store chain locker | Classification rule |
| Forward ER Bulkhead | $0.35\,L$ – $0.45\,L$ | Continuous to bulkhead deck | SOLAS II-1/26 |
| Aft ER Bulkhead | $0.55\,L$ – $0.65\,L$ | Continuous; oiltight below | SOLAS II-1/26 |
| Fish Hold Bulkhead(s) | $0.15\,L$ – $0.40\,L$ | Weathertight above WL | IMO Fishing Vessel Code |
| Aft Peak Bulkhead | $L - (0.03\,L$ to $0.05\,L)$ | Watertight; houses stern tube | SOLAS II-1/25 |
| Tween Deck Bulkhead | Variable | Supports RSW ducting | Class rules |

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Composite Bulkhead Layout

**Vessel:** 28 m side trawler.
- $L = 28\;\text{m}$
- $B = 7.5\;\text{m}$
- $d = 3.0\;\text{m}$

**Step 1 — Collision bulkhead position:**

$$
X_c = 0.07 \times 28 = 1.96\;\text{m}\;\;(approx.)
$$

**Step 2 — Engine room extent (centre at 0.5L, length ~0.20L):**

$$
X_{FERB} = 0.5L - 0.10L = 0.40 \times 28 = 11.2\;\text{m}
$$

$$
X_{AERB} = 0.5L + 0.10L = 0.60 \times 28 = 16.8\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Fish hold length:**

$$
L_{FH} = X_{FERB} - X_c = 11.2 - 1.96 = 9.24\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Choose $L_{comp} = 3.0\;\text{m}$ → $n_{FHB} = 9.24/3.0 = 3.08$ → use 3 compartments, two internal bulkheads.**

**Step 5 — Recompute with adjusted compartment length:**

$$
L_{comp,adj} = \frac{L_{FH}}{3} = \frac{9.24}{3} = 3.08\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Final positions of fish hold bulkheads:**

$$
X_{FHB,1} = 1.96 + 3.08 = 5.04\;\text{m}
$$

$$
X_{FHB,2} = 5.04 + 3.08 = 8.12\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 7 — Aft peak bulkhead:**

$$
X_{APB} = L - 0.04L = 28 - 1.12 = 26.88\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 8 — Final bulkhead schedule:**

| Bulkhead | Position (m from FP) | Type |
|---|---|---|
| CB | 1.96 | Watertight transverse |
| FHB-1 | 5.04 | Weathertight transverse |
| FHB-2 | 8.12 | Weathertight transverse |
| FERB | 11.20 | Watertight transverse |
| AERB | 16.80 | Watertight transverse |
| APB | 26.88 | Watertight transverse |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation:** The spacing between any two watertight bulkheads in a 28 m fishing vessel is **3.08 m to 5.08 m**, well within the 0.033L + 0.04B (where 0.033×28 + 0.04×7.5 = 0.924 + 0.300 = 1.224 m *minimum* floodable length is satisfied).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Longitudinal Bulkhead Arrangement — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow** of a fishing trawler's bulkhead arrangement from bow to stern, indicating each compartment's purpose and the bulkhead type that bounds it.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[BP1\nForward Peak\nChain Locker]:::peakStyle
    nodeB[CB\nCollision Bulkhead\n0.07L from FP]:::watertightStyle
    nodeC[COMP1\nBow Thruster /\nForward Store]:::normalStyle
    nodeD[FHB1\nFish Hold 1\nRSW Tank]:::fishStyle
    nodeE[FHB2\nFish Hold 2\nIced Catch]:::fishStyle
    nodeF[FERB\nForward Engine\nRoom Bulkhead]:::watertightStyle
    nodeG[ER\nEngine Room\nMain Machinery]:::machineryStyle
    nodeH[AERB\nAft Engine\nRoom Bulkhead]:::watertightStyle
    nodeI[COMP4\nRefrigeration\nPlant Room]:::normalStyle
    nodeJ[APB\nAft Peak Bulkhead\n0.96L from FP]:::watertightStyle
    nodeK[BP2\nStern Tube /\nRudder Stock]:::peakStyle

    nodeA -->|FP| nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK

    classDef watertightStyle fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef fishStyle fill:#0d9488,stroke:#fef3c7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef machineryStyle fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#fef3c7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef peakStyle fill:#374151,stroke:#fef3c7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef normalStyle fill:#52525b,stroke:#fef3c7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Each blue/red/teal block represents a compartment, and the connecting arrows indicate forward-to-aft progression. Yellow-edged blocks are **watertight bulkheads** — the structural safety backbone of the vessel. Teal blocks are **fish holds** (operational spaces).

---

## 4.2 Stepped Bulkhead Geometry — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Forward Compartment\nFull Hull Breadth]:::fullStyle
    nodeB[STEP A\nLongitudinal Step Location\nX equals 6.0 m]:::stepStyle
    nodeC[Aft Compartment\nReduced Breadth]:::reducedStyle
    nodeD[Transverse Offset\nDelta y equals 1.3 m]:::offsetStyle
    nodeE[Effective Angle\nTheta equals 12.24 deg]:::angleStyle

    nodeA -->|Vertical bulkhead plane| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Recessed bulkhead plane| nodeC
    nodeA --- nodeD
    nodeC --- nodeD
    nodeB --- nodeE
    nodeC --- nodeE

    classDef fullStyle fill:#0369a1,stroke:#fef3c7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef stepStyle fill:#ea580c,stroke:#fef3c7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef reducedStyle fill:#075985,stroke:#fef3c7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef offsetStyle fill:#a16207,stroke:#fef3c7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef angleStyle fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fef3c7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **step** in the bulkhead at the location of the second frame from amidships (Step A) is what allows the vessel to maintain a fine aft body without violating SOLAS subdivision rules. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests this concept under "Bulkhead Positions".

---

## 4.3 Damage Subdivision Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Damage at COMP2\nFish Hold 1 Breach]:::damageStyle
    nodeB[Flooding contained\nto COMP2 only]:::containStyle
    nodeC[Survival Condition\nflooded length l_f]:::survivalStyle
    nodeD[Intact Freeboard\npreserved greater than 1 third]:::intactStyle

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD

    classDef damageStyle fill:#dc2626,stroke:#fef3c7,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef containStyle fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#fef3c7,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef survivalStyle fill:#16a34a,stroke:#fef3c7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef intactStyle fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#fef3c7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Meaning:** The bulkhead arrangement above is a **defense-in-depth** design. A single breach at fish hold 1 cannot flood the engine room because the forward engine room bulkhead (FERB) blocks the water's path. This is exactly what KTU Module 2 tests.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a collision bulkhead and state the SOLAS rules for its longitudinal position in a fishing vessel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **collision bulkhead** is a watertight transverse bulkhead fitted at the forward end of the vessel, designed to prevent the entire ship from flooding in the event of a forward collision. As per **SOLAS Chapter II-1 Regulation 11**, the collision bulkhead must be located at a distance of **5% to 10% of the rule length `L`** from the forward perpendicular (FP). It must extend **weathertight to the freeboard deck**, and **no door, manhole, or opening** is permitted below the bulkhead deck. In fishing trawlers, the CB may be **stepped** to clear a bow thruster or bulbous bow recess, provided the *effective plane* of the bulkhead still lies within the prescribed 5%–10% L band. *(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: 5–10% rule with units; 1 mark: weathertight + stepped clause.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a stepped bulkhead, and why is it commonly used in fishing trawlers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **stepped bulkhead** is a transverse watertight partition that is **offset inboard (or outboard)** at a certain frame, creating a *double-plane* compartment instead of a single rectangular one. This arrangement is used in fishing trawlers with **fine aft lines (L/B > 5.5)** where the hull narrows rapidly. A single vertical bulkhead would either leave large **unusable triangular voids** or force the bulkhead to be sloped — which violates SOLAS regulations requiring bulkheads to be **transverse and vertical**. The stepped configuration **maintains hull strength**, preserves **damage stability**, and allows efficient use of the tapered aft body. *(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: reason – fine aft body; 1 mark: SOLAS rule + advantage.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Bulkhead Layout for a 30 m Fishing Trawler**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with 2024 Scheme]**

A fishing trawler has a **rule length L = 30 m**, breadth **B = 7.8 m**, and design draft **d = 3.2 m**. The vessel is to be fitted with a **collision bulkhead (CB)**, an **engine room of length 0.20L centred amidships**, an **aft peak of length 0.05L**, and **fish hold bulkheads** dividing the fish hold into compartments of approximately 3 m length.

**(a)** Determine the longitudinal positions of the **collision bulkhead**, **forward engine room bulkhead (FERB)**, **aft engine room bulkhead (AERB)**, and **aft peak bulkhead (APB)**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Determine the **number and positions of fish hold bulkheads** required, and draw a **sketch of the bulkhead arrangement** indicating all bulkhead types. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Position of Major Watertight Bulkheads**

**Step 1 — Collision bulkhead position (using the lower-mid value of 0.07L for a typical 30 m trawler):**

$$
X_c = 0.07 \times L = 0.07 \times 30 = 2.1\;\text{m}\;\;\text{from FP}
$$

**Step 2 — Engine room extent (centred at 0.50L, total length 0.20L):**

$$
X_{FERB} = 0.50L - 0.10L = 0.40L = 0.40 \times 30 = 12.0\;\text{m}\;\;\text{from FP}
$$

$$
X_{AERB} = 0.50L + 0.10L = 0.60L = 0.60 \times 30 = 18.0\;\text{m}\;\;\text{from FP}
$$

**Step 3 — Aft peak bulkhead position:**

$$
L_{AP} = 0.05L = 0.05 \times 30 = 1.5\;\text{m}
$$

$$
X_{APB} = L - L_{AP} = 30 - 1.5 = 28.5\;\text{m}\;\;\text{from FP}
$$

**(a) Final Answer Table:**

| Bulkhead | Position (m from FP) |
|---|---|
| CB | 2.1 |
| FERB | 12.0 |
| AERB | 18.0 |
| APB | 28.5 |

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 marks CB, 2 marks FERB/AERB, 1.5 marks APB, 1.5 marks tabulation.)*

---

**Part (b) — Number and Position of Fish Hold Bulkheads**

**Step 1 — Length of fish hold:**

$$
L_{FH} = X_{FERB} - X_c = 12.0 - 2.1 = 9.9\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of compartments (using $L_{comp} \approx 3.0\;\text{m}$):**

$$
n = \frac{L_{FH}}{L_{comp}} = \frac{9.9}{3.0} = 3.3 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{round to } 3 \;\text{compartments}
$$

**Step 3 — Required internal bulkheads:**

For 3 compartments, **2 internal fish hold bulkheads (FHB-1 and FHB-2)** are needed.

**Step 4 — Recompute the exact positions with adjusted compartment length:**

$$
L_{comp,adj} = \frac{L_{FH}}{3} = \frac{9.9}{3} = 3.3\;\text{m}
$$

$$
X_{FHB,1} = X_c + L_{comp,adj} = 2.1 + 3.3 = 5.4\;\text{m}
$$

$$
X_{FHB,2} = X_{FHB,1} + L_{comp,adj} = 5.4 + 3.3 = 8.7\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Sketch of Bulkhead Arrangement:**

```
FP                                          AP
|-----CB---|--FHB1-|--FHB2-|---FERB---|---AERB---|-APB-|
0   1    2.1  3.3  5.4   3.3  8.7   3.3  12.0   6.0  18.0  10.5  28.5  1.5  30
[ PEAK ][ Bow Thruster ][ FH-1 ][ FH-2 ][ FH-3 ][ ENGINE ROOM ][ REFRIG ][ AP ]
```

**(b) Final Answer:** **2 fish hold bulkheads** at $X = 5.4\;\text{m}$ and $X = 8.7\;\text{m}$.

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 marks for L_FH and compartment count, 2 marks for exact X-positions, 2 marks for sketch, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Stepped Bulkhead Geometry & SOLAS Validation**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with 2024 Scheme]**

A 26 m fishing trawler with **L/B = 5.6** has a forward fish hold compartment at frame 5 with **half-beam 4.0 m**, and an aft fish hold compartment at frame 13 with **half-beam 3.0 m**. The frame spacing is **0.6 m**.

**(a)** Calculate the **transverse offset $\Delta y$**, the **longitudinal step $\Delta x$**, and the **effective angle $\theta_{bh}$** of the stepped bulkhead. Comment on whether the step complies with KTU / SOLAS practice. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Briefly explain **why a single sloped bulkhead (without stepping) is not allowed** in SOLAS-compliant fishing vessels, and list **three advantages** of the stepped bulkhead arrangement. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Step Geometry**

**Step 1 — Transverse offset:**

$$
\Delta y = 4.0 - 3.0 = 1.0\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Longitudinal step (frames 5 to 13, spacing 0.6 m):**

$$
\Delta x = (13 - 5) \times 0.6 = 8 \times 0.6 = 4.8\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective angle of stepped bulkhead:**

$$
\theta_{bh} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.0}{4.8}\right)
$$

$$
\theta_{bh} = \tan^{-1}(0.2083) = 11.77^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — SOLAS compliance check:**

The KTU / SOLAS practical limit for a stepped bulkhead is **$\theta_{bh} < 30^{\circ}$**.

Since $11.77^{\circ} \ll 30^{\circ}$, the step **comfortably complies** with the rule.

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 marks Δy, 2 marks Δx, 2 marks θ_bh calculation, 1 mark SOLAS comment.)*

---

**Part (b) — Why Sloped Bulkheads Are Disallowed & Advantages of Stepping**

**Step 1 — Why sloped bulkheads are not permitted:**

SOLAS Chapter II-1 mandates that subdivision bulkheads must be **transverse and vertical** to ensure that the *floodable length curve* and *permeability* calculations remain valid. A sloped bulkhead would:
- Distort the assumed **compartment volume** used in damage stability.
- Cause **uneven flooding** and asymmetric heeling moments.
- Interfere with the **rule-based probabilistic damage analysis**.
- Be physically impossible to align with a **fine aft hull form** without compromising plating.

**Step 2 — Three advantages of stepped bulkheads:**

1. **Hull form accommodation:** Allows the vessel to retain its **fine, hydrodynamic aft body** while still maintaining vertical bulkhead planes in the forward portion of each compartment.
2. **Structural integrity:** Stepping the bulkhead onto a **deck or platform** adds a secondary load path, increasing the **section modulus** of the hull girder.
3. **Damage stability:** Each stepped compartment behaves as an **independent watertight unit**, so flooding in the aft portion cannot propagate forward across the step.

*(7 marks breakdown: 3 marks for the SOLAS reasoning, 4 marks for the three advantages with technical depth.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 5%–10% L range** for the collision bulkhead — students often write "10% L" alone, losing 1 mark. **Always state the lower AND upper bound.**
> 2. **Conflating "ferry" and "fishing vessel" subdivision rules** — the IMO Code of Safety for Fishing Vessels is **less stringent** than SOLAS passenger rules. Do not cite passenger-ship 1-compartment or 2-compartment criteria.
> 3. **Using 0.50L for the engine room centre without explaining the 0.20L length** — examiners expect you to **state both** the centre and the extent.
> 4. **Mixing up "stepped" and "sloped"** — a stepped bulkhead has **two vertical planes offset athwartships**, while a sloped bulkhead is a **single inclined plane**. This distinction is a favourite 2-mark KTU question.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch in Part B** — a labelled bulkhead arrangement sketch carries **2 to 3 marks** by itself. Always include one.
> 6. **Ignoring units** — a $X_c$ in centimetres or $L_{comp}$ in feet will be marked wrong. Use **metres** throughout.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Bulkhead Positions**

- **Collision Bulkhead (CB):** Positioned at **0.05L to 0.10L** from FP. Watertight. Weathertight to freeboard deck. No openings below the bulkhead deck.
- **Aft Peak Bulkhead (APB):** Positioned at **L − (0.03L to 0.05L)**. Houses stern tube. Watertight to bulkhead deck.
- **Engine Room Bulkheads (FERB, AERB):** Engine room typically **0.40L to 0.60L**, centred at 0.50L. Both bulkheads are watertight.
- **Fish Hold Bulkheads (FHB):** 2–5 transverse bulkheads subdividing the fish hold into compartments of **2.5–4.0 m** length.
- **Stepped Bulkhead:** Two vertical planes offset athwartships; used for **fine aft body** vessels (L/B > 5.5). Maximum step angle **< 30°**.
- **Sloped Bulkhead:** **Not permitted** in SOLAS subdivision rules.
- **Floodable Length `l_f`:** Used in damage stability calculations; bulkhead spacing must be ≤ `l_f`.
- **Number of bulkheads (typical 30 m trawler):** **6 to 8 watertight transverse bulkheads.**
- **Frame spacing `Δx`:** Typically **0.5–0.6 m** for fishing trawlers.
- **Coding References:** SOLAS II-1, IMO Code of Safety for Fishermen and Fishing Vessels, IRS Rules.
- **Material hint:** Steel plating thickness for transverse bulkheads in 30 m trawlers: **4 mm to 6 mm**, with stiffeners at every frame.
- **Inspection point:** All bulkheads must be **tested for tightness** by hose test or chalk test before delivery.
- **Common KTU traps:** Mis-citing 1/3-compartment subdivision, confusing CB with peak bulkhead, omitting sketch in Part B.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Resistance of Fishing Vessels

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Design of Fishing Vessels (PENST754)**, **resistance** is defined as the component of the fluid force acting **opposite to the direction of motion** of a vessel, opposing its forward translation through water (and air) at a given speed, trim, and loading condition. For a fishing vessel, the *Total Resistance* ($R_T$) is conventionally expressed in **kilograms-force (kgf)** or **kiloNewtons (kN)**, and is the single most important parameter in the **Effective Power (EHP)** calculation, which drives the selection of the main engine, propeller, and fuel-burn profile of the vessel.

Mathematically, for a vessel moving at constant speed $V$ (in knots), the *Effective Horse Power* is:

$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{75} \quad \text{(metric HP)}$$

where $R_T$ is in **kgf** and $V$ in **knots**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In fishing vessel design, resistance is NOT a single number. It is a *vector sum* of several physically distinct components, each scaling differently with speed, displacement, form, and appendages (e.g., rudder, bilge keels, trawl gear). Mastering this decomposition is mandatory for the 14-mark problem questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **pushing a large wooden fishing canoe** through shallow lagoon water. You feel three distinct "push-backs":

1. **The sticky drag of water sliding along the wet hull** → that is **Frictional Resistance** ($R_F$).
2. **A series of ripples and waves your canoe pushes outward from its bow and stern** → that is **Wave-Making Resistance** ($R_W$).
3. **A churning, turbulent loss around the stern and sharp corners** → that is **Eddy / Residual Resistance** ($R_E$).

Now add the **wind pushing against your face** (Air Resistance, $R_{AA}$), the **rudder and keel fighting the flow** (Appendage Resistance, $R_{APP}$), and — unique to fishing — the **drag of nets, warps, trawl doors, and fishing gear being towed astern** (Fishing Gear Resistance, $R_{FG}$). The sum of all of these is what the propeller must overcome.

For a trawler hauling a full net, the fishing gear can contribute **20–35%** of the total resistance at operating speed.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Sea water density | $\rho$ | **1025 kg/m³** (standard, 15 °C, 35 ‰ salinity) |
| Fresh water density | $\rho$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Kinematic viscosity of sea water | $\nu$ | **1.188 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** at 15 °C |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| 1 knot | — | **0.5144 m/s** |
| Reynolds number criterion (turbulent) | $R_n$ | $> 5 \times 10^5$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For all KTU numerical problems, use $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ unless the question explicitly states otherwise. This convention is mandatory in KTU 2024 board evaluations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Decomposition of Total Resistance

The total resistance of a fishing vessel at a given speed and condition is the **scalar sum** of the following physically distinct components:

$$R_T = R_F + R_W + R_E + R_{APP} + R_{AA} + R_{FG}$$

Where:
- $R_F$ — **Frictional Resistance** (viscous skin friction along the wetted hull)
- $R_W$ — **Wave-Making Resistance** (energy radiated as surface gravity waves)
- $R_E$ — **Eddy / Residual Resistance** (form drag, viscous pressure drag, wave-breaking losses not captured by $R_F$ or $R_W$)
- $R_{APP}$ — **Appendage Resistance** (rudder, bilge keels, shaft brackets, sonar dome, etc.)
- $R_{AA}$ — **Air Resistance** (above-water hull, superstructure, wheelhouse)
- $R_{FG}$ — **Fishing Gear Resistance** (nets, trawl doors, warps, dan lenos — UNIQUE to fishing vessels)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a clean, well-formed fishing vessel hull at design speed, $R_{FG} = 0$ (no gear towed). However, during **trawling operations**, $R_{FG}$ is significant and the design must be validated for the *trawling condition* separately.

## 2.2 The ITTC 1978 Resistance Prediction Method (Standard for KTU Problems)

The **International Towing Tank Conference (ITTC) 1978 method** is the default prediction method used in KTU 2024 scheme problems. The approach is to compute frictional resistance analytically, then apply empirical **form factors** to capture viscous pressure effects, and an empirical **wave-resistance coefficient** to capture wave-making.

### 2.2.1 Frictional Resistance (ITTC 1957 Correlation Line)

The frictional resistance is computed using the **ITTC 1957 model-ship correlation line**:

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$$

where the Reynolds number is defined as:

$$R_n = \frac{V \cdot L_{WL}}{\nu}$$

with:
- $V$ = ship speed in **m/s**
- $L_{WL}$ = length at waterline in **meters**
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity in **m²/s** (use $1.188 \times 10^{-6}$ for sea water at 15 °C)

The frictional resistance in Newtons is then:

$$R_F = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot V^2 \cdot C_F \quad \text{[N]}$$

where $S$ is the wetted surface area in **m²**.

### 2.2.2 Form Factor ($k$)

The form factor $(1+k)$ multiplies the frictional resistance to account for the **viscous pressure (form) drag** arising from the three-dimensional shape of the hull. Typical values for fishing vessels:

| Hull Form | Form Factor $(1+k)$ |
|---|---|
| Fine, slender trawler | 1.10 – 1.20 |
| Conventional fishing vessel (blocky) | 1.20 – 1.35 |
| Full-form purse seiner | 1.30 – 1.50 |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 problems, the form factor is **always given in the question** or must be estimated using the **Holtrop approximation** $1+k = 0.93 + 0.4871 \cdot c_b \cdot (B/L_{WL})^1.06806 \cdot (T/L_{WL})^{0.46106} \cdot (L_{WL}/\nabla^{1/3})^{0.121563} \cdot (L^2_B/\nabla^{1/3})^{0.36486} \cdot (1-c_p)^{-0.604247}$ only if requested.

### 2.2.3 Wave-Making & Eddy Resistance (Residual Resistance)

The residual resistance coefficient $C_R$ is **empirical** and is read from standard series data (e.g., **Taylor Standard Series**, **Series 60**, or **BSRA / NPL high-speed series**). For fishing vessels, the standard reference is the **BSRA / Series 64** or **NPL round-bilge series** appropriate to the prismatic coefficient $C_P$.

The total resistance coefficient is:

$$C_T = \frac{R_T}{\frac{1}{2} \rho S V^2} = (1+k) C_F + C_R + C_{AA} + C_{APP}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds number | $R_n = VL_{WL}/\nu$ | — | Use $\nu = 1.188 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s (sea water, 15 °C) |
| 2 | ITTC 1957 frictional coefficient | $C_F = 0.075/(\log R_n - 2)^2$ | — | Valid for turbulent boundary layer |
| 3 | Frictional resistance | $R_F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^2 C_F$ | N | $S$ = wetted surface area |
| 4 | Effective Power | $EHP = R_T \cdot V / 75$ | metric HP | $R_T$ in kgf, $V$ in knots |
| 5 | Effective Power (SI) | $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ | W | $R_T$ in N, $V$ in m/s |
| 6 | Displacement speed coefficient | $C_V = V / \sqrt{gL_{WL}}$ | — | Used in Froude scaling |
| 7 | Froude number | $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL_{WL}}$ | — | Governs wave-making similarity |
| 8 | Powering factor (full form) | $C_P = \nabla / (L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T)$ | — | Typical fishing vessel: 0.55–0.70 |
| 9 | Wetted surface (Harvald) | $S = \nabla^{2/3} (3.4 + 0.5 L_{WL}/\nabla^{1/3})$ | m² | Approximate; valid for $C_P > 0.55$ |
| 10 | Air resistance coefficient | $C_{AA} = 0.001$ (typical) | — | $R_{AA} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{air} A_T V^2 C_{AA}$ |
| 11 | Appendage allowance | $R_{APP} = (0.04 \text{ to } 0.10) R_F$ | N | Use 0.05 if not given |
| 12 | Correlation allowance | $C_A = 0.0004$ | — | ITTC 1978 standard for full-scale |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Main engine selection:** EHP → Shaft Horse Power (SHP) → Brake Horse Power (BHP) using propulsive efficiency $\eta_D$.
- **Fuel-burn estimation:** Daily fuel = (BHP × SFOC × Operating hours) / 1000, in tonnes.
- **Operational profile:** For trawlers, the *trawling* and *free-running* resistances are often **comparable**, demanding dual operating-point design.
- **Hull-form optimization:** A 1% reduction in $R_T$ saves ~$30,000$–$80,000$ USD in lifetime fuel cost for a 25 m trawler.
- **Regulatory compliance:** IMO **EEDI / EEXI** regulations now mandate resistance-based energy-efficiency certification for new fishing vessels above 24 m.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Frictional Resistance from First Principles

**Starting premise:** The frictional force on a flat plate of area $S$ in turbulent flow is given by the wall-shear stress integrated over the area:

$$R_F = \int_S \tau_w \, dS$$

**Step 1.** For a turbulent boundary layer, the local wall shear stress is:

$$\tau_w = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_f$$

where $C_f$ is the local skin-friction coefficient.

**Step 2.** The mean friction coefficient for the entire plate is obtained by integration:

$$C_F = \frac{1}{S} \int_S C_f \, dS$$

**Step 3.** Substituting back:

$$R_F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_F$$

**Step 4.** Substituting the **ITTC 1957** correlation (empirically fit to flat-plate data and corrected to full scale):

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$

**Step 5.** Final compact form for frictional resistance of a ship:

$$R_F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S \cdot \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2} \quad \text{[N]}$$

> **Conversion logic check:** If $R_n = 10^9$, then $\log_{10} R_n = 9$, so $(\log R_n - 2) = 7$, $(\log R_n - 2)^2 = 49$, hence $C_F = 0.075/49 = 1.531 \times 10^{-3}$. This matches standard reference data for a 50 m vessel at 12 knots.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Effective Power and Service Margin

**Given:** $R_T = 12{,}500$ kgf at $V = 11$ knots.

**Step 1.** Effective Horse Power (metric):

$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{75} = \frac{12{,}500 \times 11}{75} = 1833.3 \text{ HP}$$

**Step 2.** Convert to kilowatts (1 metric HP = 0.7355 kW):

$$P_E = 1833.3 \times 0.7355 = 1348.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3.** Apply a **sea margin** of 20% (typical for fishing vessels working in rough seas):

$$P_{E,\text{margined}} = 1.20 \times 1348.4 = 1618.1 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4.** Assume propulsive efficiency $\eta_D = 0.62$:

$$BHP = \frac{P_{E,\text{margined}}}{\eta_D} = \frac{1618.1}{0.62} = 2610.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5.** Round up to nearest commercial engine rating → select a **2700 kW** main engine.

> [!NOTE]
> The 20% sea margin is **empirical** and accounts for wind, waves, current, fouling, and operational conditions typical of fishing. KTU 2024 typically specifies the margin in the question, or expects 15% (trawlers) to 25% (purse seiners).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Resistance Component Estimation for a Typical 25 m Trawler

| Resistance Component | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | % of $R_T$ (free running) | % of $R_T$ (trawling) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frictional | $R_F$ | 30–40% of $R_T$ | ~35% | ~25% |
| Wave-making | $R_W$ | 15–30% of $R_T$ | ~25% | ~18% |
| Eddy / Residual | $R_E$ | 10–20% of $R_T$ | ~15% | ~12% |
| Appendage | $R_{APP}$ | 4–8% of $R_T$ | ~5% | ~5% |
| Air | $R_{AA}$ | 2–5% of $R_T$ | ~3% | ~2% |
| Correlation (ITTC) | $C_A$ allowance | ~3% of $R_T$ | ~3% | ~2% |
| Fishing gear | $R_{FG}$ | 0% (free) / 20–35% (trawl) | 0% | ~28% |
| **Total** | $R_T$ | 100% | 100% | 100% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice how the **proportion** of frictional resistance DROPS during trawling because the fishing-gear drag dominates. This is a key design insight: trawler resistance optimization is **not just about the hull** — it is equally about gear hydrodynamic efficiency.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Full KTU 2024 Style Problem

**Problem:** A 28 m fishing trawler is to operate at a service speed of 11 knots in sea water ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.188 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). The wetted surface area is $S = 360$ m² and the form factor is $(1+k) = 1.25$. The wave-making + eddy resistance coefficient from the standard series is $C_R = 0.0008$ at the design speed. Estimate the **total resistance** and the **effective power**.

**Step 1.** Convert speed to SI:

$$V = 11 \times 0.5144 = 5.658 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2.** Compute Reynolds number:

$$R_n = \frac{V L_{WL}}{\nu} = \frac{5.658 \times 28}{1.188 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.333 \times 10^9$$

**Step 3.** Compute ITTC 1957 frictional coefficient:

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(9.1248 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{50.79} = 1.4767 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4.** Compute frictional resistance:

$$R_F = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot V^2 \cdot C_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 360 \times 5.658^2 \times 1.4767 \times 10^{-3}$$

Breaking it down:
- $V^2 = 32.013$ m²/s²
- $0.5 \times 1025 \times 360 = 184{,}500$
- $184{,}500 \times 32.013 = 5.906 \times 10^6$
- $5.906 \times 10^6 \times 1.4767 \times 10^{-3} = 8{,}723$ N

**Step 5.** Viscous (form-corrected) resistance:

$$R_V = R_F \times (1+k) = 8{,}723 \times 1.25 = 10{,}904 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6.** Wave + eddy resistance:

$$R_W = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho S V^2 C_R = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 360 \times 32.013 \times 0.0008 = 4725 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7.** Appendage allowance (5% of $R_F$):

$$R_{APP} = 0.05 \times 8{,}723 = 436 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8.** Air resistance (typical frontal area $A_T = 22$ m², $C_{AA} = 0.001$, $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³):

$$R_{AA} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 22 \times 32.013 \times 0.001 = 0.431 \text{ N (negligible)}$$

**Step 9.** Correlation allowance (ITTC 1978, $C_A = 0.0004$):

$$R_A = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 360 \times 32.013 \times 0.0004 = 2362 \text{ N}$$

**Step 10.** Total resistance:

$$R_T = R_V + R_W + R_{APP} + R_{AA} + R_A = 10{,}904 + 4725 + 436 + 0.4 + 2362 = 18{,}428 \text{ N}$$

**Step 11.** Convert to kgf (divide by $g = 9.81$):

$$R_T = 18{,}428 / 9.81 = 1878.5 \text{ kgf}$$

**Step 12.** Effective Horse Power:

$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V_{\text{knots}}}{75} = \frac{1878.5 \times 11}{75} = 275.5 \text{ metric HP}$$

**Step 13.** Convert to kW:

$$P_E = 275.5 \times 0.7355 = 202.6 \text{ kW}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination insight:** Steps 8 and 9 are often interchanged in valuation keys. Students lose marks by not showing the $C_A$ term explicitly. KTU expects the full ITTC 1978 sum: $R_T = (1+k) R_F + R_W + R_{APP} + R_{AA} + R_A$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Resistance Component Decomposition (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TOTAL RESISTANCE R_T of a Fishing Vessel] --> B[Viscous Resistance R_V]
    A --> C[Wave Resistance R_W]
    A --> D[Appendage Resistance R_APP]
    A --> E[Air Resistance R_AA]
    A --> F[Fishing Gear Resistance R_FG]
    A --> G[Correlation Allowance R_A]

    B --> B1[Frictional R_F via ITTC 1957]
    B --> B2[Form Factor 1 plus k correction]

    C --> C1[Wave-making component]
    C --> C2[Eddy / residual component C_R]

    D --> D1[Rudder drag]
    D --> D2[Bilge keel drag]
    D --> D3[Shaft bracket drag]
    D --> D4[Sonar dome drag]

    E --> E1[Superstructure pressure drag]
    E --> E2[Wheelhouse drag]

    F --> F1[Trawl net drag]
    F --> F2[Trawl door drag]
    F --> F3[Warp and dan leno drag]
    F --> F4[Net sonar drag]
```

## 4.2 ITTC 1978 Prediction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Principal Particulars L B T Cb] --> P2[Compute Wetted Surface S]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Reynolds Number Rn]
    P3 --> P4[Compute CF via ITTC 1957]
    P4 --> P5[Compute RF]
    P1 --> P6[Estimate Form Factor 1 plus k]
    P6 --> P7[Compute Viscous Resistance RV]
    P1 --> P8[Read CR from Standard Series]
    P8 --> P9[Compute Wave plus Eddy RW]
    P7 --> P10[Compute Appendage R_APP]
    P9 --> P10
    P10 --> P11[Sum RT]
    P11 --> P12[Add Air R_AA]
    P12 --> P13[Add Correlation Allowance R_A]
    P13 --> P14[Final Total Resistance RT]
    P14 --> P15[Compute EHP and BHP]
```

## 4.3 Trawler Resistance Operating Conditions — Dual-Profile Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OC[Fishing Vessel Operation] --> OC1[Free Running Condition]
    OC --> OC2[Trawling Condition]
    OC --> OC3[Manoeuvring Low Speed]

    OC1 --> OC1A[No gear in water]
    OC1A --> OC1B[RT dominated by hull hull and appendage]
    OC1B --> OC1C[Design speed 10 to 12 knots]

    OC2 --> OC2A[Full trawl gear deployed]
    OC2A --> OC2B[RFG equals 20 to 35 percent of RT]
    OC2B --> OC2C[Trawl speed 3.5 to 4.5 knots]
    OC2C --> OC2D[Stern trawler or side trawler geometry]
    OC2D --> OC2E[High R_APP from warps over side]

    OC3 --> OC3A[Course keeping and station keeping]
    OC3A --> OC3B[Rudder angle drag dominant]
    OC3B --> OC3C[Low speed weather vaning]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams use a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** style as recommended for KTU 2024 board answer sheets. They map component interactions without requiring physical hull drawings, which are difficult to render as Mermaid. Students may reproduce these in their answer books with hand-drawn hull outlines and labelled force vectors for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define "Total Resistance" of a fishing vessel. List its major components with a brief one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The Total Resistance ($R_T$) of a fishing vessel is the net fluid force opposing its forward motion at a given speed, displacement, trim, and operating condition. It is conventionally expressed in kgf or kN.

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| $R_F$ (Frictional) | Viscous skin friction along the wetted hull surface |
| $R_W$ (Wave-making) | Energy lost in generating the ship wave system |
| $R_E$ (Eddy / Residual) | Viscous pressure drag and wave-breaking losses |
| $R_{APP}$ (Appendage) | Drag of rudder, bilge keels, shaft brackets, sonar dome |
| $R_{AA}$ (Air) | Aerodynamic drag of superstructure and wheelhouse |
| $R_{FG}$ (Fishing Gear) | Drag of nets, trawl doors, warps, dan lenos |
| $R_A$ (Correlation) | Empirical allowance for ship-to-ship variation (ITTC) |

*(3 marks — definition 1, components listing 1, brief description 1)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain why the ITTC 1957 frictional resistance formula is preferred over the older Froude friction formula for modern fishing vessel resistance prediction.**

**Model Answer:**

1. The **Froude formula** $R_F = f \cdot S \cdot V^{1.83}$ used a single empirical $f$ value independent of Reynolds number, which could not account for the wide size range of modern vessels.
2. The **ITTC 1957 formula** $C_F = 0.075/(\log R_n - 2)^2$ is derived from the **flat-plate turbulent boundary-layer theory** and the **Prandtl–Schlichting skin-friction line**, and is explicitly Reynolds-number dependent.
3. The ITTC formula is more **physically rigorous** and accurately scales from model to full scale (with the 1978 correlation allowance $C_A = 0.0004$).
4. The ITTC approach is the **internationally accepted standard** (ITTC 1978 procedure), adopted by all major classification societies — DNV, Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, IRS — making it a regulatory requirement for **EEDI/EEXI** compliance.

*(3 marks — Froude limitation 1, ITTC rigour 1, regulatory use 1)*

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **ITTC 1978 method** of resistance prediction for a fishing vessel. List the sequence of calculations and the physical meaning of each term.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 24 m fishing trawler has the following particulars: $L_{WL} = 24$ m, $B = 7$ m, $T = 3.0$ m, $C_B = 0.62$, wetted surface $S = 320$ m², form factor $(1+k) = 1.22$. At a service speed of 10 knots in sea water ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.188 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s), the residual resistance coefficient from standard series is $C_R = 0.0006$. Using the ITTC 1978 method, compute the **Total Resistance** and the **Effective Power in kW**. Take appendage allowance = 5% of $R_F$ and correlation allowance $C_A = 0.0004$.

#### Model Solution (Part A-a)

The ITTC 1978 method decomposes $R_T$ into physically meaningful components. The block diagram (reproduced from Section 4.2) shows the workflow:

1. **Input:** $L_{WL}, B, T, C_B, S, V, \rho, \nu$.
2. **Step 1 — Frictional resistance** $R_F$: Compute $R_n = VL_{WL}/\nu$, then $C_F$ from ITTC 1957 line, then $R_F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^2 C_F$.
3. **Step 2 — Form factor correction**: Multiply $R_F$ by $(1+k)$ to obtain $R_V = (1+k) R_F$.
4. **Step 3 — Residual resistance**: From model test or standard series, read $C_R$ at the design $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL_{WL}}$. Compute $R_W = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^2 C_R$.
5. **Step 4 — Appendage resistance** $R_{APP}$ = 0.05 $R_F$ (or 4–10% per ITTC).
6. **Step 5 — Air resistance** $R_{AA} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{air} A_T V^2 C_{AA}$.
7. **Step 6 — Correlation allowance** $R_A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^2 C_A$ with $C_A = 0.0004$.
8. **Sum:** $R_T = (1+k) R_F + R_W + R_{APP} + R_{AA} + R_A$.

*(7 marks — diagram 2, sequence 3, physical meaning 2)*

#### Model Solution (Part A-b)

**Step 1.** Convert speed: $V = 10 \times 0.5144 = 5.144$ m/s.

**Step 2.** Reynolds number:
$$R_n = \frac{5.144 \times 24}{1.188 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.039 \times 10^9$$

**Step 3.** ITTC 1957 frictional coefficient:
$$\log_{10} R_n = 9.0165 \Rightarrow C_F = \frac{0.075}{(9.0165 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{49.298} = 1.5213 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4.** Frictional resistance:
$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 320 \times 5.144^2 \times 1.5213 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 320 \times 26.461 \times 1.5213 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 320 \times 0.04026 = 6602.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5.** Viscous (form-corrected) resistance:
$$R_V = (1+k) R_F = 1.22 \times 6602.6 = 8055.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6.** Wave + eddy resistance:
$$R_W = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 320 \times 26.461 \times 0.0006 = 2604.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7.** Appendage resistance:
$$R_{APP} = 0.05 \times 6602.6 = 330.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8.** Air resistance (negligible, taken as 0 if frontal area not given):
$$R_{AA} \approx 0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 9.** Correlation allowance:
$$R_A = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 320 \times 26.461 \times 0.0004 = 1736.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 10.** Total resistance:
$$R_T = 8055.2 + 2604.1 + 330.1 + 0 + 1736.1 = 12{,}725.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 11.** Convert to kgf:
$$R_T = 12{,}725.5 / 9.81 = 1297.1 \text{ kgf}$$

**Step 12.** Effective Power:
$$EHP = \frac{1297.1 \times 10}{75} = 172.95 \text{ metric HP}$$
$$P_E = 172.95 \times 0.7355 = 127.2 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answer:** $R_T \approx 12.73$ kN (or 1297 kgf), $P_E \approx 127.2$ kW (or 173 EHP).

*(7 marks — each major step 1, total 7; rounding and units critical)*

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **frictional resistance** of a fishing vessel in detail. Derive the ITTC 1957 expression for $C_F$ and discuss its dependence on Reynolds number.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a sketch, distinguish between **frictional resistance**, **wave-making resistance**, and **residual resistance** for a typical fishing vessel. Explain how **form factor** $(1+k)$ and **correlation allowance** $C_A$ are used to bridge model test data to full-scale prediction in the ITTC 1978 method.

#### Model Solution (Part B-a)

**Frictional resistance** is the tangential viscous force exerted by water on the wetted hull surface. It is the largest single component of $R_T$ for slow, full-form vessels such as trawlers and purse seiners.

**Derivation of ITTC 1957 line:**

1. For turbulent flow over a flat plate, the local skin-friction coefficient (Prandtl–Schlichting):

$$C_f = \frac{0.455}{(\log R_x)^2.58} - \frac{A}{R_x}$$

where $R_x$ is local Reynolds number and $A$ is a roughness correction.

2. Integrating over the plate length gives the mean:

$$C_F = \frac{0.0744}{(\log R_n)^{2.58}}$$

3. The **ITTC 1957 standard** fit (slightly different exponent) is:

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$$

**Dependence on $R_n$:**
- $C_F$ **decreases** as $R_n$ increases.
- For a 24 m trawler at 10 knots, $R_n \approx 10^9$ → $C_F \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$.
- Doubling the length roughly halves the $C_F$ contribution, but the larger wetted area partly offsets this.

*(7 marks — physical description 2, derivation 3, $R_n$ dependence 2)*

#### Model Solution (Part B-b)

**Sketch description (text representation):**

- **Frictional resistance** $R_F$: Acts tangentially along the entire wetted hull; dominant in slender, slow vessels.
- **Wave-making resistance** $R_W$: Acts as a pressure force associated with the divergent and transverse wave system generated at the bow and stern; scales with $F_n^4$ approximately; vanishes at $F_n = 0$.
- **Residual resistance** $R_E$: Captures all viscous-pressure and wave-breaking losses not in $R_F$ or $R_W$; empirically derived.

**Bridging model to full scale (ITTC 1978):**

1. **Model test** is performed at scale $\lambda = L_m / L_s$ with matched $F_n$.
2. The frictional **model-to-ship** difference is removed by applying the **ITTC 1957 friction line** to both:

$$\frac{C_F^s}{C_F^m} \text{ correction}$$

3. **Form factor** $(1+k)$ is determined from the model (e.g., Procrustes / Schoenherr method) and applied to the full-scale $C_F$:

$$C_V = (1+k) C_F$$

4. **Correlation allowance** $C_A = 0.0004$ is added to account for:
   - Hull roughness beyond the ITTC standard (corrosion, marine growth, paint degradation).
   - Weather and operational effects.
   - Model-test uncertainty.

5. The full-scale total coefficient is:

$$C_T = (1+k) C_F + C_R + C_A + C_{AA} + C_{APP}$$

*(7 marks — sketch / description 2, form factor 3, correlation allowance 2)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students LOSE marks in Resistance questions (Module 3):**
> 1. **Forgetting the correlation allowance $C_A = 0.0004$** — it is NOT optional in the ITTC 1978 method. Examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for omission.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — using $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ (fresh water) when the question states sea water. Use $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 3. **Computing $R_n$ in knots** — speed MUST be in m/s for the Reynolds number formula in SI units.
> 4. **Not converting to kgf** before applying the EHP formula $EHP = R_T V / 75$. If you keep $R_T$ in Newtons, you must use $P_E = R_T V$ directly.
> 5. **Ignoring the form factor for the wave-resistance term** — only $R_F$ is multiplied by $(1+k)$; $R_W$ and $R_A$ are NOT.
> 6. **Forgetting the appendage resistance** — 0.05 $R_F$ is the standard KTU-expected value if not specified.
> 7. **Confusing $C_B$ (block coefficient) with $C_P$ (prismatic)** — these are different, and using the wrong one in the Holtrop form-factor equation will give incorrect $(1+k)$.
> 8. **For trawling-condition problems**, students often compute only the free-running $R_T$ and forget the **fishing-gear resistance** $R_{FG}$, which dominates the trawling condition. The trawler is designed for the trawling condition, not the free-running condition.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Resistance (Module 3)**

- [x] **Total Resistance** $R_T = R_F + R_W + R_E + R_{APP} + R_{AA} + R_{FG} + R_A$ — remember the 7 components.
- [x] **ITTC 1957 frictional line:** $C_F = 0.075/(\log R_n - 2)^2$ — derived from flat-plate turbulent boundary-layer theory.
- [x] **Reynolds number:** $R_n = VL_{WL}/\nu$ — use $\nu = 1.188 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s for sea water at 15 °C.
- [x] **Froude number:** $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL_{WL}}$ — governs wave-making similarity.
- [x] **Form factor** $(1+k)$ — typically 1.10 to 1.50 for fishing vessels; multiplies $R_F$ only.
- [x] **Correlation allowance** $C_A = 0.0004$ — mandatory in ITTC 1978 full-scale predictions.
- [x] **Appendage allowance** — 0.05 $R_F$ is the KTU default.
- [x] **Effective Power:** $EHP = R_T V / 75$ (metric, $R_T$ in kgf, $V$ in knots) or $P_E = R_T V$ (SI, both in N and m/s).
- [x] **Trawler-specific:** Design for **trawling condition** ($R_{FG}$ may be 20–35% of $R_T$), not just free running.
- [x] **Air resistance** is small (2–5%) but must be included for completeness; use $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³.
- [x] **Powering margin:** Apply 15–25% sea margin (rough weather, fouling, currents) before engine selection.
- [x] **Regulatory:** IMO EEDI/EEXI compliance now requires explicit resistance-based energy-efficiency certification for new fishing vessels ≥ 24 m.
- [x] **Units discipline:** Never mix SI and metric-HP units in the same calculation. Convert speed to m/s for $R_n$ and force to N, OR keep everything in kgf/knots/HP.

> **One-line memory aid for the exam:**
> *"Friction is viscous and tangential; wave-making is pressure and Froude-scaled; residual is everything left over; the form factor multiplies friction only; the correlation allowance covers everything the model didn't show."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Powering and Propeller Selection: Friction Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Powering and Propeller Selection: Friction Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Frictional Resistance ($R_F$)** is defined as the component of a vessel's total resistance to motion that arises purely from the **tangential viscous shear stresses** acting on the wetted surface of the hull as water flows along it. It is a direct manifestation of the fluid's viscosity ($\mu$) and represents the longitudinal force the hull must overcome to push through water, even if the hull were a perfectly flat, smooth plate moving edge-on through an inviscid fluid (i.e., with no pressure drag).

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$R_F = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_F$$

where:
- $\rho$ = mass density of sea water (**1025 kg/m³** at standard KTU reference conditions)
- $V$ = speed of the vessel in m/s
- $S$ = wetted surface area of the hull in m²
- $C_F$ = dimensionless frictional resistance coefficient

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In fishing vessel design, frictional resistance typically constitutes **60% to 80%** of the total resistance at moderate Froude numbers ($F_n < 0.30$). This makes its accurate prediction critical for correct Brake Power (BHP) and propeller selection.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sticky Water Film"

Imagine you are running your hand through a tub of honey versus a tub of water. The honey "grips" your skin and pulls back, requiring more effort. Water does the same thing, just less obviously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** When a fishing trawler moves forward, the water molecules in immediate contact with the hull **stick** to the hull (No-Slip Condition). The next layer of water slides over the first, the next over that, and so on. This "ladder of sliding" creates a shear stress that drags the ship backward. This drag is frictional resistance. The rougher the hull (e.g., heavy marine fouling on a trawler returning from a 10-day voyage), the more water molecules get "caught," and the higher the friction.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard KTU Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sea water density | $\rho$ | **1025** | kg/m³ |
| Fresh water density | $\rho$ | 1000 | kg/m³ |
| Kinematic viscosity (sea, 15°C) | $\nu$ | **1.188 × 10⁻⁶** | m²/s |
| Standard reference temperature | $T$ | 15 | °C |
| Standard acceleration of gravity | $g$ | 9.81 | m/s² |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Friction — The Boundary Layer

The origin of frictional resistance lies within the **Boundary Layer**, a thin layer of water adjacent to the hull (typically a few millimetres to centimetres thick) where viscous effects dominate.

The boundary layer transitions from **Laminar** to **Turbulent** flow as the ship moves forward. This transition is governed by the **Reynolds Number ($R_n$)**.

**Step-by-step logical breakdown:**
1. **Laminar Layer:** At the bow, water flows in smooth, parallel layers. Friction is low.
2. **Transition Point:** As the flow progresses aft, small disturbances grow, and the flow becomes unstable.
3. **Turbulent Layer:** Beyond the transition point, water moves in chaotic eddies, increasing the shear stress on the hull.
4. **Laminar Sub-layer:** A very thin, slow-moving laminar film persists right against the hull, even within the turbulent regime. If the hull is too rough, this sub-layer is broken, and friction increases dramatically.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL NOTE ON LaTeX:** The vertical pipe `|` is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` in all table cells to prevent markdown table syntax breakage.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_n = \dfrac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number (length-based) | Dimensionless; $L$ = length in m |
| 2 | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$ | **ITTC 1957 Friction Line** (KTU Standard) | Valid for $R_n \in [10^6, 10^9]$ |
| 3 | $R_F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_F$ | Total Frictional Resistance | Newtons (N) |
| 4 | $\Delta C_F = \left[ \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2} - \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2} \cdot 0.5 \right]$ | ITTC 1978 roughness allowance | Per unit $C_F$ form |
| 5 | $C_F^{form} = (1+k) \cdot C_F^{plate}$ | Form Factor relationship | $1+k$ is the form factor |
| 6 | $S = \nabla^{2/3} \cdot (1.7 \cdot L_{WL} + \tfrac{\nabla}{T})$ | **Mumford Wetted Surface Formula** | KTU preferred for fishing vessels |
| 7 | $P_F = R_F \cdot V$ | Effective Power to overcome friction | Watts (W) |
| 8 | $C_F^{rough} = C_F^{ITTC} + \Delta C_F$ | Roughness-corrected friction coefficient | Account for $k_s$ |

### 2.3 The ITTC 1957 Correlation Line — Why It Is Sacred in KTU Exams

The **International Towing Tank Conference (ITTC) 1957** line is the universally accepted empirical formula for the frictional coefficient of a **smooth, flat plate** (turbulent flow). It is derived from the Prandtl-Schlichting formula simplified for engineering use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The ITTC 1957 formula is *always* expected in numerical problems unless a question specifies otherwise. Memorize: $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$. Mark loss is guaranteed if you write the older Schoenherr or Froude formula.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In fishing vessel design, accurate friction prediction is essential for:
- **Fuel consumption estimation** (trawlers spend 80% of operational time cruising or towing).
- **Engine Sizing (MCR)** — under-predicting friction leads to overloaded engines.
- **Hull coating specification** — the difference between a smooth anti-fouling paint and a moderately fouled hull can change fuel burn by **15–20%**.
- **Speed-power diagrams** delivered to the shipowner for operational planning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Shear Stress to Frictional Resistance

We start with Newton's Law of Viscosity at the hull surface:

$$\tau_w = \mu \cdot \left( \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0}$$

where $\tau_w$ is the wall shear stress (N/m²) and $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity (Pa·s).

**Step 1: Non-Dimensionalize using Friction Velocity**

We define the **friction velocity** as:

$$u_\tau = \sqrt{\dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho}}$$

**Step 2: Express wall shear stress via the local skin friction coefficient**

By definition, the local skin friction coefficient $c_f$ relates the wall shear stress to the dynamic pressure of the free stream:

$$\tau_w = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 c_f$$

**Step 3: Integrate along the hull to get total force**

The total frictional resistance is the integral of the wall shear stress over the entire wetted surface $S$:

$$R_F = \int_S \tau_w \, dS = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \int_S c_f \, dS$$

**Step 4: Define the mean frictional coefficient**

By pulling the mean coefficient $C_F$ out of the integral:

$$R_F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_F \quad \text{where} \quad C_F = \dfrac{1}{S} \int_S c_f \, dS$$

This is the **fundamental frictional resistance equation** used universally in ship hydrodynamics.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A fishing trawler has the following particulars:
- Length between perpendiculars $L_{PP} = 24$ m
- Speed $V = 12$ knots
- Wetted surface area $S = 165$ m²
- Sea water density $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³
- Kinematic viscosity $\nu = 1.188 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s

**Calculate the frictional resistance $R_F$ and the effective frictional power $P_F$.**

**Step 1: Convert speed to SI units**

$$V = 12 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 = 6.173 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Reynolds Number**

$$R_n = \dfrac{V \cdot L_{PP}}{\nu} = \dfrac{6.173 \times 24}{1.188 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{R_n = 1.247 \times 10^{8}}$$

**Step 3: Calculate ITTC 1957 Friction Coefficient**

$$\log_{10} R_n = \log_{10}(1.247 \times 10^{8}) = 8.0958$$

$$C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(8.0958 - 2)^2} = \dfrac{0.075}{(6.0958)^2} = \dfrac{0.075}{37.159}$$

$$\boxed{C_F = 2.0185 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Frictional Resistance**

$$R_F = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times (6.173)^2 \times 165 \times (2.0185 \times 10^{-3})$$

Breaking this down:
- $\tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 = 512.5$
- $(6.173)^2 = 38.106$
- $512.5 \times 38.106 = 19{,}529.3$
- $19{,}529.3 \times 165 = 3{,}222{,}334$
- $3{,}222{,}334 \times 2.0185 \times 10^{-3} = 6{,}504.1$ N

$$\boxed{R_F \approx 6.50 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Effective Frictional Power**

$$P_F = R_F \cdot V = 6504.1 \times 6.173 = 40{,}150 \text{ W} \approx 40.15 \text{ kW}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Frictional Resistance

| Factor | Effect on $C_F$ | Effect on $R_F$ | Mitigation in Trawler Design |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase in speed ($V$) | $C_F$ decreases (mild) | $R_F \propto V^2$ rises sharply | Operate at moderate cruising speed |
| Hull roughness | $\Delta C_F$ rises | $R_F$ rises 5–20% | Anti-fouling paint, regular hull cleaning |
| Length increase ($L$) | $C_F$ falls as $R_n$ rises | $R_F$ rises slowly | Use longer, slender hull forms |
| Form factor $(1+k)$ | $C_F$ effective rises | $R_F$ rises | Streamlined bow and stern |
| Water temperature | $\nu$ falls when warm | $R_F$ rises | Design for tropical/temperate waters |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow: Friction Resistance Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vessel Particulars L, V, S] --> B[Convert Units SI Standards]
    B --> C[Compute Reynolds Number Rn = VL/nu]
    C --> D[Apply ITTC 1957 Line]
    D --> E[Derive Smooth Plate CF]
    E --> F[Apply Form Factor 1+k]
    F --> G[Apply Roughness Allowance Delta CF]
    G --> H[Compute RF = 0.5 rho V squared S CF]
    H --> I[Compute Effective Friction Power PF]
    I --> J[Add to Resistance Balance for BHP]
    J --> K[Select Propeller Diameter, Pitch, RPM]
```

### 4.2 Boundary Layer Development Along a Fishing Trawler Hull

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Forward Perpendicular FP] --> B[Laminar Boundary Layer]
    B --> C[Transition Point Critical Rn approx 5e5]
    C --> D[Turbulent Boundary Layer]
    D --> E[Laminar Sub-Layer at Hull]
    E --> F[Aft Perpendicular AP]
    F --> G[Wake and Separation Zone]
    G --> H[Total Frictional Drag Integration]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Role of Friction in Total Resistance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Input
        A1[Hull Form Geometry]
        A2[Speed and Sea State]
        A3[Surface Roughness]
    end
    subgraph Resistance_Components
        B1[Frictional Resistance RF]
        B2[Wave Resistance RW]
        B3[Viscous Pressure Resistance RVP]
        B4[Air Resistance RA]
    end
    subgraph Output
        C1[Total Resistance RT]
        C2[Effective Power PE]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B3
    A2 --> B2
    A2 --> B4
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note to Students:** In Module 3 of PENST754, $R_F$ is the *largest single contributor* to $R_T$ for typical fishing vessels operating at $F_n$ between 0.20 and 0.30. The flow diagrams above show how $R_F$ feeds into the power balance that ultimately drives propeller selection.
