If $R_T$ is given in **kgf** (kilogram-force) and $V_s$ in m/s: $$ \mathrm{EHP} = \frac{R_T \cdot V_s}{75} \quad \text{[metric HP]} $$ If $R_T$ is in **Newtons** and $V_s$ in knots (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s): $$ \mathrm{EHP} = \frac{R_T \cdot V_s \cdot 0.5144}{735.5} \quad \text{[metric HP]} $$ **Step 3 — Linking EHP to DHP via Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient** Since the propulsive system itself has efficiency losses (wake, thrust deduction, propeller rotational loss), the brake power at the engine must be **higher** than the effective power at the hull: $$ \mathrm{DHP} = \frac{\mathrm{EHP}}{\eta_D} $$ Combining the two: $$ \boxed{\;\mathrm{DHP} = \frac{R_T \cdot V_s}{75 \cdot \eta_D} \quad \text{(metric HP)}\;} $$ ### Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-mark style) > **Problem:** A 25 m fishing trawler has a total resistance of 12,000 kgf at a service speed of 10 knots. If the quasi-propulsive coefficient η_D = 0.58 and the overall mechanical + gear + shaft efficiency η_M·η_G·η_S = 0.85, determine the **Effective Horsepower, Delivered Horsepower, and Brake Horsepower** required. **Solution:** **Step A — Convert service speed to m/s** $$ V_s = 10 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 = 5.144 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step B — Compute EHP** $$ \mathrm{EHP} = \frac{R_T \cdot V_s}{75} = \frac{12{,}000 \times 5.144}{75} = 822.97 \text{ HP} \approx 823 \text{ HP} $$ **Step C — Compute DHP** $$ \mathrm{DHP} = \frac{\mathrm{EHP}}{\eta_D} = \frac{822.97}{0.58} = 1418.9 \text{ HP} \approx 1419 \text{ HP} $$ **Step D — Compute BHP** $$ \mathrm{BHP} = \frac{\mathrm{DHP}}{\eta_M \eta_G \eta_S} = \frac{1418.9}{0.85} = 1669.3 \text{ HP} \approx 1670 \text{ HP} $$ **Step E — Convert BHP to kW (for modern specifications)** $$ \mathrm{BHP}_{\text{kW}} = 1669.3 \times 0.7355 = 1227.7 \text{ kW} $$ > **Interpretation:** The engine must be rated at least **1230 kW** (continuous) — engineers typically select the next standard rating (1250 kW or 1500 kW) for safety margin (~10 %). ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Propeller Open-Water Efficiency from K_T and K_Q ### Conceptual Foundation Open-water efficiency is a property of the propeller **alone**, measured in open water (no hull). It is the ratio of useful thrust power to the power delivered to the propeller shaft. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Thrust Power (Useful Power)** A propeller of diameter $D$ rotating at $n$ rev/s produces thrust $T$. At advance speed $V_a$ (water flowing into the propeller), the useful thrust power is: $$ P_T = T \cdot V_a \quad \text{[W]} $$ **Step 2 — Torque Power (Power Input to Propeller)** The propeller absorbs torque $Q$ at rotational speed $n$ rev/s. Power input is: $$ P_Q = 2\pi \cdot n \cdot Q \quad \text{[W]} $$ **Step 3 — Definition of Open-Water Efficiency** $$ \eta_O = \frac{P_T}{P_Q} = \frac{T \cdot V_a}{2\pi \cdot n \cdot Q} $$ **Step 4 — Introduce Non-Dimensional Coefficients** By dimensional analysis (Buckingham Pi theorem), thrust and torque can be expressed as: $$ T = K_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4 \quad ; \quad Q = K_Q \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^5 $$ Substituting: $$ \eta_O = \frac{K_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4 \cdot V_a}{2\pi \cdot n \cdot K_Q \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^5} $$ **Step 5 — Simplify** Cancel $\rho$, $n^2$ from numerator and $D^4$: $$ \eta_O = \frac{K_T \cdot V_a}{2\pi \cdot K_Q \cdot n \cdot D} $$ **Step 6 — Introduce Advance Coefficient J** $$ J = \frac{V_a}{nD} $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\;\eta_O = \frac{K_T}{K_Q} \cdot \frac{J}{2\pi}\;} $$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** $K_T$ and $K_Q$ are functions of $J$ and the pitch ratio $P/D$. For a given propeller, B-series or Wageningen B-series charts give $K_T(J)$ and $K_Q(J)$ — these are used to find the operating point where $\eta_O$ is maximum. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hull Efficiency from Wake and Thrust Deduction ### Concept The propeller works in the **wake** of the hull — the inflow velocity is reduced. Conversely, the propeller suction **increases** the resistance (thrust deduction). Hull efficiency captures this mismatch. ### Derivation **Step 1 — Wake Fraction Definition** The wake fraction $w$ is defined as the fractional reduction in speed at the propeller plane: $$ w = \frac{V_s - V_a}{V_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_a = V_s (1 - w) $$ **Step 2 — Thrust Deduction Definition** The effective resistance $R_E$ required at the propeller is: $$ R_E = R_T (1 - t) $$ where $t$ = thrust deduction fraction. **Step 3 — Power at the Propeller (THP)** $$ \mathrm{THP} = R_E \cdot V_a = R_T (1-t) \cdot V_s(1-w) $$ **Step 4 — Effective Power (EHP)** $$ \mathrm{EHP} = R_T \cdot V_s $$ **Step 5 — Ratio** $$ \eta_H = \frac{\mathrm{EHP}}{\mathrm{THP}} = \frac{R_T V_s}{R_T (1-t) V_s (1-w)} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\eta_H = \frac{1}{(1-t)(1-w)} = \frac{1}{1-t-w+tw}\;} $$ > **Note:** Since $t$ and $w$ are small, the approximation $\eta_H \approx \dfrac{1-t}{1-w}$ is commonly used in KTU problems. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Refrigeration Load for a Fish Hold **Step 1 — Sensible Heat (cooling fish from sea temperature to 0 °C)** $$ Q_{\text{sens}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{\text{sea}} - T_{\text{fish}}) $$ where $c_p$ (fish) ≈ 3.5 kJ/(kg·K). **Step 2 — Latent Heat (freezing water in the fish)** $$ Q_{\text{lat}} = m \cdot x \cdot L_f $$ where $x$ = fraction of water in fish (≈ 0.65) and $L_f$ ≈ 335 kJ/kg. **Step 3 — Heat Ingress (through insulation)** $$ Q_{\text{loss}} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T $$ where $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient of insulation (W/m²·K). **Step 4 — Total Refrigeration Load** $$ \boxed{\;Q_0 = m c_p \Delta T + m x L_f + U A \Delta T \quad \text{[kW]}\;} $$ ### Numerical Example > **Problem:** 10 tonnes of fish are cooled from 25 °C to 0 °C. Holding time is 12 hours. Insulation gives $UA$ = 50 W/K. Outside temperature 30 °C. Find refrigeration plant capacity in **tons of refrigeration (TR)**. (1 TR = 3.517 kW) **Solution:** $$ Q_{\text{sens}} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 3.5 \times 25}{3600} = 243.06 \text{ kW} $$ $$ Q_{\text{lat}} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 0.65 \times 335}{3600} = 604.86 \text{ kW} $$ $$ Q_{\text{loss}} = \frac{50 \times 30}{1000} = 1.5 \text{ kW} $$ $$ Q_{\text{total}} = 243.06 + 604.86 + 1.5 = 849.42 \text{ kW} $$ $$ \text{Plant size} = \frac{849.42}{3.517} \approx 241.5 \text{ TR} $$ > **Interpretation:** This requires a **large** refrigeration plant (~ 240 TR) — explaining why RSW trawlers are economically viable only for vessels above 25 m LOA. ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Trawl Winch Power Requirement **Step 1 — Mechanical Power Output** $$ P_{\text{out}} = F \cdot v $$ where $F$ = line pull (N), $v$ = line speed (m/s). **Step 2 — Motor Power Input** $$ P_{\text{motor}} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{\eta_{\text{winch}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gear}}} $$ **Step 3 — Common KTU Form** $$ \boxed{\;P_{\text{motor}} = \frac{F \cdot v}{60 \cdot \eta} \quad \text{[kW]}\;} $$ where $F$ in kN, $v$ in m/min, $\eta \approx 0.75$ – $0.85$. **Example:** Pull = 50 kN, Speed = 60 m/min, η = 0.80 $$ P = \frac{50 \times 60}{60 \times 0.80} = 62.5 \text{ kW} \quad (\approx 85 \text{ HP}) $$ # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Power Flow Block Diagram — Fishing Vessel Propulsion System The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete power flow** from fuel to effective thrust, with associated efficiencies at each stage. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** as per KTU 2024 protocol. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Fuel Tank
Marine Diesel Oil] --> B[Main Engine
4-Stroke Diesel
Turbocharged] B --> C[Reduction Gear Box
Ratio 3:1 to 4:1] C --> D[Intermediate Shaft] D --> E[Tail Shaft with
Thrust Block] E --> F[Propeller
FPP or CPP
Optional Kort Nozzle] F --> G[Hull Reaction
Wake + Thrust Deduction] G --> H[Effective Thrust
Overcomes R Total] B -.ηM=0.88.-> C C -.ηG=0.97.-> D D -.ηS=0.98.-> E E -.ηO=0.65.-> F F -.ηH=1.10.-> G A1[Aux Engine 1] --> A2[Aux Generator 1
AC 440V 60Hz] A3[Aux Engine 2] --> A4[Aux Generator 2
Emergency] A2 --> A5[Main Switchboard] A4 --> A5 A5 --> A6[Refrigeration Plant
RSW Compressor] A5 --> A7[Hydraulic Power Pack
160 to 250 bar] A5 --> A8[Bilge Pump] A5 --> A9[Fire Pump] A5 --> A10[Steering Gear Motor] A7 --> A11[Trawl Winch] A7 --> A12[Net Hauler] A7 --> A13[Deck Crane] A10 --> A14[Steering Cylinder] classDef powerNode fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef auxNode fill:#5f3a1e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef loadNode fill:#1e5f3a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H powerNode class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A7,A8,A9,A10 auxNode class A11,A12,A13,A14 loadNode ``` ## 4.2 Refrigeration Plant — Functional Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Compressor
Electric or Engine Driven] --> B[Condenser
Sea Water Cooled] B --> C[Receiver
Liquid Refrigerant] C --> D[Filter Drier] D --> E[Expansion Valve
Throttling Device] E --> F[Evaporator
Plate or Coil Type] F --> G[Fish Hold
0 deg C] F -. Suction Line .-> A A1[Sea Water Pump] --> B A2[Control Thermostat] -.Modulates.-> A classDef comp fill:#2a4d7c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef flow fill:#7c4d2a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef sink fill:#2a7c4d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff class A,A1,A2 comp class B,C,D,E flow class F,G sink ``` ## 4.3 Deck Machinery — Layout Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph "Power Source" PS[Hydraulic Power Pack
Electric Motor + Pump] PS2[Independent Diesel
PTO Drive] end subgraph "Deck Machinery" W1[Trawl Winch
2 Drums] W2[Net Hauler
3 to 5 ton] W3[Line Hauler
Longline] W4[Anchor Windlass] W5[Deck Crane
3 to 10 ton SWL] W6[Purse Seine Winch] end subgraph "Control" CO[Wheelhouse Control
Joystick + PLC] CO2[Local Valves
on Deck] end PS --> W1 PS --> W2 PS --> W3 PS --> W4 PS --> W5 PS2 --> W6 CO -. Signals .-> PS CO2 -. Manual Override .-> W1 CO2 -. Manual Override .-> W2 CO2 -. Manual Override .-> W3 CO2 -. Manual Override .-> W5 classDef source fill:#1a3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef machine fill:#5f1a3a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef control fill:#3a5f1a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff class PS,PS2 source class W1,W2,W3,W4,W5,W6 machine class CO,CO2 control ``` ## 4.4 Auxiliary Systems Architecture (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph "Power Generation" GE1[Main Generator] GE2[Emergency Generator] GE3[Harbour Generator] end subgraph "Main Switchboard 440V" SB[MSB] end subgraph "Distribution" EM1[Engine Room Fans] EM2[Compressors] EM3[Pumps] EM4[Galley and Accommodation] EM5[Wheelhouse Equipment] end subgraph "DC Backup" BAT[Battery Bank 24V DC] INV[Inverter for 220V AC] end GE1 --> SB GE2 --> SB GE3 --> SB SB --> EM1 SB --> EM2 SB --> EM3 SB --> EM4 SB --> EM5 SB --> BAT BAT --> INV INV --> EM4 classDef gen fill:#5f5f1a,stroke:#fff,color:#000 classDef dist fill:#1a5f5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef dc fill:#5f1a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff class GE1,GE2,GE3,SB gen class EM1,EM2,EM3,EM4,EM5 dist class BAT,INV dc ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *Define the term "Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient (η_D)" and state its typical value range for a modern fishing trawler.* **Model Answer:** The Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient (η_D) is the ratio of the Effective Horsepower (EHP) — i.e., the useful power required to tow the vessel at service speed against its total resistance — to the Delivered Horsepower (DHP) actually delivered to the propeller. Mathematically: $$ \eta_D = \frac{\mathrm{EHP}}{\mathrm{DHP}} $$ It is also expressed as the product of three component efficiencies: $$ \eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_H \approx \eta_O \cdot \eta_H $$ where $\eta_O$ = propeller open-water efficiency, $\eta_R$ = relative rotative efficiency, and $\eta_H$ = hull efficiency. **Typical value range for a modern fishing trawler: η_D = 0.55 to 0.65.** (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for typical value) --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *List any six major machinery and equipment items fitted on a fishing vessel, grouped under "deck machinery" and "fish preservation".* **Model Answer:** **Deck Machinery (3 items):** 1. Trawl Winch (double-drum, hydraulic) 2. Net Hauler (for purse seines and trawl nets) 3. Anchor Windlass 4. Deck Crane / Gantry 5. Line Hauler (for longline fishing) 6. Purse Seine Winch **Fish Preservation Equipment (3 items):** 1. Ice-making machine (flake or tube ice) 2. Refrigerated Sea Water (RSW) plant 3. Plate Freezer / Blast Freezer 4. Insulated fish hold 5. Fish pump (vacuum or centrifugal) 6. Brine tank / chiller (3 marks: 1.5 for deck machinery, 1.5 for preservation — list at least 3 from each) --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > **KTU Format Note:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts, typically (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Internal choice is **between two full questions** (not sub-parts). --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** *A 28 m fishing trawler is to be fitted with a diesel-electric propulsion system. The following data are available:* | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Total resistance $R_T$ | 18,000 kgf at $V_s$ | | Service speed $V_s$ | 11 knots | | Quasi-propulsive coefficient η_D | 0.60 | | Overall mechanical efficiency η_M | 0.88 | | Gear-box efficiency η_G | 0.97 | | Shaft efficiency η_S | 0.98 | | Specific fuel consumption SFC | 210 g/kWh | | Voyaging hours per year | 4000 hours | #### Part (a) — Calculate the Effective Horsepower (EHP), Delivered Horsepower (DHP), Brake Horsepower (BHP) and the recommended main engine rating. (7 marks) **Step 1 — Convert $V_s$ to m/s** $$ V_s = 11 \times 0.5144 = 5.6584 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2 — Compute EHP** $$ \mathrm{EHP} = \frac{R_T \cdot V_s}{75} = \frac{18{,}000 \times 5.6584}{75} = 1358.02 \text{ HP} \approx 1358 \text{ HP} $$ **Step 3 — Convert EHP to kW** $$ \mathrm{EHP}_{\text{kW}} = 1358 \times 0.7355 = 998.8 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 4 — Compute DHP** $$ \mathrm{DHP} = \frac{\mathrm{EHP}}{\eta_D} = \frac{1358}{0.60} = 2263.3 \text{ HP} = 1664.7 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 5 — Compute BHP (account for engine + gear + shaft losses)** The combined efficiency from engine crankshaft to propeller: $$ \eta_{\text{total}} = \eta_M \cdot \eta_G \cdot \eta_S = 0.88 \times 0.97 \times 0.98 = 0.8364 $$ $$ \mathrm{BHP} = \frac{\mathrm{DHP}}{\eta_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1664.7}{0.8364} = 1990.3 \text{ kW} \approx 1991 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 6 — Recommended Engine Rating** Apply a **10 % service margin** for rough weather, fouling, and aging: $$ P_{\text{engine}} = 1.10 \times 1991 = 2190 \text{ kW} \quad (\text{select next standard: } 2200 \text{ kW or 2400 kW}) $$ #### Part (b) — Determine the annual fuel consumption and the daily fuel cost, given that the fuel price is ₹ 85 per litre and the density of marine diesel oil is 0.86 kg/L. (7 marks) **Step 1 — Annual Fuel Mass** $$ F_{\text{annual}} = \mathrm{SFC} \times \mathrm{BHP} \times \text{hours} $$ $$ F_{\text{annual}} = 210 \text{ g/kWh} \times 1991 \text{ kW} \times 4000 \text{ h} $$ $$ F_{\text{annual}} = 1.6724 \times 10^9 \text{ g} = 1{,}672{,}444 \text{ kg} \quad (\text{approx } 1672 \text{ tonnes}) $$ **Step 2 — Daily Fuel Mass (assume 330 sailing days/year)** $$ F_{\text{daily}} = \frac{1672}{330} = 5.07 \text{ tonnes/day} = 5070 \text{ kg/day} $$ **Step 3 — Daily Fuel Volume** $$ V_{\text{daily}} = \frac{5070}{0.86 \times 1000} = 5.895 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 5895 \text{ L/day} $$ Wait — for a single sailing day (24 h × 11 h sailing = 11 h/day): $$ F_{\text{per day}} = 210 \times 1991 \times 11 = 4.6 \times 10^6 \text{ g} = 4601 \text{ kg/day} $$ $$ V_{\text{per day}} = \frac{4601}{860} = 5.35 \text{ m}^3 = 5350 \text{ L/day} $$ **Step 4 — Daily Cost** $$ \text{Cost} = 5350 \text{ L} \times ₹ 85 = ₹ 4{,}54{,}750 \text{ per day} $$ **Step 5 — Annual Cost** $$ \text{Annual Cost} = 1.6724 \times 10^6 \text{ L} \times 85 = ₹ 14.21 \text{ Crore/year} $$ > **Examiner's Note:** (Marks distribution: 1 each for Step 1–3 in part a; 1 for engine selection; 1.5 each for SFC-based annual & daily cost in part b.) --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *A 22 m tuna longline vessel is to be equipped with a hydraulic deck machinery system and an ammonia-based refrigeration plant. Discuss the following:* #### Part (a) — With the help of a block diagram, describe the components of a hydraulic power transmission system used for the trawl winch and deck crane. State typical working pressures and motor power ratings. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** A hydraulic power transmission system consists of the following **six main components**: 1. **Prime Mover** — Electric motor (AC) of 50–200 kW rating, running at 1450/1750 rpm. 2. **Hydraulic Pump** — Variable-displacement axial-piston pump (e.g., Rexroth A10VSO series) with displacement 60–250 cc/rev. 3. **Reservoir (Oil Tank)** — Capacity 200–800 litres, with breather, sight glass, and level switch. 4. **Filters & Cooler** — Suction strainer (25 μm), return-line filter (10 μm), and air-cooled oil cooler. 5. **Control Valves** — Directional control valve (DCV), pressure relief valve (set at 250 bar), flow control valve, counterbalance valve. 6. **Actuators** — Hydraulic motors (for winch drum rotation) and hydraulic cylinders (for crane boom luffing). **Working Pressure:** 160 – 250 bar (modern trawlers prefer 200 bar). **Typical Motor Power Ratings:** | Equipment | Power (kW) | |---|---| | Trawl Winch (double-drum) | 75 – 150 | | Net Hauler | 30 – 75 | | Line Hauler | 15 – 30 | | Deck Crane (5 t SWL) | 30 – 60 | | Steering Gear | 7 – 15 | | Anchor Windlass | 20 – 40 | **Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes.) **Advantages of Hydraulics over Electric-Drive for Deck Machinery:** - Higher power density (small size for high torque). - Smooth, stepless speed control. - Overload protection via relief valves. - Bidirectional operation without reversing contactors. - Self-locking capability (no back-drift). (7 marks: 2 for block diagram, 2 for component description, 2 for pressures/ratings, 1 for advantage statement) #### Part (b) — Compute the refrigeration plant capacity (in kW and TR) required for an 8-tonne catch on a 22 m tuna longliner. The fish must be cooled from 28 °C to 0 °C in 6 hours. The insulated hold has $UA$ = 40 W/K and the outside ambient is 32 °C. The latent heat of fish is 220 kJ/kg and the water content of tuna is 70 %. (7 marks) **Given:** - $m$ = 8000 kg - $c_p$ (tuna) = 3.5 kJ/(kg·K) - $\Delta T$ (sensible) = 28 °C - $x$ (water content) = 0.70 - $L_f$ (latent heat of fish) = 220 kJ/kg - Cooling time = 6 h = 21,600 s - $UA$ = 40 W/K - $\Delta T$ (ambient) = 32 °C **Step 1 — Sensible Heat Load** $$ Q_{\text{sens}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 8000 \times 3.5 \times 28 = 7{,}84{,}000 \text{ kJ} $$ **Step 2 — Latent Heat Load (freezing of water content)** $$ Q_{\text{lat}} = m \cdot x \cdot L_f = 8000 \times 0.70 \times 220 = 12{,}32{,}000 \text{ kJ} $$ **Step 3 — Heat Ingress Loss (over 6 hours)** $$ Q_{\text{loss}} = UA \cdot \Delta T_{\text{amb}} \cdot t = 40 \times 32 \times 21{,}600 = 2.765 \times 10^7 \text{ J} = 27{,}648 \text{ kJ} $$ **Step 4 — Total Heat to be Removed** $$ Q_{\text{total}} = 7{,}84{,}000 + 12{,}32{,}000 + 27{,}648 = 20{,}43{,}648 \text{ kJ} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to kW (power rate over 6 h)** $$ P_{\text{plant}} = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{t_{\text{sec}}} = \frac{20{,}43{,}648}{21{,}600} = 94.6 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 6 — Convert to Tons of Refrigeration (1 TR = 3.517 kW)** $$ P_{\text{TR}} = \frac{94.6}{3.517} = 26.9 \text{ TR} \approx 27 \text{ TR} $$ **Step 7 — Select Standard Plant with 10 % Safety Margin** $$ P_{\text{selected}} = 1.10 \times 27 = 29.7 \text{ TR} \quad (\text{select 30 TR ammonia plant}) $$ > **Engineering Note:** Such a plant consumes ~ 25 kW of compressor shaft power (assuming COP = 3.5). This justifies the use of ammonia (R-717) over HFCs for marine applications because of its higher COP and zero GWP. (7 marks: 1 each for the 3 heat terms, 1 for total, 1 for kW conversion, 1 for TR conversion, 1 for plant selection with safety margin; 1 for engineering note) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **TOP 5 PLACES STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN THIS MODULE:** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency in EHP Formula:** Using $R_T$ in Newtons with the 75-divisor (which is for kgf). Always check: $R_T$ in kgf + 75 → HP (metric); $R_T$ in N + 1000 → kW. Mixing units costs **2 full marks**. > > 2. **Forgetting the Safety Margin (10–15 %):** KTU expects a standard rating. Always state: *"Selecting next standard engine rating of ___ kW with 10 % service margin."* Failing to do so = 1 mark lost. > > 3. **Confusing EHP, DHP, SHP, BHP Order:** Memorize: **E (Effective) → D (Delivered) → S (Shaft) → B (Brake)**. Each step **divides** by an efficiency (never multiplies). > > 4. **Refrigeration: Forgetting the $UA \cdot \Delta T$ term:** Many students only compute sensible and latent heat, missing the 0.5–2 kW of heat ingress. Lose 1 mark. > > 5. **Confusing "Open-water efficiency" (η_O) with "Hull efficiency" (η_H):** η_O is the propeller alone; η_H is hull-propeller interaction. η_D = η_O × η_H (assuming η_R ≈ 1). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Propulsion & Machinery (Module 3)** > > ✅ **Power Chain Mnemonic: "IDST-E"** (Indicated → Delivered → Shaft → Thrust → Effective) > - IP → BHP = η_M ≈ 0.88 > - BHP → SHP = η_G ≈ 0.97 > - SHP → DHP = η_S ≈ 0.98 > - DHP → THP = η_O ≈ 0.65 > - THP → EHP = η_H ≈ 1.10 (apparent gain) > > ✅ **Master Formulas:** > - $EHP = R_T V_s / 75$ (metric HP) > - $DHP = EHP / \eta_D$ > - $\eta_O = (K_T / K_Q) \cdot (J / 2\pi)$ > - $\eta_H = (1 - t) / (1 - w)$ > - Winch Power $P = F v / 60 \eta$ > - Refrigeration Load $Q_0 = m c_p \Delta T + m x L_f + UA \Delta T$ > - 1 TR = 3.517 kW > > ✅ **Standard KTU Values (Memorize):** > - SFC marine diesel: 190 – 220 g/kWh > - η_D for trawler: 0.55 – 0.65 > - Service speed trawler: 10 – 12 knots > - Hydraulic pressure: 160 – 250 bar > - Trawler LOA classes: < 12 m (artisanal), 12–24 m (coastal), 24–35 m (deep-sea), > 35 m (factory trawler) > > ✅ **Component Groupings to Remember:** > - **Propulsion:** Engine + Gear + Shaft + Propeller (+ Nozzle) > - **Aux. Power:** 2–3 diesel generators + emergency + batteries > - **Deck Machinery:** Trawl winch + net hauler + line hauler + crane + windlass > - **Refrigeration:** Compressor + Condenser + Expansion valve + Evaporator (vapor compression cycle) > - **Hydraulics:** Motor + Pump + Tank + Valves + Actuators > > ✅ **Common Comparison Points (for 7-mark questions):** > - FPP vs CPP (4–5 points) > - Ice preservation vs RSW (4–5 points) > - Hydraulic vs electric deck machinery (5–6 points) > - Ammonia vs HFC refrigerant (4–5 points) > > ✅ **Safety & Regulatory Standards (frequently asked):** > - SOLAS (Life-saving, Fire-fighting) > - MARPOL (Pollution prevention — oily water separator, sewage plant) > - ILO-188 (Fishery vessel crew accommodation & safety) > - Classification society rules (IRS / BV / DNV / LR)

topics.title: Different Propulsion Systems and Selection of Main Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Different Propulsion Systems and Selection of Main Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Propulsion System** in the context of fishing vessel design refers to the integrated arrangement of prime mover(s), transmission elements, thrust-generating devices (propellers), and associated control systems that collectively convert the chemical energy of fuel (or any other energy source) into useful forward motion of the vessel through water.

A **Main Engine** is the principal prime mover that delivers the **Brake Horsepower (BHP)** required at the propeller shaft to overcome the vessel's total resistance (R_T) at the service speed (V), accounting for all hydrodynamic, mechanical, and propulsive losses through the propulsive coefficient (P_C).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a fishing vessel, the propulsion system is uniquely dual-purpose — it must provide efficient transit between fishing grounds AND sustained low-speed, high-torque operation during actual fishing (trawling, purse seining, longlining), often with continuous power take-off (PTO) for hydraulic winches, net haulers, and refrigerated seawater (RSW) systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Vessel as a Living System

Imagine the fishing vessel as a **human athlete running a marathon while intermittently sprinting**:

- The **Main Engine** is the **heart and lungs** — pumping energy throughout the voyage.
- The **Propeller** is the **legs/feet** — converting rotational force into forward thrust.
- The **Gearbox/Shaft** is the **spine** — transmitting and modulating power.
- The **Fuel** is the **food** — the energy source.
- The **Hull** is the **body** — providing shape and reducing drag.

Just as a marathon runner must alternate between jogging and sprinting, a fishing vessel must efficiently handle **transit cruising** (15–18 knots for modern trawlers) AND **trolling/deployment speeds** (2–4 knots for net setting). This dual demand is the defining engineering challenge that drives main engine selection in fishing vessels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics** used in KTU 2024 syllabus:
> - **BHP** — Brake Horsepower (kW) — measured at engine flywheel
> - **SHP** — Shaft Horsepower (kW) — delivered to propeller shaft
> - **EHP** — Effective Horsepower (kW) — useful power overcoming resistance
> - **P_C** — Propulsive Coefficient = EHP/SHP (typically **0.55–0.65**)
> - **SFOC** — Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (**g/kWh**)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Marine Propulsion Systems

The propulsion systems employed in fishing vessels can be classified as follows:

#### A. Direct Diesel-Mechanical Propulsion
- **Most common** for small to medium fishing vessels (LOA 15–45 m).
- High-speed or medium-speed diesel engine directly coupled (or via reduction gearbox) to a fixed-pitch propeller (FPP) or controllable-pitch propeller (CPP).
- **Advantages:** Simplicity, high thermal efficiency (35–45%), reliability, low capital cost.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited speed range without CPP; high vibration/noise; engine-room dominated by the prime mover.

#### B. Geared Diesel-Mechanical Propulsion
- Diesel engine(s) connected via reduction gearbox (single or multi-engine, single or twin-shaft).
- Allows engine to operate at its **rated RPM** while propeller runs at optimum rotational speed.
- **Baudouin, MAN B&W, Wärtsilä, Yanmar, Caterpillar** are industry-standard makers.

#### C. Diesel-Electric Propulsion
- Diesel generator(s) produce electrical power → electric motor(s) drive propeller(s) via Z-drive or azimuth thruster.
- **Highly suitable** for modern fishing vessels requiring:
  - Precise low-speed control (e.g., trawling at 2.5–3.5 knots).
  - Multiple operating modes (transit vs. fishing).
  - Reduced fuel consumption at part loads.
- **Examples:** Norwegian purse seiners, Antarctic krill trawlers.

#### D. Gas Turbine Propulsion
- Lightweight, high power-to-weight ratio, but **poor fuel economy** and high exhaust temperatures.
- Rare in fishing vessels; used in naval/military vessels requiring high burst speeds.
- **Not recommended** for commercial fishing vessels due to high fuel cost sensitivity.

#### E. Steam Turbine Propulsion
- Obsolete in fishing vessels. Used historically in large cargo ships and naval carriers.
- **Disadvantages:** High capital cost, slow response, large auxiliary equipment (boilers, condensers).

#### F. Nuclear Propulsion
- Used in icebreakers, submarines, aircraft carriers. **No civilian fishing vessel** has employed nuclear propulsion due to prohibitive cost, regulatory burden, and safety concerns.

#### G. Hybrid Propulsion (Diesel + Electric + Battery)
- **Emerging technology** (2020s onwards) — emerging in fishing fleets to comply with **IMO MARPOL Annex VI** emissions regulations in Emission Control Areas (ECAs).
- Allows zero-emission operation in harbors and during fishing using battery storage.

#### H. Wind-Assisted / Sail-Assisted Propulsion
- Auxiliary sails, kites (e.g., SkySails), or Flettner rotors supplement engine power.
- Reduces fuel consumption by **5–30%** depending on conditions.

#### I. Outboard and Sterndrive Propulsion
- Used in small artisanal fishing boats (LOA < 12 m). Outboard motors are lightweight, portable, and easily serviced.

### 2.2 Selection Criteria for the Main Engine

The selection of a main engine is governed by a multi-criteria decision framework:

1. **Power Output Requirement (BHP/SHP)**
2. **Service Speed** (V_s) of the vessel
3. **Fuel Type & Availability** (HFO, MDO, MGO, LNG, biofuels)
4. **Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC)** in **g/kWh**
5. **Weight & Space Constraints** — specific weight (kg/kW), specific volume (m³/MW)
6. **Reliability & Maintenance Intervals** — Mean Time Between Overhaul (MTBO)
7. **Operational Profile** — % time at cruising vs. fishing vs. idling
8. **Vibration & Noise Limits** — especially important for crew welfare on long voyages
9. **Cold-Start Capability** for high-latitude fishing (Barents Sea, Antarctic)
10. **Initial Cost vs. Lifecycle Cost** (LCC)
11. **Regulatory Compliance** — IMO Tier II/III NOx limits (for engines > 130 kW installed after 2011/2016)
12. **PTO/STO Capability** — auxiliary power take-off for winches, RSW compressors, hydraulic pumps

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **all high-yield formulas** required for numerical problems on this topic in KTU 2024 ESE. Absolute values use `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Effective Horsepower | $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V_s}{75}$ | $R_T$ in kgf, $V_s$ in m/s, EHP in metric HP (multiply by 0.7355 for kW) |
| Effective Horsepower (SI) | $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V_s}{1000}$ | $R_T$ in kN, $V_s$ in m/s, EHP in kW |
| Shaft Horsepower | $SHP = \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_H \cdot \eta_R}$ | $\eta_H$ = hull efficiency, $\eta_R$ = relative rotative efficiency |
| Propulsive Coefficient | $P_C = \dfrac{EHP}{SHP}$ | Typical range: **0.55 to 0.65** |
| Brake Horsepower | $BHP = \dfrac{SHP}{\eta_{GB} \cdot \eta_{SH}}$ | $\eta_{GB}$ = gearbox efficiency, $\eta_{SH}$ = shaft efficiency |
| Quasi-propulsive Coefficient | $\eta_D = \dfrac{EHP}{BHP} = P_C \cdot \eta_{GB} \cdot \eta_{SH}$ | Overall propulsive efficiency |
| Admiralty EHP Formula | $EHP = \dfrac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V_s^3}{C}$ | $\Delta$ = displacement (tonnes), $V_s$ in knots, $C$ = Admiralty coefficient |
| Fuel Consumption | $F_C = \dfrac{BHP \cdot SFOC}{10^6}$ | $BHP$ in kW, SFOC in g/kWh, $F_C$ in tonnes/hour |
| Range (Endurance) | $Range = \dfrac{F_T \cdot V_s}{F_C \cdot 24}$ | $F_T$ = total fuel carried (tonnes), Range in nautical miles |
| Piston Speed | $v_p = \dfrac{L \cdot N}{60}$ | $L$ = stroke (m), $N$ = RPM |
| BMEP | $BMEP = \dfrac{P \cdot n_c}{L \cdot A_p \cdot N}$ | For diesel engine power correlation |
| Specific Weight | $w_s = \dfrac{W_{eng}}{BHP}$ | kg/kW — important for displacement vessels |
| Resistance (Bolder's trawler approx.) | $R_T \approx k \cdot \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V_s^2$ | Empirical for fishing vessels |
| Battery Endurance (Hybrid) | $t_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{aux}}$ | $E_{bat}$ in kWh, $P_{aux}$ in kW |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The propulsion system choice directly affects:
- **Operating economics** — fuel can be 30–50% of vessel OPEX.
- **Catch efficiency** — engine response time affects gear deployment.
- **Crew comfort** — noise and vibration impact long-voyage fatigue.
- **Environmental compliance** — IMO 2020 sulphur cap (0.5% S) and forthcoming IMO GHG strategy drive LNG, hybrid, and biofuel adoption.

> [!NOTE]
> For a typical 35 m stern trawler operating in the North Atlantic, the daily fuel consumption at cruising (12 knots) is approximately **3.5–4.5 tonnes/day**, which directly motivates the move toward diesel-electric or hybrid systems on modern European fishing fleets.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Resistance to Effective Horsepower (EHP)

The total resistance $R_T$ is the force (in the direction opposing motion) that the water exerts on the hull. To overcome this resistance at a service speed $V_s$, the **Effective Horsepower** is the rate of doing useful work:

**Step 1:** Define work done against resistance over an infinitesimal displacement $dL$.

$$dW = R_T \cdot dL$$

**Step 2:** Time taken to traverse this displacement at constant speed $V_s$.

$$dt = \dfrac{dL}{V_s}$$

**Step 3:** Rate of work (Power) = Work / Time.

$$P = \dfrac{dW}{dt} = \dfrac{R_T \cdot dL}{dL / V_s} = R_T \cdot V_s$$

**Step 4:** Convert to standard metric horsepower (1 metric HP = 75 kgf·m/s).

$$EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V_s}{75} \quad \text{[metric HP]}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to SI units (kW) using $1 \text{ kW} = 102 \text{ kgf·m/s} \approx 1.36$ metric HP.

$$EHP \, [kW] = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V_s}{75} \times 0.7355 = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V_s}{102}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The factor **75** arises because 1 metric horsepower was historically defined as the power needed to lift 75 kgf at 1 m/s. SI conversion uses $1 \text{ HP} = 735.5 \text{ W}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — From EHP to BHP (Full Propulsive Chain)

**Step 1:** Engine delivers **BHP** at the flywheel.

**Step 2:** Through the gearbox (efficiency $\eta_{GB}$) and shaft (efficiency $\eta_{SH}$), power becomes:

$$SHP = BHP \cdot \eta_{GB} \cdot \eta_{SH}$$

**Step 3:** At the propeller, the **Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient** $\eta_D$ accounts for all hydrodynamic losses (hull, propeller, rotational).

$$\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_0$$

where:
- $\eta_H = \dfrac{2}{1 + (t/R)} + \text{wake correction}$ — hull efficiency
- $\eta_R$ — relative rotative efficiency ($\approx 0.95 - 1.05$)
- $\eta_0$ — propeller open-water efficiency

**Step 4:** The useful power delivered to the hull is therefore:

$$EHP = SHP \cdot \eta_D = BHP \cdot \eta_{GB} \cdot \eta_{SH} \cdot \eta_D$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging for required BHP:

$$BHP = \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_{GB} \cdot \eta_{SH} \cdot \eta_D}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Admiralty Coefficient Method (Common in KTU)

**Step 1:** The Admiralty Coefficient $C$ correlates EHP, displacement $\Delta$, and speed $V$:

$$C = \dfrac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V_s^3}{EHP}$$

**Step 2:** For a fishing vessel of similar form, $C$ is approximately constant.

$$EHP = \dfrac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V_s^3}{C}$$

**Step 3:** Compute required SHP from EHP using assumed propulsive efficiency:

$$SHP = \dfrac{EHP}{P_C}$$

**Step 4:** Compute BHP from SHP assuming transmission efficiency:

$$BHP = \dfrac{SHP}{\eta_{GB} \cdot \eta_{SH}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fuel Consumption and Endurance

**Step 1:** Hourly fuel consumption in tonnes per hour:

$$F_{h} = \dfrac{BHP \times SFOC}{10^6}$$

where BHP is in kW and SFOC in g/kWh.

**Step 2:** Daily fuel consumption:

$$F_{d} = F_{h} \times 24$$

**Step 3:** Vessel range (in nautical miles) given fuel carried $F_T$:

$$Range = \dfrac{F_T \times V_s}{F_{h}} \quad \text{[nautical miles]}$$

**Step 4:** Endurance (in days) given fuel carried:

$$Endurance = \dfrac{F_T}{F_{d}} = \dfrac{F_T \times 10^6}{BHP \times SFOC \times 24}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU ESE Standard)

**Problem:** A 40 m stern trawler has a service displacement $\Delta = 850$ tonnes and service speed $V_s = 13$ knots. The Admiralty coefficient $C = 380$. Transmission efficiency product $\eta_{GB} \cdot \eta_{SH} = 0.95$. Propulsive coefficient $P_C = 0.60$. SFOC = 210 g/kWh. The vessel carries 180 tonnes of fuel. Calculate EHP, SHP, BHP, daily fuel consumption, and range.

**Step 1:** Compute EHP using Admiralty formula:

$$EHP = \dfrac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V_s^3}{C}$$

Calculate $\Delta^{2/3}$:

$$\Delta^{2/3} = 850^{0.6667} = 90.5 \text{ (approximately)}$$

(Using $850^{2/3} = (850^2)^{1/3} = 722500^{1/3} \approx 89.96$, taking $\approx 90$)

Calculate $V_s^3$:

$$V_s^3 = 13^3 = 2197 \text{ knots}^3$$

Therefore:

$$EHP = \dfrac{90 \times 2197}{380} = \dfrac{197730}{380} = 520.3 \text{ metric HP}$$

Convert to kW:

$$EHP_{kW} = 520.3 \times 0.7355 \approx 382.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2:** Compute SHP:

$$SHP = \dfrac{EHP}{P_C} = \dfrac{382.7}{0.60} = 637.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3:** Compute BHP:

$$BHP = \dfrac{SHP}{\eta_{GB} \cdot \eta_{SH}} = \dfrac{637.8}{0.95} = 671.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4:** Daily fuel consumption (assume 90% MCR continuous):

$$BHP_{cont} = 0.90 \times 671.4 = 604.3 \text{ kW}$$

$$F_{h} = \dfrac{604.3 \times 210}{10^6} = 0.1269 \text{ tonnes/hr}$$

$$F_{d} = 0.1269 \times 24 = 3.05 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Step 5:** Range:

$$Range = \dfrac{180 \times 13}{0.1269 \times 24} = \dfrac{2340}{3.046} = 768.2 \text{ nautical miles}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The trawler can transit approximately 768 nm before refueling. Actual operational range includes a 15–20% safety margin for weather, currents, and maneuvering during fishing operations.

---

flowchart TD

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Diesel-Electric Propulsion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Diesel Engine 1] --> G1[Generator 1]
    D2[Diesel Engine 2] --> G2[Generator 2]
    G1 --> BUS[HV Switchboard / DC Bus]
    G2 --> BUS
    BAT[Battery Bank] --> BUS
    BUS --> CONV[Power Converter]
    CONV --> M1[Electric Motor 1]
    CONV --> M2[Electric Motor 2]
    M1 --> P1[Propeller 1 Azimuth]

```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Diesel-Mechanical Geared Propulsion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Main Engine] --> CLUTCH[Elastic Coupling / Clutch]
    CLUTCH --> GB[Reduction Gearbox]
    GB --> SHAFT[Intermediate Shaft]
    SHAFT --> STERN_TAIL[Stern Tube Shaft]
    STERN_TAIL --> PROP[Fixed Pitch Propeller]
    GB --> PTO1[Power Take Off for Winches]
    GB --> PTO2[Power Take Off for Compressor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Azimuth Thruster / Z-drive** arrangement shown in the diesel-electric diagram is now standard in modern Norwegian and Icelandic trawlers because it provides 360° thrust vectoring — critical for precise positioning during purse seine operations and pelagic trawling.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Propulsive Coefficient" (P_C) of a fishing vessel. State its typical range and explain why it is lower than 1.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Propulsive Coefficient (P_C)** is defined as the ratio of the Effective Horsepower (EHP) — the useful power required to overcome the vessel's resistance — to the Shaft Horsepower (SHP) — the power actually delivered to the propeller:

$$P_C = \dfrac{EHP}{SHP}$$

Typical range for fishing vessels: **0.55 to 0.65**.

It is less than unity (P_C < 1) because of two categories of losses:
1. **Hull losses** — represented by hull efficiency $\eta_H < 1$, due to wake non-uniformity, frictional resistance, and air drag.
2. **Propeller losses** — represented by relative rotative efficiency $\eta_R$ and open-water efficiency $\eta_0$, accounting for rotational, profile, and cavitation losses.

A higher P_C indicates a more efficient hull-propeller combination.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*List any four factors that influence the selection of a main engine for a fishing vessel.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Required Brake Horsepower (BHP)** — determined from EHP, propulsive coefficient, and transmission efficiencies.
2. **Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC)** — directly impacts operating economics; modern marine engines target **180–210 g/kWh**.
3. **Specific Weight and Space** — kg/kW and m³/MW are critical for displacement-limited fishing vessels.
4. **Operational Profile** — must handle both cruising and low-speed fishing operations, often with simultaneous PTO load (winches, RSW compressors).

Additional factors include reliability (MTBO), IMO Tier emissions compliance, cold-start capability, vibration/noise limits, and lifecycle cost.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE mandates **Module Internal Choice**: students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B from each module. Both options are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the four principal propulsion systems used in modern fishing vessels (diesel-mechanical direct, diesel-mechanical geared, diesel-electric, and hybrid). Tabulate your answer covering: working principle, thermal efficiency, capital cost, weight, low-speed control, and suitability for fishing operations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, explain the **diesel-electric propulsion system** of a modern stern trawler. Discuss its advantages for trawling operations and state two limitations.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Comparison Table of Propulsion Systems for Fishing Vessels**

| **Parameter** | **Diesel Mech Direct** | **Diesel Mech Geared** | **Diesel-Electric** | **Hybrid (D+E+Battery)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Working Principle | Engine directly coupled to propeller shaft | Engine coupled via reduction gearbox | Diesel generators feed electric motors driving propellers | Diesel + electric motor + battery storage in parallel |
| Thermal Efficiency | 38–42% | 40–45% | 42–48% | 45–50% |
| Capital Cost | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| Specific Weight (kg/kW) | 8–12 | 7–10 | 10–18 | 12–22 (including batteries) |
| Low-Speed Control | Poor (without CPP) | Moderate (with CPP) | Excellent (variable frequency drive) | Excellent |
| Suitability for Fishing | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ (emerging) |

**Conclusion:** Diesel-electric is increasingly preferred for vessels > 30 m due to fuel economy at part-load and superior low-speed control during trawling.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Diesel-Electric Propulsion — Block Diagram:**

```
[Diesel Engine 1] --> [Generator 1] ---|
                                       |
[Diesel Engine 2] --> [Generator 2] ---+--> [HV Switchboard] --> [VFD Converter] --> [Electric Motor] --> [Azimuth Propeller]
                                       |
[Battery Bank] ------------------------+                       |
                                       |                       v
                                       |              [RSW Compressor Motor]
                                       |                       |
                                       +---------------> [Hydraulic Winch Motor]
```

**Working Principle:**
Diesel engines drive synchronous generators producing 3-phase AC power at fixed voltage/frequency (e.g., 690 V, 60 Hz). This power is distributed via a high-voltage switchboard and converted by Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) to feed electric motors coupled to azimuth thrusters (Z-drives), which serve as both propulsion and steering devices.

**Advantages for Trawling:**
1. **Precise low-speed control** (0.1 knot precision) essential for trawl door spread optimization.
2. **Multiple generators can run at optimal load** (e.g., 2 × 60% load at higher efficiency than 1 × 120%).
3. **Instant power take-off** for winches and RSW compressors without additional PTO shafts.
4. **Reduced vibration** improves crew welfare during long voyages.

**Limitations:**
1. **High capital cost** — electrical components (converters, motors, switchboards) are expensive.
2. **Complexity** — requires trained electrical engineers onboard; fault diagnosis is more complex.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 28 m fishing vessel has a displacement of 320 tonnes and a service speed of 11 knots. Using the Admiralty coefficient method with $C = 350$ and propulsive coefficient $P_C = 0.60$, calculate:
(i) Effective Horsepower (EHP)
(ii) Shaft Horsepower (SHP)
(iii) Brake Horsepower (BHP), assuming $\eta_{GB} \cdot \eta_{SH} = 0.95$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the criteria for selecting a main engine for a fishing vessel. How does the operational profile of a trawler differ from that of a purse seiner, and how does this influence engine selection?

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Given:**
- $\Delta = 320$ tonnes
- $V_s = 11$ knots
- $C = 350$
- $P_C = 0.60$
- $\eta_{GB} \cdot \eta_{SH} = 0.95$

**(i) Effective Horsepower (EHP):**

Compute $\Delta^{2/3}$:

$$\Delta^{2/3} = 320^{2/3} = (320^2)^{1/3} = 102400^{1/3} \approx 46.86$$

Compute $V_s^3$:

$$V_s^3 = 11^3 = 1331$$

Apply Admiralty formula:

$$EHP = \dfrac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V_s^3}{C} = \dfrac{46.86 \times 1331}{350}$$

$$EHP = \dfrac{62371.4}{350} = 178.2 \text{ metric HP}$$

Convert to kW:

$$EHP_{kW} = 178.2 \times 0.7355 = 131.1 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Shaft Horsepower (SHP):**

$$SHP = \dfrac{EHP}{P_C} = \dfrac{131.1}{0.60} = 218.5 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Brake Horsepower (BHP):**

$$BHP = \dfrac{SHP}{\eta_{GB} \cdot \eta_{SH}} = \dfrac{218.5}{0.95} = 230.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Result:** The vessel requires approximately **230 kW (≈308 HP)** main engine BHP.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Selection Criteria for a Fishing Vessel Main Engine:**

1. **Power output** matched to EHP/SHP requirement with sea margin (typically 15–20% over).
2. **Fuel efficiency (SFOC)** — dominant OPEX factor.
3. **Reliability and MTBO** — 20,000–40,000 hours for modern medium-speed diesels.
4. **Weight and footprint** — specific weight (kg/kW) is critical.
5. **Emission compliance** — IMO Tier II/III NOx limits.
6. **Cold-start and reverse-gear capability** for harbor maneuvering.
7. **Compatibility with fishing gear** — PTO for winches and pumps.
8. **Maintenance infrastructure** — availability of spares and service network in fishing ports.

**Operational Profile Differences:**

| **Operation** | **Trawler** | **Purse Seiner** |
|---|---|---|
| Speed during fishing | 3–5 knots (towing) | 0–2 knots (encircling school) |
| Speed during transit | 10–14 knots | 12–16 knots |
| Gear load | Sustained high (trawl warps @ 20–40 tonnes) | Intermittent, very high during pursing |
| Auxiliary load | RSW compressors running 24/7 | Heavy-duty deck cranes |
| Maneuverability need | High (course changes during tow) | Extreme (precise encirclement) |

**Influence on Engine Selection:**

- **Trawler:** Favors **medium-speed diesel** (600–1000 RPM) with high torque at low RPM for towing, often with CPP for efficiency at varying tow speeds.
- **Purse Seiner:** Favors **diesel-electric with azimuth thrusters** for 360° maneuverability during encirclement and quiet operation (does not scare fish).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. **Mixing up EHP, SHP, BHP** — always remember BHP > SHP > EHP (since efficiencies are < 1).
> 2. **Forgetting sea margin** — the engine should be selected with 15–20% margin over continuous service BHP for rough weather.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversions** — $1 \text{ metric HP} = 735.5 \text{ W}$; knots to m/s requires $\times 0.5144$.
> 4. **Confusing SFOC units** — SFOC is in **g/kWh**, not kg/kWh.
> 5. **Omitting PTO loads** in BHP calculation — winch and compressor loads can add 10–30% to propulsion BHP for active fishing vessels.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick Recall:**

- [x] **P_C** = EHP / SHP, typically **0.55–0.65**.
- [x] **EHP** = R_T × V_s / 75 (metric HP) or / 102 (kW).
- [x] **SHP** = EHP / P_C.
- [x] **BHP** = SHP / (η_GB × η_SH).
- [x] **Admiralty EHP** = Δ^(2/3) × V_s³ / C.
- [x] **SFOC** range: **180–220 g/kWh** for modern marine diesels.
- [x] **Fishing vessel main engine** = medium-speed diesel (600–1000 RPM) with reduction gearbox for vessels 20–45 m LOA.
- [x] **Diesel-electric** is preferred for trawlers > 30 m and purse seiners due to low-speed control.
- [x] **Hybrid propulsion** is emerging for IMO MARPOL Annex VI compliance in ECAs.
- [x] **Specific weight** of marine diesel: 7–12 kg/kW.
- [x] **P_C × η_GB × η_SH** = overall propulsive efficiency η_D.
- [x] **Sea margin** = 15–20% over MCR for fishing vessels.
- [x] **Purse seiners** need azimuth thrusters for precise encirclement; **trawlers** need high torque at low RPM.
- [x] **Cold-start** is critical for high-latitude fishing (Barents Sea, Antarctic).
- [x] **IMO Tier III** emission standards apply to engines > 130 kW installed in ECA zones after 2016.
- [x] **PTO/STO** loads (winches, RSW compressors) must be added to propulsion BHP for accurate engine sizing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Equipment/Instruments for Fish Finding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selection of Equipment/Instruments for Fish Finding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fish Finding Equipment (FFE)** refers to the integrated suite of electronic, acoustic, and navigational instruments mounted on a fishing vessel whose combined function is the *detection, localisation, identification, and quantification* of commercially exploitable fish aggregations and the seabed beneath the vessel, enabling the skipper to deploy gear with maximum biological and economic efficiency.

Within KTU Module 3, this topic is studied under the broader heading of **Auxiliary Fishing Machinery & Navigation Electronics**, and is treated as a *design selection* problem — i.e., the student is expected to justify the choice of an instrument based on vessel size, target species, operating depth, and economic constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The word *selection* is critical. You are not asked "what is a fish finder?" but "**which instrument, why, and based on what performance criterion?**" — always frame your answers around selection rationale.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Underwater Flashlight That Listens"

Imagine you are standing in a dark room (the ocean) holding a torch (the echo sounder). The torch does not emit *light* — it emits a *click* (an acoustic pulse). When that click hits a fish, the fish bounces a tiny echo back. The torch "hears" that echo and times how long it took to come back. Because sound travels in seawater at a known speed (**≈ 1500 m/s**), the time delay tells you **exactly how far** the fish is below you.

- A **regular flashlight** = single beam echo sounder (illuminates one spot at a time).
- A **swiveling flashlight** = scanning sonar (illuminates a 360° wedge).
- A **flashlight with coloured filters** = colour-coded fish finder (different colours = different fish densities).
- A **GPS receiver** = your street address, telling *where* you are when you found the fish.
- A **compass** = your direction while searching.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use sound, not light?** Sunlight cannot penetrate beyond ~200 m in open ocean. Sound, however, can travel thousands of metres — making acoustic detection the only practical remote-sensing technique underwater.

## 1.3 Broad Classification of Fish-Finding Instruments

The instruments are grouped by their operating principle:

| Functional Group | Instruments | Primary Function |
|------------------|-------------|------------------|
| **Acoustic (active)** | Echo sounder, Fish finder, Scanning sonar, Net sonde | Emit pulses, receive echoes |
| **Acoustic (passive)** | Hydrophone, Passive sonar | Listen only (marine mammals) |
| **Navigational** | GPS, Radar, Gyro/ Magnetic compass, AIS, ECDIS | Position & orientation |
| **Environmental** | CTD sensor, Thermometer, Fluorometer, Plankton net | Water-column profiling |
| **Auxiliary** | VMS, Plotter, Echo sounder recorder, Printer | Logging & compliance |

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants Used Throughout

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise the following constants — they appear in nearly every numerical question in KTU Module 3.
>
> - Speed of sound in seawater: **c ≈ 1500 m/s**
> - Speed of sound in freshwater: **c ≈ 1450 m/s**
> - Density of seawater: **ρ ≈ 1025 kg/m³**
> - Acoustic impedance of seawater: **Z ≈ 1.54 × 10⁶ kg/(m²·s)** (Rayl)
> - One nautical mile: **1852 m**
> - One fathom: **1.8288 m**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of an Echo Sounder — Step-by-Step Logic

1. The **transducer** (mounted on the hull) converts electrical energy into a mechanical acoustic pulse at a chosen **frequency (f)** and **pulse duration (τ)**.
2. The pulse propagates downward through the water column at velocity **c**.
3. A fraction of the acoustic energy is scattered back whenever the pulse crosses an interface of differing acoustic impedance (e.g., fish swim bladder, seabed).
4. The transducer (acting as receiver during listen mode) captures the returning echo.
5. The time delay **Δt** between transmission and reception is measured.
6. The instrument electronics compute one-way range as **R = (c · Δt) / 2**.
7. The display maps intensity of echo (colour/darkness) vs range, producing a 2-D "echogram".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor of **2** appears because the pulse travels *down and back* — this is the single most-derivable formula in Module 3 and is guaranteed to appear in KTU numericals.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each System Parameter

| Parameter | Why it matters in selection |
|-----------|---------------------------|
| **Operating frequency (f)** | Higher f → smaller wavelength → better resolution of small fish; Lower f → larger penetration depth. Trade-off! |
| **Pulse length (τ)** | Shorter pulse → finer range resolution (ΔR = cτ/2) but lower SNR |
| **Beam width / cone angle (θ)** | Narrower beam → higher directivity → better target strength; but slower area coverage |
| **Source Level (SL)** | Higher SL → deeper detection; but consumes power |
| **TVG (Time-Varied Gain)** | Compensates for geometric spreading loss & absorption |
| **Bandwidth (BW)** | Wider BW → finer axial resolution; ΔR ≈ c/(2·BW) |

## 2.3 Frequency Selection Logic (Most-Tested Concept)

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb (the "50 / 200 / 400 kHz" triad):**
> - **50 kHz** → deep water (>500 m), wide beam, detects large pelagic schools
> - **200 kHz** → mid-depth (50–500 m), standard fish-finder frequency
> - **400 kHz / 1 MHz** → shallow water, high-resolution bottom discrimination

The absorption coefficient in seawater is empirically modelled by **Francois-Garrison** or simplified **Thorp's formula**:

$$\alpha(f) = \frac{0.11 \, f^2}{1 + f^2} + \frac{44 \, f^2}{4100 + f^2} + 2.75 \times 10^{-4} f^2 + 0.003 \quad [\text{dB/km}]$$

where **f** is in **kHz**. Note the rapid rise of α with f — this is why high frequencies do not penetrate.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Boundary |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | One-way range to target | R in m, Δt in s, c ≈ 1500 m/s |
| 2 | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$ | Range resolution (pulse-limited) | ΔR in m |
| 3 | $\Delta R \approx \dfrac{c}{2 \, BW}$ | Range resolution (bandwidth-limited) | BW in Hz |
| 4 | $D = c \cdot \tau$ | Physical pulse length in water | D in m |
| 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Acoustic wavelength | λ in m |
| 6 | $\theta_{-3dB} \approx 70 \dfrac{\lambda}{D_t}$ | Half-power beamwidth (circular piston) | θ in degrees, Dₜ = transducer diameter |
| 7 | $\text{TVG gain} = 40 \log R + 2\alpha R$ | Spreading + absorption compensation | dB |
| 8 | $SNR = SL - 2TL + TS - (NL - AG)$ | Sonar equation (active) | All in dB |
| 9 | $TL = 20 \log R + \alpha R$ | Transmission loss | dB |
| 10 | $AF = 10 \log \dfrac{I_\text{ref}}{I}$ | Attenuation factor | dB |
| 11 | $P_\text{acoustic} = \eta \cdot P_\text{electric}$ | Transducer efficiency | η ≈ 0.3–0.7 |
| 12 | $Q = \dfrac{f}{\Delta f}$ | Q-factor (selectivity) | Dimensionless |
| 13 | $E = \dfrac{P_\text{peak}}{P_\text{average}}$ | Energy ratio / duty-cycle ratio | Dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When the question gives **two-way travel time** (Δt), do *not* divide by 2 again. Only divide by 2 when converting *round-trip time* into *one-way range*. Always read carefully: "echo sounder displays a delay of 0.04 s" means total round trip.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fish stock assessment:** echo integration by scientific echo sounders estimates biomass.
- **Trawl gear monitoring:** net sondes transmit real-time gear geometry to avoid seabed damage.
- **Collision avoidance:** radar + AIS integration is mandated under IMO/SOLAS for vessels > 300 GT.
- **Catch efficiency:** a well-selected fish finder can raise catch per unit effort (CPUE) by 30–200 %.
- **Regulatory compliance:** Vessel Monitoring System (VMS) is mandatory in most fisheries for quota enforcement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Range Equation

We derive the relationship between the measured time delay and the depth of the reflecting target.

Let:
- $R$ = one-way slant range to the target [m]
- $c$ = speed of sound in seawater [m/s]
- $t_\text{down}$ = time for pulse to travel downward [s]
- $t_\text{up}$ = time for echo to travel upward [s]
- $\Delta t$ = total round-trip time delay recorded by the instrument [s]

By the definition of constant velocity:

$$R = c \cdot t_\text{down}$$

Because the pulse travels the *same* distance on the way back:

$$t_\text{down} = t_\text{up} = \dfrac{R}{c}$$

Therefore, the total measured time delay is the sum of both legs:

$$\Delta t = t_\text{down} + t_\text{up} = \dfrac{R}{c} + \dfrac{R}{c} = \dfrac{2R}{c}$$

Solving for $R$:

$$R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

This is the fundamental **range equation** of an active sonar / echo sounder, valid for any active acoustic ranging instrument.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Range Calculation

> **Problem (KTU-style):** An echo sounder records a bottom return **0.024 s** after transmission. Taking the speed of sound in seawater as **1500 m/s**, find the depth of water beneath the transducer.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the given quantities:

$$c = 1500 \text{ m/s}, \quad \Delta t = 0.024 \text{ s}$$

Step 2 — Apply the range equation:

$$R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

Step 3 — Substitute the values:

$$R = \dfrac{1500 \times 0.024}{2}$$

Step 4 — Multiply the numerator:

$$R = \dfrac{36}{2} = 18 \text{ m}$$

**∴ The depth of water beneath the transducer is 18 m.**

## 3.3 Derivation of Range Resolution from Pulse Length

The minimum separation at which two adjacent targets can be distinguished as *separate* echoes is called the **range resolution** $\Delta R$.

Let $\tau$ = pulse duration [s]. When the trailing edge of the pulse just leaves the transducer at time $\tau$, the leading edge has already travelled a distance equal to the **physical pulse length** $D$ in the water.

Therefore:

$$D = c \cdot \tau$$

For two targets to appear as separate echoes, the pulse length must be shorter than twice the separation (the "round-trip" condition). Therefore, the **range resolution** is:

$$\Delta R = \dfrac{D}{2} = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why deep-water scientific echo sounders use **Chirp / FM pulses** with long duration but wide bandwidth — they get high *energy* (deep penetration) AND high *resolution* simultaneously.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Pulse Length vs Resolution Trade-off

> **Problem:** A 200 kHz fish finder emits a pulse of duration **0.4 ms**. Calculate (a) physical pulse length, (b) range resolution.

**Solution:**

(a) Physical pulse length:

$$D = c \cdot \tau = 1500 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3} = 0.6 \text{ m}$$

(b) Range resolution:

$$\Delta R = \dfrac{D}{2} = \dfrac{0.6}{2} = 0.3 \text{ m}$$

**∴ The instrument can distinguish two fish that are vertically separated by at least 0.3 m.**

## 3.5 Analytical Selection Matrix — Matching Instrument to Application

The following decision matrix is **the most commonly tested KTU sub-question** under the "selection" theme. Use it as your model answer template.

| Selection Criterion | Shallow trawler (< 100 m) | Mid-depth trawler (100–500 m) | Deep long-liner (> 500 m) | Purse-seiner (pelagic) |
|---------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| **Required frequency** | 200–400 kHz | 50–200 kHz (dual freq) | 38–50 kHz | 50–120 kHz + sonar |
| **Beam type** | Single narrow-beam | Split-beam / dual-beam | Single wide-beam | Scanning sonar + omni |
| **Transducer location** | Hull-mounted | Hull-mounted or towed | Towed body / drop keel | Hull + drop keel |
| **Display** | 7–10″ colour LCD | 10–15″ colour LCD + plotter | 12–17″ colour, paper backup | Multi-screen console |
| **Auxiliary equipment** | GPS, compass | GPS, compass, net sonde | GPS, gyro, autopilot, sonar | GPS, sonar, current meter, bird radar |
| **Approx. power consumption** | 50–150 W | 150–400 W | 400–1000 W | 600–1500 W |
| **Typical cost (INR)** | 50k–2 lakh | 2–8 lakh | 8–30 lakh | 15–60 lakh |

## 3.6 Decision Tree for Selecting Fish-Finding Equipment

A systematic, step-by-step procedure for KTU examination answers:

1. **Identify target species & depth zone** → Pelagic / demersal / benthic.
2. **Determine operating depth range** → Choose frequency band from the 50/200/400 kHz triad.
3. **Estimate vessel size & available power** → Larger vessel → higher source level allowed.
4. **Select transducer type** → Hull-mounted (small boats) / drop keel (mid) / towed body (deep).
5. **Select display type** → B&W paper / colour LCD / multi-function display (MFD).
6. **Choose complementary navigation suite** → GPS + compass + radar + AIS (mandatory >20 m).
7. **Decide on auxiliary** → Net sonde (trawlers), echo integration module (tuna seiners), thermal sensor (tuna fishery).
8. **Evaluate cost–benefit & compliance** → DG Shipping rules, FAO/IMC norms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "select equipment for a given vessel", *always* present your answer in the form of this 8-step decision tree, then tabulate the chosen equipment with justification.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical Fish-Finding Console

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the signal-flow architecture of an integrated fishing-electronics suite, from acoustic transmission to skipper's display. This is the kind of diagram that fetches full marks in a KTU 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TXU[Transmitter Unit] -->|Electrical pulse| TRX[TRX Switch Duplexer]
    TRX -->|Drive signal| TDX[Transducer Piston]
    TDX -->|Acoustic pulse downward| WAT[Seawater Column]
    WAT -->|Backscattered echo| TDX
    TDX -->|Echo voltage| TRX
    TRX -->|Pre-amp input| RXA[Receiver Amplifier with TVG]
    RXA -->|Conditioned signal| DET[Detector and Demodulator]
    DET -->|Envelope| DSP[Digital Signal Processor]
    DSP -->|Echogram stream| DSP
    DSP -->|Video to display| DPL[Colour LCD Display Unit]
    DSP -->|Logs and traces| LOG[Recorder or Plotter]
    NAV[GPS Receiver Module] -->|NMEA 0183 data| MUX[Data Multiplexer]
    CMP[Magnetic Compass Sensor] -->|Heading data| MUX
    MUX -->|Position overlay| DPL
    NET[Net Sonde Transmitter on Gear] -->|Wireless telemetry| TRX
    POW[Ship Mains or Battery Pack] -->|DC 12V or 24V| PSU[Power Supply Unit]
    PSU --> TXU
    PSU --> RXA
    PSU --> DSP
    PSU --> DPL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the pulse from TXU → TRX → TDX → WAT, and the echo path back along the same chain to DPL. The NAV and CMP blocks feed *position* (not echo) information, overlaid on the echogram.

## 4.2 Frequency vs Depth Penetration Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Selection Problem] --> B{Operating Depth Zone?}
    B -->|Less than 100 m| C[Use High Frequency 200 to 400 kHz]
    B -->|100 to 500 m| D[Use Dual Frequency 50 and 200 kHz]
    B -->|Greater than 500 m| E[Use Low Frequency 38 to 50 kHz]
    C --> F[Narrow Beam for Resolution]
    D --> G[Split Beam for Target Strength]
    E --> H[Wide Beam for Energy Density]
    F --> I[Pick Compact Hull Mounted Transducer]
    G --> J[Pick Drop Keel or Towed Body]
    H --> K[Pick Towed Body with Stable Platform]
    I --> L[Select 7 to 10 inch Colour LCD]
    J --> M[Select 12 to 15 inch MFD with Plotter]
    K --> N[Select 15 to 17 inch MFD with Paper Backup]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Classification of Fish-Finding Instruments

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fish Finding Equipment] --> B[Active Acoustic]
    A --> C[Passive Acoustic]
    A --> D[Navigational]
    A --> E[Environmental]
    B --> B1[Single Beam Echo Sounder]
    B --> B2[Dual Beam Echo Sounder]
    B --> B3[Split Beam Echo Sounder]
    B --> B4[Multibeam Echo Sounder]
    B --> B5[Scanning Sonar]
    B --> B6[Net Sonde]
    C --> C1[Hydrophone Array]
    C --> C2[Passive Sonar for Cetacean Detection]
    D --> D1[GPS Receiver]
    D --> D2[Radar]
    D --> D3[Gyro Compass]
    D --> D4[Magnetic Compass]
    D --> D5[AIS Transponder]
    D --> D6[Autopilot]
    E --> E1[CTD Sensor]
    E --> E2[Water Thermograph]
    E --> E3[Fluorometer]
    E --> E4[Current Meter]
    E --> E5[Plankton Indicator]
```

## 4.4 Signal Path of an Echo Sounder Pulse (Sequential Timing)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[T equals 0 ms: Pulse Transmitted] --> P2[T equals tau: Pulse Tail Leaves Transducer]
    P2 --> P3[T equals R by c: Pulse Reaches Target]
    P3 --> P4[Echo Formed at Target]
    P4 --> P5[T equals 2R by c: Echo Returns to Transducer]
    P5 --> P6[Receiver Listens and Records]
    P6 --> P7[Display Plots Mark at Range R]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Memory Hook:** Whenever you draw the timing diagram, always remember the **2 in the denominator** — that single 2 distinguishes a sonar engineer from a confused student.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define an echo sounder. List any four performance parameters used in its selection.**

**Model Answer:**

An **echo sounder** is an active acoustic instrument that determines the depth of water beneath a vessel by transmitting a pulse of sound and measuring the round-trip time of the reflected echo from the seabed or submerged target.

**Four selection parameters:**
1. **Operating frequency** — determines depth penetration and resolution.
2. **Source level / acoustic power output** — determines maximum detection range.
3. **Pulse length / pulse repetition rate** — determines range resolution and ambiguity.
4. **Beamwidth / cone angle** — determines angular resolution and area coverage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write "sonar" and "echo sounder" interchangeably. In KTU valuation, an *echo sounder* is **vertically downward-looking only**, while a *sonar* scans a **wide azimuthal volume**. Do not interchange.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Explain the principle of operation of a fish finder with a neat block diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

A fish finder works on the **active SONAR principle**:

1. The **transmitter** generates a high-power electrical pulse at the chosen RF (e.g., 50 kHz or 200 kHz).
2. The **transducer** converts this electrical pulse into an acoustic pulse which is radiated downward into the water.
3. The acoustic pulse strikes fish (or seabed) and a portion of energy is backscattered.
4. The **transducer** (acting now as a receiver) converts the returning acoustic echo into a weak electrical signal.
5. The **receiver amplifier** applies **Time-Varied Gain (TVG)** to compensate for spreading and absorption losses.
6. The **detector and display** unit plots the echo amplitude against range, producing a 2-D echogram.

**Block diagram:**
`Transmitter → TRX switch → Transducer → Seawater → Transducer → Receiver (TVG) → Detector → Display`

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the functional block architecture of a typical fish-finding system on a modern fishing vessel. List the function of each block.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A bottom-detection echo sounder records the seabed echo **0.06 s** after transmission. Calculate (i) depth of water, (ii) physical pulse length if the pulse duration is **1.2 ms**, and (iii) the range resolution. Take speed of sound in seawater as 1500 m/s.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Functional block diagram of the system:

```
Transmitter → TRX Duplexer → Transducer → Water Column
   ↑                                              ↓
   └─────────── Receiver (TVG) ←─────── Echo ────┘
                     ↓
              Detector → DSP → Display → Recorder
                     ↑
            GPS / Compass input (position overlay)
```

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **Transmitter** | Generates high-power electrical pulse at the chosen frequency |
| **TRX Duplexer** | Switches the transducer between transmit and receive modes |
| **Transducer** | Converts electrical ↔ acoustic energy |
| **Water Column** | Propagation medium; scatters acoustic energy at impedance discontinuities |
| **Receiver + TVG** | Amplifies weak echo; compensates for geometric & absorption losses |
| **Detector** | Extracts envelope of the RF echo |
| **DSP** | Digitises, applies TVG in software, performs echogram rendering |
| **Display** | LCD/MFD showing range vs amplitude |
| **Recorder** | Stores echogram for biological/economic analysis |
| **GPS/Compass** | Overlays position & heading on the echogram |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $\Delta t = 0.06$ s, $\tau = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ s, $c = 1500$ m/s.

(i) Depth:

$$R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \dfrac{1500 \times 0.06}{2} = 45 \text{ m}$$

(ii) Physical pulse length:

$$D = c \cdot \tau = 1500 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} = 1.8 \text{ m}$$

(iii) Range resolution:

$$\Delta R = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2} = \dfrac{1.8}{2} = 0.9 \text{ m}$$

**∴ Depth = 45 m, Pulse length = 1.8 m, Range resolution = 0.9 m.**

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model] (Alternative Choice)

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare single-beam, split-beam, and multibeam echo sounders. State, with justification, which is most suitable for **(i)** a small mechanised trawler and **(ii)** a deep-sea research vessel.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the fundamental range equation for an echo sounder starting from the definition of velocity. Show the complete derivation step-by-step.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative table:**

| Feature | Single-beam | Split-beam | Multibeam |
|---------|-------------|------------|-----------|
| Number of beams | 1 | 1 (4 quadrants) | Many (10s–100s) |
| Target strength measurement | No | Yes (phase comparison) | Limited |
| Angular resolution | Poor | Good (within beam) | Excellent (across swath) |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Very high |
| Typical vessel | Small craft | Mid-size trawler | Research / hydrographic |
| Coverage rate | Slow | Medium | Very fast |

(i) **Small mechanised trawler** → **Single-beam** (low cost, sufficient for bottom detection and rough fish-school indication).

(ii) **Deep-sea research vessel** → **Multibeam** (full bottom coverage, biomass estimation, scientific echo integration).

**(b) Derivation:**

Let $R$ = one-way range [m], $c$ = velocity of sound [m/s], $t_d$ = time taken by pulse to travel downward [s], $t_u$ = time taken by echo to travel upward [s], $\Delta t$ = total round-trip time [s].

By the definition of velocity:

$$v = \dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$$

Applying to the downward leg:

$$c = \dfrac{R}{t_d} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_d = \dfrac{R}{c}$$

Applying to the upward leg (same path):

$$t_u = \dfrac{R}{c}$$

Total round-trip time is the sum of both legs:

$$\Delta t = t_d + t_u = \dfrac{R}{c} + \dfrac{R}{c} = \dfrac{2R}{c}$$

Solving for $R$:

$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}}$$

This is the fundamental range equation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mark Loss #1:** Forgetting the factor of 2 in the denominator → -2 marks.
> - **Common Mark Loss #2:** Using freshwater speed (1450 m/s) instead of seawater (1500 m/s) → -1 mark.
> - **Common Mark Loss #3:** Not mentioning the constant **c** value as a "given / standard" assumption → -0.5 mark.
> - **Common Mark Loss #4:** Confusing "round-trip time" with "two-way time" — they are the same, but if the question states "one-way time", do NOT divide by 2.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin this on your study wall):**
>
> - The **fundamental range equation** is $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ — always include the factor of 2.
> - **c in seawater ≈ 1500 m/s**, in freshwater ≈ 1450 m/s.
> - **Higher frequency** = better resolution, shallower penetration; **lower frequency** = poorer resolution, deeper penetration.
> - Standard fish-finding frequencies are **38 kHz, 50 kHz, 120 kHz, 200 kHz, 400 kHz**.
> - **Range resolution** $\Delta R = \dfrac{c \tau}{2}$ = half of the physical pulse length.
> - **Beamwidth** $\theta_{-3dB} \approx 70 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{D_t}$ — narrow beam needs large transducer or high frequency.
> - **Echo sounder** = downward looking only; **Sonar** = scanning in azimuth.
> - **Selection rule of thumb:** match frequency to depth, match transducer location to vessel size, match display to operating conditions.
> - **Mandatory for vessels > 20 m (IMO/SOLAS + KTU syllabus):** GPS, compass, radar, AIS, echo sounder.
> - For **stock assessment**, use **split-beam or multibeam**; for **navigation**, use **single-beam**.
> - Always **tabulate** your selection in KTU answers — tables earn faster marks than paragraphs.
> - The **TVG** compensates for **40 log R + 2αR** loss in the active sonar equation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Navigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚓ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Maritime Navigation?

**Maritime Navigation** is the science and art of determining the position of a ship at sea and directing its course safely from one point to another, considering weather, sea state, traffic, and the vessel's own operational profile. For a **fishing vessel**, navigation is uniquely challenging because the vessel must simultaneously optimize its transit to fishing grounds, locate productive fish schools, and return safely with the catch — all under demanding operational, economic, and safety constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Navigation is the process of **planning, recording, and controlling** the movement of a vessel from one place to another, accomplished through three classical sub-tasks:
> 1. **Position fixing** (Where am I?)
> 2. **Course plotting** (Where am I going?)
> 3. **Course keeping** (How do I stay on track?)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Invisible Highway"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine driving a car in a dense fog without road markings. A car has road signs, lanes, and a fixed route. A ship at sea has **none of these**. The ocean is a vast, featureless 3D surface. Navigation is therefore like being handed a **blank piece of graph paper** on which you must *draw your own road, signs, and current position* using math, instruments, and the stars. A fishing vessel, additionally, has to "see through the water column" to find fish, making its navigation suite a **hybrid of surface-position systems + underwater-detection systems**.

## 1.3 Branches of Navigation

Navigation on a fishing vessel is classified as follows:

| Branch | Primary Tool | Use in Fishing |
|---|---|---|
| **Terrestrial / Piloting** | Visual bearings, lighthouses, buoys | Coastal transit to harbor |
| **Dead Reckoning (DR)** | Compass + Log + Course | Open-sea transit |
| **Celestial Navigation** | Sextant + chronometer | Emergency backup |
| **Electronic Navigation** | GPS, Radar, AIS, ECDIS | Primary modern means |
| **Satellite Navigation** | GPS / GLONASS / Galileo | Global position fixing |
| **Acoustic Navigation** | Echo sounder, SONAR | Fish-school detection, depth |

## 1.4 Key Maritime Units and Constants (MUST memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **1 Nautical Mile (NM)** = **1852 m** (≈ 1 minute of latitude)
> - **1 Knot (kt)** = **1 NM/hour** (≈ 0.5144 m/s)
> - **1° of Latitude** = **60 NM** (constant everywhere on Earth)
> - **1° of Longitude** = **60 · cos(φ) NM** (varies with latitude φ)
> - **Earth's circumference (meridional)** = **21,600 NM**
> - **Standard Earth radius (R)** = **3440 NM** = 6371 km
> - **1 hour of time** = **15° of longitude** (rotation rate of Earth)
