# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Fishing-Vessel Navigation

### Pillar 1 — Position Fixing (Where am I?)
Position fixing is the determination of the vessel's coordinates **(Latitude φ, Longitude λ)**. Two broad methods exist:
- **Absolute fixing** — Independent of previous position (e.g., GPS, celestial sight, radar range/bearing).
- **Relative fixing (Running fix)** — Depends on previous position; uses compass and log (Dead Reckoning).

### Pillar 2 — Course Plotting
A **course** is the direction in which the vessel is to be steered. Three reference norths must be clearly understood:

- **True North (TN)** — Geographic North Pole.
- **Magnetic North (MN)** — Direction the compass needle points.
- **Compass North (CN)** — Direction the compass card actually reads.

> [!NOTE]
> The famous **"Cadet" mnemonic**:
> - **T**rue is the **C**orrect (TVMDC: True → Variation → Magnetic → Deviation → Compass)
> - Going **DOWN** the chain (T→C) you **ADD West** (or subtract East).
> - Going **UP** the chain (C→T) you **ADD East** (or subtract West).
> 
> In equation form:
> 
> $$\text{True} = \text{Magnetic} + \text{Variation (East +ve)}$$
> 
> $$\text{Magnetic} = \text{Compass} + \text{Deviation (East +ve)}$$

### Pillar 3 — Course Keeping (Helmsmanship)
The Automatic Pilot (Gyro-Pilot) maintains the vessel on the ordered heading using feedback from the **gyrocompass** and a hydraulic/electric rudder actuator.

### Pillar 4 — Underwater Navigation (Fishing-Specific)
Unlike merchant vessels, a fishing vessel uses an **echo sounder** and **SONAR** to "navigate" vertically through the water column to locate fish concentrations, assess seabed type, and determine trawlable bottom.

## 2.2 The Classical Sailings (Mercator & Great-Circle)

### Mercator Sailing (Rhumb-Line)
Used for short-to-medium courses on a **Mercator chart**. The course angle is constant.

**Departure (in NM):**
$$\text{Dep} = \Delta\lambda \cdot \cos(\phi_{m}) \cdot 60$$

Where $\phi_m = (\phi_1 + \phi_2)/2$ is the mean latitude.

**Course Angle:**
$$\tan(C) = \frac{\text{Dep}}{\text{Distance}} = \frac{\Delta\lambda \cdot \cos(\phi_{m}) \cdot 60}{D_{L}}$$

Where $D_L$ = difference of latitude (NM).

### Great-Circle Sailing (Orthodrome)
The shortest path on a sphere. For very long ocean voyages, great-circle sailing saves distance. Computed via spherical trigonometry:

$$\cos(D) = \sin\phi_1 \sin\phi_2 + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda)$$

Where $D$ = angular distance (in degrees; multiply by 60 for NM).

## 2.3 Electronic Navigation: The Modern Stack

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 expects students to be aware of the following systems:**

### a) GPS (Global Positioning System)
- **24+ MEO satellites** at ~20,200 km altitude.
- Position from **trilateration** on received signals.
- **Accuracy** — Civilian (SPS): ~**5–10 m** horizontal; with WAAS/SBAS: < 3 m.
- Fishing vessels use **DGPS** (Differential GPS) for precision fish-school mapping.

### b) Radar (RAdio Detection And Ranging)
- Measures **range** via round-trip time of a microwave pulse (X-band ~9 GHz, S-band ~3 GHz).
- **Radar range equation (maximum instrumented range):**
$$R_{max} = \sqrt[4]{\frac{P_t \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{P_{min} \cdot (4\pi)^3}}$$

  where $P_t$ = transmitted power, $G$ = antenna gain, $\lambda$ = wavelength, $\sigma$ = target RCS, $P_{min}$ = minimum detectable signal.

- **Geometric horizon range (NM):** $R = 2.2\sqrt{h}$ where $h$ is antenna height in **metres**.

### c) AIS (Automatic Identification System)
- VHF-based transponder; transmits **MMSI, position, course, speed** of own ship and nearby vessels.
- Mandatory for vessels > **300 GT** under SOLAS; progressively required on fishing vessels.

### d) ECDIS (Electronic Chart Display & Information System)
- **Digital nautical chart** (IHO S-57/S-101) integrated with GPS, radar overlay, AIS.
- Carriage requirement: SOLAS Chapter V — for fishing vessels per **Torremolinos/Cape Town** instruments.

### e) Echo Sounder & SONAR
- Echo sounder: vertical beam, measures **water depth** beneath the hull.
- SONAR (echo-ranging): tilted or omnidirectional beam; used to **detect fish schools** in a 2D/3D cone.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is a **High-Yield Formula Box** every KTU 2024 student must memorize.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 NM definition | 1 NM = 1852 m | m |
| 2 | Speed conversion | 1 kt = 0.5144 m/s | m/s |
| 3 | Latitude 1° | 1° lat = 60 NM | NM |
| 4 | Longitude 1° | 1° long = 60·cos(φ) | NM |
| 5 | Time–Longitude | 1 h = 15° long | — |
| 6 | Distance/Speed/Time | D = S × T | NM, kt, h |
| 7 | Compass correction | T = M + Var(E+) ; M = C + Dev(E+) | degrees |
| 8 | Mercator course | tan(C) = Dep ÷ Dlat | degrees |
| 9 | Great-circle distance | cos D = sinφ₁sinφ₂ + cosφ₁cosφ₂cos(Δλ) | NM × 60 |
| 10 | Radar horizon | R = 2.2√h | NM, m |
| 11 | Pulse round-trip range | R = (c·t) ÷ 2 | m, s |
| 12 | Earth radius | R = 3440 NM = 6371 km | — |
| 13 | Magnetic dip (tan) | tan(θ) = 2·tan(φ) ÷ 2 | degrees |
| 14 | North-component speed | N = S·cos(C) | kt |
| 15 | East-component speed | E = S·sin(C) | kt |
| 16 | Set & Drift (current) | Δφ = (N ± N_c)·T ; Δλ = (E ± E_c)·T ÷ (60·cos φ) | ° |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Fuel optimization** on fishing transits uses **Great-Circle vs Rhumb-Line** decision analysis.
> - **ECDIS + AIS** integration is the cornerstone of **COLREGs (1972) compliance** during fishing.
> - **Fishing gear navigation** (trawl door positioning) uses **hydroacoustic tracking**, sharing principles with SONAR.
> - **IMO MSC.1/Circ.1503** prescribes ECDIS performance standards; fishing vessels ≥ 24 m LOA increasingly bound.

# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Departure and Mercator Course

**Given:** A fishing vessel departs position **A (φ₁ = 10°00′ N, λ₁ = 060°00′ W)** and arrives at **B (φ₂ = 14°30′ N, λ₂ = 055°30′ W)**. Compute the rhumb-line **distance** and **course**.

### Step 1 — Compute the Difference of Latitude (Dlat)
$$D_{L} = \phi_2 - \phi_1 = 14°30' - 10°00' = +4°30' = 4.5°$$

### Step 2 — Convert Dlat to NM
$$D_{L_{NM}} = D_{L} \times 60 = 4.5 \times 60 = 270 \text{ NM}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Difference of Longitude (DLo)
$$\Delta\lambda = \lambda_2 - \lambda_1 = 055°30' - 060°00' = -4°30' = 4.5°$$

(Absolute value is used for distance; the sign is carried for direction.)

### Step 4 — Compute the Mean Latitude
$$\phi_m = \frac{\phi_1 + \phi_2}{2} = \frac{10°00' + 14°30'}{2} = 12°15'\ \text{N}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Departure (in NM)
$$\text{Dep} = \Delta\lambda \cdot \cos(\phi_m) \cdot 60$$

$$\text{Dep} = 4.5 \cdot \cos(12.25°) \cdot 60$$

$$\cos(12.25°) = 0.9771$$

$$\text{Dep} = 4.5 \times 0.9771 \times 60 = 263.82 \text{ NM}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Rhumb-Line Distance
By the Pythagorean theorem on the Mercator plot:
$$D = \sqrt{D_{L_{NM}}^{2} + \text{Dep}^{2}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{270^{2} + 263.82^{2}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{72{,}900 + 69{,}601} = \sqrt{142{,}501}$$

$$D \approx 377.5 \text{ NM}$$

### Step 7 — Compute the Mercator Course
$$\tan(C) = \frac{\text{Dep}}{D_{L_{NM}}} = \frac{263.82}{270} = 0.9771$$

$$C = \arctan(0.9771) = 44.34°$$

Since both Dlat is positive (North) and Dep is positive (West is positive if we set up an East-positive convention — adjust by sign convention), the vessel is steering roughly **N 44° W** (toward the North-West).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A fishing vessel steaming at 10 knots will cover 377.5 NM in **37.75 hours**, demanding careful **fuel planning** and **provisioning** before the voyage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Compass-to-True Conversion (Cadet Walk)

**Given:** A trawler is steering **Compass course = 132°**. The compass deviation card shows **Deviation = 3° W** at that heading. The local magnetic variation is **8° E**.

**Step 1 — Apply Deviation (Compass → Magnetic):**
$$M = C + \text{Dev (East +ve)}$$
$$M = 132° + (-3°) = 129°$$

**Step 2 — Apply Variation (Magnetic → True):**
$$T = M + \text{Var (East +ve)}$$
$$T = 129° + (+8°) = 137°$$

**Answer:** The true course is **T = 137°**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification with "Add West" rule (going UP the chain, C → T):**
> - Deviation = 3° W → ADD 3°
> - Variation = 8° E → SUBTRACT 8°
> - Net: 132° + 3° − 8° = **127°** ??
> 
> ⚠️ *Self-correction:* The rule is "**Add West Going Down (T→C)**". To go UP (C→T) we must **Subtract West, Add East**. Hence:
> - 132° + 3° (Add West) then 132°+3°−8°? Let me re-evaluate carefully using a unified method.

> [!WARNING]
> **Reconciliation using a single formula (TVMDC):**
> 
> $$T = C + \text{Dev} + \text{Var}$$
> 
> Sign convention: **East is positive, West is negative**.
> 
> $$T = 132 + (-3) + (+8) = 137°$$
> 
> This is the **canonical** answer. The student's pitfall is mixing "+W" rules in two different directions. **Always use TVMDC with sign convention.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Radar Range from Pulse Round-Trip

**Given:** A fishing vessel's radar emits a pulse of duration **τ = 0.1 μs** and the return echo from a target is received **t = 0.000185 s** later. The pulse repetition frequency is **PRF = 1000 Hz**. Find the range.

**Step 1 — Speed of EM wave in air (c):**
$$c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — One-way distance to target:**
$$R = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \times 1.85 \times 10^{-4}}{2}$$

$$R = \frac{55{,}500}{2} = 27{,}750 \text{ m} = 27.75 \text{ km}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to NM:**
$$R_{NM} = \frac{27{,}750}{1852} = 14.98 \text{ NM}$$

**Step 4 — Pulse length (spatial):**
$$\Delta R = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 15 \text{ m}$$

This is the **radar's range resolution** — two targets must be at least 15 m apart along the line of sight to be distinguished.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** A pulse length of 15 m is excellent for navigation; fishing vessels use shorter pulses (~0.05 μs) when measuring **net-mesh opening geometry** in trawl-mounted scanning SONAR.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Great-Circle Distance (Spherical Law of Cosines)

**Given:** Vessel at **A (φ₁ = 50° N, λ₁ = 010° W)** sailing to **B (φ₂ = 60° N, λ₂ = 020° W)**.

**Step 1 — Compute DLo:**
$$\Delta\lambda = 020° - 010° = 10°$$

**Step 2 — Apply the spherical law of cosines:**
$$\cos(D) = \sin(50°)\sin(60°) + \cos(50°)\cos(60°)\cos(10°)$$

$$\sin(50°) = 0.7660, \quad \sin(60°) = 0.8660$$
$$\cos(50°) = 0.6428, \quad \cos(60°) = 0.5000, \quad \cos(10°) = 0.9848$$

$$\cos(D) = (0.7660)(0.8660) + (0.6428)(0.5000)(0.9848)$$

$$\cos(D) = 0.6634 + 0.3165 = 0.9799$$

$$D = \arccos(0.9799) = 11.55°$$

**Step 3 — Convert to NM:**
$$D_{NM} = 11.55 \times 60 = 693.0 \text{ NM}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A rhumb-line on the same route would yield ~696 NM. The great-circle saves **3 NM** — a small absolute saving, but for very long ocean voyages (e.g., a trans-Pacific tuna seiner) great-circle savings can exceed 100 NM.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Navigation Method

| Vessel Profile | Method | Justification | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coastal trawler, 12 m, 5 NM off coast | Visual piloting + GPS + Echo sounder | Frequent land sightings, short transit | High |
| Offshore purse seiner, 35 m, 200 NM off coast | GPS + Radar + AIS + Gyrocompass | Long transit, no land in sight | High |
| Deep-sea long-liner, 45 m, 2000 NM off coast | GPS + DGPS + Gyro + Satellite comms | Extreme isolation, weather routing | Medium |
| Inland waterway fishing boat, 8 m | Handheld GPS + magnetic compass | Shallow waters, narrow channels | Low |
| Emergency / Equipment failure | Celestial (Sextant) | Back-up when electronics fail | High (theoretical) |

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Integrated Bridge System of a Modern Fishing Vessel (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Sensors["SENSOR LAYER: GPS Antenna, Gyrocompass, Log, Echo Sounder, Wind Vane, Radar Scanner, AIS VHF"]
    PS["POSITION & ATTITUDE: GPS, GLONASS, DGPS, Gyrocompass, Magnetic Compass, Fathometer"]
    NavDis["NAVIGATION DISPLAYS: ECDIS, Radar PPI, AIS MFD, Conning Display, Repeater"]
    AutoPilot["AUTOMATION: Autopilot, Track Control, Engine Telegraph, RPM Governor"]
    Comm["COMMUNICATION: GMDSS VHF, MF/HF, INMARSAT, EPIRB, SART"]
    Fish["FISH-FINDING SUITE: Echo Sounder, SONAR, Trawl Eye, Net Recorder"]
    Wx["WEATHER: Anemometer, Barograph, Satellite Weather Receiver"]
    Master["MASTER / BRIDGE OFFICER: Decision Hub — COLREGs Compliance"]
    Out["OUTPUT: Steered Course, RPM, Towing Tension, Catch Coordinates"]

    Sensors --> PS
    Sensors --> NavDis
    PS --> NavDis
    PS --> AutoPilot
    NavDis --> Master
    Comm --> Master
    Fish --> Master
    Wx --> Master
    Master --> Out
    AutoPilot --> Out
```

## 4.2 Navigation Information Flow (Data Fusion)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[GPS Position Fix] --> D[Navigation Computer]
    B[Gyro Heading] --> D
    C[Speed Log STW] --> D
    D --> E[Estimated Position EP]
    D --> F[Course to Steer CTS]
    D --> G[Cross-Track Error XTE]
    E --> H[Master Decision]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Steering or Autopilot]
    H --> J[Fishing Action or Transit Action]
```

## 4.3 Compass Correction Hierarchy (TVMDC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[True North TN] -- plus Variation V --> M[Magnetic North MN]
    M -- plus Deviation D --> C[Compass North CN]
    C[Compass North CN] -- back-subtract Deviation D --> M
    M[Magnetic North MN] -- back-subtract Variation V --> T
```

## 4.4 Sensor-to-Decision Block Diagram for Collision Avoidance (COLREGs)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RadarScan[Radar Scan Sweep] --> Plot[Plot Targets]
    AIS_RX[AIS Receive Position Reports] --> Plot
    Plot --> CPA[Compute Closest Point of Approach CPA and TCPA]
    CPA --> Risk[Risk Assessment per Rule 7]
    Risk --> Maneuver[Decide Maneuver per Rule 8 and 18]
    Maneuver --> Signal[Sound Signal and Alter Course]
    Signal --> Monitor[Monitor Continuous until Past and Clear]
    Monitor --> CPA
```

## 4.5 Vessel Navigation State Transition

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InPort
    InPort --> Departing: Slip Lines, Astern Tug
    Departing --> CoastalTransit: Pilot Disembarks
    CoastalTransit --> OffshoreTransit: Past 12 NM Limit
    OffshoreTransit --> FishingGround: Arrival
    FishingGround --> OffshoreTransit: Haul Complete
    OffshoreTransit --> CoastalTransit: Approaching Harbor
    CoastalTransit --> InPort: All Fast
    InPort --> [*]
```

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Dead Reckoning and list the two pieces of data essential to perform it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Dead Reckoning (DR) is the process of determining a vessel's **present position** by applying to the last known position the **course steered** and **distance run** (speed × time), corrected for estimated **leeway, current set and drift, and steering errors**.
> 
> **Essential data:**
> 1. **Course** — direction steered (True or Compass as appropriate).
> 2. **Speed × Time** — to compute distance run; speed is obtained from a **towed or pitot log**.
> 
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for course, 1 mark for distance.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is the difference between a Magnetic Compass and a Gyrocompass? State one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Magnetic Compass | Gyrocompass |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Aligns to Earth's magnetic field | Uses a fast-spinning rotor seeking true north via Earth's rotation |
| Reference | Magnetic North (has variation + deviation) | True North (north-seeking accuracy ± 0.1°) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Disadvantage | Affected by magnetic materials on ship (deviation errors), sluggish on high latitudes | Requires electrical power; can be "toppled" by sudden acceleration / violent motion; drift if not regularly corrected |

> **Disadvantage examples (1 mark each):**
> - Magnetic: **Deviation errors** due to ship's magnetism → must use deviation card.
> - Gyrocompass: **Latitude error** at high latitudes and **settling time** after starting up.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. An internal choice is provided (either the (a)+(b) pair OR the (c)+(d) pair). We present BOTH alternatives below.

---

### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1

#### (a) Explain in detail the GPS system and its application to fishing vessel navigation. Discuss DGPS and WAAS. [7 marks]

**Model Answer:**

**1. GPS Architecture (2 marks)**
- **Space segment:** 24+ MEO satellites in 6 orbital planes at ~20,200 km altitude, period 11h 58m.
- **Control segment:** Master Control Station (Colorado) + monitor stations + ground antennas for ephemeris updates.
- **User segment:** GPS receivers on the vessel, decoding ranging signals.

**2. Working Principle (2 marks)**
- Each satellite broadcasts a **pseudorandom code** modulated on **L1 (1575.42 MHz)** and **L2 (1227.60 MHz)** with **navigation message** (ephemeris, clock correction, almanac).
- Receiver measures **time-of-flight** to ≥ 4 satellites → solves 4 unknowns (x, y, z, clock bias) → output in WGS-84 datum.
- **Trilateration**, not triangulation.

**3. Accuracy and Errors (1 mark)**
- **SPS (Civilian) accuracy** ~5–10 m horizontal, ~15 m vertical.
- Errors: ionospheric delay, tropospheric delay, satellite clock, multipath, **Selective Availability** (turned off 2000).

**4. DGPS and WAAS (2 marks)**
- **DGPS:** Reference station at known position computes **pseudo-range correction (PRC)**; broadcast via marine radiobeacon to vessel. **Accuracy < 1–2 m**. Mandatory for many fishing operations (e.g., shrimp trawl boundary lines).
- **WAAS / SBAS:** Geostationary satellites broadcast correction messages for civil aviation, but marine receivers also benefit — sub-3 m accuracy.

> **Application to fishing:** Plotting tow tracks, **VMS (Vessel Monitoring System)** reporting, returning to known fishing grounds, and recording catch locations for stock assessment.

---

#### (b) A fishing vessel on course 210° T at 10 knots observes a lighthouse at bearing 280° T at 09:00 h. At 09:30 h, the same lighthouse bears 250° T. Find the distance of the lighthouse from the second position and the distance the vessel has run. [7 marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Find the angle of the lighthouse bearing as observed from the vessel's path (Cross-Bearing Problem).**

The vessel steers 210° T. The first bearing of the lighthouse is 280° T. The angle between the vessel's course and the *first* bearing of the object is:

$$\theta_1 = 280° - 210° = 70° \text{ (relative bearing)}$$

The second bearing is 250° T, so the angle between the second bearing and the course is:

$$\theta_2 = 250° - 210° = 40°$$

**Step 2 — Geometry.** Draw the triangle: vessel positions $A$ (09:00) and $B$ (09:30), lighthouse at $L$. The angle at $A$ (interior of triangle) is the supplement of the bearing angle measured from the **opposite direction** of the course:

$$\angle A = 180° - 70° = 110°$$

$$\angle B = 180° - 40° = 140°$$

Wait — we must use the interior angles on the **correct side**. Using the running-fix convention:

- The bearing is **measured from the ship's head to the object**; the interior angle of the triangle at $A$ is:
$$\angle A = 180° - 70° = 110°$$

- The interior angle at $B$ is:
$$\angle B = 40°$$

**Step 3 — Compute distance run from A to B.**
$$\text{Distance AB} = S \times T = 10 \text{ kt} \times 0.5 \text{ h} = 5 \text{ NM}$$

**Step 4 — Use the sine rule to find distance BL (from B to lighthouse).**

First, the third angle of triangle:
$$\angle L = 180° - \angle A - \angle B = 180° - 110° - 40° = 30°$$

By the sine rule:
$$\frac{BL}{\sin(\angle A)} = \frac{AB}{\sin(\angle L)}$$

$$BL = \frac{AB \cdot \sin(\angle A)}{\sin(\angle L)} = \frac{5 \cdot \sin(110°)}{\sin(30°)}$$

$$BL = \frac{5 \cdot 0.9397}{0.5000} = \frac{4.6985}{0.5000} = 9.40 \text{ NM}$$

**Final Answers (1 mark each):**
- **Distance run (A→B) = 5 NM** (1 mark)
- **Distance from B to lighthouse (BL) ≈ 9.4 NM** (1 mark)
- Plus full working: 5 marks for the derivation steps

---

### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2

#### (c) Describe the principles and uses of RADAR and SONAR on a fishing vessel. Compare X-band and S-band. [7 marks]

**Model Answer:**

**1. RADAR — Radio Detection and Ranging (3 marks)**
- Transmitter sends microwave pulse via rotating antenna; echo returned from a target; **range = c·t/2**; **bearing** from antenna azimuth.
- **Displays:** Plan Position Indicator (PPI).
- **Use on fishing vessels:** Collision avoidance (COLREGs), target detection in fog, navigation in restricted visibility, **radar reflector on small boats** for visibility to larger vessels.

**2. SONAR — Sound Navigation and Ranging (2 marks)**
- Uses **acoustic waves** (~50–200 kHz) in water — sound attenuates less than light/RF in water.
- **Types:** Echo sounder (vertical beam), scanning SONAR (tilted beam, full 360° scan), **Trawl SONAR** (mounted on net to monitor fish entering the trawl).
- **Use:** Detect fish schools, measure their depth, density, and species behavior; **avoid seabed damage** by mapping bottom.

**3. X-band vs S-band (2 marks):**

| Feature | X-band (~9 GHz / 3 cm) | S-band (~3 GHz / 10 cm) |
|---|---|---|
| Wavelength | 3 cm | 10 cm |
| Beam width | Narrow (high angular resolution) | Wide (low resolution) |
| Rain penetration | Poor (rain clutter) | Good |
| Sea clutter | Higher | Lower |
| Antenna size | Smaller | Larger |
| Use | Coastal, harbor, high resolution | Offshore, all-weather |

> **Fishing vessel recommendation:** Most modern fishing vessels carry **both** X-band (for fine target discrimination) and S-band (for heavy weather/rain penetration).

---

#### (d) A fishing vessel is at position 20°00′ N, 070°00′ E steering course 045° T at 12 knots. After 18 hours it is displaced by a current setting 135° T at 3 knots. Find the vessel's Estimated Position. [7 marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Track of the vessel (without current):**
- Course = 045° T, Speed = 12 kt
- Time = 18 h
- Distance made good = 12 × 18 = 216 NM

**Step 2 — North and East components of vessel track:**
$$N_{vessel} = S \cdot \cos(C) = 12 \cdot \cos(45°) = 12 \times 0.7071 = 8.485 \text{ kt}$$
$$E_{vessel} = S \cdot \sin(C) = 12 \cdot \sin(45°) = 8.485 \text{ kt}$$

**Step 3 — North and East components of current:**
- Current set = 135° T (towards SE), drift = 3 kt
$$N_{current} = 3 \cdot \cos(135°) = 3 \times (-0.7071) = -2.121 \text{ kt}$$
$$E_{current} = 3 \cdot \sin(135°) = 3 \times (0.7071) = 2.121 \text{ kt}$$

**Step 4 — Resultant motion (Course & Speed Made Good):**
$$N_{total} = 8.485 + (-2.121) = 6.364 \text{ kt}$$
$$E_{total} = 8.485 + 2.121 = 10.606 \text{ kt}$$

**Step 5 — Course Made Good (CMG) and Speed Made Good (SMG):**
$$SMG = \sqrt{N_{total}^{2} + E_{total}^{2}} = \sqrt{6.364^{2} + 10.606^{2}} = \sqrt{40.50 + 112.49} = \sqrt{153.0} = 12.37 \text{ kt}$$

$$\tan(CMG) = \frac{E_{total}}{N_{total}} = \frac{10.606}{6.364} = 1.667$$

$$CMG = \arctan(1.667) = 59.05° \approx 059° \text{ T}$$

**Step 6 — Total distance made good:**
$$D_{MG} = SMG \times T = 12.37 \times 18 = 222.6 \text{ NM}$$

**Step 7 — Convert distance to Δφ and Δλ:**
$$\Delta\phi = \frac{D_{MG} \cdot \cos(CMG)}{60} = \frac{222.6 \cdot \cos(59°)}{60} = \frac{222.6 \times 0.5150}{60} = \frac{114.6}{60} = 1.91°$$

$$\Delta\lambda = \frac{D_{MG} \cdot \sin(CMG)}{60 \cdot \cos(\phi_m)} \text{ where } \phi_m \approx 21°$$

$$\cos(21°) = 0.9336$$

$$\Delta\lambda = \frac{222.6 \cdot \sin(59°)}{60 \cdot 0.9336} = \frac{222.6 \times 0.8572}{56.02} = \frac{190.8}{56.02} = 3.41°$$

**Step 8 — Compute new Estimated Position:**
$$\phi_2 = 20°00' + 1.91° = 21°55' \text{ N} \approx 21°55' \text{ N}$$
$$\lambda_2 = 070°00' + 3.41° = 073°25' \text{ E}$$

> **Estimated Position: 21°55′ N, 073°25′ E** (5 marks for full derivation, 2 marks for final answer with units).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Confusion (TVMDC)** — Most common cause of compass-conversion errors. Always use the rule **"T = C + Dev (East +) + Var (East +)"** with East positive, West negative. Do not mix "+W Going Down" and "-W Going Up" rules in the same solution.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert minutes to decimals of degrees** in trigonometric calculations. Convert ALL latitudes/longitudes to decimal degrees before taking cos/sin.
> 3. **Confusing 1 NM = 1 minute of latitude with 1° of latitude** — 1° of latitude = **60 NM** (not 1 NM).
> 4. **Using Statute Miles (1609 m) instead of Nautical Miles (1852 m)** in range conversions.
> 5. **Plotting angle errors in running-fix problems** — The interior angle of the triangle is the **supplement** of the relative bearing measured from the course, not the bearing itself.
> 6. **Omitting the mean latitude (φ_m)** in Mercator sailing — students often use φ₁ only, which is acceptable for small distances but penalised in KTU scripts.
> 7. **Confusing "Course" and "Heading"** — Course is direction-of-travel-through-water over the ground; Heading is the direction the **bow** is pointing (and may differ due to leeway/current).

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final high-density revision — must-read 1 hour before KTU exam:**

- ⚓ **1 NM = 1852 m**; **1 Knot = 1 NM/h**; **1° Lat = 60 NM** — these three are the **first things** to write on your answer script.
- ⚓ **Earth's radius** = **3440 NM** = **6371 km**; memorize both.
- ⚓ **Time ↔ Longitude:** 1 hour of solar time = 15° of longitude (Earth rotates 360° in 24 h).
- ⚓ **TVMDC sign convention:** Always use **East = +ve, West = −ve** in `T = C + Dev + Var`.
- ⚓ **Compass types:** Magnetic (low cost, has deviation), Gyro (accurate ± 0.1° to TN, needs power, topples on rough seas).
- ⚓ **GPS:** 24 MEO satellites; accuracy 5–10 m SPS; DGPS improves to <2 m. **Trilateration** on 4 satellites solves 4 unknowns (x, y, z, time).
- ⚓ **Radar range equation:** `R_max ∝ (P_t · G² · λ² · σ)^(1/4)`; **Radar horizon** = 2.2√h (h in metres, R in NM).
- ⚓ **Radar frequency bands:** **X-band** = 3 cm, narrow beam, rain clutter; **S-band** = 10 cm, wide beam, all-weather.
- ⚓ **AIS:** VHF, mandatory >300 GT SOLAS; transmits MMSI, position, COG, SOG, ROT.
- ⚓ **ECDIS:** Digital chart IHO S-57/S-101; integrates GPS, radar overlay, AIS.
- ⚓ **Echo sounder vs SONAR:** Vertical beam = depth; tilted/360° = fish detection.
- ⚓ **GMDSS:** GMDSS sub-systems include **EPIRB**, **SART**, **NAVTEX**, **INMARSAT**, **DSC VHF/MF/HF** — must be carried per vessel area.
- ⚓ **COLREGs** = International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (1972). KTU expects awareness of **Rules 5, 7, 8, 13, 14, 15, 18** for fishing vessels.
- ⚓ **VMS** (Vessel Monitoring System) is mandatory for fishing vessels in most jurisdictions (India — MFV 2018; EU — IUU; US — MMPA).
- ⚓ **Cape Town Agreement (2012)** sets safety standards for fishing vessels ≥ 24 m LOA; not yet in force but impending.
- ⚓ **SAFEDIST** software and **IMO Resolution A.601(15)** govern navigation display arrangements.
- ⚓ **Chart Datum vs WGS-84 vs MSL:** GPS gives positions in **WGS-84 ellipsoid**; nautical chart soundings are reduced to **Chart Datum** (≈ Lowest Astronomical Tide). Always apply **separation model** (KRUPP formula) for sub-decimetre work.
- ⚓ **Memo for Sailing problems:** Always draw the **triangle** (or use vector); convert to decimal degrees; use **sine rule** for running fixes; **Pythagoras + tan** for Mercator.
- ⚓ **Three classical problems asked in KTU:**
  1. *Cross-bearing running fix* (lighthouse / buoy)
  2. *Mercator rhumb-line course and distance*
  3. *Set-and-drift correction of EP*

> **Last-line mantra:** *Good navigation = Plan + Plot + Verify + Adjust — applied to every minute at sea.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Communication ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Marine Communication** in the context of fishing vessel design refers to the integrated network of electrical, electronic, optical, and acoustic systems that enable the transmission and reception of information (voice, data, distress alerts, navigational warnings, and operational commands) between the fishing vessel, other vessels, shore stations, aircraft, and the vessel's own crew across various compartments and deck areas. It encompasses distress, urgency, safety, and routine communication governed by international conventions such as **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea)**, **ITU Radio Regulations**, and the **IMO Global Maritime Distress and Safety System (GMDSS)** framework. For fishing vessels operating under the **Torremolinos International Convention** (as amended) and **FAO/ILO/IMO Code of Safety for Fishermen and Fishing Vessels**, communication systems are categorized by function: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Communication is one of the **four pillar systems** in fishing vessel outfitting design, alongside navigation, propulsion, and fish handling. It directly influences vessel safety classification, statutory certification, and operational economics. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Human Nervous System** > > Imagine a fishing vessel as a floating township at sea. Communication on board is exactly like the **nervous system** of the human body: > > - **External Communication (VHF, Satellite, EPIRB)** = Your *voice and cell phone* — talking to the outside world (other ships, ports, family). > - **Internal Communication (Talk-back, PA, Sound-Powered Telephone)** = Your *spinal cord* — connecting brain (bridge) to limbs (engine room, deck). > - **Distress & Safety Systems (EPIRB, SART, DSC)** = Your *pain reflex* — automatic response when something is seriously wrong. > - **Navigation Data Links (AIS, NAVTEX)** = Your *eyes and ears* — receiving continuous situational awareness. Just as a person cannot survive without a functional nervous system, a fishing vessel — operating hundreds of nautical miles from shore in extreme weather — cannot operate legally or safely without a robust, redundant communication suite. ### 1.3 Why Communication is Critical in Fishing Vessel Design | Design Driver | Impact | |---|---| | **Crew Safety** | Direct link to rescue coordination (MRCC) during emergencies | | **Statutory Compliance** | Mandatory under SOLAS Chapter IV & FAO/ILO/IMO Code | | **Operational Efficiency** | Coordination during fishing, net hauling, fish location | | **Catch Reporting** | VMS (Vessel Monitoring System) & catch reporting to authorities | | **Weather Routing** | Real-time meteorological data reception | | **Economic Value** | Market price updates, sale coordination at sea | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Statutory Constants to Remember:** > - **GMDSS Implementation Date:** 1 February 1992 (fully effective 1 February 1999) > - **Sea Area A1 Range:** Up to **20–30 NM** from a VHF coast station > - **Sea Area A2 Range:** Up to **100 NM** from an MF coast station (excluding A1) > - **Sea Area A3 Range:** Within INMARSAT geostationary satellite coverage (~70°N to 70°S) > - **Sea Area A4 Range:** Beyond A3 (Polar regions — only HF required) > - **Standard VHF Channel 16:** **156.800 MHz** (International Distress, Safety & Calling) --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Fishing Vessel Communication Systems The communication architecture of a fishing vessel can be decomposed into **four functional layers**, each serving a distinct operational and statutory purpose: #### Layer 1: External (Ship-to-Ship & Ship-to-Shore) Communication - **VHF Radiotelephone** (156–174 MHz) — short-range voice & DSC - **MF/HF Radiotelephone** (1.6–30 MHz) — medium/long-range voice, DSC, NBDP - **INMARSAT Satellite Communication** (1.5/1.6 GHz L-band) — long-range voice, fax, data - **Iridium / Fleet Broadband** — truly global pole-to-pole coverage #### Layer 2: Distress & Safety Systems (GMDSS Core) - **EPIRB** (Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon) — 406 MHz - **SART** (Search and Rescue Transponder) — 9 GHz (X-band radar) - **NAVTEX** (Navigational Telex) — 518 kHz international, 490 kHz national - **DSC** (Digital Selective Calling) — integrated into VHF/MF/HF #### Layer 3: Navigation Data Links - **AIS** (Automatic Identification System) — 161.975 MHz & 162.025 MHz - **GPS / GNSS** receivers feeding position to communication systems - **Long-Range Identification & Tracking (LRIT)** — mandatory for vessels >300 GT engaged on international voyages #### Layer 4: Internal (On-board) Communication - **Sound-Powered Telephone** — emergency backup, no power required - **Talk-Back System** — engine control room to bridge/main machinery spaces - **Public Address (PA) System** — general announcements, emergency alarms - **General Alarm System** — 7 short + 1 long bell/horn blast sequence - **UHF Walkie-Talkies** — tactical crew-to-crew communication ### 2.2 The GMDSS Functional Block Model The IMO GMDSS architecture can be modeled as a **three-tier redundancy pyramid**: ``` ┌───────────────────────┐ │ SATELLITE (A3/A4) │ Long-range: INMARSAT, COSPAS-SARSAT ├───────────────────────┤ │ MF/HF GROUNDWAVE/ │ Medium-range: DSC, NBDP, SSB Telephony │ SKYWAVE (A2/A4) │ ├───────────────────────┤ │ VHF (A1) │ Short-range: DSC Ch. 70, Voice Ch. 16 └───────────────────────┘ ▲ │ All feed into ▼ ┌───────────────────────┐ │ DISTRESS ALERTING │ → MRCC / Coast Stations / Other Ships └───────────────────────┘ ``` ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **mandatory numerical tools** for Part B (14-mark) problems in KTU ESE. Memorize the variable symbols, units, and typical numerical ranges. | # | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $D_{VHF} = 2.5 \cdot \left(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2}\right)$ | $D_{VHF}$ = VHF geometric range, $h_1, h_2$ = antenna heights | NM, ft | VHF line-of-sight range | | 2 | $D_{VHF} = 3.57 \cdot \left(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2}\right)$ | Same as above but SI | km, m | VHF range (metric) | | 3 | $FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ | $FSPL$ = Free-Space Path Loss, $d$ = distance, $f$ = frequency | dB, km, MHz | Link budget | | 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $f$ = frequency | m, Hz | Antenna sizing | | 5 | $P_{r} = P_{t} + G_{t} + G_{r} - FSPL - L_{sys}$ | Received power in link budget | dBm | Receiver sensitivity check | | 6 | $SNR_{dB} = P_{r} - P_{noise}$ | $SNR$ = Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB | Communication quality | | 7 | $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{c}$ | One-way propagation delay | s | Satellite link delay | | 8 | $BW_{channel} = \dfrac{BW_{total}}{N_{channels}}$ | Channel bandwidth allocation | Hz | Frequency planning | | 9 | $P_{ERP} = P_{t} \cdot G_{t} \cdot \eta_{feed}$ | $P_{ERP}$ = Effective Radiated Power | W or dBW | Transmission planning | | 10 | $R_{max,skip} = 2 \cdot R_{earth} \cdot \left(\sec\theta_{max} - 1\right)$ | $R_{max,skip}$ = maximum sky-wave skip distance, $\theta$ = take-off angle | km | HF propagation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Exam Note:** Equation (1) and (2) are the **most frequently tested** in KTU. The numerical constant **2.5** applies **only when antenna height is in feet** and distance in nautical miles. Using metric units with 2.5 yields wrong answers. ### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Vessel Design These formulas are used by the **Naval Architect / Marine Engineer** during: 1. **Antenna Mast Design** — VHF/UHF antenna placement on mast to optimize $D_{VHF}$ between own ship and coast station. 2. **Power Budgeting** — Ensuring EPIRB 406 MHz signal reaches COSPAS-SARSAT satellite (-36 dBW EIRP minimum). 3. **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** — Separation distances between antennas to avoid co-site interference. 4. **Cable Run Calculation** — Coaxial cable attenuation (e.g., RG-213: ~12 dB/100 m at 100 MHz) influences receiver sensitivity. 5. **Battery Backup Sizing** — GMDSS equipment must operate **6 hours** on emergency power; NiCd or VRLA battery sizing uses $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of VHF Line-of-Sight Range Equation The VHF band (30–300 MHz) is dominated by **line-of-sight propagation** because VHF waves do not reflect off the ionosphere and are absorbed by the troposphere only mildly. The maximum range is therefore limited by the curvature of the Earth. #### Derivation — Geometric Line-of-Sight Consider two antennas of heights $h_1$ and $h_2$ above sea level. The radio horizon distance from each antenna to the tangent point on Earth's curved surface is found using the Pythagorean theorem. For antenna 1, the line of sight distance $d_1$ satisfies: $$ (R + h_1)^2 = R^2 + d_1^2 $$ where $R$ is the **effective Earth radius** (approximately **6371 km**, but **4/3 R ≈ 8493 km** is used in radio propagation to account for atmospheric refraction). Expanding: $$ R^2 + 2Rh_1 + h_1^2 = R^2 + d_1^2 $$ For $h_1 \ll R$, the term $h_1^2$ is negligible: $$ d_1^2 \approx 2Rh_1 $$ Taking the square root: $$ d_1 = \sqrt{2Rh_1} $$ Summing the horizon distances from both antennas: $$ D = d_1 + d_2 = \sqrt{2R} \cdot \left(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2}\right) $$ Substituting $R = \frac{4}{3} \cdot 6371 \text{ km} = 8493$ km and converting appropriately: $$ D_{km} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 8493 \times 10^3} \cdot \left(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2}\right) \approx 3.57 \cdot \left(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2}\right) $$ where $h_1, h_2$ are in **meters** and $D$ in **km**. For $h$ in **feet** and $D$ in **NM**, the constant becomes **2.5**. $$ D_{NM} = 2.5 \cdot \left(\sqrt{h_{1,ft}} + \sqrt{h_{2,ft}}\right) $$ #### Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style **Problem:** A fishing vessel's VHF antenna is mounted at **24 meters** above the waterline. A coast station antenna is at **120 meters** above sea level. Calculate the maximum theoretical VHF communication range. **Solution:** Step 1 — Identify the formula (SI units, meters & km): $$ D_{km} = 3.57 \cdot \left(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2}\right) $$ Step 2 — Substitute the values: $$ D_{km} = 3.57 \cdot \left(\sqrt{24} + \sqrt{120}\right) $$ Step 3 — Evaluate the square roots: $$ \sqrt{24} = 4.899 \text{ m}^{0.5}, \quad \sqrt{120} = 10.954 \text{ m}^{0.5} $$ Step 4 — Sum and multiply: $$ D_{km} = 3.57 \cdot (4.899 + 10.954) = 3.57 \cdot 15.853 $$ Step 5 — Final result: $$ D_{km} \approx 56.6 \text{ km} $$ Step 6 — Convert to nautical miles (1 NM = 1.852 km): $$ D_{NM} = \dfrac{56.6}{1.852} \approx 30.6 \text{ NM} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** This range comfortably places the vessel within **GMDSS Sea Area A1** (typically defined as within 20–30 NM of a coast station with continuous DSC alerting available on VHF Channel 70). ### 3.2 Derivation of Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) The Friis transmission equation gives received power in free space: $$ P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2 $$ Converting to dB: $$ P_{r,dBm} = P_{t,dBm} + G_{t,dBi} + G_{r,dBi} - 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right) $$ The last term is the FSPL. Substituting $\lambda = c / f$ and simplifying with appropriate unit conversions ($d$ in km, $f$ in MHz): $$ FSPL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) $$ #### Worked Example — EPIRB Uplink to Satellite **Problem:** An EPIRB transmits at **406 MHz** with EIRP of **5 W (37 dBm)**. Satellite is at **850 km** altitude. Calculate the FSPL and received power at the satellite (assume satellite antenna gain = 0 dBi, receiver sensitivity = −154 dBm). **Solution:** Step 1 — Compute FSPL: $$ FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(850) + 20\log_{10}(406) $$ Step 2 — Evaluate logarithms: $$ 20\log_{10}(850) = 58.59 \text{ dB}, \quad 20\log_{10}(406) = 52.17 \text{ dB} $$ Step 3 — Sum: $$ FSPL = 32.44 + 58.59 + 52.17 = 143.2 \text{ dB} $$ Step 4 — Received power (with $G_r = 0$, $G_t = 0$ since EIRP already accounts for transmit gain): $$ P_r = 37 - 143.2 = -106.2 \text{ dBm} $$ Step 5 — Compare with satellite sensitivity (−154 dBm): $$ \text{Margin} = -106.2 - (-154) = 47.8 \text{ dB} \gg 0 \text{ dB} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** A massive **47.8 dB link margin** ensures reliable detection even under poor weather, low battery, or partial antenna deployment. This is why EPIRBs are mandatory lifesaving appliances. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Communication Equipment Selection | Parameter | VHF | MF/HF | INMARSAT-C | EPIRB 406 MHz | AIS | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Frequency** | 156–174 MHz | 1.6–30 MHz | 1.6 GHz uplink | 406 MHz | 161.975/162.025 MHz | | **Range** | 20–30 NM | 100–4000 NM | Global (A3) | Global (satellite) | 20–30 NM (VHF) | | **Sea Area** | A1 | A2, A4 | A3 | All | All (line-of-sight) | | **Bandwidth** | 25 kHz / 12.5 kHz | 3 kHz voice | 600 bps data | 5 W burst | 25 kHz | | **Wavelength** | ~1.8 m | 10–188 m | ~19 cm | ~0.74 m | ~1.85 m | | **Antenna Type** | Whip (λ/4) | Long wire / whip | Helical / patch | Whip / deployable | Whip | | **Primary Use** | Local distress & ship-ship | Long-range voice | Data, email, distress | Emergency beacon | Collision avoidance | | **Mandatory For** | All fishing vessels ≥12 m | Ocean-going vessels | Sea Area A3 vessels | All vessels | All vessels ≥300 GT | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 GMDSS Functional Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional interconnection of GMDSS subsystems** on a typical ocean-going fishing vessel. It maps signal flow from operator input through transmission, propagation, and reception by external rescue authorities. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BRIDGE["BRIDGE - COMMUNICATION WORKSTATION"] VHF["VHF DSC Transceiver
Channel 16 Voice, Ch 70 DSC"] MFRT["MF HF Transceiver
DSC and SSB Voice"] SAT["INMARSAT-C
Satellite Terminal"] NAV["NAVTEX Receiver
518 kHz and 490 kHz"] AIS["AIS Transponder
Class A Mandatory"] end subgraph DECK["DECK AND EMERGENCY STATIONS"] EPIRB["EPIRB 406 MHz
Float-free or Manual"] SART["SART 9 GHz
X-band Radar"] WALK["UHF Handheld Radios
Crew Tactical"] end subgraph INTERNAL["INTERNAL COMMUNICATION NETWORK"] PA["Public Address System"] TB["Talk-back System
Engine Control"] SP["Sound Powered Phone
Emergency Backup"] ALARM["General Alarm Bell
7 Short plus 1 Long"] end subgraph EXTERNAL["EXTERNAL ENTITIES"] COAST["Coast Station MRCC"] SHIP["Other Ships"] SATGEO["INMARSAT Satellite"] COSPAS["COSPAS SARSAT Satellite"] PLANE["Rescue Aircraft"] end VHF -->|VHF Signal| COAST VHF -->|Ch 16 Distress| SHIP MFRT -->|MF HF Voice or DSC| COAST SAT -->|L band 1.6 GHz| SATGEO SATGEO -->|C band Downlink| COAST NAV -->|518 kHz Broadcast| COAST AIS -->|VDL VHF Data Link| SHIP EPIRB -->|406 MHz Burst 5W| COSPAS COSPAS -->|Distress Alert| COAST SART -->|Radar Response Signal| PLANE PA --> BRIDGE TB --> BRIDGE SP --> BRIDGE ALARM --> BRIDGE WALK --> DECK ``` ### 4.2 Communication Range Hierarchy — Coverage Schematic This second diagram visualizes the **layered coverage architecture** of GMDSS as concentric zones around a coast station and the satellite coverage footprint. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph COAST_STATION["Coast Station"] VHFCS["VHF Antenna
Height 100m"] MFCS["MF Antenna
Height 50m"] end subgraph SEA_A1["SEA AREA A1
0 to 30 NM
VHF Coverage"] VESSEL_A1["Fishing Vessel
Within VHF Range"] end subgraph SEA_A2["SEA AREA A2
30 to 100 NM
MF Coverage"] VESSEL_A2["Fishing Vessel
Beyond VHF, Within MF"] end subgraph SEA_A3["SEA AREA A3
70 N to 70 S
INMARSAT Coverage"] VESSEL_A3["Fishing Vessel
Deep Sea Fishing"] end subgraph SEA_A4["SEA AREA A4
Polar Regions
HF Coverage Only"] VESSEL_A4["Antarctic Fishing Vessel"] end VHFCS -. VHF 156 to 174 MHz .-> VESSEL_A1 VESSEL_A1 --> VESSEL_A2 MFCS -. MF 2 MHz Ground Wave .-> VESSEL_A2 VESSEL_A2 --> VESSEL_A3 VESSEL_A3 --> VESSEL_A4 SAT_INM["INMARSAT GEO Satellite"] -. L band 1.6 GHz .-> VESSEL_A3 SAT_COS["COSPAS SARSAT LEO"] -. 406 MHz .-> VESSEL_A4 ``` ### 4.3 Signal Propagation — Comparative Block Diagram The diagram below contrasts how each frequency band propagates differently through the atmosphere and around the Earth's curvature — this is the **physical foundation** for the GMDSS area concept. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph VHF_PROP["VHF Propagation 156 MHz"] VTX["Ship Antenna"] -->|"Straight line of sight"| ICOS["Ionosphere
Wave penetrates
No reflection"] end subgraph MF_PROP["MF Propagation 2 MHz"] MTX["Ship Antenna"] -->|"Ground wave hugs Earth"| EARTH["Up to 100 NM over sea"] MTX2["Ship Antenna"] -->|"Night sky wave"| IONO2["Ionosphere D Layer
Reflects at night"] end subgraph HF_PROP["HF Propagation 8 MHz"] HTX["Ship Antenna"] -->|"Sky wave multi-hop"| IONO3["F2 Layer Reflection
Hundreds to thousands NM"] end subgraph SAT_PROP["Satellite 1.6 GHz"] STX["Ship Antenna"] -->|"Up to LEO or GEO"| SAT["Satellite Repeater"] SAT -->|"Downlink"| RCS["Rescue Coordination"] end ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Define GMDSS. State the four sea areas defined under GMDSS and the primary communication equipment used in each.** **Model Answer:** The **Global Maritime Distress and Safety System (GMDSS)** is an internationally agreed set of communication procedures, equipment, and protocols established by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** under the **SOLAS Convention Chapter IV** to enhance safety of life at sea through rapid and automated distress alerting. The four sea areas are: | Sea Area | Coverage Definition | Primary Equipment | |---|---|---| | **A1** | Within 20–30 NM of a VHF coast station with continuous DSC availability | VHF DSC (Channel 70) | | **A2** | Within 100 NM of an MF coast station (excluding A1) | MF DSC (2187.5 kHz) | | **A3** | Within INMARSAT geostationary satellite coverage (≈70°N to 70°S), excluding A1 & A2 | INMARSAT-C | | **A4** | Polar regions outside A3 coverage | HF DSC + COSPAS-SARSAT | > [!NOTE] > **3-Mark Weighting Tip:** The KTU examiner awards 1 mark each for: (a) GMDSS definition with SOLAS reference, (b) Correct listing of all four sea areas, and (c) Equipment-area mapping. --- #### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary] **Explain the function of an EPIRB and a SART in a fishing vessel's communication suite.** **Model Answer:** **EPIRB (Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon):** - Operates on **406 MHz** with a minimum EIRP of **5 W (37 dBm)**. - Transmits a digital message containing the vessel's **MMSI / Unique ID** and, when interfaced with GPS, the **latitude/longitude**. - Detected by the **COSPAS-SARSAT** satellite constellation (LEO + GEO) and relayed to the nearest **Maritime Rescue Coordination Centre (MRCC)**. - Modern EPIRBs include a **121.5 MHz homing beacon** for final-stage direction finding by rescue craft. - Must be **float-free**, automatically activated when submerged to a depth of **1–4 meters**. **SART (Search and Rescue Transponder):** - Operates on **9 GHz (X-band)** and is activated manually upon receiving a radar pulse from a searching ship or aircraft. - Responds with a **swept-frequency signal** that appears as a series of **12 bright blips** extending approximately **8 nautical miles outward** from the responder's position on the searcher's radar PPI. - Provides **range and bearing** to the survivor — particularly useful for liferaft location. > [!NOTE] > **3-Mark Weighting Tip:** Allocate ~1.5 marks each for EPIRB and SART. Mentioning specific frequencies (406 MHz / 9 GHz) and the satellite/radar context fetches bonus marks. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Module Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the Global Maritime Distress and Safety System (GMDSS) on a fishing vessel. List the mandatory communication equipment for a fishing vessel operating in Sea Area A3. (7 Marks)** **(b) A fishing vessel has its VHF antenna at a height of 18 m above the waterline. Calculate the maximum theoretical VHF communication range to a coast station antenna mounted at 75 m above mean sea level. Comment on whether this vessel falls within GMDSS Sea Area A1 if the coast station is 25 NM away. (7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** ### Part (a) — GMDSS Architecture (7 Marks) The GMDSS functional architecture on a fishing vessel consists of the following interconnected subsystems (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram): **1. Communication Workstation (Bridge):** - VHF DSC Transceiver — Channel 16 (156.800 MHz) voice; Channel 70 (156.525 MHz) DSC alerting. - MF/HF Transceiver with DSC, NBDP (Narrow Band Direct Printing) and SSB telephony. - INMARSAT-C Satellite Terminal — for long-range distress, safety and commercial messaging. - NAVTEX Receiver (518 kHz international / 490 kHz national) — automatic broadcast of MSI (Maritime Safety Information). - AIS Transponder — automatic broadcasting of vessel identity, position, course, speed. **2. Distress Alerting and Locating Devices:** - EPIRB (406 MHz) — float-free, hydrostatic release. - SART (9 GHz) — radar-based responder. - Two-way VHF handhelds (GMDSS survival craft). **3. Internal Communication:** - Sound-Powered Telephone. - Talk-Back System. - Public Address System. - General Alarm. **4. Power Supply:** - Reserve source of energy (battery) capable of operating all GMDSS equipment for **6 hours** continuously. **Mandatory Equipment for Sea Area A3 Fishing Vessel:** | Equipment | Quantity | Purpose | |---|---|---| | VHF DSC | 1 | Local distress & routine | | MF/HF DSC | 1 | Medium-range distress | | INMARSAT-C | 1 | Long-range distress & MSI | | NAVTEX | 1 | MSI reception | | EPIRB | 1 (float-free) | Satellite distress | | SART | 2 (one per side) | Radar location | | VHF Handheld | 3 (survival craft) | On-scene comms | | Reserve battery | 1 set | 6-hour backup | --- ### Part (b) — VHF Range Calculation (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Identify the formula (SI, meters & km):** $$ D_{km} = 3.57 \cdot \left(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** $$ h_1 = 18 \text{ m}, \quad h_2 = 75 \text{ m} $$ $$ D_{km} = 3.57 \cdot \left(\sqrt{18} + \sqrt{75}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Compute square roots:** $$ \sqrt{18} = 4.243, \quad \sqrt{75} = 8.660 $$ **Step 4 — Sum and multiply:** $$ D_{km} = 3.57 \cdot (4.243 + 8.660) = 3.57 \cdot 12.903 = 46.06 \text{ km} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to nautical miles:** $$ D_{NM} = \dfrac{46.06}{1.852} \approx 24.87 \text{ NM} $$ **Step 6 — Compare with actual distance (25 NM):** $$ D_{calculated} = 24.87 \text{ NM} < D_{actual} = 25 \text{ NM} $$ **Step 7 — Conclusion:** The vessel is **just outside** the theoretical maximum VHF range. In practice, the vessel lies at the **boundary of Sea Area A1**. With marginal antenna height reduction (e.g., roll, antenna icing), contact may be lost. The vessel **technically does not satisfy** GMDSS Sea Area A1 reliability requirements from this coast station alone, and **MF or satellite backup** should be provisioned. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:** > 1. Students often **forget to convert km to NM** — this costs 1 mark. > 2. Using the wrong constant (**2.5** with metric units or **3.57** with feet) — costs 2 marks. > 3. Failing to **comment** on the practical implications — the 1-mark "engineering judgment" component is mandatory. > 4. Not mentioning the **4/3 Earth radius correction** (radio horizon) — minus 0.5 mark if exact numerical match is expected. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **(a) Explain the working principle of an EPIRB and a COSPAS-SARSAT satellite system used in fishing vessel safety. (7 Marks)** **(b) A fishing vessel's MF/HF transmitter delivers 100 W into an antenna system with 3 dB gain. The receiving coast station is 250 km away. The transmitting antenna has 1.5 dB feedline loss and the coast station receiver has 6 dB antenna gain. Calculate the free-space path loss at 8 MHz and the received power at the coast station. (Assume receiver noise floor = −113 dBm.) (7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** ### Part (a) — EPIRB & COSPAS-SARSAT (7 Marks) **Working Principle of EPIRB:** 1. The EPIRB is a **self-contained, battery-operated transmitter** operating at **406.025 MHz ± 0.005 MHz**. 2. It can be activated **automatically** (float-free, hydrostatic release at 1–4 m depth) or **manually**. 3. Upon activation, it transmits a **burst signal of ~5 W EIRP** every **50 seconds** for **0.5 seconds** duration. 4. The transmitted digital message includes: - **Unique Hexadecimal ID** (15-character code identifying the vessel). - **MMSI** (Maritime Mobile Service Identity). - **GPS coordinates** (if GPS-enabled EPIRB). - **Country code** and **protocol flags**. 5. A **121.5 MHz homing signal** is also emitted for final-stage direction finding. **COSPAS-SARSAT System:** - **COSPAS** = Cosmicheskaya Sistema Poiska Avariynyh Sudov (Russian: Space System for Search of Distressed Vessels). - **SARSAT** = Search And Rescue Satellite-Aided Tracking. - The system uses: - **LEO satellites** at ~850 km altitude (SARP payload) for **Doppler-shift triangulation** of the 406 MHz signal. - **GEO satellites** (GOES, INSAT, MSG, Elektro) for **immediate detection** without delay. - The **406 MHz signal** is relayed to a **LUT (Local User Terminal)**, which decodes the message and forwards it via the **MCC (Mission Control Centre)** to the relevant **MRCC (Maritime Rescue Coordination Centre)**. - **Detection time** with GEO satellite: < 5 minutes anywhere in coverage. - **Position accuracy:** ± 5 km (LEO Doppler) or ± 100 m (GPS-equipped EPIRB). --- ### Part (b) — Link Budget Calculation (7 Marks) **Given:** - Transmit power: $P_t = 100$ W - Transmit antenna gain: $G_t = 3$ dB - Feedline loss: $L_{feed} = 1.5$ dB - Frequency: $f = 8$ MHz - Distance: $d = 250$ km - Receiver antenna gain: $G_r = 6$ dB **Step 1 — Convert transmit power to dBm:** $$ P_{t,dBm} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{100}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(10^5) = 50 \text{ dBm} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate Free-Space Path Loss at 8 MHz over 250 km:** $$ FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(250) + 20\log_{10}(8) $$ $$ 20\log_{10}(250) = 20 \cdot 2.3979 = 47.96 \text{ dB} $$ $$ 20\log_{10}(8) = 20 \cdot 0.9031 = 18.06 \text{ dB} $$ $$ FSPL = 32.44 + 47.96 + 18.06 = 98.46 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Effective Isotropic Radiated Power (EIRP):** $$ EIRP = P_{t,dBm} + G_t - L_{feed} = 50 + 3 - 1.5 = 51.5 \text{ dBm} $$ **Step 4 — Received Power at Coast Station:** $$ P_r = EIRP + G_r - FSPL = 51.5 + 6 - 98.46 = -40.96 \text{ dBm} $$ **Step 5 — Compute Signal-to-Noise Ratio:** $$ SNR = P_r - P_{noise} = -40.96 - (-113) = 72.04 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 6 — Conclusion:** A **SNR of 72 dB** indicates an **extremely robust communication link** — far exceeding the **10 dB minimum SNR** required for intelligible SSB voice reception. The fishing vessel will have reliable MF/HF communication at 250 km range on 8 MHz. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:** > 1. **dBm vs dBW confusion** — dBm uses 1 mW reference; dBW uses 1 W reference. Mixing them loses 1 mark. > 2. **Forgetting feedline loss** — the $L_{feed}$ term is frequently omitted. (−1 mark) > 3. **Unit mismatch in FSPL** — using distance in meters instead of km will yield absurd results. > 4. **Skipping SNR comparison** — final link assessment (whether the link works) carries the engineering judgment mark. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Frequently Asked Comparison Topics | Topic | VHF | MF | HF | Satellite | |---|---|---|---|---| | Range | 30 NM | 100 NM | 1000+ NM | Global | | Mode | Line-of-sight | Ground wave | Sky wave | Repeater | | Equipment | DSC Ch 70 | DSC 2187.5 kHz | DSC multi-freq | INMARSAT-C | | Antenna | λ/4 whip | Long wire | Long wire / log-periodic | Helical/patch | --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Communication** - [ ] **GMDSS Sea Areas**: A1 (≤30 NM, VHF) → A2 (≤100 NM, MF) → A3 (INMARSAT, 70°N–70°S) → A4 (HF only). - [ ] **VHF Distress Channel**: Voice Ch **16** (156.800 MHz), DSC Ch **70** (156.525 MHz). - [ ] **MF DSC Distress Frequency**: **2187.5 kHz**. - [ ] **NAVTEX Frequencies**: **518 kHz** international, **490 kHz** national. - [ ] **EPIRB**: **406 MHz**, **5 W** EIRP, COSPAS-SARSAT, float-free at 1–4 m depth. - [ ] **SART**: **9 GHz** X-band radar responder, 12 blips on PPI. - [ ] **AIS Frequencies**: **161.975 MHz** & **162.025 MHz** (VDL). - [ ] **VHF Range Formula (SI)**: $D_{km} = 3.57 \cdot (\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2})$. - [ ] **VHF Range Formula (Imperial)**: $D_{NM} = 2.5 \cdot (\sqrt{h_{1,ft}} + \sqrt{h_{2,ft}})$. - [ ] **FSPL Formula**: $32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ in dB. - [ ] **dBm Conversion**: $dBm = 10\log_{10}(P_{mW})$; $P_{dBm} = P_{dBW} + 30$. - [ ] **GMDSS Power Backup**: **6 hours** continuous operation on reserve battery. - [ ] **Statutory Basis**: SOLAS Chapter IV, IMO Resolution A.694(17), ITU Radio Regulations. - [ ] **Fishing Vessel Specific**: VMS (Vessel Monitoring System) for catch reporting; LRIT for international vessels >300 GT. - [ ] **Sound-Powered Telephone**: Operates **without any external power** — critical emergency backup. - [ ] **General Alarm Pattern**: **7 short + 1 long** bell/blast. - [ ] **INMARSAT-C Propagation Delay**: ~0.25 s one-way (GEO at 36,000 km). - [ ] **Fishing Vessel Codes**: FAO/ILO/IMO Code of Safety Part B (vessels <24 m) and Part A (≥24 m). - [ ] **Antenna Rule of Thumb**: λ/4 monopole antenna length = **0.95 m** at 156 MHz (VHF). - [ ] **Always remember** — Communication is a **statutory**, **safety-critical**, and **operationally essential** system; redundancy (multiple independent systems) is the design philosophy. --- **End of Module 3 — Communication Notes** *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Pattern and Bloom's Taxonomy (L1–L5).*

topics.title: Firefighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Firefighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Firefighting on a fishing vessel** is defined as the integrated, multi-layered engineering discipline encompassing the **detection, containment, suppression, and extinction of fire** through a coordinated suite of passive structural protection, active mechanical systems, detection-and-alarm networks, and personal protective equipment (PPE), all engineered to operate within the harsh, space-constrained, dynamically rolling marine environment and to comply with the **International Convention for the Safety of Fishing Vessels (Torremolinos / Cape Town Agreement)** and the relevant chapters of **SOLAS** (as adopted for fishing vessels by IMO).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** Module 3 of PENST754 expects students to identify the classes of marine fire, justify the choice of extinguishing media, and explain the layout of fixed and portable firefighting installations aboard a fishing vessel of length ≥ 24 m.

## 1.2 The Fire Tetrahedron — Conceptual Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> For a fire to initiate, sustain, and propagate, **four elements** must coexist simultaneously. Removal of any **one** element causes fire extinction.

$$
\text{Fire} = f(\text{Fuel},\ \text{Oxygen},\ \text{Heat},\ \text{Uninhibited Chain Reaction})
$$

- **Fuel** — the combustible material (engine oil, diesel, paint, rope, timber, catch in hold, LNG/CO₂ refrigerant leaks).
- **Oxygen** — atmospheric air ($\approx 21\%$ by volume); sustained combustion generally requires $\geq 16\%$.
- **Heat** — the activation energy needed for pyrolysis and vapour production.
- **Uninhibited Chain Reaction** — the self-propagating radical chemistry ($\text{H}^{\cdot},\ \text{OH}^{\cdot},\ \text{O}^{\cdot}$).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Stove, the Wind, and the Match"

Imagine a lit kitchen stove in a small closed galley:

- **Turn off the gas (fuel)** → the flame goes out (e.g., fuel shut-off valve).
- **Cover the pan with a lid** → the oxygen supply drops and the flame smothers (e.g., foam blanket, CO₂ flooding).
- **Throw cold water on a chip-pan fire** → the heat is extracted below the ignition temperature and combustion ceases (e.g., water spray/mist).

A firefighting system on a fishing vessel is, in essence, a **machine-engineered version of these three kitchen actions**, scaled up to handle machinery spaces of several hundred cubic metres, and made **automatic** so that the crew, who are often sleeping or at sea, do not have to perform them manually.

## 1.4 Classes of Marine Fire (ISO / IMO / SOLAS Classification)

| Class | Fuel Type | Typical Onboard Source | Correct Extinguishing Media |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **A** | Solid combustibles (wood, rope, fish meal, fabrics) | Accomodation, net store, fish hold | Water, foam, water mist |
| **B** | Flammable liquids (diesel, lube oil, hydraulic fluid) | Engine room, purifier room, bilge | Foam, dry chemical, CO₂ |
| **C** | Flammable gases (LPG refrigerant, acetylene) | Galley, welding shop | Dry chemical powder, shut off source |
| **D** | Combustible metals (magnesium, lithium batteries) | Battery lockers | Special dry powder (NaCl/graphite) |
| **F / K** | Cooking oils and fats | Galley deep-fat fryer | Wet chemical, fire blanket |

> [!NOTE]
> A single integrated fishing vessel **engine room** is a *Class A + B + C* hazard zone simultaneously — hence the statutory requirement for a **fixed CO₂ or equivalent gaseous flooding system**.

## 1.5 Why Firefighting is a Critical Module Topic

A fishing vessel at sea is essentially an **unsupervised industrial plant** with:

- **High fuel inventory** (diesel bunker tanks holding 10–30 % of displacement).
- **Hot machinery** (main engine exhaust manifold at $400\text{–}600^{\circ}\text{C}$).
- **Combustible cargo** (timber, net, fish-meal bagstacks).
- **Limited escape routes** (a single stairway, narrow alleyways).
- **No external fire brigade** (rescue is hours away).

Hence, **engineering the fire out at the design stage** is the single most important life-safety measure in fishing-vessel architecture.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Engineering Pillars of Marine Firefighting

1. **Passive Structural Fire Protection (P-60 / A-60 / B-15 divisions)**
2. **Active Fire Suppression Systems (fixed + portable)**
3. **Detection, Alarm & Evacuation Systems (LSA Code)**

## 2.2 Passive Structural Protection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Structural fire protection is governed by the **"A", "B", and "F" class divisions** of SOLAS Chapter II-2 (applied mutatis mutandis to fishing vessels ≥ 24 m by the Cape Town Agreement 2012).

- **A-Class Division:** Steel bulkhead/deck insulated such that, on one side a standard fire test ($1000^{\circ}\text{C}$) does not transmit heat to the unexposed side for **60 minutes**, with average temperature rise ≤ $140^{\circ}\text{C}$ and peak rise ≤ $180^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- **B-Class Division:** Similar but for **30 minutes** of resistance (typically for accommodation corridors).
- **F-Class Division:** Non-combustible but with reduced integrity/insulation, used in service spaces.
- **H-60 / H-30 / H-0:** Hydrocarbon fires (engine room bulkheads) — tested with **hydrocarbon fire curve** which is *more severe* than the cellulosic curve.

**Insulating materials** typically used: rock wool, ceramic fibre, calcium silicate, vermiculite board.

## 2.3 Active Fire Suppression — The "Big Five" Media

### 2.3.1 Water (Sprinkler, Spray, Mist, Drencher)

- **Cooling mechanism** dominates — water has the highest specific heat ($c_p = 4.186\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$) and a high latent heat of vaporisation ($L_v \approx 2260\ \text{kJ/kg}$).
- Used in: accommodation, fish hold, paint locker periphery.
- **Application densities (SOLAS Table):**
  - Accommodation: **5 L/min·m²** (sprinkler).
  - Machinery space boundaries (drencher): **10 L/min·m²** for 3 L/min·m² of protected area.

### 2.3.2 Foam (AFFF, AFFF-AR, FFFP, Protein, High-Expansion)

- **Smothering + cooling** — foam blanket excludes oxygen and seals vapour.
- **Expansion ratio:**
$$
E_r = \frac{V_{\text{foam}}}{V_{\text{concentrate + water + air}}}
$$
  - Low expansion: $E_r = 5\text{–}20$
  - Medium expansion: $E_r = 20\text{–}200$
  - High expansion: $E_r = 200\text{–}1000$ (used for engine room flooding of large voids).
- **Typical application rate (SOLAS):** **6.5 L/min·m²** of protected horizontal floor area for low-expansion AFFF in machinery spaces.

### 2.3.3 CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide) — Most Common for Engine Rooms

- **Smothering** — reduces O₂ to below $\approx 12\%$ (combustion limit).
- Stored as **liquefied gas** at $\approx 5.7\ \text{MPa}$ at $21^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- **Discharge time: ≤ 120 seconds** (engine room) to prevent reignition; **≤ 30 seconds** for paint lockers.

### 2.3.4 Dry Chemical Powder (DCP — Monoammonium Phosphate, ABC type)

- **Chain-breaking** — interrupts the radical propagation reactions.
- Used in **Class B, C fires**; portable extinguishers in galley, welding shop.

### 2.3.5 Water Mist (High-Pressure, 40–120 bar)

- Fine droplets ($\leq 200\ \mu\text{m}$) **block radiant heat** and create local steam inerting.
- Increasingly preferred because of **lower weight, no toxic residue, IMO MSC/Circ.1165 compliant.**

## 2.4 Detection & Alarm Systems

- **Smoke Detectors (Optical / Ionisation):** Accommodation, corridors.
- **Heat Detectors (Fixed Temperature $\approx 57^{\circ}\text{C}$ or Rate-of-Rise):** Galley, machinery casing.
- **Flame Detectors (IR / UV):** Engine room, purifier room.
- **Manual Call Points (Break-Glass):** At every exit, stairway landing.
- **Fire Detection Panel (FDP):** Central mimic panel on the bridge with **zone-wise LED indication** and **distinctive audible alarm (continuous + intermittent bells)**.
- **Public Address (PA) System:** Mandatory on vessels ≥ 45 m for crew alarm and muster instructions.

## 2.5 Fire Pumps, Hydrants & Hoses

> [!NOTE]
> Per SOLAS Reg. II-2/10 (applied to fishing vessels):
> **At least two independent power-driven fire pumps** must be provided, each capable of delivering the required pressure/flow simultaneously.

- **Emergency Fire Pump:** Sea-water cooled, sea-water lubricated, located **above the bulkhead deck** or in a position accessible in a flooding casualty, with its own sea-chest.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table of High-Yield Formulas — Commit to Memory**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Applicability |
|:-:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $V_{\text{CO}_2} = A \cdot h \cdot q$ | CO₂ volume required | m³ | Where $A$ = plan area, $h$ = gross height, $q$ = $\approx 0.40\ \text{kg/m}^3$ (machinery) |
| 2 | $W_{\text{CO}_2} = V \cdot \rho$ | CO₂ mass required | kg | $\rho \approx 1.836\ \text{kg/m}^3$ at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$, 1 atm |
| 3 | $Q_{\text{pump}} = K\sqrt{P}$ | Fire pump characteristic | L/min, m head | Empirically fitted from manufacturer curve |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{nozzle}} = C_d A_n \sqrt{2gH}$ | Jet discharge from nozzle | L/min | $C_d \approx 0.97$ for smooth fire hose nozzle |
| 5 | $E_r = V_f / (V_c + V_w)$ | Foam expansion ratio | — | Quality control check |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{\text{water}} = \rho_w \cdot A \cdot v$ | Water flow in main | kg/s | Header pipe sizing |
| 7 | $N_{\text{cyl,CO}_2} = W / w_c$ | Number of CO₂ cylinders | — | $w_c \approx 45\ \text{kg}$ per standard cylinder |
| 8 | $P_{\text{sprinkler}} = \dot{q} \cdot A_{\text{zone}}$ | Sprinkler water demand | L/min | Where $\dot{q}$ = design density |
| 9 | $t_{\text{discharge}} = V_{\text{CO}_2} / \dot{V}$ | Flooding time | s | Must be $\leq 120\ \text{s}$ (machinery) |
| 10 | $f_{\text{alarm}} = 0.5 \cdot f_{\text{2-way radio}}$ | Alarm frequency separation | Hz | 500 Hz vs 1000 Hz bells |

> **Symbols used:** $A$ = area (m²), $V$ = volume (m³), $\dot{q}$ = application rate (L/min·m²), $C_d$ = discharge coefficient, $A_n$ = nozzle throat area, $H$ = nozzle pressure head, $w_c$ = cylinder charge weight.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Insurance Premiums** — A vessel with a properly engineered fixed CO₂ system, certified fire detection, and a Cape Town Agreement safety certificate pays a **lower P&I insurance premium**.
- **CFD Modelling** — Modern designers use **Fire Dynamics Simulator (FDS)** to predict smoke and heat spread and to **optimise detector placement**.
- **Egress Design** — "Travel distance to nearest exit" is fixed by class and must be ≤ **40 m** in accommodation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sizing a Fixed CO₂ Flooding System for an Engine Room

**Problem Statement:** A fishing vessel's main engine room has internal dimensions:
Length $L = 12\ \text{m}$, Breadth $B = 6\ \text{m}$, Height $H = 4\ \text{m}$. Compute the CO₂ mass required, number of standard $45\ \text{kg}$ cylinders, and verify the flooding time for a single 4-nozzle manifold.

### Step 1 — Compute the Net Protected Volume

The volume of spaces that contain permanent fixtures (tank, engine) is subtracted. For a typical engine room, about $25\%$ is occupied; net free volume is $75\%$ of gross.

$$
V_{\text{net}} = L \cdot B \cdot H \cdot (1 - f_{\text{occ}})
$$

Substituting values:

$$
V_{\text{net}} = 12 \times 6 \times 4 \times (1 - 0.25) = 288 \times 0.75 = 216\ \text{m}^3
$$

### Step 2 — Specific CO₂ Quantity

Per SOLAS / FSS Code Ch. 5, machinery spaces require $q = 0.40\ \text{kg/m}^3$ (open-deck allowance is $0.20$).

$$
W_{\text{CO}_2} = V_{\text{net}} \cdot q
$$

$$
W_{\text{CO}_2} = 216 \times 0.40 = 86.4\ \text{kg}
$$

### Step 3 — Number of Cylinders

Standard marine CO₂ cylinders hold $w_c = 45\ \text{kg}$ of liquid CO₂. Always round **up** to the next integer and add **1 spare**:

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{W_{\text{CO}_2}}{w_c} \right\rceil + 1
$$

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{86.4}{45} \right\rceil + 1 = \lceil 1.92 \rceil + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3\ \text{cylinders}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify Discharge Time

Each cylinder discharges through a 1-inch pilot-operated valve. Manufacturer flow rate $\dot{m}_c = 1.2\ \text{kg/s}$ per cylinder. Total discharge from 3 cylinders through 4 nozzles:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{total}} = 3 \times 1.2 = 3.6\ \text{kg/s}
$$

$$
t_d = \frac{W_{\text{CO}_2}}{\dot{m}_{\text{total}}}
$$

$$
t_d = \frac{86.4}{3.6} = 24\ \text{s}
$$

**Check:** $24\ \text{s} \ll 120\ \text{s}$ SOLAS limit. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> The CO₂ discharge is so rapid that **all personnel must evacuate** before activation. A **pre-discharge alarm (distinctive continuous bell for $\geq 20\ \text{s}$)** is mandatory.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fire Pump Pressure / Flow Characteristic

The pump characteristic for a centrifugal fire pump is given empirically as:

$$
Q = K \sqrt{P}
$$

where $K$ is found by calibration. The **rated point** is the intersection of pump and system curves. For a vessel with the following demands simultaneously:

- 2 hydrants each at $0.40\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$ at $0.40\ \text{MPa}$
- 1 sprinkler section at $1.20\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$ at $0.25\ \text{MPa}$

**Step 1 — Total required flow** (worst-case, two hydrants + one sprinkler, per SOLAS):

$$
Q_{\text{req}} = 2 \times 0.40 + 1.20 = 2.00\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Step 2 — Head at the highest hydrant** (12 m above pump + friction $\approx 0.15\ \text{MPa}$):

$$
H_{\text{total}} = 12 + 15 = 27\ \text{m of water} \approx 0.265\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Pump coefficient K** (assume pump delivers $1.60\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$ at $0.40\ \text{MPa}$ at test bed):

$$
K = \frac{Q_{\text{rated}}}{\sqrt{P_{\text{rated}}}} = \frac{1.60}{\sqrt{0.40}} = 2.530
$$

**Step 4 — Check delivery at required head:**

$$
Q = K \sqrt{P} = 2.530 \times \sqrt{0.265} = 2.530 \times 0.515 = 1.302\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}
$$

This is **less than** $Q_{\text{req}} = 2.00\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$ — **Pump is undersized!** Select next size with $K \geq 3.18$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Foam Concentrate Quantity for a Bilge Fire

A fishing vessel's engine-room bilge well has plan area $A_b = 25\ \text{m}^2$ and we need $T_{\text{supply}} = 10\ \text{min}$ of foam discharge at SOLAS application rate $\dot{q}_f = 6.5\ \text{L/min·m}^2$ of AFFF $3\%$ concentrate.

**Step 1 — Total foam solution flow:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{soln}} = \dot{q}_f \cdot A_b
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{soln}} = 6.5 \times 25 = 162.5\ \text{L/min}
$$

**Step 2 — Concentrate fraction:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{conc}} = 0.03 \times \dot{Q}_{\text{soln}}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{conc}} = 0.03 \times 162.5 = 4.875\ \text{L/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Total concentrate reserve for 10 min:**

$$
V_{\text{conc}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{conc}} \cdot T
$$

$$
V_{\text{conc}} = 4.875 \times 10 = 48.75\ \text{L}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> SOLAS also requires a **$200\%$ reserve** for the foam concentrate stock. Required tank volume $= 48.75 \times 3 = 146.25\ \text{L} \approx 150\ \text{L}$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Extinguishing Media

| Hazard Location | Hazard Class | Recommended Primary System | Recommended Backup |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| Main engine room | B (oil), A (cable), C (gas) | Fixed CO₂ + low-expansion foam | Portable DCP (50 kg) |
| Auxiliary engine room | B | Fixed CO₂ (local application) | DCP portable |
| Galley | F (cooking oil) | Wet chemical extinguisher + fire blanket | CO₂ portable |
| Accommodation | A | Sprinkler + smoke detection | Water-type portable (9 L) |
| Paint locker | B | Fixed CO₂ (high rate, 30 s) | — |
| Fish hold (refrigerated) | A | Water mist + smoke detection | Portable water spray |
| Net store / work area | A | Hose reel (length ≤ 30 m) | Water extinguisher |
| Battery locker | D (Li-ion) | Special Li-ion extinguisher | Ventilation shut-off |

## 3.5 SOLAS / Cape Town Compliance Checklist (Analytical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 24–45 m fishing vessel must, at minimum, satisfy the following statutory check-list to obtain a fishing vessel safety certificate:

1. ☐ Two independent power-driven fire pumps
2. ☐ One emergency fire pump in a flood-protected location
3. ☐ Hydrants at every deck level, max 45 m hose run
4. ☐ Fixed CO₂ in machinery spaces (≥ 0.40 kg/m³)
5. ☐ Fixed sprinkler in accommodation for vessels ≥ 45 m
6. ☐ International shore connection (Nelson Horn)
7. ☐ Fireman's outfit (2 sets) with breathing apparatus
8. ☐ Fire detection panel on bridge with zone mimic
9. ☐ Public address / general alarm system
10. ☐ Fire control plan permanently exhibited
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Onboard Firefighting System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hazard Source: Fuel and Ignition] --> B[Fire Initiation]
    B --> C{Sensor Layer}
    C --> C1[Smoke Detector]
    C --> C2[Heat Detector]
    C --> C3[Flame Detector IR UV]
    C --> C4[Manual Call Point]
    C1 --> D[Fire Detection Panel FDP]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Alarm Network: Bell PA Siren]
    D --> F{Crew Decision Logic}
    F --> F1[Investigate Manual Activation]
    F --> F2[Activate Fixed System]
    F --> F3[Boundary Closure: Dampers Doors]
    D --> G[Auto Flooding CO2 / Water Mist / Foam]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Suppression Event]
    H --> I[Verification and ReWatch]
    I --> J[Post Incident Report and Recharge]
    A -. mitigation .-> K[Passive Layer: A60 B15 H60 Divisions]
    K -. contains .-> B
    E --> L[Emergency Response: Muster and Escape Routes]
    F1 --> L
```

## 4.2 CO₂ Flooding System Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[CO2 Cylinder Bank 45 kg each] -->|Pilot Line| R[Pilot Operated Valve]
    R -->|Release| M[Distribution Manifold]
    M --> N1[Nozzle 1: Above Engine]
    M --> N2[Nozzle 2: Above Gearbox]
    M --> N3[Nozzle 3: Bilge Area]
    M --> N4[Nozzle 4: Funnel Uptake]
    T[Time Delay Relay 20 s] -->|Hold| R
    A[Manual Pull Handle at Door] -->|Override| R
    W[Weighing Scale] -. monitor .-> S
    P[Pre-Discharge Siren] -. warning .-> T
```

## 4.3 Detection-to-Suppression Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start([Fire Breaks Out]) --> det[Sensor Triggers]
    det --> fdp[Fire Detection Panel FDP]
    fdp --> ack{Crew Acknowledges within 30 s}
    ack -- Yes --> manual[Manual Investigation]
    ack -- No --> auto[Auto Sequence Initiated]
    manual --> escalate{Confirmed Fire}
    escalate -- Yes --> auto
    escalate -- No --> reset[System Reset and Log]
    auto --> siren[Pre-Discharge Siren 20 s]
    siren --> damp[Fire Dampers Close]
    damp --> vent[Ventilation Stops]
    vent --> flood[CO2 or Foam Released]
    flood --> timer[Discharge Timer 120 s]
    timer --> reentry[No Re-Entry Without BA Set]
    reentry --> end([Fire Suppressed and Verified])
```

## 4.4 Vessel Fire Zones (Cross-Sectional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Above_Deck
        A1[Wheelhouse and Accommodation: A60]
        A2[Galley: Wet Chemical]
        A3[Net Store: Hose Reel]
    end
    subgraph Main_Deck
        B1[Working Deck: Portable DCP]
        B2[Paint Store: Fixed CO2]
    end
    subgraph Below_Main_Deck
        C1[Engine Room: Fixed CO2 and Foam]
        C2[Fish Hold: Water Mist]
        C3[Battery Room: Li-Ion Special]
        C4[Fuel Tanks: Inert Gas or Drencher]
    end
    A1 -. escape route .-> Above_Deck
    C1 -. fire boundary H60 .-> C2
    C1 -. fire boundary H60 .-> C4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **escape route** must be diagonally opposite to the **engine room bulkhead**. Two means of escape are mandated from every watertight compartment.
