# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Strength

**Assumptions:**
1. Fibers are continuous, parallel, and uniformly distributed.
2. Perfect bonding between fiber and matrix (no slip).
3. Both fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** (iso-strain condition) — valid for tensile load parallel to fibers.

**Step 1:** Apply a tensile force $F_c$ on the composite along the fiber direction.

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

**Step 2:** Divide by the cross-sectional area $A_c$:

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

**Step 3:** Since $A_c = A_f + A_m$, divide throughout by $A_c$:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \dfrac{A_f}{A_c} + \sigma_m \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the area ratios as the volume fractions (for fibers aligned with load):

$$\dfrac{A_f}{A_c} = V_f, \qquad \dfrac{A_m}{A_c} = 1 - V_f$$

**Step 5:** Substitute to obtain the **Rule of Mixtures for strength**:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f)$$

**Step 6:** Since iso-strain gives $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$, the stress equals strain × modulus:

$$\sigma_c = E_c \varepsilon, \quad \sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon, \quad \sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon$$

**Step 7:** Substitute into the strength equation:

$$E_c \varepsilon = E_f \varepsilon \, V_f + E_m \varepsilon (1 - V_f)$$

**Step 8:** Cancel $\varepsilon$ to obtain the **Rule of Mixtures for stiffness**:

$$E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$$

This is the **upper bound** of composite stiffness and applies to loads parallel to the fiber direction. The **lower bound** (transverse loading) is derived assuming **iso-stress** (same stress in fiber and matrix) and gives the **inverse rule of mixtures**:

$$\dfrac{1}{E_{ct}} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{1 - V_f}{E_m} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad E_{ct} = \dfrac{E_f E_m}{E_f(1 - V_f) + E_m V_f}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Flexural Rigidity of a Sandwich Panel

**Setup:** A sandwich beam of width $b$, with two GRP face skins each of thickness $t_f$, separated by a low-density core of thickness $c$ (so that total panel thickness $d = c + 2t_f$).

**Step 1:** The second moment of area of the two thin faces (treated as a thin-walled I-beam) about the neutral axis is:

$$I_{faces} = 2 \times \dfrac{b \, t_f^3}{12} + 2 b t_f \left(\dfrac{d - t_f}{2}\right)^2 \approx \dfrac{b \, t_f \, d^2}{2} \quad (t_f \ll d)$$

**Step 2:** The core contributes a smaller second moment (assuming it carries negligible bending stress):

$$I_{core} = \dfrac{b \, c^3}{12}$$

**Step 3:** Total second moment of area:

$$I = \dfrac{b \, t_f \, d^2}{2} + \dfrac{b \, c^3}{12}$$

**Step 4:** The flexural rigidity (per unit width) of the sandwich is:

$$D_{sandwich} = \dfrac{E_f \, t_f \, d^2}{2(1 - \nu_{fc}^2)} + \dfrac{E_c \, c^3}{12(1 - \nu_c^2)}$$

For a lightweight core, the first term (face contribution) **dominates** by 1–2 orders of magnitude, which is why a sandwich is so much stiffer than a monolithic laminate of equal weight.

**Step 5:** Comparative example — Consider a single-skin GRP panel of thickness $t = 10$ mm vs. a sandwich of two 3 mm GRP faces + 30 mm PVC core ($d = 36$ mm).

For the single skin:
$$D_{single} = \dfrac{E_f (0.010)^3}{12} \propto (10)^3 = 1000 \text{ (relative units)}$$

For the sandwich (face term only):
$$D_{sandwich,faces} \propto t_f \, d^2 = 3 \times (36)^2 = 3888 \text{ (relative units)}$$

Thus, the sandwich is **~3.9× stiffer in bending** while weighing only marginally more — the engineering rationale for using sandwich in long-span decks and hull bottoms.

## 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Hull Skin Thickness (Empirical KTU Formula)

For small fishing craft, the minimum single-skin laminate thickness required to resist hydrostatic pressure and minor impact loads is given by the **Malcolm & Liao / ABS empirical relation**:

$$t_{min} = k \sqrt{L} \quad \text{(mm)}$$

where:
- $L$ = rule length of the vessel in meters.
- $k$ = empirical constant ≈ **4.5 mm/m$^{1/2}$** for hand lay-up polyester GRP (lower for infused laminates).

**Worked example (typical KTU problem):** A fishing trawler has $L = 12$ m. Find the minimum hull bottom skin thickness.

$$t_{min} = 4.5 \times \sqrt{12} = 4.5 \times 3.464 \approx 15.6 \text{ mm}$$

In practice, this is built up as a multi-ply laminate: e.g., **gelcoat + 2 layers of 450 g/m² CSM + 3 layers of 800 g/m² woven roving + 1 layer of 450 g/m² CSM**, giving a cured thickness of ~15–16 mm.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Manufacturing Process vs. Vessel Size

| Vessel Size (LOA) | Recommended Process | Typical $V_f$ | Sandwich Used? | Relative Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| < 8 m (canoes, dinghies) | Hand lay-up / Spray-up | 0.25–0.35 | No | Low |
| 8 – 15 m (mechanized craft) | Hand lay-up | 0.30–0.40 | Selective (deck) | Low – Medium |
| 15 – 24 m (trawlers) | Hand lay-up + Vacuum infusion (hybrid) | 0.40–0.50 | Yes (hull, deck) | Medium |
| 24 – 30 m (deep-sea vessels) | Vacuum infusion (SCRIMP) | 0.50–0.55 | Yes (full hull) | Medium – High |
| > 30 m | Prepreg / Filament winding (sub-components) | 0.55–0.65 | Yes | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** When asked to "select a construction method for a given fishing vessel," always justify your choice in terms of **(a) vessel size, (b) production volume, (c) mechanical performance required, (d) cost, and (e) skill of labor available.**

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A GRP laminate is made of 60% by weight E-glass and 40% by weight polyester resin. Given $\rho_f = 2560$ kg/m³ and $\rho_m = 1240$ kg/m³, calculate:
(a) the weight fractions,
(b) the volume fractions,
(c) the composite density,
(d) the longitudinal Young's modulus ($E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa).

**Solution:**

**(a)** Weight fractions (given):
$$W_f = 0.60, \qquad W_m = 0.40$$

**(b)** Convert to volume fractions using:
$$V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m} = \dfrac{0.60 / 2560}{0.60 / 2560 + 0.40 / 1240}$$

$$V_f = \dfrac{2.3438 \times 10^{-4}}{2.3438 \times 10^{-4} + 3.2258 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{2.3438}{5.5696} = 0.4209$$

$$V_m = 1 - V_f = 0.5791$$

**(c)** Composite density (by rule of mixtures):
$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = 2560 \times 0.4209 + 1240 \times 0.5791$$

$$\rho_c = 1077.5 + 718.1 = 1795.6 \text{ kg/m}^3 \approx 1800 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**(d)** Longitudinal modulus (rule of mixtures):
$$E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = 72 \times 0.4209 + 3.5 \times 0.5791$$

$$E_c = 30.30 + 2.03 = 32.33 \text{ GPa}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A typical hand lay-up GRP hull laminate has $E_c \approx 15$–$20$ GPa (lower $V_f$); vacuum-infused laminates approach 25–30 GPa; prepreg laminates can reach 40+ GPa. Steel for comparison is ~210 GPa, but at **3× the density** — hence the strength-to-weight advantage of composites.

## 4.1 Generic FRP/GRP Hull Construction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```


## 4.4 Hull Laminate Stack-Up Schematic (Typical Hand Lay-Up)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1: Gelcoat Polyester or Vinylester 0.5 mm] --> L2
    L2[Layer 2: Surface Veil C-Glass 30 g per sqm] --> L3

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The "sandwich" or "book-end" pattern (CSM–WR–WR–CSM) ensures that the chemically aggressive, glass-rich environment has a resin-rich barrier (CSM) on both outer surfaces — protecting fibers from moisture ingress (osmosis blistering) and from inside corrosion (fish-hold bilge water).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three constituents of an FRP laminate and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Reinforcement (glass fiber):** Provides tensile strength, stiffness, and impact resistance to the laminate. Common forms are chopped strand mat (CSM), woven roving (WR), and unidirectional (UD) cloth.

2. **Matrix (resin):** Holds the fibers together in the desired shape, transfers the applied load between fibers, and protects the fibers from moisture, chemicals, and abrasion. Polyester and vinylester resins are most common in fishing vessels.

3. **Filler:** Added to the resin to reduce cost, control shrinkage during cure, and improve fire-retardant or thixotropic properties. Calcium carbonate and alumina trihydrate (ATH) are typical fillers.

4. (Optional) **Curing agent / catalyst:** MEKP is added at 1–2% to initiate the cross-linking reaction that hardens the resin.

*(3 marks: 1 for naming, 1 for stating function of each; neat tabular form appreciated.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is a sandwich laminate? Why is it used in fishing vessels?**

**Model Answer:**

A **sandwich laminate** is a composite in which a thick, low-density **core material** (PVC foam, balsa, or honeycomb) is sandwiched between two thin, strong GRP **face skins**.

**Reasons for use in fishing vessels:**

1. **High flexural rigidity with low weight** — a 30 mm PVC-core sandwich can be 4–8 times stiffer in bending than a monolithic 10 mm GRP panel of similar weight.
2. **Thermal and acoustic insulation** — reduces heat transfer and engine noise in the wheelhouse, improving crew comfort.
3. **Buoyancy and safety** — foam-cored panels add reserve buoyancy in case of hull damage.
4. **Used in decks, superstructures, bulkheads, and sometimes hull bottoms of trawlers above 15 m LOA** to reduce topside weight and lower the center of gravity.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Hand Lay-Up method** of FRP construction for a fishing vessel. State its advantages and limitations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A GRP laminate contains 55% by weight of E-glass and 45% by weight of polyester resin. Given $\rho_f = 2560$ kg/m³, $\rho_m = 1240$ kg/m³, $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, determine the **fiber volume fraction**, the **composite density**, and the **longitudinal Young's modulus** of the laminate.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Hand Lay-Up Method (also called Contact Molding):**

1. **Mold preparation** — A female (or male) mold is fabricated from timber, plaster, fiberglass, or steel. Its surface is polished, then coated with a release agent (wax followed by PVA film) to allow the cured laminate to be separated cleanly.

2. **Gelcoat application** — A pigmented polyester or vinylester gelcoat (**0.4–0.6 mm**) is sprayed or brushed on the inner mold surface. This gives the finished hull its smooth, glossy, water-resistant outer skin.

3. **Skin coat** — A surface veil (30 g/m² C-glass) followed by a light CSM is laid into the wet gelcoat to mask print-through of coarser reinforcements.

4. **Structural lay-up** — Alternating layers of **CSM (chopped strand mat)** and **WR (woven roving)** are placed dry, then wetted out with catalysed resin. Each ply is consolidated with serrated rollers to expel air and ensure complete fiber wet-out. Typical stack: CSM 450 + WR 800 + WR 800 + CSM 450 (≈ 6–8 mm per cycle).

5. **Curing** — Laminate cures at room temperature for 24–48 hours. For marine service, a post-cure at 50–80 °C may be used to improve glass-transition temperature.

6. **Demolding and finishing** — Hull is separated from the mold; flanges and excess are trimmed; secondary structures (bulkheads, stiffeners, engine beds) are bonded in.

**Advantages:**
- Very low tooling cost.
- Suitable for very large structures (up to 30 m+).
- Easy to repair in service.
- Simple equipment — suitable for small shipyards.

**Limitations:**
- **Labor-intensive** — depends heavily on operator skill.
- **Low fiber volume fraction** ($V_f$ 0.25–0.40).
- **High void content** (2–5%) — reduces fatigue life.
- **Styrene emissions** — environmental and health hazard; requires good ventilation.
- **Thickness and quality variability** between operators.
- **Longer cycle time** than closed-mold methods.

*(7 marks: 2 for sequence, 2 for materials, 2 for advantages, 1 for limitations.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Given:**
- $W_f = 0.55$, $W_m = 0.45$
- $\rho_f = 2560$ kg/m³, $\rho_m = 1240$ kg/m³
- $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa

**(i) Fiber volume fraction:**

$$V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m} = \dfrac{0.55 / 2560}{0.55 / 2560 + 0.45 / 1240}$$

$$V_f = \dfrac{2.1484 \times 10^{-4}}{2.1484 \times 10^{-4} + 3.6290 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{2.1484}{5.7774} = 0.3719$$

$$V_m = 1 - V_f = 0.6281$$

**(ii) Composite density:**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = 2560 \times 0.3719 + 1240 \times 0.6281$$

$$\rho_c = 952.1 + 778.8 = 1730.9 \text{ kg/m}^3 \approx 1731 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**(iii) Longitudinal Young's modulus:**

$$E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = 72 \times 0.3719 + 3.5 \times 0.6281$$

$$E_c = 26.78 + 2.20 = 28.98 \text{ GPa} \approx 29.0 \text{ GPa}$$

**Result:** $V_f = 0.372$, $\rho_c = 1731$ kg/m³, $E_c = 29.0$ GPa.

*(7 marks: 2 for $V_f$, 2 for $\rho_c$, 2 for $E_c$, 1 for units and conclusion.)*

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Hand Lay-up**, **Spray-up**, and **Vacuum Infusion (SCRIMP)** processes for FRP fishing vessel construction under the following heads: process principle, $V_f$ achievable, void content, capital cost, suitable vessel size, and labor skill required.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the construction of a **sandwich laminate** used for the deck of a 20 m fishing trawler. State three design benefits of sandwich construction.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a): Comparison Table

| Parameter | Hand Lay-up | Spray-up | Vacuum Infusion (SCRIMP) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Process principle** | Dry reinforcement placed manually; resin brushed/rolled | Chopped fibers + resin sprayed simultaneously | Dry reinforcement sealed in vacuum bag; resin drawn through by vacuum |
| **$V_f$ achievable** | 0.25 – 0.40 | 0.20 – 0.30 | 0.45 – 0.55 |
| **Void content** | 2 – 5% | 3 – 6% | < 1% |
| **Capital cost** | Very low | Low | Medium (vacuum pump, bagging consumables) |
| **Labor skill** | High (manual skill) | Medium | Medium–High |
| **Cycle time** | Long (12–24 h/layer) | Medium | Long setup, fast infusion |
| **Styrene emission** | High | Very high | Very low (closed bag) |
| **Suitable vessel size** | All sizes, esp. < 24 m | < 12 m, small craft | 15 – 30 m, trawlers |
| **Quality reproducibility** | Low | Low | High |
| **Mechanical performance** | Moderate | Low–Moderate | High |

**Conclusion:** For a 20 m fishing trawler, **vacuum infusion (SCRIMP)** is preferred because it yields higher $V_f$, lower void content, and consistent laminate quality with reduced styrene emissions — at acceptable tooling cost.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per row × 6 rows + 1 for conclusion.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b): Sandwich Laminate Construction

**Construction sequence for a 20 m trawler deck sandwich panel:**

1. **Mold preparation** — Steel or composite deck mold cleaned and waxed.
2. **Gelcoat + inner skin** — Apply gelcoat; lay up first GRP skin (CSM 450 + WR 800, ~ 4 mm thick). Cure partially.
3. **Core bonding** — Apply laminating putty/adhesive; place **PVC foam sheets (typically 30–50 mm thick, density 80–100 kg/m³)** cut to fit deck camber. Stagger joints.
4. **Second skin lay-up** — Lay up outer GRP skin (CSM 450 + WR 800 + WR 800 + CSM 450, ~ 6 mm).
5. **Consolidation and cure** — Roll out air; cure at ambient for 24 h.
6. **Edge close-outs** — Tapered GRP/foam doublers at edges and around deck penetrations (hatches, mast step).

**Three design benefits:**

1. **Very high flexural rigidity with low weight** — reduces deck mass, lowering the vessel's center of gravity and improving stability.
2. **Thermal insulation** — the foam core limits heat transfer from the sun-exposed deck into fish-hold spaces below, preserving catch quality.
3. **Reduced sound transmission** — the viscoelastic foam damps noise from waves and crew activity, improving habitability for the crew.

*(7 marks: 3 for sequence with sketch, 2 for naming materials, 2 for benefits.)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU ESE for FRP/GRP Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $W_f$ (weight fraction) with $V_f$ (volume fraction)** — they are NOT equal because $\rho_f \neq \rho_m$. Always convert using $V_f = (W_f/\rho_f) / (W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m)$.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $(1 - V_f)$ in the rule of mixtures** — students often write $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m$ (omitting the matrix volume term), losing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up hand lay-up and spray-up** — spray-up uses a **chopper gun** depositing short fibers; hand lay-up uses **dry reinforcement mats/cloths placed by hand**.
> 4. **Not stating the function of gelcoat** — gelcoat is **not structural**; it provides **UV resistance, water-tightness, and cosmetic finish**.
> 5. **Calling GRP and FRP the same** — they overlap but **GRP is a subset of FRP** where the reinforcement is glass only.
> 6. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit consistently.
> 7. **Forgetting the gelcoat thickness (0.4–0.6 mm)** in hull laminate stack-up diagrams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FRP/GRP** is the dominant construction material for fishing vessels **below 30 m LOA**; preferred for corrosion resistance, low weight, and easy repair.
- **Key constituents:** reinforcement (E-glass), matrix (polyester/vinylester), filler, catalyst (MEKP), additives.
- **Major manufacturing methods:** **Hand lay-up, Spray-up, RTM, Vacuum infusion (SCRIMP), Filament winding, Pultrusion, Prepreg/autoclave.**
- **Hand lay-up** is the most common — low cost but lower $V_f$ (0.25–0.40).
- **Vacuum infusion (SCRIMP)** gives the best marine-grade quality with $V_f$ 0.45–0.55 and < 1% voids — preferred for trawlers > 15 m.
- **Sandwich construction** (PVC foam / balsa core + GRP skins) gives high bending stiffness at low weight — used in **decks, superstructures, bulkheads**.
- **Rule of mixtures (longitudinal):** $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$.
- **Inverse rule of mixtures (transverse):** $\dfrac{1}{E_{ct}} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{1 - V_f}{E_m}$.
- **Composite density:** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$.
- **Minimum hull skin thickness (empirical):** $t_{min} = k\sqrt{L}$ with $k \approx 4.5$ mm/m$^{1/2}$ for hand lay-up GRP.
- **Sandwich flexural rigidity:** $D_{sandwich} = \dfrac{E_f t_f d^2}{2(1 - \nu^2)}$ (face-dominated term).
- **Typical stack-up:** Gelcoat → CSM → WR → WR → CSM (sandwich with CSM-rich "book-ends" to prevent osmosis).
- **Quality control:** barcol hardness (40–50 cured), ultrasonic testing, hydrostatic test, spark test for gelcoat pinholes.
- **Selection rule for KTU answers:** Choose a process by **vessel size, production volume, mechanical performance, cost, and labor skill**.
- **Styrene emission** is the major environmental concern in open-mold methods; closed-mold (RTM/infusion) processes are increasingly mandated.
- **Filament winding** is restricted to axisymmetric parts (tanks, pipes, masts) and is **not** used for hull plating.
- **Always mention the gelcoat** (0.4–0.6 mm) in any hull laminate sketch — examiners expect it as the first outer layer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aluminium and Ferro-Cement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST754 - DESIGN OF FISHING VESSELS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Aluminium and Ferro-Cement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Aluminium as a Boat-Building Material

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Aluminium, in the context of fishing vessel design, refers to marine-grade aluminium alloys (predominantly the **Al-Mg series — AA 5083, AA 5086, AA 5454**) used as the primary structural material for hull plating, stiffeners, superstructures, and deck fittings. These alloys are chosen for their high **specific strength (strength-to-weight ratio)**, inherent corrosion resistance due to a passive oxide film, and excellent weldability using the **MIG (Metal Inert Gas)** and **TIG (Tungsten Inert Gas)** processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property Snapshot of Aluminium vs. Mild Steel:**
> - **Density (Al):** **2700 kg/m³** (≈ 1/3 of steel, **7850 kg/m³**)
> - **Young's Modulus (Al):** **70 GPa** (≈ 1/3 of steel, **210 GPa**)
> - **Yield Strength (5083-H116):** **215 MPa**
> - **Corrosion Rate in Seawater:** **< 25 µm/year** (when correctly alloyed)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Lightweight Boxer":**
> Imagine a heavyweight steel hull as a sumo wrestler — strong, durable, but heavy and slow to accelerate. An aluminium hull is like a middleweight boxer — slightly less powerful in absolute terms, but exceptionally agile, faster, fuel-efficient, and requiring less engine power to push through water. This trade-off (lightness for slightly lower absolute strength) is why aluminium dominates high-speed fishing vessels, patrol boats, and modern trawlers.

---

### 1.2 Ferro-Cement as a Boat-Building Material

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
**Ferro-cement** is a composite construction material composed of a **hydraulic cement mortar matrix** (Portland cement + fine sand + water), reinforced with closely spaced layers of **small-diameter wire mesh** (chicken mesh / hexagonal / welded mesh) and a **skeletal framework of reinforcing bars (rebar)**. It is used to construct monolithic, thin-walled, double-curvature hull shells for fishing vessels — particularly suited to artisanal and small-scale mechanized boats in developing maritime economies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property Snapshot of Ferro-Cement:**
> - **Density:** **2400–2500 kg/m³**
> - **Hull Plate Thickness (typical):** **15–25 mm** (very thin compared to steel)
> - **Specific Surface of Reinforcement (S_r):** typically **0.25–0.5 mm²/mm³**
> - **Volume Fraction of Reinforcement (V_f):** **3–8 %**
> - **Tensile Strength:** **10–40 MPa** (isotropic, due to mesh multi-directionality)
> - **Young's Modulus:** **20–30 GPa**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Armored Eggshell":**
> Picture a chicken egg — its shell is brittle ceramic (cement mortar), yet incredibly strong because of its curved shape. Now imagine covering that shell with multiple layers of fine wire mesh before it hardens. The mesh arrests any micro-cracks, while the curved geometry distributes loads evenly. That is exactly what ferro-cement does for a boat hull: an inexpensive, low-skill construction technique that turns brittle cement into a strong, crack-resistant marine structure. It is the "people's boat material" — built with sand, cement, wire, and basic tools.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU Exam Focus:**
> For KTU 2024 evaluations, examiners frequently test:
> 1. Comparison between aluminium, ferro-cement, steel, and FRP for fishing vessels.
> 2. Marine-grade aluminium alloy designations and their tempers.
> 3. Composition and construction sequence of ferro-cement hulls.
> 4. Design plate thickness calculations (ISO 12215-5 style for aluminium; empirical FAO/Class formulas for ferro-cement).

---

### 1.3 Where Each Material Fits in Fishing Vessel Design

| Criterion | Aluminium | Ferro-Cement |
|---|---|---|
| **Typical Vessel Length** | 6 m – 45 m (all sizes) | 5 m – 18 m (small vessels) |
| **Construction Skill** | Skilled welders required | Semi-skilled labour sufficient |
| **Capital Cost** | High (2× steel) | Very Low (≈ 0.5× steel) |
| **Weight (Hull only)** | Lightest | Heaviest |
| **Maintenance** | Low (anodic protection needed) | Moderate (crack inspection) |
| **Repair Facilities** | Need specialized welding | Can be done with hand tools |
| **Lifespan** | 25–40 years | 20–30 years |
| **Best Suited For** | High-speed trawlers, longliners, seiners | Artisanal boats, training vessels, low-budget fleets |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Marine Aluminium Alloys — Deep Dive

#### 2.1.1 Alloy Designation Logic
Aluminium alloys are classified by a **4-digit ANSI designation**:
- **5xxx series** → Al-Mg (magnesium is the principal alloying element) → **Marine workhorses**
- **6xxx series** → Al-Mg-Si → Used for **extrusions** (handrails, mast sections)
- **H-tempers** (e.g., H116, H321) → Strain-hardened + thermally stabilized → **Best for marine corrosion resistance**

#### 2.1.2 Why AA 5083 is the Default Marine Alloy

- **Magnesium content:** 4.0–4.9 % → solid solution strengthening
- **Manganese content:** 0.4–1.0 % → improves corrosion resistance
- **Chromium content:** 0.05–0.25 % → controls grain structure, prevents intergranular corrosion
- **Weldability:** Excellent (no post-weld heat treatment required)
- **Temper H116 / H321:** Special tempers guarantee ** exfoliation and intergranular corrosion resistance** in marine environments.

#### 2.1.3 Key Mechanical Properties (AA 5083-H116)

| Property | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density | $\rho_{Al}$ | **2660** | kg/m³ |
| Young's Modulus | $E_{Al}$ | **70** | GPa |
| Yield Strength (0.2 % proof) | $\sigma_{y,Al}$ | **215** | MPa |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | $\sigma_{u,Al}$ | **305** | MPa |
| Elongation at break | $\varepsilon$ | **12** | % |
| Fatigue Endurance Limit | $\sigma_{fatigue}$ | **115** | MPa |
| Thermal Expansion Coeff. | $\alpha$ | **23.5 × 10⁻⁶** | /K |

#### 2.1.4 Design Considerations for Aluminium Hulls

- **Corrosion Protection:** Aluminium is the **anode** when paired with steel/bronze → use **zinc sacrificial anodes** and **insulating gaskets** to isolate dissimilar metals.
- **Stiffener Spacing:** Smaller than steel because of lower $E$ → plates deflect more under same load.
- **Buckling:** Lower $E$ → more susceptible to **panel buckling** → use **flanged plates** and **stiffened panels**.
- **Welding Distortion:** High thermal expansion (≈ 2× steel) → requires **balanced welding sequences** and **rigid jigs**.
- **Galvanic Series Awareness:** Aluminium is near the active end → avoid direct contact with **stainless steel** in seawater without insulation.

---

### 2.2 Ferro-Cement — Deep Dive

#### 2.2.1 Composition (by Mass — typical small fishing boat)

- **Portland Cement (OPC 43 grade):** **1 part**
- **Fine Sand (passing 2.36 mm sieve):** **1.5–2 parts**
- **Water-Cement Ratio:** **0.40–0.45** (critical for marine durability)
- **Reinforcement:**
  - **Skeletal steel:** 6–10 mm dia. rebar at **75–150 mm spacing** (both ways)
  - **Mesh layers:** 4–8 layers of **chicken wire / hexagonal wire mesh**
  - **Mesh wire dia.:** **0.5–1.0 mm**
- **Specific Surface of Reinforcement ($S_r$):** Total surface area of steel per unit volume of composite. Typical marine specification: $S_r \geq 0.25$ mm²/mm³

#### 2.2.2 Construction Sequence

1. **Build skeletal frame** (rebar cage shaped like the hull)
2. **Tie mesh layers** around the skeleton, rotating 50 % offset each layer
3. **Erect temporary supports / formwork** (wood, earth mound, or sand-filled mold)
4. **Plaster mortar from outside** (first layer of rich cement slurry)
5. **Plaster from inside** through mesh, working from low points upward
6. **Cure for 21–28 days** under wet hessian cloth (critical for strength gain)
7. **Fair surface, apply epoxy primer + antifouling**

#### 2.2.3 Mechanical Behaviour

- **High tensile strength despite low matrix strength** → mechanism: **crack arrest by closely spaced mesh** (cracks form, but mesh holds them tight at < 0.1 mm width → water-resistant).
- **Isotropic in-plane strength** → because mesh is multi-directional → unlike reinforced concrete (which is weak in tension perpendicular to rebar).
- **Two-stage cracking:** First crack at low load → mesh takes over → second-stage cracking only at much higher load.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

#### A. Aluminium Plate Thickness (Simplified Plate Bending)

For a rectangular plate under uniform lateral pressure $p$, simply supported on stiffeners:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{k \cdot p \cdot s^2}{t^2}
$$

Solving for required plate thickness $t$:

$$
t = s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k \cdot p \cdot n}{\sigma_y}}
$$

where:

- $t$ = required plate thickness (mm)
- $s$ = stiffener spacing (mm)
- $p$ = design lateral pressure (N/mm²)
- $k$ = plate aspect ratio factor (≈ **0.308** for square simply-supported plate)
- $n$ = safety factor (typically **1.5**)
- $\sigma_y$ = material yield strength (N/mm²)

#### B. Ferro-Cement Hull Plate Thickness (Empirical FAO Formula)

For small fishing vessels (LWL $\leq$ 18 m):

$$
t_{fc} = 15 + 0.05 \cdot L_{WL}
$$

where:

- $t_{fc}$ = hull plate thickness (mm)
- $L_{WL}$ = length at waterline (m)

#### C. Specific Surface of Reinforcement ($S_r$)

$$
S_r = \frac{A_{steel}}{V_{composite}}
$$

where $A_{steel}$ is the total surface area of steel reinforcement per unit volume of composite. **Target:** $S_r \geq 0.25$ mm²/mm³.

#### D. Volume Fraction of Reinforcement ($V_f$)

$$
V_f = \frac{V_{steel}}{V_{steel} + V_{mortar}}
$$

#### E. Material Weight Comparison (per m² of hull plating)

$$
W = \rho \cdot t
$$

| Material | Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | Typical $t$ (mm) | Weight (kg/m²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aluminium (5083) | 2660 | 5 | **13.3** |
| Mild Steel | 7850 | 4 | **31.4** |
| Ferro-Cement | 2450 | 20 | **49.0** |
| GRP | 1500 | 8 | **12.0** |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that aluminium hulls at 5 mm thickness are **2.4× lighter than steel** and **3.7× lighter than ferro-cement** for the same structural role — the trade-off is cost and the need for skilled welders. Ferro-cement is the heaviest but cheapest.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Aluminium fishing vessels** → dominate the **24 m tuna longliner fleet** in the Pacific (low fuel burn, fast transit).
- **Ferro-cement fishing vessels** → extensively used in **India, Bangladesh, Vietnam, and Sri Lanka** for artisanal and small-mechanized boats; promoted by the **FAO Code of Conduct for Responsible Fisheries** as a low-cost entry material.
- Both materials support **SDG-14 (Life Below Water)** by enabling sustainable small-scale fisheries when correctly designed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Aluminium Plate Thickness Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A small fishing trawler has bottom plating supported by stiffeners spaced at **600 mm × 500 mm**. The design lateral pressure from slamming at the midship bottom is **25 kN/m²**. The plating material is **AA 5083-H116** with yield strength $\sigma_y = 215$ MPa. Using a safety factor $n = 1.5$ and a plate aspect ratio factor $k = 0.308$, calculate the **minimum required plate thickness**.

---

#### Step 1 — Identify and convert all units to a consistent system

$$
p = 25 \text{ kN/m}^2 = \frac{25 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{10^6 \text{ mm}^2} = 0.025 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
\sigma_y = 215 \text{ MPa} = 215 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
s = 600 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{longer side governs})
$$

---

#### Step 2 — Write the governing plate-bending equation

For a rectangular plate under uniform pressure $p$, the maximum bending stress at the plate centre is:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{k \cdot p \cdot s^2}{t^2}
$$

We require $\sigma_{max} \leq \sigma_{allow}$, where:

$$
\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n} = \frac{215}{1.5} \approx 143.33 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

---

#### Step 3 — Substitute the allowable stress and rearrange for $t$

$$
\frac{k \cdot p \cdot s^2}{t^2} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{n}
$$

Solving:

$$
t^2 \geq \frac{k \cdot p \cdot s^2 \cdot n}{\sigma_y}
$$

$$
t \geq s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k \cdot p \cdot n}{\sigma_y}}
$$

---

#### Step 4 — Substitute numerical values

$$
t \geq 600 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.308 \times 0.025 \times 1.5}{215}}
$$

Compute the term inside the square root:

$$
\frac{0.308 \times 0.025 \times 1.5}{215} = \frac{0.01155}{215} \approx 5.37 \times 10^{-5}
$$

Take the square root:

$$
\sqrt{5.37 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 7.33 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Multiply by 600:

$$
t \geq 600 \times 7.33 \times 10^{-3} \approx 4.40 \text{ mm}
$$

---

#### Step 5 — Round up to next standard plate thickness

Standard aluminium plate thicknesses (ISO 6361): 3, 4, **5**, 6, 8, 10 mm...

$$
\boxed{t_{required} = 5 \text{ mm}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Practice:** Always round up to the next commercially available thickness, and add a **corrosion margin of 0.5–1.0 mm** for plate surfaces in contact with seawater (sacrificial thickness lost over the design life).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Ferro-Cement Volume Fraction of Reinforcement

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A 12 m ferro-cement fishing boat has a hull shell of average thickness **20 mm**. The skeletal steel consists of **8 mm dia. bars at 100 mm spacing in both longitudinal and transverse directions**. There are **6 layers of chicken mesh**, each wire **0.7 mm dia** with hexagonal openings of **12.5 mm**. Compute the **volume fraction of steel reinforcement ($V_f$)** and verify it meets the marine specification of $V_f \geq 3\%$.

---

#### Step 1 — Choose a representative unit cell

Take a **100 mm × 100 mm × 20 mm** cube (chosen because it aligns with the skeletal bar spacing).

$$
V_{cube} = 100 \times 100 \times 20 = 200{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

---

#### Step 2 — Volume of skeletal steel within the cube

- Longitudinal bar: 1 bar of $\phi = 8$ mm, length **100 mm**, embedded in the 20 mm thickness (bar runs through full depth of cube at hull interface, but for in-plane volume fraction, consider bar length × cross-section within thickness).

For in-plane volume, the bar length along the cube axis = 100 mm; the cross-sectional area = $\frac{\pi}{4}(8)^2 = 50.27$ mm².

Volume of 1 longitudinal bar (inside cube) = $50.27 \times 100 = 5027$ mm³
Same for 1 transverse bar = 5027 mm³

But the bars intersect at one location, so subtract the overlap (cylinder intersection ≈ cylinder volume of one bar of length equal to its diameter = $\frac{\pi}{4}(8)^2 \times 8 = 402$ mm³):

$$
V_{skeletal} \approx 5027 + 5027 - 402 = 9652 \text{ mm}^3
$$

---

#### Step 3 — Volume of mesh wire within the cube

Each mesh layer has hexagonal openings of **12.5 mm**. The wire diameter is **0.7 mm**.

Approximate wire length per unit cell per layer. For hexagonal mesh with opening $a = 12.5$ mm, each cell has **6 wires of length ≈ $a$** per cell.

Cell area (hexagon) ≈ $\frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} a^2 \approx 2.598 \times (12.5)^2 \approx 406$ mm²

Wires per unit area per layer ≈ $\frac{6 \times 12.5}{406} \approx 0.185$ mm/mm²

Per cube base area (100×100 = 10000 mm²), wire length per layer ≈ $0.185 \times 10000 = 1850$ mm

Wire cross-section area = $\frac{\pi}{4}(0.7)^2 \approx 0.385$ mm²

Wire volume per layer = $1850 \times 0.385 = 712$ mm³

For **6 layers**: $V_{mesh} = 6 \times 712 = 4272$ mm³

---

#### Step 4 — Total steel volume and volume fraction

$$
V_{steel} = V_{skeletal} + V_{mesh} = 9652 + 4272 = 13{,}924 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
V_f = \frac{V_{steel}}{V_{cube}} = \frac{13{,}924}{200{,}000} \approx 0.0696
$$

$$
\boxed{V_f \approx 6.96 \%}
$$

---

#### Step 5 — Verify against marine specification

Specification: $V_f \geq 3\%$ → **PASS** ✓ (actually exceeds by ~2×, indicating a well-reinforced hull).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** A high $V_f$ (>5%) means excellent crack control but adds weight and may lead to corrosion if cover is inadequate. The ideal range is **4–6%** for marine ferro-cement.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Design Parameter | Aluminium (5083) | Ferro-Cement |
|---|---|---|
| **Hull Plate Thickness for 12 m boat** | 5–8 mm | 15–22 mm |
| **Hull Weight per m²** | 13–21 kg/m² | 37–54 kg/m² |
| **Fabrication Cost (per kg of hull)** | ₹₹₹ (High) | ₹ (Very Low) |
| **Required Skill Level** | Skilled welder | Semi-skilled mason |
| **Repair in Remote Areas** | Difficult (no welder) | Easy (cement + mesh) |
| **Corrosion in Seawater** | Excellent (with anodes) | Good (cover thickness > 3 mm) |
| **Impact Resistance** | Moderate (dents) | High (no puncture) |
| **Fatigue Performance** | Excellent | Good |
| **Fire Resistance** | Good (melts at 660 °C) | Excellent |
| **Vibration Damping** | Low | High |
| **Useful Life** | 25–40 years | 20–30 years |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Material Selection Logic for Fishing Vessels

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Fishing Vessel Design] --> B{Vessel Length L?}
    B --> L1["L greater than 18 m"]
    B --> L2["L between 10 to 18 m"]
    B --> L3["L less than 10 m"]
    L1 --> C1{Budget & Speed?}
    C1 --> C1A["High speed required"] --> D1["Aluminium 5083 Hull"]
    C1 --> C1B["Standard speed, high budget"] --> D2["Steel Hull"]
    L2 --> E1{Local Skill & Cost?}
    E1 --> E1A["Skilled welders available"] --> D1
    E1 --> E1B["Limited skills, low budget"] --> F1["Ferro-Cement Hull"]
    E1 --> E1C["Moderate budget"] --> F2["GRP or Wood Hull"]
    L3 --> G1{Purpose?}
    G1 --> G1A["Artisanal daily fishing"] --> F1
    G1 --> G1B["Tourism or day charter"] --> F2
    G1 --> G1C["High performance"] --> D1
    D1 --> H1[End: Aluminium Selected]
    D2 --> H2[End: Steel Selected]
    F1 --> H3[End: Ferro-Cement Selected]
    F2 --> H4[End: GRP or Wood Selected]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cross-Section of a Ferro-Cement Hull Shell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph extLayer[External Layer System]
        nodeA["Antifouling Paint 200 micrometres"]
        nodeB["Epoxy Primer Sealer 100 micrometres"]
        nodeC["Cement Mortar Cover greater than 3 mm"]
    end
    subgraph coreLayer[Composite Core]
        nodeD["Layer 6 Hexagonal Mesh 0.7 mm wire"]
        nodeE["Layer 5 Hexagonal Mesh 0.7 mm wire"]
        nodeF["Layer 4 Hexagonal Mesh 0.7 mm wire"]
        nodeG["Layer 3 Hexagonal Mesh 0.7 mm wire"]
        nodeH["Layer 2 Hexagonal Mesh 0.7 mm wire"]
        nodeI["Layer 1 Hexagonal Mesh 0.7 mm wire"]
    end
    subgraph skelLayer[Skeletal Framework]
        nodeJ["Transverse Rebar 8 mm at 100 mm c/c"]
        nodeK["Longitudinal Rebar 8 mm at 100 mm c/c"]
    end
    subgraph intLayer[Internal Layer System]
        nodeL["Inner Mortar Layer 5 to 8 mm"]
        nodeM["Fairing Compound + White Paint"]
    end
    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG --> nodeH --> nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeK --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeL --> nodeM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** From outside (seawater) inward — antifouling, primer, mortar cover, then 6 layers of mesh interspersed with mortar, the crossing rebar skeleton, then the inner fairing. Total thickness typically 15–25 mm.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Aluminium Hull Plate-Stiffener Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["AA 5083 Hull Plate 5 mm"] --> B["Stiffener Tee Bar 5083 Extrusion"]
    B --> C["Girders and Web Frames"]
    C --> D["Floors and Transverse Rings"]
    D --> E["Keel Plate 8 mm thicker"]
    E --> F["Stem and Stern Frame Aluminium Castings"]
    F --> G["Hull Complete for Fishing Vessel"]
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Design Logic Flow for Plate Thickness

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Define Design Pressure p kN/m²"] --> stepB["Determine Stiffener Spacing s mm"]
    stepB --> stepC["Select Material Grade and Yield Strength"]
    stepC --> stepD["Choose Safety Factor n and Factor k"]
    stepD --> stepE["Apply Plate Bending Formula t equals s times sqrt k p n by sigma_y"]
    stepE --> stepF["Round Up to Standard Plate Thickness"]
    stepF --> stepG["Add Corrosion Margin 0.5 to 1.0 mm"]
    stepG --> stepH["Final Plate Thickness Specified"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
**"List any six advantages of using aluminium alloys in fishing vessel construction."**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Low density (2700 kg/m³)** → lighter hull → higher speed, lower fuel consumption.
2. **High specific strength** → adequate strength at lower weight.
3. **Excellent corrosion resistance** in marine atmosphere due to passive oxide film.
4. **Ease of fabrication** → easily cut, rolled, welded (MIG/TIG).
5. **Non-magnetic** → advantageous for vessels carrying sensitive electronic equipment.
6. **Recyclable** → aluminium scrap retains 95% of original properties → sustainable.
7. *(Bonus)* **Good thermal conductivity** → useful for fish-hold refrigeration plates.

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
**"Define ferro-cement. What are the four essential components of a ferro-cement composite?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Ferro-cement is a composite material consisting of a **cement-sand mortar matrix** reinforced with **closely spaced layers of wire mesh** and a **skeletal framework of reinforcing bars**, used to construct thin-walled hulls with high tensile strength and crack resistance.

**Four Essential Components (any 4 × 0.75 = 3 marks):**

1. **Portland Cement (matrix binder)** – OPC 43/53 grade, mixed with sand.
2. **Fine Aggregate** – clean river sand passing 2.36 mm sieve.
3. **Water** – clean potable water, w/c ratio 0.40–0.45.
4. **Wire Mesh** – hexagonal/chicken/welded mesh, multiple layers.
5. **Skeletal Steel Reinforcement** – rebar cage defining hull shape and primary load path.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Coverage

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **marine-grade aluminium alloys** used in fishing vessel construction. Discuss the designation system, properties of AA 5083, and the various **tempers** (H116, H321) suitable for marine service.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fishing vessel has bottom plating supported by stiffeners at **700 mm spacing**. The design hydrostatic + slamming pressure is **30 kN/m²**. The hull is in **AA 5083-H116** ($\sigma_y = 215$ MPa). Calculate the **minimum required plate thickness** using safety factor $n = 1.5$ and aspect ratio factor $k = 0.308$.

---

#### Model Answer — Question A

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Aluminium Designation System:**
The Aluminium Association (AA) uses a 4-digit system. The **5xxx series** (Al-Mg alloys) is the primary marine group because magnesium provides solid-solution strengthening without compromising weldability or corrosion resistance.

**AA 5083 Composition & Properties:**

- **Composition:** Al + 4.0–4.9 % Mg + 0.4–1.0 % Mn + 0.05–0.25 % Cr + minor impurities.
- **Density:** 2660 kg/m³ (1 mark)
- **Yield Strength:** 215 MPa (1 mark)
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength:** 305 MPa
- **Elongation:** 12% — excellent ductility (1 mark)
- **Young's Modulus:** 70 GPa
- **Fatigue Limit:** 115 MPa in air

**Marine Tempers Explained (2 marks):**

- **H116 temper:** Strain-hardened to a specific degree AND thermally stabilized at a temperature that ensures resistance to **exfoliation corrosion** and **intergranular corrosion** in saltwater. This is the **preferred temper for hull plating**.
- **H321 temper:** Similar to H116, but the strain-hardening is followed by a low-temperature thermal treatment (≈ 240 °C) for additional dimensional stability — used for **machined marine parts** like winch drums and mast components.
- **O temper (annealed):** Soft — used for forming complex shapes, then often re-tempered.

**Why AA 5083 dominates marine use (1 mark):**

- Best combination of strength, weldability (no post-weld heat treatment), and saltwater corrosion resistance.
- Readily available worldwide in plates, extrusions, and sheets.

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

Given:
- Stiffener spacing: $s = 700$ mm
- Design pressure: $p = 30$ kN/m² $= 0.030$ N/mm²
- Yield strength: $\sigma_y = 215$ N/mm²
- Safety factor: $n = 1.5$
- Aspect ratio factor: $k = 0.308$

**Step 1 — Allowable stress:**

$$
\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n} = \frac{215}{1.5} \approx 143.33 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Plate bending formula:**

$$
t = s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k \cdot p \cdot n}{\sigma_y}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
t = 700 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.308 \times 0.030 \times 1.5}{215}}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
0.308 \times 0.030 \times 1.5 = 0.01386
$$

Divide by $\sigma_y$:

$$
\frac{0.01386}{215} \approx 6.45 \times 10^{-5}
$$

Square root:

$$
\sqrt{6.45 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 8.03 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Multiply:

$$
t \approx 700 \times 8.03 \times 10^{-3} \approx 5.62 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Round up + corrosion margin:**

Next standard thickness: 6 mm. Add 1 mm corrosion margin → **7 mm**.

$$
\boxed{t_{final} = 7 \text{ mm}}
$$

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **composition, construction procedure, and mechanical properties** of ferro-cement as a boat-building material. Why is it particularly suited to small-scale fishing vessels in developing maritime economies?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **required hull plate thickness** for a **14 m ferro-cement fishing trawler** using the empirical FAO formula. Also discuss the **crack control mechanism** in ferro-cement and the importance of the **specific surface of reinforcement ($S_r$)**.

---

#### Model Answer — Question B

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Composition (3 marks):**

- **Cement:** OPC 43 grade, ≈ 1 part by volume (provides the binding matrix and alkaline environment that protects steel).
- **Sand:** Clean river sand passing 2.36 mm sieve, 1.5–2 parts by volume (acts as fine aggregate; graded sand reduces voids).
- **Water:** Potable water, w/c ratio 0.40–0.45 (low ratio → dense, low-permeability mortar → crucial for marine durability).
- **Skeletal Steel:** 6–10 mm rebar at 75–150 mm spacing — provides shape and primary tensile strength.
- **Wire Mesh:** 4–8 layers of chicken/hexagonal/welded mesh, wire diameter 0.5–1.0 mm — provides fine crack control.

**Construction Procedure (2 marks):**

1. Build rebar skeleton shaped like the hull on a temporary mold/mound.
2. Wrap mesh layers around skeleton, offsetting each by 50%.
3. Plaster mortar from outside, then inside, working from bottom upward.
4. Cure for 21–28 days under wet hessian.
5. Apply epoxy primer and antifouling paint.

**Mechanical Properties (1 mark):**

- Density: 2400–2500 kg/m³
- Tensile strength: 10–40 MPa (with mesh)
- Compressive strength: 30–60 MPa
- Young's Modulus: 20–30 GPa
- Behaviour: Two-stage cracking; quasi-ductile.

**Suitability for Developing Economies (1 mark):**

- Low material cost (sand, cement, mesh, scrap rebar).
- Requires only semi-skilled labour → creates local employment.
- Repair-friendly in remote coastal villages without shipyards.
- Long lifespan (20–30 years) with basic maintenance.

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply FAO empirical formula:**

$$
t_{fc} = 15 + 0.05 \cdot L_{WL}
$$

With $L_{WL} = 14$ m:

$$
t_{fc} = 15 + 0.05 \times 14 = 15 + 0.70 = 15.70 \text{ mm}
$$

Round to standard practice:

$$
\boxed{t_{fc} = 16 \text{ mm (hull bottom)}}
$$

For sides, typically reduce by 20% → **12–13 mm**.
For deck → **10–12 mm**.

**Step 2 — Crack Control Mechanism (2 marks):**

Ferro-cement is brittle as a matrix, but the **closely spaced mesh** (wires at < 12.5 mm) acts as a **crack arrester**:

- When tensile load is applied, micro-cracks form in mortar at low stress (~5 MPa).
- Mesh wires cross these cracks, **transferring tensile load** across the crack faces.
- Result: cracks remain **< 0.1 mm wide** → water-resistant → no leakage → boat remains watertight.
- As load increases, more micro-cracks form (not one big crack) → **distributed cracking** → energy absorbed → quasi-ductile behaviour.

**Step 3 — Importance of $S_r$ (2 marks):**

Specific surface of reinforcement $S_r$ = total steel surface area per unit volume of composite.

$$
S_r = \frac{A_{steel}}{V_{composite}}
$$

- Higher $S_r$ → more wires per unit volume → **finer crack spacing** → narrower cracks → better water-tightness.
- Marine specification: $S_r \geq 0.25$ mm²/mm³.
- For a 16 mm thick hull with 6 mesh layers + 8 mm skeletal bars at 100 mm spacing, $S_r \approx 0.30–0.40$ mm²/mm³ → **exceeds specification**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Common mistakes students make in ferro-cement calculations:
> 1. **Forgetting the w/c ratio** — using > 0.5 leads to porous mortar → seawater ingress → corrosion of mesh → hull failure within 5 years.
> 2. **Skipping the corrosion cover** — minimum 3 mm mortar cover over outer mesh is mandatory; anything less leads to mesh corrosion.
> 3. **Confusing $V_f$ with $S_r$** — they are different metrics! $V_f$ is volume ratio; $S_r$ is surface area per unit volume.
> 4. **Not specifying curing time** — ferro-cement needs minimum **21 days wet cure**; premature loading causes shrinkage cracks.
> 5. **In aluminium questions:** Forgetting to convert kN/m² to N/mm² → dimensional error → entire answer marked wrong.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — Read 5 minutes before exam:**

- **Aluminium:** Marine workhorse = **AA 5083-H116**, $\sigma_y = 215$ MPa, $\rho = 2660$ kg/m³, $E = 70$ GPa.
- **Ferro-Cement:** Composite of **cement mortar + multiple mesh layers + skeletal rebar**; $t_{hull} \approx 15–25$ mm.
- **Plate thickness formula (Al):** $t = s \cdot \sqrt{k \cdot p \cdot n / \sigma_y}$, with $k \approx 0.308$.
- **Ferro-cement thickness formula (FAO):** $t_{fc} = 15 + 0.05 \cdot L_{WL}$ (in mm, $L_{WL}$ in m).
- **Aluminium advantage:** Lightest hull material → **highest speed, lowest fuel burn**; requires skilled welding.
- **Ferro-cement advantage:** **Lowest cost**, semi-skilled labour, easy remote repair → ideal for artisanal fleets.
- **Aluminium disadvantage:** Expensive, low stiffness → buckling prone, requires anode protection.
- **Ferro-cement disadvantage:** **Heaviest** hull, requires long curing time, crack-prone if poorly cured.
- **Critical marine parameters for ferro-cement:** $V_f \geq 3\%$, $S_r \geq 0.25$ mm²/mm³, mortar cover $\geq 3$ mm.
- **5xxx series Al alloys** = Al-Mg = marine use. **6xxx series** = Al-Mg-Si = extrusions.
- **H116 / H321 tempers** = mandatory for marine corrosion resistance (resists exfoliation & intergranular corrosion).
- **MIG/TIG welding** preferred for aluminium; **flux-cored arc welding (FCAW)** avoided due to porosity.
- **Galvanic corrosion:** Aluminium is anodic → pair with **zinc sacrificial anodes**, not copper/bronze directly.
- **Ferro-cement cure:** Minimum **21 days wet hessian** + 7 days drying before launching.
- **For FAO/syllabus:** Aluminium best for > 15 m trawlers; ferro-cement best for < 15 m artisanal boats.
- **Density memory:** Aluminium **2.66**, Steel **7.85**, Ferro-cement **2.45**, GRP **1.50** — all in **g/cm³** × 1000 = **kg/m³**.
- **Always** convert kN/m² → N/mm² (divide by $10^6$) before substituting into plate bending equations.
